items.question: Ich _____ Deutsch.
items.explanation: Welcome to German verb conjugation, where every person has its own unique ending. In English, you say 'I speak' with minimal change across persons. German transforms the verb for each person. 'Sprechen' (to speak) becomes 'spreche' for 'ich' (I). The -e ending signals first person singular, like a signature identifying who is speaking. Unlike English where 'I/you/we/they speak' looks the same, German gives each person its distinct form. This richness means you can often drop the pronoun in German - just 'spreche' tells everyone it's 'I'. The verb carries all the information English needs a pronoun for.

items.question: Du _____ in Berlin.
items.explanation: For 'du' (informal you), German uses the -st ending. 'Wohnen' (to live) becomes 'wohnst'. English 'you live' is the same whether speaking to a friend or a stranger. German distinguishes: 'du' for casual relationships, 'Sie' for formal ones. The verb ending changes accordingly. 'Wohnst' immediately tells a German speaker this is informal conversation. The -st ending is your marker for second person singular informal. This formality distinction runs deep in German - it affects not just verbs but pronouns, possessives, and the entire tone of conversation. Choosing du versus Sie is a social decision, not just grammatical.

items.question: Sie _____ Kaffee.
items.explanation: Third person singular uses the -t ending. 'Trinken' (to drink) becomes 'trinkt' for er/sie/es (he/she/it). English adds -s to make 'drinks'. German uses -t as its third person marker. This -t ending serves for he, she, it, and formal you (Sie, when capitalized). Context and pronouns tell you which one. The beauty is in the precision - every ending maps to specific persons. 'Sie trinkt' (she drinks) versus 'Sie trinken' (they drink or formal you drink). The same pronoun 'Sie' but different verb endings. This might seem complex at first, but it allows for nuanced, precise communication.

items.question: Wir _____ Deutsch.
items.explanation: First person plural takes the -en ending. 'Lernen' (to study/learn) stays as 'lernen' for 'wir'. English 'we study' uses the base form. German 'lernen' happens to look like the infinitive, but this is the conjugated first person plural form. For 'wir', 'sie' (they), and formal 'Sie', the ending is -en. This -en pattern appears across all regular verbs. The consistency helps - once you learn the pattern for one verb, you can apply it to hundreds. 'Wir' can be dropped in conversation because 'lernen' alone identifies the subject clearly through its ending.

items.question: Ihr _____ zu Hause.
items.explanation: Second person plural uses the -t ending. 'Arbeiten' (to work) becomes 'arbeitet' for 'ihr' (you all, informal plural). English 'you work' is the same for singular and plural. German distinguishes: du (one person), ihr (multiple people), Sie (formal, any number). The verb ending changes for each. 'Arbeitet' tells you immediately this is informal plural. German maintains this plural informal form that English lost centuries ago. It creates a warm, collective feeling when addressing a group of friends or family. This form is alive and well in modern German, unlike English's archaic 'ye'.

items.question: Sie _____ Fußball.
items.explanation: Third person plural uses the -en ending. 'Spielen' (to play) stays as 'spielen' for 'sie' (they). The same ending works for formal 'Sie' (you, formal). English 'they play' is simple. German 'spielen' immediately signals multiple people or formal address. This -en ending is your marker for plural third person and formal second person. The pattern is consistent - 'wir/sie/Sie' all share -en. This consistency within plurality helps memory. Once you recognize -en as the plural/formal marker, you can decode and produce correct forms across hundreds of verbs.

items.question: Er _____ ein Buch.
items.explanation: 'Lesen' (to read) is irregular in present tense. The stem changes from 'les-' to 'lies-' for du/er/sie/es. 'Er liest' shows this vowel shift. English 'reads' just adds -s. German vowel changes are common in strong verbs. They make the language melodic but require memorization. The good news: these changes are predictable within verb families. Many verbs follow similar patterns. 'Lesen' becomes 'liest', 'sehen' (to see) becomes 'sieht'. The e→ie pattern repeats. Learning one helps you recognize others.

items.question: Ich _____ gern.
items.explanation: 'Essen' (to eat) changes its stem in present tense. First person 'ich esse' keeps the -e ending but the stem shifts. English 'eat' stays regular. German strong verbs undergo vowel changes that make them more expressive. These changes have historical roots in Old German. While they complicate learning, they add character to the language. 'Essen' is one of the most common verbs, so its forms become automatic quickly. The irregularity is worth mastering for such a frequently used verb.

items.question: Du _____ nach Hause.
items.explanation: 'Gehen' (to go) is regular but important. 'Du gehst' uses the standard -st ending. English 'you go' is straightforward. German 'gehst' embeds the person marker. Movement verbs like 'gehen', 'kommen', 'fahren' are fundamental in German. They combine with prefixes to create countless meanings. 'Gehen' is versatile and frequent. Master its conjugation and you unlock many expressions of movement and action.

items.question: Wir _____ Musik.
items.explanation: 'Hören' (to hear/listen) uses standard first person plural -en. 'Wir hören' is straightforward. The umlaut (ö) is a distinct German feature. It changes the vowel sound and sometimes meaning. 'Hören' vs 'horen' would be different words if 'horen' existed. Umlauts are integral to German pronunciation and spelling. They're not decorative but functional. Learning to recognize and produce them is essential for clear German communication.

items.question: Sie _____ Englisch.
items.explanation: Formal 'Sie' uses the -en ending, same as 'wir' and plural 'sie'. 'Sie sprechen' could mean 'you speak' (formal) or 'they speak'. Context clarifies. Capitalized 'Sie' indicates formal you. This formality system is deeply embedded in German culture. Using 'Sie' shows respect for strangers, elders, and professional contexts. The verb form matches the respect shown in the pronoun choice. Formal address is not just polite but expected in many situations.

items.question: Es _____ kalt.
items.explanation: 'Sein' (to be) is highly irregular. 'Es ist' means 'it is'. English 'is' is also irregular but simpler. German 'sein' has completely different forms for each person: bin, bist, ist, sind, seid, sind. These must be memorized - no pattern helps. As the most fundamental verb, this irregularity is unavoidable. The good news: 'sein' is so frequent that its forms become automatic through exposure. Every German conversation uses it repeatedly.

items.question: Ihr _____ müde.
items.explanation: 'Ihr seid' is the second person plural of 'sein'. English 'you are' is the same for singular and plural. German distinguishes with 'seid' specifically for 'ihr'. This form is unique to 'ihr' - no other verb has exactly this ending. The irregularity of 'sein' extends to all its forms. 'Seid' is phonetically distinct, making 'ihr' addresses clear. This form carries a warm, familiar tone when used with friends or family.

items.question: Du _____ ein Student.
items.explanation: 'Du bist' is second person singular of 'sein'. English 'you are' doesn't change. German 'bist' is distinct to 'du', signaling informal address. Professions in German often use indefinite articles where English might omit them. 'Ein Student' translates literally as 'a student'. Both languages align here. The 'sein' verb is fundamental for identity statements. Master its irregular forms and you can express who and what things are across all contexts.

items.question: Wir _____ Freunde.
items.explanation: 'Wir sind' uses the first person plural of 'sein'. English 'we are' is straightforward. German 'sind' also works for third person plural 'sie' and formal 'Sie'. This overlap might confuse at first, but context always clarifies. 'Freunde' is plural (friends), matching 'wir'. German grammar emphasizes agreement between subject and verb, subject and predicate. These agreements reinforce meaning and help comprehension. They're not obstacles but aids to clarity.

items.question: _____ Mann ist groß.
items.explanation: German nouns have grammatical gender. 'Mann' (man) is masculine, taking 'der' in nominative case. English 'the' works for everything. German has three genders: masculine (der), feminine (die), neuter (das). Learning noun genders is essential - they affect articles, adjectives, and pronouns. There are patterns: -ung endings are feminine, -chen endings are neuter. But many nouns must be memorized with their gender. Think of gender as part of the noun itself. When learning 'Mann', learn 'der Mann' as one unit.

items.question: _____ Frau ist nett.
items.explanation: 'Frau' (woman) is feminine, taking 'die'. English 'the' doesn't distinguish. German 'die' marks feminine nouns in nominative. Feminine nouns often (but not always) refer to female beings or have certain endings like -heit, -keit, -ung. 'Die' appears in all feminine nominative contexts. This gendered article system seems complex but becomes intuitive with exposure. Native speakers don't think about it - it sounds right. You'll develop this instinct too with practice.

items.question: _____ Kind spielt.
items.explanation: 'Kind' (child) is neuter, taking 'das'. English 'the' is universal. German neuter is the third gender category. Many nouns referring to young beings are neuter: das Kind, das Baby, das Mädchen (girl). Diminutives with -chen or -lein are always neuter. 'Das' signals neuter gender in nominative. While it seems arbitrary that 'girl' (Mädchen) is neuter not feminine, these are grammatical categories, not biological. Grammatical gender is a classification system, not a statement about reality.

items.question: Ich sehe _____ Mann.
items.explanation: Accusative case changes masculine articles. 'Der Mann' becomes 'den Mann' as direct object. English 'the' doesn't change. German articles decline based on case (function in sentence). Accusative marks direct objects. Masculine changes der→den, but feminine and neuter stay the same (die, das). This case system seems daunting but it clarifies meaning. Word order can flex in German because cases show grammatical function. Cases give German precision English achieves through word order.

items.question: Ich gebe _____ Mann das Buch.
items.explanation: Dative case shows indirect objects. 'Dem Mann' means 'to the man'. English uses word order or 'to'. German uses dative: der→dem, die→der, das→dem. Dative appears with indirect objects and certain prepositions. It's the case of receiving or benefiting. Many verbs like 'helfen' (to help) take dative objects. The dative case embeds relationships between words. It shows who receives or benefits from actions. This precision helps when word order becomes flexible.

items.question: Das ist _____ Buch des Mannes.
items.translation: (That is the book of the man)
items.explanation: Genitive case shows possession. 'Des Mannes' means 'of the man'. English uses 'of' or possessive 's. German uses genitive: der→des, die→der, das→des. Nouns often add -s or -es in genitive. This case appears less in spoken German, replaced by 'von' + dative. But in formal writing, genitive is proper. Understanding it helps you read German literature and formal texts. Genitive adds sophistication and clarity to possessive relationships.

items.question: Ich kaufe _____ Buch.
items.explanation: Indefinite articles also have gender. 'Ein Buch' uses neuter form. English 'a/an' doesn't change. German indefinite articles: ein (masc/neut), eine (fem). Like definite articles, they decline through cases. Accusative masculine becomes 'einen', but neuter stays 'ein'. Learning articles means learning four forms for definite (der/die/das + plurals) and three for indefinite (ein/eine, no plural form). This system provides grammatical information in every noun phrase.

items.question: Das ist _____ Katze.
items.explanation: 'Katze' (cat) is feminine, taking 'eine'. English 'a' doesn't distinguish gender. German indefinite feminine is always 'eine' in nominative/accusative. Animals have grammatical gender in German. 'Der Hund' (dog) is masculine, 'die Katze' is feminine, 'das Pferd' (horse) is neuter. These don't reflect animal biology - they're grammatical classifications. The gender affects all modifiers. Learning animal words means learning their genders.

items.question: Ich habe _____ Frage.
items.explanation: 'Frage' (question) is feminine. Accusative feminine stays 'eine'. English 'a' doesn't change. Unlike masculine articles which change in accusative (ein→einen), feminine indefinite stays 'eine'. This consistency helps - one less form to remember. Feminine and neuter indefinite articles don't change in accusative. Only masculine transforms. This pattern reduces the memorization burden.

items.question: _____ Bücher sind interessant.
items.explanation: Plural articles are 'die' for all genders. English 'the' is the same. German simplifies in plural - all nouns take 'die' regardless of singular gender. Der Mann→die Männer, die Frau→die Frauen, das Kind→die Kinder. This unification in plural is a relief after tracking three genders in singular. Plural 'die' is your friend - one form covers everything. In plural, gender distinctions disappear.

items.question: Ich mag _____ Musik.
items.explanation: Abstract nouns often take definite articles in German. 'Die Musik' uses the article where English omits it. German treats abstractions as definite entities. 'Die Liebe' (love), 'das Leben' (life), 'die Freiheit' (freedom) - all use articles. This feels strange to English speakers who say 'I like music' not 'the music'. German's article usage is broader. When generalizing about a category, German includes the article. This is a key difference from English article patterns.

items.question: Er spielt _____ Fußball.
items.explanation: Sports and games omit articles. 'Fußball spielen' (to play football) uses no article. English also omits it. Both languages agree: sports don't need articles. 'Tennis spielen', 'Schach spielen' (play chess) - no articles. But instruments need them: 'Klavier spielen' (play piano) actually says 'play the piano' in German too. This parallel with English makes sports vocabulary easier to master. The pattern is intuitive for English speakers.

items.question: Ich spreche _____ Deutsch.
items.explanation: Languages as objects of 'sprechen' (speak) omit articles. 'Ich spreche Deutsch' matches English 'I speak German'. Neither uses articles with languages in this context. But when referring to the language as a subject: 'Das Deutsche ist schwer' (German [language] is difficult). Article patterns with languages depend on grammatical role. When speaking a language, no article. When discussing it as a thing, article appears. Context determines usage.

items.question: _____ Sonne scheint.
items.explanation: Unique natural phenomena take definite articles. 'Die Sonne' (the sun), 'der Mond' (the moon), 'die Erde' (the Earth). English also uses articles with these. Both languages treat unique celestial bodies as definite. The gender must be learned: Sonne is feminine, Mond is masculine, Erde is feminine. These words are fundamental and worth memorizing with their genders. Natural phenomena are common in conversations about weather and nature.

items.question: Ich gehe in _____ Schule.
items.explanation: 'In die Schule' means 'to school'. The preposition 'in' with accusative (motion toward) uses 'die'. 'Schule' is feminine. Prepositions govern cases in German. 'In' takes accusative for motion toward, dative for location. 'Ich bin in der Schule' (I am in school - dative location) vs 'Ich gehe in die Schule' (I go to school - accusative motion). This distinction shows direction vs location. The case system embeds meaning into the grammar.

items.question: Ich _____ ein Buch gelesen.
items.explanation: German present perfect uses haben/sein + past participle. 'Ich habe gelesen' translates as 'I have read' or 'I read' depending on context. German present perfect functions like English simple past for most contexts. Where English distinguishes 'I read' (past) from 'I have read' (present perfect), German uses 'ich habe gelesen' for both. This tense is preferred in conversation over simple past. The past participle 'gelesen' comes from 'lesen' (to read). Most past participles start with ge- and end in -t or -en.

items.question: Sie _____ nach Hause gegangen.
items.explanation: Motion verbs use 'sein' instead of 'haben'. 'Sie ist gegangen' means 'she has gone' or 'she went'. English uses 'has/have' for all verbs. German splits: most verbs use 'haben', motion/change verbs use 'sein'. Common sein-verbs: gehen (go), kommen (come), fahren (drive), werden (become), sein (be), bleiben (stay). Learning which verbs take sein is crucial. The pattern: movement from A to B or change of state → sein. All other actions → haben.

items.question: Wir _____ Deutsch gelernt.
items.explanation: 'Haben' is used for most transitive verbs. 'Wir haben gelernt' covers English 'we learned' or 'we have learned'. German collapses this distinction in conversation. The past participle 'gelernt' is regular: lernen → ge+lern+t. Regular verbs follow this pattern predictably. German present perfect is the conversational past - you'll hear it constantly. Simple past is more common in writing and formal narratives. But in daily speech, present perfect dominates.

items.question: Er _____ gekommen.
items.explanation: 'Kommen' uses sein as auxiliary. 'Er ist gekommen' means 'he came' or 'he has come'. This movement toward the speaker makes kommen a classic sein-verb. The past participle 'gekommen' is irregular - from kommen (to come). Many common verbs have irregular past participles. These must be memorized, but frequency makes them automatic. Sein-verbs represent a minority - most verbs use haben. The pattern is consistent once learned.

items.question: Ich _____ jung.
items.explanation: Simple past of 'sein' (to be) is commonly used even in conversation. 'Ich war' means 'I was'. Unlike other verbs, sein's simple past is preferred over present perfect. You'll hear 'war' more than 'bin gewesen'. The same goes for 'haben': 'hatte' (had) is more common than 'habe gehabt'. These high-frequency verbs break the general rule favoring present perfect. Their simple past forms are so ingrained in speech that present perfect sounds awkward.

items.question: Sie _____ ein Auto.
items.explanation: Simple past of 'haben' is 'hatte'. 'Sie hatte' means 'she had'. Like sein, haben's simple past is preferred in speech. 'Hatte' is more natural than 'hat gehabt'. These auxiliary verbs are fundamental - you use them to build all perfect tenses. Their simple past forms are short and frequent, making them stick in memory. English 'had' parallels German 'hatte' closely in usage and frequency.

items.question: Wir _____ nach Berlin gefahren.
items.explanation: 'Fahren' (to drive/travel) uses sein. 'Wir sind gefahren' means 'we drove' or 'we have driven'. The motion from one place to another triggers sein. 'Gefahren' is the past participle of fahren. This strong verb changes its stem: fahren → fuhr → gefahren. Strong verbs have vowel changes that must be memorized. But patterns exist: many strong verbs follow similar paths. Learning one helps recognize others.

items.question: Ihr _____ das gesehen.
items.explanation: 'Sehen' (to see) takes haben. 'Ihr habt gesehen' means 'you all saw' or 'you all have seen'. The past participle 'gesehen' is irregular - from sehen (to see). The ihr-form of haben is 'habt'. Each person needs its form: ich habe, du hast, er/sie/es hat, wir haben, ihr habt, sie/Sie haben. These forms must become automatic - they're used constantly in present perfect construction.

items.question: Sie _____ Arzt geworden.
items.explanation: 'Werden' (to become) uses sein. 'Er ist geworden' means 'he became' or 'he has become'. Change of state verbs take sein. Becoming something is a change, so sein is required. The past participle 'geworden' drops the ge- when used as passive auxiliary, but keeps it in present perfect. This nuance matters in advanced German. For now: werden + sein for present perfect.

items.question: Ich _____ das Buch gekauft.
items.explanation: 'Kaufen' (to buy) uses haben. 'Ich habe gekauft' means 'I bought' or 'I have bought'. Regular past participle: kaufen → ge+kauf+t. Transitive verbs (taking direct objects) virtually always use haben. The pattern is reliable: if the verb can have an accusative object, it takes haben. This rule covers most verbs you'll encounter. Sein-verbs are the exception, not the norm.

items.question: Du _____ spät aufgestanden.
items.explanation: 'Aufstehen' (to get up) uses sein. 'Du bist aufgestanden' means 'you got up'. Separable prefix verbs place ge- between prefix and stem: auf-ge-stand-en. This construction looks odd at first but follows logical rules. The verb's core is 'stehen' (to stand) which can use sein (standing up is motion). The prefix 'auf' separates in present tense but reunites with past participle. Separable verbs require attention to where ge- lands.

items.question: Wir _____ Fußball gespielt.
items.explanation: 'Spielen' (to play) uses haben. 'Wir haben gespielt' means 'we played'. Regular past participle follows the pattern: spielen → ge+spiel+t. Sports and games are activities, not motion, so haben is required. This distinction between activities (haben) and motion (sein) is key to mastering German past tenses. Once internalized, auxiliary selection becomes intuitive.

items.question: Sie _____ zu Hause geblieben.
items.explanation: 'Bleiben' (to stay/remain) uses sein despite not involving movement. 'Sie ist geblieben' means 'she stayed'. Bleiben is sein-verb because staying is a state change (from being elsewhere to being here). It's one of the exceptions that proves sein isn't only for physical motion. Change of condition also triggers sein. The past participle 'geblieben' is irregular - from bleiben (to stay).

items.question: Ihr _____ schnell gelaufen.
items.explanation: 'Laufen' (to run) uses sein. 'Ihr seid gelaufen' means 'you all ran'. Running implies movement through space, triggering sein. The past participle 'gelaufen' is irregular - from laufen (to run). Strong verbs like laufen undergo vowel changes: laufen → lief → gelaufen. These changes follow historical patterns from Old German. While seemingly arbitrary, they're linguistically systematic.

items.question: Ich _____ müde gewesen.
items.explanation: 'Sein' as main verb uses itself as auxiliary. 'Ich bin gewesen' means 'I was' or 'I have been'. However, simple past 'ich war' is more common in speech. Present perfect 'bin gewesen' sounds formal or emphatic. Both are grammatically correct. German gives you options for expressing past states. Simple past for sein and haben feels more natural in conversation. Present perfect for other verbs is conversational standard.

items.question: Ich _____ heute ins Kino.
items.translation: (I am going to the cinema today)
items.explanation: German main clauses follow V2 rule: verb is second element. 'Ich gehe heute ins Kino' places verb second. English fixes verb after subject. German allows flexibility but verb stays second. This means you can start with other elements: 'Heute gehe ich ins Kino' (today I'm going to cinema). Time or place can come first, but verb remains second element. V2 rule is fundamental to German sentence structure. It creates the characteristic rhythm of German speech.

items.question: Gestern _____ ich ein Buch.
items.explanation: Time expressions can start sentences. 'Gestern las ich' inverts subject and verb because 'gestern' occupies first position. Verb must be second, so subject follows. This inversion is automatic in German when anything other than subject starts the sentence. English maintains subject-verb order regardless: 'Yesterday I read'. German's flexibility allows emphasis through word order. Starting with 'gestern' emphasizes time. V2 rule creates this characteristic inversion.

items.question: Ich weiß, dass er _____ kommt.
items.explanation: Subordinate clauses send verb to end. 'Dass er heute kommt' places 'kommt' last. English keeps standard order: 'that he is coming'. German subordinate clause order is Subject-Modifiers-Verb. The conjunction 'dass' (that) triggers this reordering. All subordinate conjunctions (weil, wenn, obwohl, etc.) cause verb-final position. This is one of German's most distinctive features. The wait for the verb creates suspense in complex sentences.

items.question: Wann _____ du nach Hause?
items.explanation: Questions starting with question words invert subject-verb. 'Wann gehst du' places verb second, subject third. English also inverts but uses auxiliary: 'when are you going'. German main verb moves directly. Yes/no questions start with verb: 'Gehst du nach Hause?' (Are you going home?). W-questions (wann, wo, wie, etc.) place question word first, verb second. This inversion is automatic and similar to English patterns.

items.question: Ich habe das Buch _____ gelesen.
items.explanation: In perfect tenses, auxiliary verb is second, past participle goes to end. 'Ich habe das Buch gestern gelesen' creates frame: haben...gelesen brackets the sentence. Time expressions fit between auxiliary and participle. This 'verbal bracket' (Satzklammer) is central to German syntax. Elements nest inside the frame formed by verb parts. German sentences build toward the final verb, creating anticipation. The structure is orderly once you grasp the pattern.

items.question: Morgen _____ ich nach Berlin.
items.explanation: Time expressions starting sentences trigger inversion. 'Morgen fahre ich' maintains V2 rule. Verb second, subject third. This flexibility lets German emphasize different elements. Starting with time emphasizes when. Starting with place emphasizes where. Starting with subject emphasizes who. German word order is more flexible than English but follows strict rules. V2 rule governs main clauses absolutely. This rule is non-negotiable in declarative sentences.

items.question: Ich kann nicht _____, weil ich krank bin.
items.translation: (I cannot come because I am sick)
items.explanation: Modal verbs send infinitives to end of clause. 'Ich kann nicht kommen' places 'kommen' last. Modal (kann) is finite verb in second position. Infinitive goes to end. This creates another verbal bracket. In subordinate clause 'weil ich krank bin', verb 'bin' goes to end. German stacks verbs at clause ends systematically. This clustering is distinctive and must be mastered for fluency.

items.question: _____ du mir das Buch?
items.explanation: Yes/no questions start with verb. 'Gibst du mir das Buch?' places verb first. English uses auxiliary: 'Will you give me the book?'. German main verb moves to first position directly. No auxiliary needed. This verb-first structure immediately signals a question. The intonation rises at the end. Verb-first order is unambiguous question marker. The pattern is simple and consistent.

items.question: Ich möchte ins Kino _____, aber ich habe keine Zeit.
items.translation: (I would like to go to the cinema, but I have no time)
items.explanation: Modal + infinitive: infinitive to end. 'Möchte...gehen' brackets the clause. 'Aber' (but) starts new main clause, so normal V2 order resumes: 'ich habe keine Zeit'. Coordinating conjunctions (und, aber, oder, denn) don't affect word order. They simply link clauses. Subordinating conjunctions (dass, weil, wenn) send verb to end. This distinction between coordinating and subordinating is crucial for German syntax.

items.question: Wenn es regnet, _____ ich zu Hause.
items.explanation: Subordinate clause first inverts main clause. 'Wenn es regnet' is complete clause in first position. Main clause verb must be second element of overall sentence. So: 'bleibe ich'. The subordinate clause counts as first element. This creates verb-verb sequence: regnet, bleibe. It sounds odd but is correct. When subordinate clause leads, main clause inverts. This pattern is consistent and must be mastered.

items.question: Ich glaube, dass das Buch _____ interessant ist.
items.translation: (I believe that the book is very interesting)
items.explanation: Subordinate clause order: Subject-Modifiers-Predicate-Verb. 'Dass das Buch sehr interessant ist' places 'ist' last. Adjective 'interessant' with modifier 'sehr' precede verb. Everything piles up before final verb. This creates long sequences before you reach the verb. German speakers are accustomed to holding information in memory until the verb arrives. This patience is essential for comprehension. The payoff: German allows complex ideas in single subordinate clauses.

items.question: _____ du das verstehst, ist gut.
items.explanation: 'Dass' starts subordinate clause functioning as subject. 'Dass du das verstehst' is entire subject of 'ist gut'. This noun clause structure is common in German. The subordinate clause can be subject, object, or predicate. Word order in subordinate clause: verb final. Main clause verb is second: 'ist'. The subordinate clause counts as first element, so 'ist' is second. German syntax nests clauses with precision.

items.question: Ich habe ihn _____ gesehen, als ich einkaufen war.
items.translation: (I saw him when I was shopping)
items.explanation: Negation 'nicht' usually comes before the element negated or before infinitive/participle. 'Habe ihn nicht gesehen' places nicht before gesehen. Time expression 'als ich einkaufen war' is subordinate clause with verb final. German negation placement is flexible but follows patterns. Nicht typically comes late in sentence, before verb cluster. This differs from English 'not' which comes early. German back-loads important information.

items.question: Wir wollen heute Abend ins Restaurant _____.
items.translation: (We want to go to the restaurant tonight)
items.explanation: Modal + infinitive: infinitive to end. 'Wollen...gehen' brackets the clause. Time 'heute Abend' and place 'ins Restaurant' fit between. German's verbal bracket creates clear sentence frame. Everything nests inside this frame. The bracket is so fundamental that German is called a 'bracket language' (Klammersprache). Understanding the bracket unlocks German sentence structure. It organizes all the flexibility German allows.

items.question: Nachdem ich _____ hatte, ging ich schlafen.
items.translation: (After I had eaten, I went to sleep)
items.explanation: Subordinate clause with past perfect: auxiliary to end in subordinate clause. 'Nachdem ich gegessen hatte' places 'hatte' last. Perfect tenses in subordinate clauses stack: participle before auxiliary at end. Main clause 'ging ich schlafen' uses simple past. This sentence shows multiple German word order rules interacting. Subordinate: verb-final. Main: V2 with inversion after subordinate clause. Past perfect in subordinate: participle-auxiliary. Mastering these patterns takes practice but becomes natural.

items.question: _____ Mann ist hier.
items.explanation: Nominative case marks the subject. 'Der Mann' is subject of 'ist'. English 'the' doesn't change by function. German articles reflect grammatical role through case. Nominative is the base form: der/die/das. It answers 'wer?' (who?) or 'was?' (what?). Every sentence has a nominative subject (unless impersonal). Recognizing nominative is first step in German grammar. It's the foundation from which other cases deviate.

items.question: Ich sehe _____ Mann.
items.explanation: Accusative marks direct objects. 'Den Mann' receives the action of 'sehen'. English 'the' doesn't change. German masculine article changes: der→den. Feminine and neuter stay same (die, das). Accusative answers 'wen?' (whom?) or 'was?' (what?). Most transitive verbs take accusative objects. This case is fundamental for expressing who/what receives action. Memorizing masculine accusative 'den' is essential.

items.question: Ich helfe _____ Mann.
items.explanation: Dative marks indirect objects. 'Dem Mann' is recipient of help. English uses word order or 'to'. German uses dative: der→dem, die→der, das→dem. Dative answers 'wem?' (to whom?). Certain verbs always take dative: helfen, danken, folgen, gehören. Learning dative verbs is crucial. They break the pattern of accusative direct objects. Dative shows relationships of benefit, possession, or involvement.

items.question: Das ist das Buch _____ Mannes.
items.translation: (That is the book of the man/the man's book)
items.explanation: Genitive marks possession. 'Des Mannes' means 'of the man'. English uses 's or 'of'. German uses genitive: der→des, die→der, das→des. Masculine/neuter nouns often add -s/-es. Genitive answers 'wessen?' (whose?). While declining in casual speech (replaced by 'von'+dative), genitive remains standard in writing. It adds formality and precision. Understanding genitive helps read literature and formal texts.

items.question: Ich gebe _____ Frau das Buch.
items.explanation: Feminine dative is 'der'. 'Der Frau' is indirect object. Die→der in dative. This change can confuse because 'der' is also masculine nominative. Context clarifies: after 'geben', indirect object needs dative. 'Das Buch' is accusative direct object. German allows both objects in one sentence elegantly. Case endings make grammatical relationships clear regardless of word order.

items.question: Ich kaufe _____ Auto.
items.explanation: Neuter accusative stays 'ein'. Unlike masculine (ein→einen), neuter indefinite doesn't change in accusative. 'Ein Auto' looks same as nominative. This simplification helps - one less form to memorize. Only masculine articles change in accusative. Feminine and neuter remain stable. This pattern reduces cognitive load once recognized.

items.question: Das gehört _____ Kind.
items.explanation: 'Gehören' takes dative. 'Dem Kind' shows neuter dative. Das→dem in dative. Belonging expresses relationship best shown through dative. The case of possession/involvement. 'Gehören' is always dative - memorize this with the verb. Certain verbs have case requirements that don't follow logical patterns. These must be learned as properties of individual verbs.

items.question: Ich sehe _____ Kinder.
items.explanation: Plural accusative is 'die' for all genders. 'Die Kinder' is plural accusative. Same as nominative plural. German simplifies in plural - articles don't change between nominative and accusative. Only dative plural adds -n to article (den Kindern). This consistency in plural is relief after tracking gender changes in singular. Plural 'die' is universal for nominative and accusative.

items.question: Ich danke _____ Lehrerin.
items.explanation: 'Danken' takes dative. 'Der Lehrerin' is feminine dative. Die→der for dative. Thanking someone expresses relationship shown through dative. English 'thank you' uses accusative logic, German 'danken' requires dative. These verb-case requirements must be memorized. They're not always intuitive but become automatic with exposure. Dative verbs are finite list worth learning.

items.question: Das ist der Hund _____ Nachbarn.
items.explanation: Genitive shows possession. 'Des Nachbarn' means 'of the neighbor'. Masculine genitive des + noun often adds -n or -en. 'Nachbar' becomes 'Nachbarn' in genitive. This noun ending with case is distinctive German feature. Not just articles decline - nouns do too, especially in genitive/dative. Genitive adds -s/-es to most masculines/neuters, sometimes -n/-en. These endings reinforce the possessive relationship.

items.question: Ich schreibe _____ Brief.
items.explanation: Masculine accusative indefinite is 'einen'. 'Einen Brief' is direct object. Ein→einen for masculine accusative. This is the key masculine accusative marker. Brief is masculine (der Brief), so accusative needs 'einen'. Gender determines which articles change in accusative. Only masculine transforms. This pattern must become automatic for correct German.

items.question: Das ist das Zimmer _____ Mädchens.
items.explanation: Neuter genitive is 'des'. 'Des Mädchens' shows possession. Das→des + noun adds -s. 'Mädchen' is neuter despite meaning girl. Diminutives (-chen, -lein) are always neuter. Genitive construction shows whose room. Alternative: 'das Zimmer von dem Mädchen' (from+dative). Modern German often prefers this, but genitive remains standard in writing.

items.question: Ich vertraue _____ Freund.
items.explanation: 'Vertrauen' takes dative. 'Dem Freund' is masculine dative. Der→dem for dative. Trust expresses relationship shown through dative. These dative-only verbs must be learned. They include: helfen, danken, gefallen, gehören, folgen, vertrauen, glauben. The pattern: verbs of benefit, possession, or relationship often take dative. This makes semantic sense once internalized.

items.question: Ich frage _____ Studentin.
items.explanation: 'Fragen' takes accusative. 'Die Studentin' is direct object. Feminine accusative stays 'die'. Same as nominative. Feminine articles simplify case learning - die stays die in nominative and accusative. Only in dative does it change (der). This stability helps. When you see feminine article unchanged, it's either nominative or accusative. Context determines which.

items.question: Das ist das Ende _____ Films.
items.translation: (That is the end of the film)
items.explanation: Genitive possession. 'Des Films' means 'of the film'. Masculine genitive des + -s on noun. 'Film' is masculine (der Film), genitive is 'des Films'. This construction is formal. Casual German might say 'das Ende vom Film' (from+dative). But genitive is proper German, especially in writing. Mastering it shows sophistication. It's worth learning for reading comprehension and formal communication.

items.question: Ich gehe _____ Kino.
items.explanation: Two-way preposition 'in' with accusative shows motion. 'Ins Kino' (in+das contracted) means 'to the cinema'. Accusative shows direction/goal. Two-way prepositions (in, an, auf, über, unter, vor, hinter, neben, zwischen) take accusative for motion toward, dative for location. This accusative/dative distinction is crucial. 'Ins' contracts 'in das'. Such contractions are mandatory in German, not optional like English.

items.question: Ich bin _____ Kino.
items.explanation: 'In' with dative shows location. 'Im Kino' (in+dem contracted) means 'at the cinema'. Dative indicates where you are. Same preposition, different case, different meaning. This dative usage answers 'wo?' (where?). Motion uses accusative (wohin? - where to?), location uses dative (wo? - where?). This distinction parallels English 'to' vs 'at' but is more systematic in German.

items.question: Das Buch liegt _____ Tisch.
items.translation: (The book is lying on the table)
items.explanation: 'Auf' with dative shows location. 'Auf dem Tisch' means 'on the table' (where it is). Der Tisch (masculine) becomes 'dem Tisch' in dative. The book's position is static, so dative. If you put the book onto the table (motion), you'd use accusative: 'Ich lege das Buch auf den Tisch'. This motion/location distinction is systematic across all two-way prepositions.

items.question: Ich fahre _____ Deutschland.
items.explanation: 'Nach' takes dative and means 'to' for countries/cities without articles. 'Nach Deutschland' is standard. Countries used without articles (most of them) use 'nach'. Countries with articles (die Schweiz, die Türkei, die USA) use 'in die': 'in die Schweiz'. Geographic prepositions have specific rules. 'Nach' also means 'after' in time contexts. The preposition's meaning comes from context.

items.question: Das Geschenk ist _____ dich.
items.explanation: 'Für' takes accusative and means 'for'. 'Für dich' is for you (informal). Accusative pronouns: mich, dich, ihn, sie, es, uns, euch, sie/Sie. 'Für' always takes accusative, regardless of context. Accusative-only prepositions (durch, für, gegen, ohne, um, entlang) always require accusative. These must be memorized as a group. They're called accusative prepositions or 'durch-für-gegen' group.

items.question: Ich komme _____ der Arbeit.
items.explanation: 'Von' takes dative and means 'from'. 'Von der Arbeit' means from work. Die Arbeit (feminine) becomes 'der Arbeit' in dative. 'Von' indicates origin or source. Dative-only prepositions (aus, bei, mit, nach, seit, von, zu, gegenüber) always require dative. These form the 'aus-bei-mit' group. Memorizing them as a set helps. They're fundamental prepositions used constantly.

items.question: Ich wohne _____ meinen Eltern.
items.explanation: 'Bei' takes dative and means 'at/with' (location near someone). 'Bei meinen Eltern' means at my parents' place. 'Mit' also means 'with' but for accompaniment: 'Ich gehe mit meinen Eltern' (I go with my parents). 'Bei' is for location, 'mit' for accompaniment. This distinction is important. Both take dative but serve different functions. Context determines which preposition fits.

items.question: Das Bild hängt _____ Wand.
items.explanation: 'An' with dative shows location on vertical surface. 'An der Wand' means on the wall (where it is). Die Wand (feminine) becomes 'der Wand' in dative. Walls are vertical, so 'an' not 'auf'. 'Auf' is for horizontal surfaces. This verticality distinction is consistent in German. Pictures hang AN walls, books lie AUF tables. The preposition encodes spatial relationships precisely.

items.question: Ich gehe _____ Freund.
items.explanation: 'Zu' takes dative and means 'to' for people/specific places. 'Zu meinem Freund' means to my friend's place. Mein Freund (masculine) becomes 'meinem Freund' in dative after 'zu'. 'Zu' is for destinations involving people or specific establishments. 'Zum Arzt' (to the doctor), 'zur Schule' (to school). 'Nach' is for cities/countries, 'zu' for people/places. This distinction must be learned.

items.question: Ich komme _____ Italien.
items.explanation: 'Aus' takes dative and means 'from' for inside/countries. 'Aus Italien' means from Italy. Use 'aus' for origin from inside something or from countries. 'Von' is from a source/person. 'Aus' is from within or geographic origin. 'Ich komme aus Deutschland' (from Germany), 'Ich komme vom Markt' (from the market). The distinction: aus for inside/origin, von for from a place/person. Both take dative.

items.question: Wir fahren _____ die Stadt.
items.explanation: 'Durch' takes accusative and means 'through'. 'Durch die Stadt' means through the city. Die Stadt (feminine) stays 'die' in accusative. 'Durch' always uses accusative. Movement through something uses 'durch'. This preposition is straightforward - through in English, durch in German, both conceptually similar. Accusative marks the space traversed.

items.question: Das Café ist _____ Bahnhof.
items.translation: (The café is opposite the train station)
items.explanation: 'Gegenüber' takes dative and means 'opposite'. 'Gegenüber dem Bahnhof' means opposite the station. Der Bahnhof (masculine) becomes 'dem Bahnhof' in dative. 'Gegenüber' can come before or after its object: 'dem Bahnhof gegenüber' is also correct. This flexibility is unusual - most prepositions strictly precede objects. 'Gegenüber' is unique in its positioning options.

items.question: Ich lerne Deutsch _____ drei Jahren.
items.translation: (I have been learning German for three years)
items.explanation: 'Seit' takes dative and means 'since/for' with time. 'Seit drei Jahren' means for three years (ongoing). English uses present perfect, German uses present tense with 'seit'. This is a key difference. 'Ich lerne seit drei Jahren' (I have been learning for three years). 'Seit' indicates duration from past into present. It takes dative always. Time expressions with 'seit' are very common.

items.question: Die Katze schläft _____ Sofa.
items.explanation: 'Unter' with dative shows location. 'Unter dem Sofa' means under the sofa (where it is). Das Sofa (neuter) becomes 'dem Sofa' in dative. Static location uses dative. If the cat crawls under (motion), you'd use accusative: 'Die Katze kriecht unter das Sofa'. This two-way preposition pattern repeats. Motion toward = accusative, location = dative. Systematic and logical.

items.question: Ich bin _____ achte Uhr hier.
items.explanation: 'Bis' means 'until/to'. 'Bis acht Uhr' (until eight). 'Bis' typically doesn't show case because it often merges with another preposition: 'bis zum Bahnhof' (until/to the station). Here 'zu' + 'dem' contracts to 'zum', showing dative. 'Bis' alone with time expressions doesn't require article. Time prepositions have special rules. 'Bis' marks temporal endpoint. It's essential for expressing duration limits.

name: Modalverben
description: German modal verbs (können, müssen, dürfen, sollen, wollen, mögen)

items.question: Ich _____ Deutsch sprechen. (can)
items.explanation: 'Können' (can/to be able to) conjugates irregularly: ich kann, du kannst, er/sie kann, wir können, ihr könnt, sie können. With 'ich', use 'kann'. KEY RULE: The main verb goes to the END in infinitive form: 'Ich kann Deutsch sprechen' — NOT 'Ich kann spreche Deutsch'. This infinitive-at-end structure is unique to German modals and doesn't exist in English.

items.question: Du _____ das nicht tun! (must not)
items.explanation: CAREFUL: 'must not' = 'dürfen' (nicht), NOT 'müssen'. 'Du darfst das nicht tun' = you are not allowed/permitted. 'Dürfen' is about PERMISSION. 'Müssen' is about NECESSITY. This is a common trap: English 'must not' = prohibition, German 'nicht müssen' = no obligation (don't have to). 'Dürfen' conjugation: ich darf, du darfst, er darf.

items.question: Wir _____ morgen früh aufstehen. (must)
items.explanation: 'Müssen' (must/have to) expresses necessity or obligation. 'Wir müssen' = we must. The infinitive 'aufstehen' (to get up) goes to the END of the sentence. With separable verbs like 'aufstehen', the prefix stays attached in infinitive form. Conjugation: ich muss, du musst, er muss, wir müssen. Note: no umlaut in singular forms (muss, not müss).

items.question: Er _____ eine neue Jacke kaufen. (wants to)
items.translation: (He wants to buy a new jacket.)
items.explanation: FALSE FRIEND ALERT: German 'will' means 'wants to', NOT future tense! 'Er will kaufen' = he wants to buy. For future, German uses 'werden': 'Er wird kaufen'. 'Wollen' conjugation: ich will, du willst, er will, wir wollen. 'Möchte' (would like to) is the polite form of 'wollen'. This German-English 'will' confusion is one of the most common mistakes.

items.question: _____ ich hier rauchen? (may)
items.explanation: 'Dürfen' (may/to be permitted) is used for asking permission politely. 'Darf ich...?' = May I...? is the polite way to ask. While 'Kann ich...?' (Can I...?) works informally, 'Darf ich' is more proper. In questions, the modal verb comes first (verb-first word order). 'Rauchen' stays at the end as infinitive. Conjugation: ich darf, du darfst, er/sie darf.

items.question: Ihr _____ mehr Wasser trinken. (should)
items.explanation: 'Sollen' (should/ought to) gives advice or expresses expectation. 'Ihr sollt' = you (plural) should. 'Sollen' is unique because it keeps its vowel unchanged: ich soll, du sollst, er soll, wir sollen, ihr sollt. No vowel change unlike können (kann) or müssen (muss). 'Sollen' can also mean 'is supposed to': 'Er soll nett sein' = he's supposed to be nice.

items.question: Sie _____ gerne Schokolade. (likes)
items.explanation: 'Mögen' (to like) is the sixth modal verb, often used without an infinitive: 'Sie mag Schokolade' (she likes chocolate). Conjugation: ich mag, du magst, er/sie mag, wir mögen. 'Möchte' (would like) comes from mögen but has become a separate modal of its own: 'Ich möchte Kaffee' = I'd like coffee. Difference: 'mag' = general preference, 'möchte' = specific current wish.

items.question: Die Kinder _____ nicht so spät ins Bett gehen. (should)
items.translation: (The children shouldn't go to bed so late.)
items.explanation: 'Sollten' is the Konjunktiv II (subjunctive) of 'sollen' — it softens advice to a suggestion. 'Die Kinder sollten' = the children should (it would be advisable). All modals have subjunctive forms: könnte, müsste, dürfte, sollte, wollte, möchte. These are used for politeness, hypotheticals, and softened statements. English uses 'should/could/would' similarly.

items.question: Ich _____ als Kind gut schwimmen. (could)
items.translation: (I could swim well as a child.)
items.explanation: 'Konnte' is the PAST TENSE (Präteritum) of 'können'. 'Ich konnte schwimmen' = I was able to swim (fact). CAREFUL: 'könnte' (with umlaut) = COULD/would be able to (hypothetical). 'Konnte' vs 'könnte' is like English 'could' (past fact) vs 'could' (possibility). German makes this distinction clearer than English. Modal verbs typically use Präteritum (not Perfekt) in past.

items.question: _____ du mir bitte helfen? (could)
items.explanation: 'Könntest' (could you) is Konjunktiv II of 'können' — used for polite requests. Much more polite than 'Kannst du?' (can you?). The umlaut marks subjunctive: können → könn-. Add subjunctive endings: ich könnte, du könntest, er könnte, wir könnten. 'Bitte' (please) adds extra politeness. This polite modal pattern is essential in German everyday communication.

items.question: Er _____ gestern nicht kommen. (was able to)
items.explanation: Past tense of modals: use Präteritum, not Perfekt. 'Er konnte nicht kommen' = he couldn't come. With Perfekt, modals use a special double infinitive: 'Er hat nicht kommen können' — but this is rare and mainly written. Präteritum forms: konnte, musste, durfte, sollte, wollte, mochte. These are the standard way to express modal past in spoken German.

items.question: Man _____ hier nicht parken. (is allowed to)
items.explanation: 'Man darf hier nicht parken' = parking is not allowed here. 'Man' (one/people in general) + 'darf nicht' = prohibition for everyone. Signs often use this: 'Hier darf man nicht rauchen' (no smoking). Remember: 'nicht dürfen' = prohibition (must not), 'nicht müssen' = no obligation (don't have to). The man + modal construction is very common for expressing general rules.

items.question: Ich _____ lieber zu Hause bleiben. (would)
items.explanation: 'Möchte' (would like to) + 'lieber' (rather/preferably) = would prefer to. 'Ich möchte lieber bleiben' = I'd rather stay. 'Möchte' is technically Konjunktiv II of 'mögen' but functions as its own modal for polite wishes: 'Ich möchte einen Kaffee' = I'd like a coffee. Never say 'Ich will einen Kaffee' in a café — that's too direct. Möchte is the polite standard.

items.question: Das Kind _____ seine Hausaufgaben machen. (is supposed to)
items.translation: (The child is supposed to do homework.)
items.explanation: 'Sollen' for external expectation: 'Das Kind soll' = someone expects the child to (parents, teachers). Compare: 'will' = the child itself wants to. 'Sollen' often implies someone else's instruction or wish: 'Der Arzt sagt, ich soll mehr Sport machen' = the doctor says I should exercise more. The source of obligation is key: sollen = external, müssen = necessity, wollen = internal.

items.question: Wir _____ nächstes Jahr nach Japan reisen. (want to)
items.translation: (We want to travel to Japan next year.)
items.explanation: 'Wollen' (want to) expresses strong intention: 'Wir wollen reisen' = we want to travel. DON'T confuse with 'werden' (will/shall for future): 'Wir werden reisen' = we will travel. 'Wollen' = desire, 'werden' = future tense auxiliary. 'Wir wollen' keeps the infinitive 'reisen' at the end. In subordinate clauses, the modal goes to the very end: 'Ich weiß, dass wir reisen wollen'.

items.question: Ich _____ gestern einen Film gesehen.
items.explanation: German Perfekt uses 'haben' or 'sein' as a helper verb plus a past participle at the end. Most verbs use 'haben'. For 'ich' (I), the conjugated form is 'habe'. Unlike English, where present perfect ('I have seen') and simple past ('I saw') are distinct, German Perfekt covers both meanings in everyday speech. You'll hear 'Ich habe gesehen' far more often than the simple past 'Ich sah' in conversation. The past participle 'gesehen' always goes to the end of the clause — this is the famous German verb-final rule in action.

items.question: Er _____ nach Berlin gefahren.
items.explanation: Verbs of motion and change of state use 'sein' instead of 'haben' in the Perfekt. 'Fahren' (to drive/travel) involves movement from one place to another, so it takes 'sein'. For 'er' (he), the form is 'ist'. This is one of the trickiest parts of German grammar for English speakers: you must memorize which verbs take 'sein'. Common sein-verbs include gehen, kommen, fahren, fliegen, laufen, fallen, sterben, werden. A helpful rule: if it involves movement to a destination or a change of state, it likely uses 'sein'.

items.question: Wir _____ den ganzen Tag gearbeitet.
items.explanation: 'Arbeiten' (to work) uses 'haben' because it doesn't involve movement to a destination or a fundamental change of state. For 'wir' (we), the conjugated form is 'haben'. Notice the past participle 'gearbeitet' — regular verbs form their participle with the prefix 'ge-' and the suffix '-t' (or '-et' when the stem ends in -t or -d). The participle sits at the very end of the sentence, after all other sentence elements. This two-part verb structure (auxiliary at position 2, participle at the end) creates the 'sentence bracket' (Satzklammer) that defines German sentence structure.

items.question: _____ du schon gegessen?
items.explanation: In questions, the auxiliary verb moves to the first position. For 'du' (you informal), 'haben' conjugates to 'hast'. 'Essen' has an irregular past participle: 'gegessen' (not 'geesst'). Many common German verbs have irregular participles that must be memorized. Notice how 'schon' (already) sits between the auxiliary and the participle — adverbs and other elements fill the middle field of the sentence bracket. This question format (Verb-Subject-...-Participle) is the standard yes/no question pattern in German Perfekt.

items.question: Die Kinder _____ im Garten gespielt.
items.translation: (The children played/have played in the garden)
items.explanation: 'Spielen' (to play) uses 'haben' as its auxiliary. 'Die Kinder' (the children) is plural, requiring 'haben'. The participle 'gespielt' follows the regular pattern: ge- + stem + -t. In spoken German, the Perfekt has almost entirely replaced the simple past (Präteritum) for most verbs. You would rarely hear 'Die Kinder spielten' in conversation — 'Die Kinder haben gespielt' is the natural choice. The main exceptions are 'sein' (war), 'haben' (hatte), and modal verbs, which still commonly use simple past even in speech.

items.question: Sie _____ gestern spät eingeschlafen.
items.explanation: 'Einschlafen' (to fall asleep) uses 'sein' because it represents a change of state — from awake to asleep. This surprises many learners because there's no physical movement involved. The key insight is that 'sein' verbs include both movement verbs AND change-of-state verbs. Other examples: aufwachen (to wake up), einschlafen (to fall asleep), sterben (to die), werden (to become). Notice 'eingeschlafen' — for separable verbs, 'ge-' is inserted between the prefix and the stem: ein-ge-schlafen. This is a consistent rule for all separable prefix verbs in the Perfekt.

items.question: Ich habe das Buch nicht _____.
items.explanation: 'Lesen' (to read) has the irregular past participle 'gelesen'. Strong (irregular) verbs often change their stem vowel in the participle: lesen → gelesen, schreiben → geschrieben, trinken → getrunken. The participle always goes to the very end of the sentence, even after 'nicht' (not). In fact, nothing can come after the past participle in a main clause — it is the absolute final element. This is why negation with 'nicht' appears before the participle. Learning these irregular participles is essential: there are roughly 170 strong verbs in German, but the most common 50 cover everyday conversation.

items.question: _____ ihr die Nachricht bekommen?
items.explanation: 'Ihr' (you all/you plural informal) conjugates with 'habt' when using 'haben'. 'Bekommen' is a verb with an inseparable prefix (be-), and these verbs do NOT add 'ge-' in the past participle — it's simply 'bekommen', not '*gebekommen'. This is a crucial rule: inseparable prefixes (be-, emp-, ent-, er-, ge-, miss-, ver-, zer-) absorb the 'ge-' prefix. Compare: kaufen → gekauft, but verkaufen → verkauft. Also note: 'bekommen' means 'to receive/get' in German — it's a classic false friend with English 'become' (which is 'werden' in German).

items.question: Wir sind gestern Abend ins Kino _____.
items.translation: (We went to the cinema yesterday evening)
items.explanation: 'Gehen' (to go) is a movement verb that uses 'sein' and has the irregular participle 'gegangen'. It's one of the most frequently used verbs in German Perfekt. The expression 'ins Kino gehen' (to go to the cinema) uses the contraction 'ins' = 'in das'. Notice how the time expression 'gestern Abend' (yesterday evening) sits in the middle field between the auxiliary 'sind' and the participle 'gegangen'. German is very flexible about where time expressions go, but they typically appear early in the middle field. This verb is a must-know irregular form.

items.question: Die Lehrerin hat den Schülern alles _____.
items.translation: (The teacher explained everything to the students)
items.explanation: 'Erklären' (to explain) has the inseparable prefix 'er-', so its past participle is 'erklärt' without any additional 'ge-'. This is one of the most common patterns that trips up learners. The inseparable prefixes (be-, emp-, ent-, er-, ge-, miss-, ver-, zer-) are never stressed and never separated from the verb. Compare with separable verbs like 'aufmachen' → 'aufgemacht' (ge- inserted). A helpful test: if the prefix is stressed when speaking, it's separable and gets 'ge-' inserted; if the verb stem is stressed, the prefix is inseparable and no 'ge-' is added.

items.question: Er hat die ganze Nacht _____.
items.explanation: Verbs ending in '-ieren' (borrowed from French/Latin) do NOT take the 'ge-' prefix in the past participle. 'Studieren' becomes 'studiert', 'telefonieren' becomes 'telefoniert', 'fotografieren' becomes 'fotografiert'. These verbs are always regular (weak) — they just add '-t' to the stem without 'ge-'. This is a wonderfully simple rule with no exceptions. German has hundreds of '-ieren' verbs: reservieren, diskutieren, organisieren, trainieren. All follow the same pattern. Think of it this way: these verbs already sound 'foreign' enough — adding 'ge-' would make them even more cumbersome.

items.question: Sie _____ heute Morgen aufgestanden.
items.explanation: 'Aufstehen' (to get up) uses 'sein' because it involves a change of state (from lying/sitting to standing). It's a separable verb, so the participle is 'auf-ge-standen' with 'ge-' inserted between prefix and stem. The stem 'stehen' is irregular: stehen → gestanden. This daily routine verb perfectly illustrates three rules at once: (1) change-of-state verbs use 'sein', (2) separable verbs insert 'ge-' between prefix and stem, (3) strong verbs change their stem vowel. Mastering 'aufstehen' means you understand a big chunk of Perfekt formation.

items.question: Wir haben lange auf dich _____.
items.translation: (We waited for you for a long time)
items.explanation: 'Warten' (to wait) is a regular verb whose stem ends in '-t', so the participle adds '-et' instead of just '-t': ge-wart-et. This extra 'e' is needed for pronunciation — 'gewartt' would be unpronounceable. The same rule applies to arbeiten → gearbeitet, reden → geredet, öffnen → geöffnet. Notice 'auf dich' — 'warten auf' (to wait for) is one of many German verbs with fixed prepositions. Unlike English 'wait for', German uses 'auf' (on/upon). These verb-preposition combinations must be memorized as units, and they often determine which case follows (warten auf + accusative).

items.question: Das Kind ist vom Baum _____.
items.explanation: 'Fallen' (to fall) uses 'sein' — it's both a movement verb and a change-of-state verb. The participle is irregular: 'gefallen' (the stem vowel changes from 'a' to 'a' but the ending is '-en' instead of '-t', marking it as a strong verb). Don't confuse 'gefallen' (fallen, past participle of 'to fall') with 'gefallen' (to please/like), which is a completely different verb! Context makes it clear: 'ist gefallen' = fell, 'hat gefallen' = pleased. This is a great example of how German recycling of forms requires you to pay attention to the auxiliary verb.

items.question: Hast du die E-Mail schon _____?
items.explanation: 'Schicken' (to send) is a regular verb: ge- + schick + -t = 'geschickt'. The word order in this question is important: Auxiliary (Hast) → Subject (du) → middle field (die E-Mail schon) → Participle (geschickt). The adverb 'schon' (already) comes just before the participle, which is the most common position for it. In statements, you could also say 'Du hast die E-Mail schon geschickt.' German Perfekt questions mirror English present perfect questions closely in meaning, making this tense feel natural once you master the auxiliary choice and participle formation.

items.question: Ich _____ schon gegessen, als er ankam.
items.translation: (I had already eaten when he arrived)
items.explanation: The Plusquamperfekt (past perfect) describes an action completed before another past action. It uses the simple past (Präteritum) of 'haben' or 'sein' plus a past participle. For 'ich' with 'haben', the form is 'hatte'. This is identical in concept to English 'had eaten'. The subordinate clause 'als er ankam' (when he arrived) uses Präteritum because 'als' (when, for single past events) triggers verb-final word order. The Plusquamperfekt establishes the earlier event in a clear timeline: first eating, then his arrival.

items.question: Sie _____ schon abgefahren, bevor wir ankamen.
items.translation: (She had already departed before we arrived)
items.explanation: 'Abfahren' (to depart) is a movement verb, so it uses 'sein' in the Perfekt. In the Plusquamperfekt, 'sein' becomes its Präteritum form 'war'. For 'sie' (she), it's 'war'. The same haben/sein distinction from the Perfekt applies here — if a verb uses 'sein' in the Perfekt, it uses 'war' (Präteritum of sein) in the Plusquamperfekt. 'Bevor' (before) introduces a subordinate clause where the conjugated verb goes to the end. This sentence perfectly shows how German uses the Plusquamperfekt to sequence two past events clearly.

items.question: Nachdem er das Buch _____ hatte, gab er es zurück.
items.translation: (After he had read the book, he returned it)
items.explanation: 'Nachdem' (after) is almost always paired with the Plusquamperfekt in the subordinate clause and Präteritum in the main clause. This combination is a grammatical standard in German. In the subordinate clause after 'nachdem', the conjugated verb 'hatte' goes to the very end, and the past participle 'gelesen' comes just before it. This double-verb-at-the-end pattern (participle + auxiliary) is standard in subordinate clauses. The main clause then uses simple past 'gab...zurück' (returned). Mastering 'nachdem' + Plusquamperfekt is essential for expressing sequences of past events.

items.question: Wir hatten den Film schon _____, als du kamst.
items.translation: (We had already seen the movie when you came)
items.explanation: 'Sehen' (to see) has the irregular participle 'gesehen'. In this Plusquamperfekt sentence, 'hatten' is the Präteritum of 'haben' for 'wir' (we). The time adverb 'schon' (already) emphasizes that the action was completed before the second event. 'Als' (when) introduces the second, later past event in Präteritum: 'du kamst' (you came). Note that 'als' is used for single events in the past, while 'wenn' is used for repeated past events or present/future events. This distinction is important for natural-sounding German.

items.question: Er _____ nie zuvor in Deutschland gewesen.
items.translation: (He had never been to Germany before)
items.explanation: 'Sein' (to be) uses itself as the auxiliary — 'war gewesen' is the Plusquamperfekt of 'sein'. This might seem odd, but it's perfectly logical: sein uses sein in compound tenses. 'War' is the Präteritum of sein for 'er' (he). 'Nie zuvor' (never before) emphasizes this was a first-time experience up to that point in the past. The form 'gewesen' is the irregular past participle of 'sein'. This construction is essential for talking about past experiences that preceded other events. In English, 'had never been' works the same way conceptually.

items.question: Die Gäste hatten das Hotel schon _____, als der Regen begann.
items.translation: (The guests had already left the hotel when the rain started)
items.explanation: 'Verlassen' (to leave) has the inseparable prefix 'ver-', so the past participle is 'verlassen' — no 'ge-' prefix is added. The participle happens to look identical to the infinitive, which is common for inseparable prefix verbs with strong conjugation patterns. Examples: beschreiben → beschrieben, verstehen → verstanden, empfinden → empfunden. The Plusquamperfekt 'hatten verlassen' clearly establishes that the guests' departure happened before the rain. This is a sophisticated tense that adds precision and professionalism to your German writing and speech.

items.question: Nachdem wir _____ waren, haben wir gefrühstückt.
items.translation: (After we had gotten up, we had breakfast)
items.explanation: 'Aufstehen' (to get up) uses 'sein' and is separable, giving us 'auf-ge-standen'. In the subordinate clause after 'nachdem', the auxiliary 'waren' (Präteritum of sein for wir) goes to the very end. Notice that while classical grammar pairs nachdem-Plusquamperfekt with Präteritum in the main clause, modern spoken German often uses Perfekt ('haben gefrühstückt') in the main clause instead. Both are acceptable, but mixing them is actually the most common real-world pattern. This sentence shows natural, modern German usage.

items.question: Sie _____ die Prüfung nicht bestanden, weil sie nicht gelernt hatte.
items.translation: (She had not passed the exam because she had not studied)
items.explanation: 'Bestehen' (to pass an exam) uses 'haben'. The Präteritum form for 'sie' (she) is 'hatte'. The inseparable prefix 'be-' means the participle is 'bestanden' without 'ge-'. The 'weil' (because) clause adds a reason, also in Plusquamperfekt: 'weil sie nicht gelernt hatte' — here the auxiliary 'hatte' goes to the end of the subordinate clause. This double Plusquamperfekt structure (both clauses using past perfect) is perfectly valid when both events occurred before the current past narrative moment. It shows causality between two prior events.

items.question: Bevor ich nach Hause _____, hatte ich eingekauft.
items.translation: (Before I went home, I had shopped)
items.explanation: 'Bevor' (before) introduces the later event and typically takes Präteritum, while the main clause uses Plusquamperfekt for the earlier event. This is the reverse of 'nachdem' — with 'bevor', the subordinate clause has the LATER event. 'Ging' is the Präteritum of 'gehen' for 'ich'. The main clause 'hatte ich eingekauft' uses Plusquamperfekt with 'einkaufen' (separable: ein-ge-kauft). Understanding bevor vs. nachdem is crucial: nachdem = after (earlier event in subordinate clause), bevor = before (later event in subordinate clause). Both create clear temporal sequences.

items.question: Nachdem die Sonne _____ war, wurde es kalt.
items.translation: (After the sun had set, it became cold)
items.explanation: 'Untergehen' (to set/go down - for the sun) is a separable movement/change-of-state verb using 'sein'. The participle is 'unter-ge-gangen'. In the subordinate clause after 'nachdem', the word order places 'war' (Präteritum of sein) at the very end: 'Nachdem die Sonne untergegangen war'. The main clause uses Präteritum 'wurde' (became). This poetic sentence demonstrates natural Plusquamperfekt usage — the sun setting clearly preceded the cold arriving. 'Untergehen' is also used metaphorically: 'Das Schiff ist untergegangen' (The ship sank).

items.question: Wir hatten uns schon _____, bevor die Party anfing.
items.translation: (We had already gotten ready before the party started)
items.explanation: 'Vorbereiten' combines a separable prefix 'vor-' with 'bereiten' (which has inseparable prefix 'be-'). When a separable prefix is added to a verb that already has an inseparable prefix, the 'ge-' of the participle is NOT inserted. So: vor-bereitet, not 'vor-ge-bereitet'. This is because the inseparable prefix 'be-' blocks 'ge-'. The reflexive 'uns vorbereitet' (prepared ourselves) adds another layer. Reflexive pronouns in compound tenses go right after the subject: 'Wir hatten uns...' This is a complex but important construction for advanced German.

items.question: Er hatte den Brief schon _____, als sie anrief.
items.translation: (He had already written the letter when she called)
items.explanation: 'Schreiben' (to write) has the strong/irregular participle 'geschrieben' with a vowel change (ei → ie). This is part of the ablaut pattern, one of the oldest features of Germanic languages. The Plusquamperfekt 'hatte geschrieben' places the letter-writing before the phone call. 'Anrufen' in the subordinate clause uses Präteritum: 'anrief' (called). The separable prefix 'an-' joins back with 'rief' in the subordinate clause because the conjugated verb goes to the end anyway. This sentence exemplifies the standard narrative past sequence in German storytelling.

items.question: Nachdem ich den Kaffee _____ hatte, fühlte ich mich besser.
items.translation: (After I had drunk the coffee, I felt better)
items.explanation: 'Trinken' (to drink) has the irregular participle 'getrunken' with a double vowel change (i → u). This is one of the classic German strong verb patterns. In the subordinate clause, the word order is: Subject (ich) → Object (den Kaffee) → Participle (getrunken) → Auxiliary (hatte). The main clause 'fühlte ich mich besser' uses Präteritum of 'sich fühlen' (to feel). The reflexive 'mich' follows the subject directly. This sentence shows how Plusquamperfekt combined with nachdem creates elegant, precise temporal narratives.

items.question: Die Kinder waren schon _____, als die Eltern nach Hause kamen.
items.translation: (The children had already fallen asleep when the parents came home)
items.explanation: 'Einschlafen' (to fall asleep) is a separable change-of-state verb using 'sein'. The participle inserts 'ge-' between prefix and stem: ein-ge-schlafen. 'Waren' is Präteritum of 'sein' for 'die Kinder' (plural). The 'als' clause uses Präteritum: 'kamen' (came). This sentence paints a vivid domestic scene: children asleep (earlier event), parents arriving (later event). The Plusquamperfekt makes the chronology crystal clear. 'Nach Hause kommen' (to come home) is a common expression — 'nach Hause' for direction (going home), 'zu Hause' for location (at home).

items.question: Ich hatte nicht _____, dass er schon gegangen war.
items.translation: (I had not realized that he had already left)
items.explanation: 'Bemerken' (to notice/realize) has the inseparable prefix 'be-', so the participle is 'bemerkt' without 'ge-'. This sentence uses Plusquamperfekt in BOTH clauses — the 'dass' clause also has Plusquamperfekt 'gegangen war' because both events happened before the current narrative moment. In the 'dass' clause, the auxiliary 'war' goes to the very end. Double Plusquamperfekt occurs when you need to show two events that both preceded a later reference point. This is sophisticated German that demonstrates full mastery of the tense system.

items.question: Ich _____ morgen nach München fahren.
items.explanation: German forms the future tense (Futur I) with 'werden' + infinitive. For 'ich', the conjugated form is 'werde'. This is fundamentally different from English 'will' — German 'will' actually means 'want'! The infinitive 'fahren' goes to the very end of the clause. In everyday speech, Germans often use the present tense with a time word ('Ich fahre morgen nach München') instead of Futur I. The explicit future tense adds emphasis, formality, or removes ambiguity. 'Werden' is one of the most important German verbs — it serves as future auxiliary, passive auxiliary, and means 'to become'.

items.question: Er _____ nächstes Jahr Deutsch lernen.
items.explanation: For 'er' (he), 'werden' conjugates to 'wird'. The infinitive 'lernen' sits at the end. The Futur I conjugation of werden is: ich werde, du wirst, er/sie/es wird, wir werden, ihr werdet, sie/Sie werden. Notice the vowel change in du/er forms (e→i), which is typical for strong verbs. 'Nächstes Jahr' (next year) uses the neuter accusative because Jahr is neuter and it's a time adverbial in accusative case. The Futur I structure (werden + infinitive at end) creates another example of the Satzklammer that defines German clause structure.

items.question: Wir _____ euch bald besuchen.
items.explanation: For 'wir' (we), 'werden' stays as 'werden' — the infinitive and the wir-form are identical. Don't let this confuse you: context and position make it clear which is which. The conjugated 'werden' sits in position 2 (the verb position), while the infinitive 'besuchen' goes to the end. 'Euch' (you all, accusative) is the informal plural object pronoun. 'Bald' (soon) is a time adverb sitting in the middle field. This sentence demonstrates a warm, reassuring promise — Futur I is commonly used for promises and assurances in German.

items.question: _____ du morgen zur Arbeit gehen?
items.explanation: In yes/no questions, the conjugated verb comes first: 'Wirst' (du form of werden) moves to position 1. The infinitive 'gehen' stays at the end. Don't confuse 'wirst' with 'willst' (want) — they look similar but are completely different verbs. 'Zur Arbeit' is a contraction of 'zu der Arbeit' (to work). In everyday speech, this question would more commonly be asked as 'Gehst du morgen zur Arbeit?' using present tense — the Futur I version sounds slightly more formal or is used when there's genuine uncertainty about the future plan.

items.question: Das Wetter _____ morgen schön sein.
items.explanation: 'Das Wetter' (the weather) is neuter singular (das), taking 'wird'. When the main verb is 'sein' (to be), it appears as an infinitive at the end: 'schön sein'. The Futur I is particularly common for predictions and assumptions about the future — weather forecasts are a natural context. German Futur I can also express assumptions about the present: 'Er wird jetzt wohl zu Hause sein' (He's probably at home now). This secondary use as a 'probability werden' is unique and very common in spoken German. Context determines whether werden means future or assumption.

items.question: Die Kinder werden im Sommer viel _____.
items.translation: (The children will swim a lot in summer)
items.explanation: In Futur I, the main verb always appears as an infinitive at the end of the clause. 'Schwimmen' (to swim) stays in its base form. Never use a conjugated form or participle with 'werden' for Futur I — that would create a different tense. 'Im Sommer' (in summer) uses the contraction of 'in dem'. 'Viel' (a lot/much) is an adverb here modifying how much they'll swim. This sentence illustrates a prediction about habitual future activity. The Futur I here adds a sense of anticipation that the simple present ('Die Kinder schwimmen im Sommer viel') lacks.

items.question: Ihr _____ das sicher verstehen.
items.explanation: For 'ihr' (you all, informal plural), 'werden' conjugates to 'werdet'. This form is distinctive and easy to recognize with its '-et' ending. 'Sicher' (surely/certainly) is an adverb expressing confidence. 'Verstehen' is an inseparable prefix verb that stays as a complete infinitive at the end. This sentence could also be interpreted as an assumption about the present: 'You probably understand that.' The word 'sicher' tips it toward confidence/assumption. This dual meaning of Futur I (future action or present assumption) is an elegant feature of German grammar.

items.question: Ich werde das Problem _____.
items.explanation: The infinitive 'lösen' (to solve) goes to the end of the Futur I construction. This creates the sentence bracket: 'werde' (position 2) ... 'lösen' (final position). Everything else fits between them. German Futur I is structurally simple — it's just werden + infinitive. The complexity comes from knowing when to use it versus present tense. Futur I is preferred when: (1) there's no time word to indicate future, (2) you want to emphasize intention or determination, (3) you're making a formal promise, or (4) you're expressing an assumption. 'Lösen' also works metaphorically: 'ein Rätsel lösen' (solve a puzzle).

items.question: Sie _____ wohl schon zu Hause sein.
items.explanation: This is the 'assumption/probability' use of werden + infinitive. Here, 'wird' doesn't refer to the future — it expresses a present-time assumption. 'Wohl' (probably) is the tip-off that this is a probability statement, not a future prediction. 'Zu Hause' (at home) uses 'zu' for location, while 'nach Hause' (homeward) uses 'nach' for direction. 'Schon' (already) adds the nuance of expectation. This usage is extremely common in everyday German: 'Das wird stimmen' (That's probably right), 'Er wird krank sein' (He's probably sick). It's equivalent to English 'must be' or 'probably is'.

items.question: Wir werden uns morgen _____.
items.explanation: 'Treffen' (to meet) stays as an infinitive in Futur I. The reflexive pronoun 'uns' goes in the middle field, right after the subject. 'Sich treffen' (to meet each other) is a reciprocal reflexive verb — the 'sich' indicates mutual action. In Futur I with a reflexive verb: werden + reflexive pronoun + ... + infinitive. 'Morgen' (tomorrow) can be placed either in the middle field or at position 1 for emphasis: 'Morgen werden wir uns treffen.' This is a natural way to make plans in German, though 'Wir treffen uns morgen' (present tense) is equally common in casual speech.

items.question: Es wird bald _____.
items.explanation: 'Es regnet' (it rains) becomes 'Es wird regnen' in Futur I. Weather expressions commonly use 'es' as a dummy subject, just like English 'it'. 'Regnen' is a regular verb that follows standard patterns. 'Bald' (soon) sits in the middle field. Weather predictions are a classic context for Futur I because they genuinely refer to the future. Other weather expressions: 'Es wird schneien' (it will snow), 'Es wird stürmen' (it will storm), 'Die Sonne wird scheinen' (The sun will shine). These are straightforward and very practical for everyday conversation.

items.question: Du _____ das nicht bereuen.
items.explanation: For 'du' (you informal), 'werden' conjugates to 'wirst'. Negation in Futur I places 'nicht' in the middle field, before the infinitive: 'wirst ... nicht bereuen'. 'Bereuen' (to regret) is a regular verb. This sentence has a reassuring tone — Futur I is often used for promises and assurances: 'Du wirst sehen' (You'll see), 'Das wirst du schaffen' (You'll manage that). The emotional weight of Futur I makes it preferred over present tense in these motivational contexts. It signals confidence about what will happen.

items.question: Die Firma wird nächstes Jahr neue Mitarbeiter _____.
