items.translation: (The company will hire new employees next year)
items.explanation: 'Einstellen' (to hire/employ) is a separable verb. In Futur I, separable verbs appear as one unseparated infinitive at the end: 'einstellen', not 'ein...stellen'. This is because the infinitive is the base form where the prefix is attached. Compare with present tense where they separate: 'Die Firma stellt ein.' In Futur I, the bracket is werden...infinitive, so the verb stays whole. This is good news — Futur I actually simplifies separable verbs! 'Mitarbeiter' (employees/co-workers) is a compound word: Mit (with) + Arbeiter (workers).

items.question: Ich werde dir morgen alles _____.
items.translation: (I will explain everything to you tomorrow)
items.explanation: 'Erklären' (to explain) is an inseparable prefix verb that simply appears as an infinitive at the end. 'Dir' (to you, dative) is the indirect object. German word order in the middle field typically places pronouns before nouns and dative before accusative when both are pronouns, but when one is a noun, the pronoun comes first regardless of case: 'dir (dative pronoun) alles (accusative pronoun)'. The Futur I construction here expresses a promise — you're committing to explaining something. This is more emphatic than present tense 'Ich erkläre dir morgen alles.'

items.question: _____ Sie bitte einen Moment warten?
items.explanation: 'Sie' (you formal) takes 'werden' — the form is identical to 'sie' (they) and 'wir' (we). In polite questions, Futur I softens the request: 'Werden Sie warten?' is more polite than 'Warten Sie!' (Wait!). The word 'bitte' (please) adds courtesy. 'Einen Moment' (a moment, accusative) is a common time expression. This is a very useful formal German pattern for professional and service contexts. Futur I questions are frequently used to make polite requests in formal German, similar to English 'Will you...?' or 'Would you...?' This register awareness is essential for professional communication.

items.question: Ich sehe _____ Mann.
items.explanation: The accusative case marks the direct object — the person or thing receiving the action. 'Mann' is masculine, so 'der' changes to 'den' in accusative. This is the ONLY gender where the definite article changes in accusative: der→den. Feminine 'die', neuter 'das', and plural 'die' all stay the same. This makes accusative relatively easy once you know which nouns are masculine. The accusative answers 'wen?' (whom?) or 'was?' (what?). Here: 'Wen sehe ich? Den Mann.' This is the most common case after nominative and the first one to master.

items.question: Er kauft _____ Buch.
items.explanation: 'Buch' is neuter (das Buch), and neuter indefinite articles don't change in accusative: 'ein' stays 'ein'. Only masculine indefinite articles change: ein→einen. This parallel the definite articles: only masculine changes (der→den, ein→einen). For neuter: das/ein remain das/ein. For feminine: die/eine remain die/eine. This pattern — only masculine changes in accusative — is wonderfully consistent across both definite and indefinite articles. 'Kaufen' (to buy) is a transitive verb that takes an accusative object. Most transitive verbs (seeing, buying, having, eating) use accusative.

items.question: Wir haben _____ Katze.
items.explanation: 'Katze' is feminine (die Katze). Feminine articles don't change in accusative: 'eine' stays 'eine'. The verb 'haben' (to have) always takes an accusative object — you 'have' something (direct object). Many common verbs require accusative: haben, sehen, kaufen, essen, trinken, lesen, finden, suchen, brauchen. Learning which verbs take accusative is straightforward because most transitive verbs (those that can have a direct object in English) use accusative in German. If you can ask 'what?' or 'whom?' after the verb, it's likely accusative.

items.question: Kennst du _____ Frau dort?
items.explanation: 'Frau' is feminine (die Frau). In accusative, feminine 'die' remains 'die' — unchanged from nominative. 'Kennen' (to know a person/be familiar with) takes accusative. Don't confuse 'kennen' with 'wissen' (to know a fact) — 'kennen' is for people and places, 'wissen' is for information. 'Dort' (there) is a location adverb that helps identify which woman. German question words also show case: 'Wen kennst du?' (Whom do you know?) uses 'wen' (accusative of 'wer'). This question demonstrates natural spoken German with an accusative direct object.

items.question: Sie trinkt _____ Kaffee.
items.explanation: 'Kaffee' is masculine (der Kaffee). Masculine indefinite articles change in accusative: ein→einen. This '-en' ending is the hallmark of masculine accusative across all article-like words: den, einen, meinen, deinen, keinen, diesen, jeden, welchen. Once you recognize this pattern, you can apply it universally. 'Trinken' (to drink) is a classic transitive verb requiring accusative. Many beverages are masculine in German: der Kaffee, der Tee, der Saft, der Wein. So you'll frequently need 'einen': einen Kaffee trinken, einen Tee bestellen. This is one of the most practical accusative patterns.

items.question: Ich suche _____ Schlüssel.
items.explanation: 'Schlüssel' is masculine (der Schlüssel). Accusative changes 'der' to 'den'. 'Suchen' (to search for/look for) takes a direct accusative object — no preposition needed, unlike English 'look FOR'. This is a common difference: German often uses direct objects where English uses prepositional phrases. Other examples: 'auf etwas warten' (wait for something) — but here German uses a preposition! Each verb has its own pattern. 'Suchen' is straightforward: accusative direct object, no preposition. Learning these verb patterns is key to accurate German.

items.question: Er liest _____ Zeitung.
items.explanation: 'Zeitung' is feminine (die Zeitung). Feminine accusative is identical to nominative: 'die' stays 'die'. 'Lesen' (to read) is a transitive verb requiring accusative. Many nouns ending in '-ung' are feminine: die Zeitung, die Wohnung, die Übung, die Meinung, die Lösung. This '-ung = feminine' rule is one of the most reliable gender patterns in German, with virtually no exceptions. Knowing gender patterns like this dramatically reduces the amount of memorization needed. Other reliable patterns: '-heit'/'-keit' = feminine, '-chen'/'-lein' = neuter.

items.question: Wir brauchen _____ neuen Computer.
items.explanation: 'Computer' is masculine (der Computer). Masculine accusative: ein→einen. Notice the adjective 'neuen' — after indefinite articles in accusative masculine, adjectives take the '-en' ending. This is part of the adjective declension system: after ein-words in accusative masculine, the adjective ending is '-en'. Compare: 'einen neuen Computer' (accusative) vs. 'ein neuer Computer' (nominative). The adjective ending helps signal the case when the article form might be ambiguous. 'Brauchen' (to need) takes accusative and is one of the most useful everyday verbs.

items.question: Hast du _____ Bruder?
items.explanation: 'Bruder' is masculine (der Bruder). With 'haben' in accusative: ein→einen. Family members have predictable genders: der Bruder, der Vater, der Onkel (masculine); die Schwester, die Mutter, die Tante (feminine); das Kind, das Baby (neuter). 'Haben' always takes accusative — you 'have' something directly. This question is a classic conversation starter. Note that German doesn't use 'a' and 'an' distinctions — 'einen' works before any masculine noun regardless of the following sound. This simplification compared to English is one of German's hidden ease-of-use features.

items.question: Ich möchte _____ Tasse Kaffee, bitte.
items.translation: (I would like a cup of coffee, please)
items.explanation: 'Tasse' is feminine (die Tasse). Feminine accusative: eine stays eine. 'Möchte' (would like) is the subjunctive II of 'mögen' and is used as a polite way to express desires. It always takes accusative for the desired object. This is one of the most practical phrases for daily life in Germany: ordering at cafés, restaurants, shops. The structure 'Ich möchte + accusative + bitte' is your go-to polite ordering formula. Note: 'Tasse Kaffee' — German doesn't need 'of' (von) for quantity expressions: eine Tasse Kaffee, ein Glas Wasser, ein Stück Kuchen.

items.question: Sie besucht _____ Großeltern.
items.explanation: 'Großeltern' is plural (always). Plural accusative uses 'die' — same as nominative plural. In fact, plural articles are the same in nominative and accusative for all genders: always 'die'. This is reassuring — you only need to worry about masculine singular changing in accusative. 'Besuchen' (to visit) takes accusative. Compound family words in German are logical: Groß (grand) + Eltern (parents) = Großeltern. Similarly: Großmutter, Großvater, Urgroßeltern (great-grandparents). German compound nouns always take the gender of the LAST component.

items.question: Er hat _____ Hund und _____ Katze.
items.translation: (He has a dog and a cat)
items.explanation: 'Hund' is masculine (der Hund) → einen in accusative. 'Katze' is feminine (die Katze) → eine in accusative (unchanged). This sentence perfectly demonstrates how accusative affects masculine but not feminine articles. Both are direct objects of 'haben'. When listing multiple objects, each keeps its own gender-appropriate article. There's no shortcut — you must know each noun's gender. However, animal genders often follow biological gender: der Hund (male dog), die Katze (female cat), though there are exceptions: das Pferd (the horse, neuter), die Maus (the mouse, feminine regardless).

items.question: Ich finde _____ Film langweilig.
items.explanation: 'Film' is masculine (der Film). Accusative: der→den. 'Finden' here doesn't mean 'to find' (locate) but 'to find/consider' (opinion) — a common double meaning. The adjective 'langweilig' (boring) is a predicative adjective after 'finden' and therefore takes NO ending. This is important: predicative adjectives (after sein, finden, werden) are never declined. Compare: 'den langweiligen Film' (attributive, with ending) vs. 'Ich finde den Film langweilig' (predicative, no ending). This distinction between attributive and predicative adjectives is fundamental in German.

items.question: Für _____ kaufst du das Geschenk?
items.translation: (For whom are you buying the gift?)
items.explanation: The preposition 'für' always takes accusative. The accusative form of 'wer' (who) is 'wen' (whom). German question words decline: wer (nominative), wen (accusative), wem (dative), wessen (genitive). Certain prepositions ALWAYS take accusative: für, durch, gegen, ohne, um, bis, entlang. These are sometimes remembered with mnemonics like 'DOGFU' (durch, ohne, gegen, für, um). 'Geschenk' (gift) is neuter (das Geschenk) and appears in accusative as 'das Geschenk' (unchanged). Preposition-case combinations are non-negotiable rules in German.

items.question: Ohne _____ Wörterbuch kann ich nicht übersetzen.
items.explanation: 'Ohne' (without) always takes accusative. 'Wörterbuch' is neuter (das Wörterbuch): ein stays ein in neuter accusative. Compound word: Wörter (words) + Buch (book) = dictionary. 'Ohne' is one of the accusative prepositions (für, durch, gegen, ohne, um, bis). After 'ohne', German typically drops the article in abstract senses: 'ohne Zweifel' (without doubt), 'ohne Problem' (without problem). But with specific objects, the article remains. 'Übersetzen' (to translate) is an inseparable prefix verb (the stress is on '-setzen': überSETzen = translate; ÜBERsetzen = ferry across — stress changes meaning!).

items.question: Ich gebe _____ Kind einen Apfel.
items.explanation: The dative case marks the indirect object — the recipient of an action. 'Kind' is neuter (das Kind), and in dative, 'das' becomes 'dem'. Dative article changes: der→dem (masculine), die→der (feminine), das→dem (neuter), die→den (plural, +n on noun). 'Geben' (to give) is the classic dative verb — someone gives something (accusative: einen Apfel) TO someone (dative: dem Kind). The pattern is: Subject + verb + dative (recipient) + accusative (thing given). This double-object construction is fundamental to German sentence structure.

items.question: Er hilft _____ Frau.
items.explanation: 'Frau' is feminine (die Frau). In dative, 'die' becomes 'der'. 'Helfen' (to help) is one of the most important dative-only verbs — it takes a dative object, NOT accusative. In English, you 'help someone' (direct object), but in German, you 'help TO someone' (indirect object). Other common dative-only verbs: danken (to thank), gehören (to belong to), gefallen (to please), folgen (to follow), glauben (to believe someone), antworten (to answer someone). These must be memorized because they don't follow English logic.

items.question: Das Buch gehört _____ Lehrer.
items.explanation: 'Lehrer' is masculine (der Lehrer). Dative masculine: der→dem. 'Gehören' (to belong to) takes dative — the owner is in dative case. This verb is incredibly useful: 'Wem gehört das?' (To whom does this belong?) is a question you'll hear constantly. 'Wem' is the dative of 'wer' (who). Notice the dative pattern for masculine and neuter is the same: both use 'dem'. This simplification is helpful — if you know it's not feminine (der in dative) and not plural (den in dative), then it's 'dem'. Dative masculine = dem, dative neuter = dem.

items.question: Ich danke _____ Freunden für die Hilfe.
items.translation: (I thank the friends for the help)
items.explanation: 'Freunde' is plural. In dative plural, the article becomes 'den' AND the noun adds '-n' if it doesn't already end in '-n' or '-s': Freunde→Freunden. This dative plural '-n' rule is consistent and has very few exceptions. 'Danken' (to thank) takes dative — you thank TO someone in German. 'Für die Hilfe' (for the help) uses accusative after 'für'. This sentence contains both dative (den Freunden) and accusative (die Hilfe) — different cases triggered by different elements. The verb danken requires dative; the preposition für requires accusative.

items.question: Kannst du _____ bitte helfen?
items.explanation: Personal pronouns also change in dative: ich→mir, du→dir, er→ihm, sie→ihr, es→ihm, wir→uns, ihr→euch, sie/Sie→ihnen/Ihnen. 'Helfen' requires dative, so 'ich' becomes 'mir' (to me). Don't confuse 'mir' (dative, to me) with 'mich' (accusative, me). A classic learner error is saying 'Kannst du mich helfen?' — this is incorrect because helfen demands dative. Helpful mnemonic: 'mir' for receiving/benefiting, 'mich' for being acted upon directly. 'Helfen' is the #1 verb to remember with dative.

items.question: Sie gibt _____ Mutter die Blumen.
items.explanation: 'Mutter' is feminine (die Mutter). Dative feminine: die→der. In this double-object sentence, 'der Mutter' (dative, recipient) comes before 'die Blumen' (accusative, thing given). The standard German word order with two nouns is: dative before accusative. But with pronouns, it reverses: 'Sie gibt sie ihr' (She gives them to her) — accusative pronoun before dative pronoun. This ordering rule (noun: dat before acc; pronouns: acc before dat) is a key German word order principle that even advanced learners sometimes struggle with.

items.question: Er antwortet _____ Lehrerin.
items.explanation: 'Lehrerin' is feminine (die Lehrerin). Dative feminine: die→der. 'Antworten' (to answer) takes dative — you answer TO someone. In English, 'answer' takes a direct object, but German treats it as an indirect relationship. The '-in' suffix makes feminine forms of professions: Lehrer→Lehrerin, Arzt→Ärztin, Student→Studentin. This is systematically applied in modern German for gender-inclusive language. Note: some German verbs that take dative may surprise you. Useful list: helfen, danken, antworten, gefallen, gehören, folgen, glauben, gratulieren, begegnen, widersprechen.

items.question: Ich kaufe _____ Schwester ein Geschenk.
items.explanation: Possessive pronouns follow the same pattern as 'ein' words. 'Schwester' is feminine. Dative feminine for ein-words: eine→einer, meine→meiner. The '-er' ending signals dative feminine across all ein-words: einer, meiner, deiner, seiner, ihrer, unserer, eurer, keiner. This sentence has two objects: 'meiner Schwester' (dative, who receives) and 'ein Geschenk' (accusative, what is given). 'Kaufen' normally takes accusative only, but when you buy FOR someone, that person goes in dative: 'Ich kaufe mir ein Buch' (I buy myself a book) — 'mir' is dative.

items.question: Das Kleid gefällt _____ sehr.
items.explanation: 'Gefallen' (to please/to be pleasing to) takes dative. German expresses 'liking' backwards from English: 'The dress pleases TO ME' rather than 'I like the dress.' The subject is 'das Kleid' (the dress), and the person who likes it is in dative: 'mir' (to me). This is similar to Spanish 'me gusta'. Other similar verbs: schmecken (to taste good to), fehlen (to be missing to/to miss), gehören (to belong to), passen (to fit/suit). Mastering this reversed perspective (I like it → It pleases to me) opens up a whole category of natural German expressions.

items.question: Wir fahren mit _____ Zug nach Hamburg.
items.explanation: 'Mit' (with/by) ALWAYS takes dative. 'Zug' is masculine (der Zug): der→dem in dative. The dative prepositions are: aus, außer, bei, gegenüber, mit, nach, seit, von, zu. These ALWAYS take dative, no exceptions. A mnemonic: 'aus-außer-bei-mit, nach-seit-von-zu' (often sung to a melody by German students). 'Mit dem Zug' (by train) is the standard expression for train travel. Similarly: mit dem Auto (by car), mit dem Bus (by bus), mit dem Fahrrad (by bike). Transportation always uses 'mit' + dative.

items.question: Seit _____ Jahr lerne ich Deutsch.
items.translation: (I have been learning German for a year)
items.explanation: 'Seit' (since/for) always takes dative. 'Jahr' is neuter (das Jahr): ein→einem in dative neuter. 'Seit' with present tense expresses an action that started in the past and continues now — where English uses present perfect continuous ('I have been learning'), German uses present tense with 'seit'. This is a major structural difference. 'Seit drei Jahren' (for three years), 'seit einer Woche' (for a week), 'seit gestern' (since yesterday). The dative preposition 'seit' is one of the most practical for everyday time expressions.

items.question: Er kommt aus _____ Türkei.
items.explanation: 'Aus' (from/out of) always takes dative. 'Türkei' is feminine (die Türkei): die→der in dative. Most country names in German are neuter and used without articles: 'aus Deutschland', 'aus Frankreich'. But some countries take articles: die Türkei, die Schweiz, die USA (plural), der Iran, der Irak. These countries ALWAYS use their articles, including in dative after 'aus': 'aus der Schweiz' (from Switzerland), 'aus dem Iran' (from Iran). Knowing which countries take articles is a practical vocabulary point for introductions and conversations about origin.

items.question: Nach _____ Essen gehen wir spazieren.
items.translation: (After the meal we go for a walk)
items.explanation: 'Nach' (after/to) always takes dative. 'Essen' (meal/food) is neuter (das Essen): das→dem. 'Nach' has two main meanings: 'after' (temporal) and 'to' (directional, for cities/countries without articles): 'nach Berlin', 'nach Deutschland'. For temporal 'nach', dative is required: 'nach dem Unterricht' (after class), 'nach der Arbeit' (after work). 'Spazieren gehen' (to go for a walk) is a common idiomatic expression. Notice the inverted word order: time expression first, then verb, then subject — this is perfectly natural in German.

items.question: Ich wohne bei _____ Großeltern.
items.explanation: 'Bei' (at/with, in someone's home) always takes dative. 'Großeltern' is plural. Dative plural for possessive pronouns: meine→meinen (and the noun adds -n if possible, but Großeltern already ends in -n). The dative plural pattern always adds '-n' to the article/determiner and to the noun (if it doesn't already end in -n or -s). 'Bei' indicates proximity or staying at someone's place: 'bei meinen Großeltern' (at my grandparents'), 'beim Arzt' (at the doctor's). 'Beim' is an everyday contraction of 'bei dem'. Understanding 'bei' + dative is essential for describing where you live or are staying.

items.question: Sie fährt zu _____ Arzt.
items.explanation: 'Zu' (to) always takes dative. 'Arzt' is masculine (der Arzt): der→dem. 'Zu' is used for destinations that are people or specific places: 'zum Arzt' (to the doctor), 'zur Schule' (to school), 'zum Bahnhof' (to the station). 'Zum' (zu + dem) and 'zur' (zu + der) are standard contractions used in everyday German — the uncontracted forms sound unnaturally formal. The dative prepositions (aus, außer, bei, gegenüber, mit, nach, seit, von, zu) should be memorized as a group early in your learning. They never change — always dative, always reliable.

items.question: Das ist das Auto _____ Mannes.
items.explanation: The genitive case shows possession in German. For masculine and neuter nouns, the article 'der/das' becomes 'des' and the noun typically adds '-s' or '-es'. 'Des Mannes' = of the man. This is equivalent to the English possessive 's or 'of the'. While spoken German often replaces genitive with 'von + dative', formal and written German consistently uses the genitive case.

items.question: Die Farbe _____ Hauses ist weiß.
items.translation: (The color of the house is white)
items.explanation: Neuter nouns in the genitive also take 'des' as their article, just like masculine nouns. 'Haus' becomes 'Hauses' in the genitive — monosyllabic nouns typically add '-es' rather than just '-s'. The genitive answers the question 'Wessen?' (Whose?). The structure 'die Farbe des Hauses' (the color of the house) is a classic genitive construction showing possession or belonging.

items.question: Das Büro _____ Chefin ist groß.
items.explanation: Feminine nouns use 'der' as the genitive article — this is a common source of confusion because 'der' is also the masculine nominative article. Unlike masculine and neuter nouns, feminine nouns do NOT add an '-s' or '-es' ending in the genitive. So 'die Chefin' becomes 'der Chefin'. Remember: genitive feminine = 'der', genitive masculine/neuter = 'des'. This distinction is crucial for accurate German.

items.question: Trotz _____ schlechten Wetters blieben wir draußen.
items.translation: (Despite the bad weather, we stayed outside)
items.explanation: The preposition 'trotz' (despite) requires the genitive case. This is one of the most important genitive prepositions in German, along with 'wegen' (because of), 'während' (during), and 'statt/anstatt' (instead of). Here, 'das Wetter' (neuter) becomes 'des Wetters' in the genitive. The adjective 'schlecht' also takes a genitive ending '-en'. Mastering genitive prepositions elevates your German significantly.

items.question: Während _____ Unterrichts darf man nicht telefonieren.
items.translation: (During class, one may not make phone calls)
items.explanation: 'Während' (during) is a genitive preposition. 'Der Unterricht' is masculine, so in the genitive it becomes 'des Unterrichts'. The '-s' ending on 'Unterrichts' is the genitive noun marker for masculine/neuter nouns. Common genitive prepositions to memorize: trotz (despite), während (during), wegen (because of), statt/anstatt (instead of), innerhalb (within), außerhalb (outside of).

items.question: Wegen _____ Staus kamen wir zu spät.
items.translation: (Because of the traffic jam, we arrived late)
items.explanation: 'Wegen' (because of) takes the genitive case. 'Der Stau' is masculine, becoming 'des Staus' in genitive. Note that in colloquial German, many speakers incorrectly use 'wegen dem Stau' (dative), but the grammatically correct form is always genitive. In formal writing, academic texts, and professional contexts, always use the genitive with 'wegen'. This is a hallmark of educated German usage.

items.question: Statt _____ Kuchens nahm ich einen Salat.
items.translation: (Instead of cake, I took a salad)
items.explanation: 'Statt' or 'anstatt' (instead of) requires the genitive. 'Der Kuchen' (masculine) becomes 'des Kuchens' in genitive. The '-s' ending is added because 'Kuchen' has more than one syllable — the rule is: monosyllabic nouns usually add '-es' (des Mannes), while polysyllabic nouns add '-s' (des Kuchens). This genitive noun ending is unique to masculine and neuter nouns; feminine and plural nouns don't change.

items.question: Die Meinung _____ Expertinnen ist wichtig.
items.translation: (The opinion of the female experts is important)
items.explanation: Plural nouns in the genitive take the article 'der' — regardless of the noun's original gender. So 'die Expertinnen' (plural) becomes 'der Expertinnen' in genitive. This is another potential confusion point since 'der' serves as masculine nominative singular AND genitive feminine singular AND genitive plural. Context and noun endings help disambiguate. Plural nouns themselves don't add extra endings in the genitive.

items.question: Innerhalb _____ Stadt gibt es viele Parks.
items.translation: (Within the city there are many parks)
items.explanation: 'Innerhalb' (within) is a genitive preposition. 'Die Stadt' is feminine, so it becomes 'der Stadt' in genitive. Remember: feminine nouns don't change their form in the genitive — only the article changes (die → der). Other spatial genitive prepositions include 'außerhalb' (outside of), 'oberhalb' (above), 'unterhalb' (below), 'diesseits' (this side of), and 'jenseits' (that side of).

items.question: Er ist ein Freund _____ Familie.
items.translation: (He is a friend of the family)
items.explanation: This classic genitive construction shows belonging: 'ein Freund der Familie' (a friend of the family). 'Die Familie' is feminine, becoming 'der Familie' in the genitive. The genitive is structurally equivalent to English 'of the...' constructions. In everyday speech, Germans might say 'ein Freund von der Familie' (dative with 'von'), but the genitive version is more elegant and preferred in written German.

items.question: Jenseits _____ Flusses liegt ein kleines Dorf.
items.translation: (Beyond the river lies a small village)
items.explanation: 'Jenseits' (beyond, on the other side of) takes the genitive. 'Der Fluss' is masculine, becoming 'des Flusses' in genitive. The double 's' + '-es' ending is required because 'Fluss' ends in a sibilant sound. This is a consistent rule: nouns ending in -s, -ß, -z, -tz, -x always add '-es' in the genitive (des Flusses, des Platzes, des Indexes). This ensures pronunciation clarity.

items.question: Aufgrund _____ Regens wurde das Spiel abgesagt.
items.translation: (Due to the rain, the game was cancelled)
items.explanation: 'Aufgrund' (due to, on account of) is a formal genitive preposition, often used in news and official communication. 'Der Regen' is masculine, becoming 'des Regens' in genitive. 'Aufgrund' is synonymous with 'wegen' but carries a more formal register. Using genitive prepositions correctly signals language proficiency and is expected in business correspondence, academic papers, and formal presentations.

items.question: Die Länge _____ Films beträgt zwei Stunden.
items.translation: (The length of the film is two hours)
items.explanation: Here the genitive expresses a measurable property of something: 'die Länge des Films' (the length of the film). 'Der Film' is masculine, becoming 'des Films' in genitive. This construction — abstract noun + genitive — is extremely common in academic and technical German: die Höhe des Berges (the height of the mountain), die Breite des Flusses (the width of the river), die Tiefe des Ozeans (the depth of the ocean).

items.question: Er kam anstatt _____ Bruders zur Feier.
items.translation: (He came instead of his brother to the celebration)
items.explanation: 'Anstatt' (instead of) is the full form of 'statt' and takes the genitive case. 'Der Bruder' is masculine, becoming 'des Bruders' in genitive. Note the possessive meaning is implied here — 'seines Bruders' would make it explicit, but context makes 'des Bruders' clear. 'Anstatt' and 'statt' are interchangeable, with 'anstatt' being slightly more formal. Both always require the genitive in standard German.

items.question: Die Ergebnisse _____ Forschung sind vielversprechend.
items.translation: (The results of the research are promising)
items.explanation: 'Die Forschung' is feminine, so it becomes 'der Forschung' in the genitive. This academic/scientific register heavily relies on genitive constructions. 'Die Ergebnisse der Forschung' is more formal than 'die Forschungsergebnisse' (compound noun) or 'die Ergebnisse von der Forschung' (von + dative). All three are grammatically possible, but the genitive form is most appropriate in formal writing and presentations.

items.question: Ein Hund, zwei _____.
items.explanation: German plural formation follows several patterns. 'Der Hund' (dog) forms its plural by adding '-e': 'die Hunde'. This '-e' plural is one of the most common patterns for masculine nouns. Unlike English, which mostly just adds '-s', German has five main plural endings: -e, -er, -(e)n, -s, and no change (sometimes with umlaut). There's no single rule — you must learn each noun's plural alongside its gender.

items.question: Das Kind hat viele _____.
items.explanation: 'Das Buch' (book) forms its plural as 'die Bücher' — adding '-er' AND changing the vowel to an umlaut (u → ü). This '-er' plural with umlaut is typical for many neuter nouns: das Kind → die Kinder, das Haus → die Häuser, das Land → die Länder. The umlaut change affects a, o, u (→ ä, ö, ü). This pattern is one of the trickiest for English speakers since it involves both a suffix and a vowel change.

items.question: Wir haben drei _____ im Haus.
items.translation: (We have three cats in the house)
items.explanation: 'Die Katze' forms its plural by adding '-n': 'die Katzen'. Most feminine nouns ending in '-e' form their plural this way — simply add '-n'. Examples: die Blume → die Blumen, die Straße → die Straßen, die Schule → die Schulen. This is one of the most predictable plural patterns in German. Feminine nouns ending in '-ung', '-keit', '-heit', '-schaft', '-tion' add '-en': die Zeitung → die Zeitungen.

items.question: Im Garten stehen viele _____.
items.translation: (There are many trees in the garden)
items.explanation: 'Der Baum' (tree) becomes 'die Bäume' — adding '-e' plus an umlaut (au → äu). This pattern of '-e' plus umlaut is common for masculine monosyllabic nouns: der Gast → die Gäste, der Satz → die Sätze, der Platz → die Plätze. The umlaut is not optional — 'Baume' without the umlaut would be incorrect. This is why learning plurals alongside the noun is so important in German.

items.question: Sie hat zwei _____.
items.explanation: 'Die Tochter' (daughter) becomes 'die Töchter' — no ending is added, but the vowel takes an umlaut (o → ö). This zero-ending-plus-umlaut pattern applies to some feminine and masculine nouns: die Mutter → die Mütter, der Vater → die Väter, der Bruder → die Brüder. These are typically two-syllable nouns ending in '-er', '-el', or '-en'. The umlaut alone signals the plural.

items.question: Im Büro gibt es fünf _____.
items.translation: (There are five computers in the office)
items.explanation: Some nouns, especially those ending in '-er', '-el', '-en', or borrowed words, have identical singular and plural forms (zero plural). 'Der Computer' → 'die Computer'. Other examples: der Lehrer → die Lehrer, das Fenster → die Fenster, das Mädchen → die Mädchen. You can only tell singular from plural by the article or context. This zero-change pattern is common for masculine and neuter nouns with these endings.

items.question: Er hat drei neue _____.
items.explanation: Borrowed words and informal short forms often take the '-s' plural: das Auto → die Autos, das Hotel → die Hotels, das Foto → die Fotos, das Sofa → die Sofas. This '-s' plural is the rarest native German plural but very common for loanwords, especially from English and French. It's also used for abbreviations (die CDs, die PCs) and words ending in vowels other than '-e' (das Oma → die Omas — colloquial).

items.question: Die _____ in der Schublade sind scharf.
items.translation: (The knives in the drawer are sharp)
items.explanation: 'Das Messer' (knife) has a zero plural — 'die Messer'. This is the same pattern as 'Computer' (no change between singular and plural). For neuter nouns ending in '-er' and '-chen' (diminutive suffix), the plural form is always identical to the singular: das Zimmer → die Zimmer, das Mädchen → die Mädchen, das Brötchen → die Brötchen. Only the article change (das → die) signals plural.

items.question: In der Stadt gibt es viele _____.
items.translation: (There are many museums in the city)
items.explanation: Words of Latin/Greek origin often have special plural forms. 'Das Museum' becomes 'die Museen' — the '-um' ending is replaced by '-en'. Similar patterns: das Zentrum → die Zentren, das Praktikum → die Praktika, das Visum → die Visa. These irregular plurals reflect the original language's plural patterns adapted into German. They are especially common in academic and scientific vocabulary.

items.question: Er kaufte zwei _____ Brot.
items.explanation: 'Das Brot' (bread/loaf) forms its plural as 'die Brote' with just the '-e' ending and no umlaut. Not all nouns that CAN take an umlaut actually do so in the plural. Compare: das Brot → die Brote (no umlaut) vs. das Buch → die Bücher (with umlaut). There's no reliable rule to predict which nouns umlaut and which don't — this must be memorized. A good dictionary always lists the plural form.

items.question: Die _____ diskutieren über das Projekt.
items.explanation: 'Der Kollege' is a weak masculine noun (schwache Deklination). These nouns add '-(e)n' in ALL cases except nominative singular: der Kollege, den/dem Kollegen, die Kollegen (plural). Other weak nouns: der Student → die Studenten, der Mensch → die Menschen, der Junge → die Jungen. Recognizing weak nouns is important because they follow this unique pattern across all cases, not just in the plural.

items.question: Auf dem Tisch liegen drei _____.
items.translation: (There are three apples on the table)
items.explanation: 'Der Apfel' becomes 'die Äpfel' — a zero-ending plural with umlaut (a → ä). This is similar to Mutter → Mütter and Vater → Väter. Masculine nouns ending in '-el' often follow this pattern: der Vogel → die Vögel, der Mantel → die Mäntel, der Nagel → die Nägel. The key indicator is the '-el' ending combined with masculine gender. The umlaut is the only change signaling plural.

items.question: Die _____ sind im Winter kalt.
items.explanation: 'Die Nacht' (night) becomes 'die Nächte' — adding '-e' plus umlaut. Many monosyllabic feminine nouns follow this pattern: die Hand → die Hände, die Stadt → die Städte, die Kraft → die Kräfte, die Wand → die Wände. This '-e' + umlaut pattern for feminine monosyllables is fairly reliable and worth memorizing as a group. Note: in dative plural, an additional '-n' is added: den Nächten.

items.question: Das Geschäft hat viele _____ im Angebot.
items.translation: (The store has many shirts on offer)
items.explanation: 'Das Hemd' (shirt) becomes 'die Hemden' — adding '-en'. This is less common for neuter nouns but does occur: das Bett → die Betten, das Hemd → die Hemden, das Auge → die Augen, das Ohr → die Ohren. The '-en' pattern is most common with feminine nouns, but these neuter exceptions must be learned individually. Always check a dictionary when unsure about a noun's plural form.

items.question: Im Park spielen viele _____.
items.translation: (Many children are playing in the park)
items.explanation: 'Das Kind' (child) becomes 'die Kinder' — adding '-er'. This is a classic neuter noun plural pattern. Other examples: das Bild → die Bilder, das Lied → die Lieder, das Kleid → die Kleider. Some also add an umlaut: das Haus → die Häuser, das Wort → die Wörter. The '-er' plural is almost exclusively used for neuter nouns (rare exception: der Mann → die Männer). It's one of the most distinctive German plural patterns.

items.question: Der _____ Mann trinkt Kaffee.
items.explanation: After the definite article 'der' (masculine nominative), the adjective takes the weak ending '-e'. This is the weak declension pattern: when a definite article (der/die/das) or similar determiner already shows the gender/case, the adjective takes a reduced 'weak' ending. In nominative: der alte Mann, die alte Frau, das alte Haus. All three genders get '-e' after the definite article in nominative. This simplifies things considerably!

items.question: Ich sehe den _____ Hund.
items.explanation: After 'den' (masculine accusative definite article), the adjective takes '-en'. In fact, the weak declension ending '-en' appears in MOST case/gender combinations after definite articles — all dative and genitive forms, masculine accusative, and all plurals use '-en'. Only nominative (all genders) and feminine/neuter accusative use '-e'. Rule of thumb: weak declension = mostly '-en', with '-e' in nominative and some accusative slots.

items.question: Ein _____ Auto steht vor dem Haus.
items.translation: (A red car is parked in front of the house)
items.explanation: After 'ein' (neuter nominative), the adjective must show the gender because 'ein' doesn't distinguish between masculine and neuter. The adjective takes '-es' (matching 'das'). This is the mixed declension: after ein-words (ein, kein, mein, dein, etc.), the adjective carries the strong ending when the ein-word doesn't show gender. Compare: ein roter Wagen (masc), eine rote Tasche (fem), ein rotes Auto (neut).

items.question: _____ Wein schmeckt gut.
items.explanation: When NO article precedes the noun (strong declension), the adjective itself must carry the full case/gender information. 'Wein' is masculine nominative, so the adjective takes '-er' (like the definite article 'der'). Strong endings essentially mirror the definite article endings: guter Wein (der), gute Milch (die), gutes Brot (das), gute Leute (die/plural). This makes strong declension the most demanding but also the most logical pattern.

items.question: Sie trinkt _____ Tee.
items.explanation: Without an article, masculine accusative takes the strong ending '-en'. This is because the accusative masculine strong ending mirrors 'den'. Strong declension accusative: heißen Tee (masc), heiße Suppe (fem), heißes Wasser (neut). Notice that masculine accusative '-en' is the same in all three declension types (strong, weak, mixed) — it's always '-en'. This is one consistent rule amidst the complexity of adjective endings.

items.question: Mit einem _____ Freund habe ich geredet.
items.explanation: After 'einem' (masculine/neuter dative), the adjective takes '-en' (mixed declension dative). In the dative case, ALL declension types (strong, weak, mixed) use '-en' for the adjective — making dative adjective endings the easiest to remember: always '-en'! Examples: mit dem/einem/gutem alten Freund. This universal '-en' in dative applies to all genders and plural forms as well.

items.question: Die Kinder essen _____ Eis.
items.explanation: Without an article before neuter accusative 'Eis', the adjective takes the strong ending '-es'. The strong endings reflect what the definite article would show: das Eis → kaltes Eis. This strong neuter '-es' ending appears in both nominative and accusative because neuter nouns have identical forms in these two cases. This nominative = accusative rule for neuter (and feminine) nouns simplifies adjective declension significantly.

items.question: Er hat eine _____ Idee.
items.explanation: After 'eine' (feminine accusative/nominative), the adjective takes '-e'. Since 'eine' already clearly signals feminine gender, the adjective only needs the weak ending '-e'. Feminine nominative and accusative are always identical in German — this applies to articles, adjectives, and the nouns themselves. So 'eine tolle Idee' works in both nominative (Eine tolle Idee kam mir) and accusative (Er hat eine tolle Idee).

items.question: Ich mag die _____ Blumen.
items.explanation: After 'die' (plural accusative definite article), the adjective takes '-en'. In the plural, weak declension adjectives take '-en' in ALL cases (nominative, accusative, dative, genitive). The definite article in plural always shows the case: die (nom/acc), den (dat), der (gen). Since the article carries the case information, the adjective stays at '-en' throughout. This makes plural adjective endings after definite articles simple: always '-en'.

items.question: Im Kühlschrank ist _____ Milch.
items.translation: (There is fresh milk in the fridge)
items.explanation: Without an article, feminine nominative takes the strong ending '-e'. This mirrors 'die' → '-e'. Strong feminine endings: frische Milch (nom/acc). Compare: an indefinite quantity context like this often omits the article entirely, requiring strong declension. The three scenarios that trigger each declension: definite article → weak, ein-word → mixed, no article → strong. Recognizing which scenario you're in is the first step to correct endings.

items.question: Das ist das Haus der _____ Nachbarn.
items.translation: (That is the house of the new neighbors)
items.explanation: After 'der' (genitive plural), the adjective takes '-en'. As mentioned earlier, genitive adjective endings are ALWAYS '-en' in all declension types, all genders, and plural. This makes genitive the easiest case for adjective endings: always '-en', no exceptions. Combined with dative (also always '-en'), you only need to focus on nominative and accusative distinctions — the other two cases are uniform.

items.question: Mein _____ Bruder studiert Medizin.
items.explanation: 'Mein' is an ein-word that doesn't show gender in masculine nominative (there's no ending on 'mein'). Therefore, the adjective must carry the strong ending '-er' to show masculine nominative. This is the mixed declension: the adjective shows strong endings where the ein-word is ambiguous (masc nom, neut nom/acc) and weak endings everywhere else. Compare: mein älterer Bruder (strong -er) vs. meinem älteren Bruder (weak -en in dative).

items.question: Wir brauchen _____ Hilfe.
items.explanation: Without an article, feminine accusative takes the strong ending '-e' (same as nominative feminine). Remember: feminine and neuter nouns always have identical nominative and accusative forms. The adjective endings follow this rule too. Strong feminine: schnelle Hilfe (both nom and acc). This reduces the number of unique strong endings you need to learn from 16 theoretical slots down to about 10 distinct forms.

items.question: Er fährt mit dem _____ Zug.
items.explanation: After 'dem' (masculine dative definite article), the adjective takes '-en'. This is the weak declension dative. As we've established, dative adjective endings are universally '-en' across all three declension types. The dative case is signaled by the article 'dem' (masc/neut) or 'der' (fem), and the adjective simply adds '-en'. This consistency makes dative the easiest case for adjective declension — one ending fits all.

items.question: Keine _____ Leute waren im Park.
items.translation: (No young people were in the park)
items.explanation: 'Keine' is an ein-word in the plural (note: unlike 'ein', 'kein' has plural forms). After 'keine' (plural nominative), the adjective takes '-en' because plural ein-words like 'keine/meine/deine' function like definite articles in plural — they clearly show the case. Therefore, the adjective uses weak endings in plural after ein-words, which means '-en' in all cases. This is the same behavior as after definite articles in the plural.

items.question: Ich _____ jeden Morgen um 6 Uhr _____.
items.translation: (I get up every morning at 6 o'clock)
items.explanation: 'Aufstehen' (to get up) is a separable verb. In main clauses with present or simple past tense, the prefix separates from the verb stem and moves to the END of the clause. The conjugated stem 'stehe' stays in second position (V2 rule), while the prefix 'auf' goes to the final position. This creates a 'sentence bracket' (Satzklammer) that is fundamental to German sentence structure. Common separable prefixes: auf-, an-, ab-, aus-, ein-, mit-, vor-, zu-.

items.question: Er _____ seine Freunde zum Essen _____.
items.explanation: 'Einladen' (to invite) separates into 'lädt ... ein'. Note the vowel change in the stem: laden → lädt (a → ä) for the third person singular. This is because 'laden' is a strong verb with a stem vowel change. Separable verbs follow ALL the regular conjugation patterns of their base verb — if the base verb has irregular forms, the separable version does too. The prefix simply detaches and moves to the end.

items.question: Wann _____ der Zug _____?
items.explanation: 'Abfahren' (to depart) separates in main clause questions: the conjugated verb 'fährt' takes first position (yes/no questions) or second position (W-questions), while the prefix 'ab' goes to the end. 'Fahren' has the stem change a → ä in second/third person singular. The prefix 'ab-' means 'away/off', giving 'abfahren' its meaning of 'to drive away' = 'to depart'. Understanding prefix meanings helps you guess new separable verbs' meanings.

items.question: Bitte _____ das Formular _____!
items.explanation: In imperative sentences, separable verbs also split: the verb stem takes first position and the prefix goes to the end. 'Ausfüllen' (to fill out) → 'Füllen Sie ... aus!' (formal) or 'Füll ... aus!' (informal). The prefix 'aus-' here means 'out/completely', transforming 'füllen' (to fill) into 'ausfüllen' (to fill out). Imperative separable verbs follow the same splitting pattern as declarative sentences.

items.question: Sie hat gestern _____ (einkaufen).
items.explanation: In the Perfekt (present perfect), separable verbs insert 'ge-' between the prefix and the stem: ein + ge + kauft = eingekauft. The prefix does NOT separate in the Perfekt because the past participle is a single word. This 'prefix-ge-stem' pattern is universal for separable verbs: aufgestanden, mitgebracht, angefangen, zugemacht. Compare inseparable verbs which have no 'ge-' at all: bestellt, verkauft, empfohlen.

items.question: Er muss morgen früh _____.
items.translation: (He has to get up early tomorrow)
items.explanation: When a modal verb (müssen, können, wollen, sollen, dürfen, mögen) is present, the separable verb goes to the END of the clause as an infinitive — and stays together! The modal verb is conjugated in V2 position, and the separable infinitive remains unsplit at the end. Compare: 'Er steht auf' (no modal, verb splits) vs. 'Er muss aufstehen' (with modal, verb stays together). This also applies to 'werden' (future) and other constructions using infinitives.

items.question: Ich weiß, dass er morgen _____.
items.explanation: In subordinate clauses (after dass, weil, wenn, ob, etc.), the conjugated verb moves to the FINAL position — and the separable verb stays together as one word. 'Ankommen' remains 'ankommt' at the clause end. Compare: Main clause: 'Er kommt morgen an.' / Subordinate: '...dass er morgen ankommt.' This is because the verb-final position in subordinate clauses eliminates the need for separation, since the verb is already at the end.

items.question: Sie _____ jeden Abend _____.
items.explanation: 'Fernsehen' (to watch TV) is a separable verb that splits into 'sieht ... fern'. The base verb 'sehen' has the stem change e → ie in second/third person singular: ich sehe, du siehst, er/sie sieht. 'Fern' means 'far/distant', so 'fernsehen' literally means 'to see far' — referring to television's ability to show distant images. Past participle: ferngesehen. Note: this is one of the separable verbs where the prefix is an adverb rather than a preposition.

items.question: _____ bitte die Tür _____!
items.explanation: 'Zumachen' (to close) separates in the imperative: 'Mach ... zu!' (informal singular). The prefix 'zu-' means 'closed/shut', while 'auf-' means 'open'. So 'zumachen' = to close, 'aufmachen' = to open. These are complementary pairs that are very common in everyday German. Other pairs: anmachen/ausmachen (turn on/off), einsteigen/aussteigen (board/exit). Learning separable verbs in such pairs is an efficient memory strategy.

items.question: Der Film _____ um 20 Uhr _____.
items.explanation: 'Anfangen' (to begin/start) separates into 'fängt ... an'. The stem change a → ä occurs in second/third person singular. 'Anfangen' and 'beginnen' both mean 'to start', but 'anfangen' is separable while 'beginnen' is inseparable (it has the inseparable prefix 'be-'). Both are equally common. Knowing whether a prefix is separable or inseparable is critical: separable prefixes are stressed (ÁNfangen), inseparable prefixes are unstressed (beGINnen).

items.question: Wir _____ am Wochenende _____.
items.explanation: 'Ausgehen' (to go out) separates in main clauses: 'gehen ... aus'. The prefix 'aus-' means 'out'. 'Ausgehen' can mean both 'to go out socially' and 'to turn off/go out' (Das Licht geht aus = The light goes out). Many separable verbs have multiple meanings depending on context. Other 'aus-' verbs: aussehen (to look/appear), aussteigen (to exit/get off), ausgeben (to spend money). Context determines the meaning.

items.question: Ich habe meine Jacke _____ (anziehen).
items.explanation: 'Anziehen' (to put on clothes) has the past participle 'angezogen': prefix 'an-' + 'ge-' + stem 'zogen'. Since 'ziehen' is a strong verb, the past participle uses '-en' instead of '-t' and has a vowel change (ziehen → gezogen). This double pattern — separable prefix insertion AND strong verb irregularity — stacks two challenges. Strong separable verbs: anfangen → angefangen, ausgehen → ausgegangen, einladen → eingeladen.

items.question: Sie _____ ihre Hausaufgaben immer pünktlich _____.
items.translation: (She always hands in her homework on time)
items.explanation: 'Abgeben' (to hand in/submit) separates into 'gibt ... ab'. The stem change e → i in 'geben' carries over: ich gebe, du gibst, er/sie gibt. The prefix 'ab-' means 'away/off'. The verb 'abgeben' is essential in academic and professional contexts: Aufgaben abgeben (submit assignments), eine Stimme abgeben (cast a vote), eine Erklärung abgeben (make a statement). The separated prefix always occupies the final position.

items.question: Wir wollen morgen früh _____.
items.explanation: 'Losfahren' (to set off/depart by vehicle) stays together as an infinitive after the modal verb 'wollen'. The prefix 'los-' means 'off/away' and conveys the start of movement: losgehen (to set off on foot), loslaufen (to start running), loslassen (to let go). Remember: with modal verbs, 'werden' (future), and 'zu + infinitive' constructions, the separable verb remains intact. Only in main clause present/past tense does it split.

items.question: Er _____ nicht gern am Wochenende _____.
items.translation: (He doesn't like to get up on the weekend)
items.explanation: Even with negation ('nicht') and adverbs ('gern'), the basic separation rule holds: conjugated stem in V2, prefix at the end. Negation and adverbs go between the two parts: 'Er steht nicht gern am Wochenende auf.' The sentence bracket (Satzklammer) between 'steht' and 'auf' contains ALL middle field elements — objects, adverbs, negation, time/manner/place expressions. This bracket structure is the backbone of German main clause word order.

items.question: Ich _____ mich jeden Morgen.
items.explanation: 'Sich waschen' (to wash oneself) is a reflexive verb requiring the reflexive pronoun 'mich' (accusative) for 'ich'. German reflexive pronouns in accusative: mich, dich, sich, uns, euch, sich. Reflexive verbs are much more common in German than in English — many actions you do to yourself require 'sich' in German. The reflexive pronoun usually follows the conjugated verb directly in main clauses: Ich wasche mich, du wäschst dich, er wäscht sich.

items.question: Er _____ sich für Musik.
items.explanation: 'Sich interessieren für' (to be interested in) is a reflexive verb that MUST always be used with 'sich' — it has no non-reflexive form in this meaning. These are called 'echt reflexive Verben' (truly reflexive verbs). Other examples: sich freuen (to be happy), sich beeilen (to hurry), sich erinnern (to remember). The preposition 'für' is fixed with this verb — 'sich interessieren für' is a verb-preposition combination that must be learned as a unit.

items.question: Wir müssen _____ beeilen.
items.explanation: 'Sich beeilen' (to hurry) requires the reflexive pronoun matching the subject. For 'wir' (we), the reflexive pronoun is 'uns'. With modal verbs, the reflexive pronoun comes after the modal's conjugated form: 'Wir müssen uns beeilen.' The infinitive 'beeilen' goes to the end. Complete reflexive pronoun set — accusative: mich (ich), dich (du), sich (er/sie/es), uns (wir), euch (ihr), sich (sie/Sie).

items.question: _____ du dich an den Urlaub?
items.explanation: 'Sich erinnern an' (to remember) is typically reflexive in German, unlike English 'to remember' which isn't. In questions, the verb comes first (yes/no questions) or after the question word (W-questions): 'Erinnerst du dich an den Urlaub?' The preposition 'an' + accusative is fixed with this verb. Many German reflexive verbs have associated prepositions: sich freuen auf (look forward to), sich freuen über (be glad about), sich kümmern um (take care of).

items.question: Die Kinder _____ sich die Hände.
items.explanation: When 'sich waschen' is used with a body part, the reflexive pronoun changes to DATIVE: 'Die Kinder waschen sich die Hände.' Here 'sich' is dative (the hands are accusative). German doesn't use possessive adjectives with body parts like English does — you say 'Ich wasche mir die Hände' (I wash myself the hands) NOT 'Ich wasche meine Hände'. Dative reflexive pronouns: mir, dir, sich, uns, euch, sich.

items.question: Sie _____ sich über das Geschenk.
items.explanation: 'Sich freuen über' (to be happy about — present reaction) uses 'sich' + 'über' + accusative. Compare with 'sich freuen auf' (to look forward to — future anticipation). This über/auf distinction is crucial: 'Ich freue mich über das Geschenk' (I'm glad about the gift I received) vs. 'Ich freue mich auf die Party' (I'm looking forward to the party). Third person singular: er/sie freut sich. The reflexive pronoun follows the verb directly.

items.question: Ich kann _____ das nicht vorstellen.
items.explanation: 'Sich etwas vorstellen' (to imagine something) takes a DATIVE reflexive pronoun because 'etwas' (something) is the accusative object. When there's already an accusative object in the sentence, the reflexive pronoun must be dative. Dative reflexive pronouns differ from accusative only in first and second person singular: mir (not mich), dir (not dich). All other forms are identical: sich, uns, euch, sich. Compare: Ich stelle mich vor (I introduce myself — acc) vs. Ich stelle mir das vor (I imagine that — dat).

items.question: Er hat _____ erkältet.
items.explanation: 'Sich erkälten' (to catch a cold) is a truly reflexive verb — it only exists in reflexive form. In the Perfekt, the reflexive pronoun stays with the helping verb: 'Er hat sich erkältet.' The past participle goes to the end. Other truly reflexive verbs with 'haben' in Perfekt: sich beeilt (hurried), sich interessiert (was interested), sich gewundert (wondered). The reflexive pronoun typically comes right after the conjugated auxiliary verb.

items.question: Setzt _____ bitte!
items.explanation: 'Sich setzen' (to sit down) is reflexive, meaning the action is directed at oneself. The imperative for 'ihr' (informal plural) uses 'euch': 'Setzt euch!' Imperative reflexive forms: Setz dich! (du), Setzt euch! (ihr), Setzen Sie sich! (Sie/formal). Note that for 'du', the reflexive pronoun is 'dich', for 'ihr' it's 'euch', and for 'Sie' it's 'sich'. Reflexive verbs in imperatives are very common for instructions: Beeil dich! Erinnere dich!

items.question: Wir treffen _____ morgen um 10 Uhr.
items.explanation: 'Sich treffen' (to meet each other) is a reciprocal reflexive verb — the action goes back and forth between the subjects. Other reciprocal reflexives: sich kennenlernen (to get to know each other), sich streiten (to argue with each other), sich unterhalten (to converse). For 'wir', the reflexive pronoun is 'uns'. Reciprocal reflexives only work with plural subjects (wir, ihr, sie) since you need at least two people for mutual action.

items.question: Hast du _____ schon die Zähne geputzt?
items.explanation: 'Sich die Zähne putzen' (to brush one's teeth) takes the dative reflexive because 'die Zähne' is the accusative object. In German, body care routines with body parts use dative reflexive: Ich putze mir die Zähne, ich wasche mir die Haare, ich trockne mir die Hände ab. For 'du', dative reflexive is 'dir' (not 'dich'). In the Perfekt question: 'Hast du dir die Zähne geputzt?' — reflexive pronoun follows the subject.

items.question: Das Buch _____ sich gut.
items.explanation: Some verbs use 'sich' to express a passive-like meaning without being truly passive. 'Das Buch verkauft sich gut' = The book sells well (the book isn't actively selling itself — it's being sold well). Other examples: Das lässt sich machen (That can be done), Das erklärt sich von selbst (That speaks for itself). These pseudo-reflexive constructions are idiomatic and very common in German as alternatives to the passive voice.

items.question: Ich _____ mich bei Ihnen für die Hilfe.
items.explanation: 'Sich bedanken bei + dative + für + accusative' (to thank someone for something) is a formal reflexive construction. 'Bei Ihnen' = at/with you (formal), 'für die Hilfe' = for the help. Compare the non-reflexive 'danken' (+ dative): 'Ich danke Ihnen.' Both are correct, but 'sich bedanken' is slightly more formal. The inseparable prefix 'be-' combines with the reflexive 'sich' — these can co-occur. Past participle: bedankt (no 'ge-' because of inseparable prefix).

items.question: Sie _____ sich über den Lärm.
items.explanation: 'Sich beschweren über' (to complain about) is a truly reflexive verb with an inseparable prefix 'be-'. The preposition 'über' + accusative is always used with this verb. Third person singular: 'sie beschwert sich.' In subordinate clauses, the reflexive pronoun follows the subject: '...weil sie sich über den Lärm beschwert.' Complaints in German: sich beschweren (formal), sich beklagen (literary), meckern (colloquial, not reflexive).

items.question: Wir haben _____ sehr über deinen Besuch gefreut.
items.translation: (We were very happy about your visit)
items.explanation: In the Perfekt, 'sich freuen über' becomes 'haben + sich + über ... gefreut'. For 'wir', the reflexive pronoun 'uns' comes after the auxiliary 'haben': 'Wir haben uns gefreut.' In a more complex sentence with adverbs and prepositional phrases, the reflexive pronoun still follows the conjugated verb/auxiliary: 'Wir haben uns sehr über deinen Besuch gefreut.' The past participle 'gefreut' goes to the final position as usual in the Perfekt.

items.question: Ich lerne Deutsch, _____ es ist interessant.
items.translation: (I learn German because it is interesting)
items.explanation: German has two words for 'because': 'denn' and 'weil'. The crucial difference is word order. 'Denn' is a coordinating conjunction — the verb stays in position 2 (normal word order): 'es ist interessant.' 'Weil' is a subordinating conjunction that sends the verb to the end: 'weil es interessant ist.' Both mean 'because,' but 'denn' keeps the sentence structure simpler. Coordinating conjunctions (und, oder, aber, denn, sondern) never change word order. This is a fundamental rule in German sentence construction.

items.question: Er spielt Fußball, _____ sie liest ein Buch.
items.translation: (He plays football and she reads a book)
items.explanation: 'Und' (and) is the most basic coordinating conjunction in German, identical in function to English 'and.' As a coordinating conjunction, 'und' connects two equal clauses without changing word order in either clause. The verb remains in position 2 in both parts. In German, coordinating conjunctions (und, oder, aber, denn, sondern) sit between clauses like bridges but don't affect the internal structure. Compare: 'Er spielt und sie liest' — both verbs stay right after their subjects, exactly as they would in standalone sentences.

items.question: Ich mag Kaffee, _____ ich trinke lieber Tee.
items.translation: (I like coffee but I prefer to drink tea)
items.explanation: 'Aber' (but) expresses contrast, just like English 'but.' It's a coordinating conjunction, so it doesn't change word order. The verb stays in position 2 in both clauses. German has a related conjunction 'sondern' (but rather), which is used specifically after negation to present an alternative: 'Ich trinke nicht Kaffee, sondern Tee.' The distinction between 'aber' (general contrast) and 'sondern' (correction after negation) doesn't exist in English, making it a uniquely German feature that learners must master.

items.question: Möchtest du Kaffee _____ Tee?
items.explanation: 'Oder' (or) presents alternatives, functioning like English 'or.' As a coordinating conjunction, it connects options without affecting word order. In German, 'oder' can also be used as a tag question at the end of a sentence: 'Du kommst morgen, oder?' (You're coming tomorrow, right?). When listing multiple options, German typically uses commas between items with 'oder' before the last: 'Kaffee, Tee oder Wasser.' Unlike English, German never uses an 'Oxford comma' before 'oder' or 'und' in lists.

items.question: Er isst nicht Fleisch, _____ Fisch.
items.translation: (He doesn't eat meat but rather fish)
items.explanation: 'Sondern' (but rather/instead) is a uniquely challenging conjunction for English speakers because English has no exact equivalent. It's used ONLY after a negation (nicht, kein, nie) to present what is true instead. 'Aber' shows contrast: 'Er ist müde, aber er arbeitet' (He's tired but he works). 'Sondern' corrects: 'Er ist nicht müde, sondern hungrig' (He's not tired but rather hungry). The rule is simple: if you can say 'but rather' or 'but instead' in English, use 'sondern.' If it's just general contrast, use 'aber.'

items.question: Ich gehe spazieren, _____ das Wetter schön ist.
items.translation: (I go for a walk because the weather is nice)
items.explanation: 'Weil' (because) is a subordinating conjunction — and here comes the critical difference from 'denn.' After 'weil,' the conjugated verb moves to the END of the clause: 'weil das Wetter schön IST' (not 'weil das Wetter IST schön'). This verb-final rule applies to ALL subordinating conjunctions in German (weil, dass, wenn, ob, obwohl, etc.). This is one of the most fundamental word order rules in German. The subordinate clause acts as a unit, and the verb anchors it at the end. Mastering this verb-final pattern is essential for sounding natural in German.

items.question: Ich weiß nicht, _____ er heute kommt.
items.translation: (I don't know whether he comes today)
items.explanation: 'Ob' (whether/if) introduces indirect yes/no questions and sends the verb to the end. Compare: Direct question: 'Kommt er heute?' (Does he come today?) → Indirect: 'Ich weiß nicht, ob er heute kommt.' The verb 'kommt' moves from position 1 (question format) to the end of the clause. Don't confuse 'ob' with 'wenn' — 'ob' is for indirect yes/no questions (whether), while 'wenn' means 'if' (conditional) or 'when' (temporal). This distinction doesn't exist clearly in English where 'if' serves both purposes.

items.question: Ich esse gern Pizza, _____ ich keinen Hunger habe.
items.translation: (I like eating pizza even though I'm not hungry)
items.explanation: 'Obwohl' (although/even though) is a subordinating conjunction expressing concession. Like all subordinating conjunctions, it pushes the verb to the end: 'obwohl ich keinen Hunger HABE.' The concessive meaning is similar to English 'although' but the word order transformation is uniquely German. Related concessive connectors include 'trotzdem' (nevertheless) — but 'trotzdem' is an adverb, not a conjunction, so it DOESN'T send the verb to the end: 'Ich habe keinen Hunger. Trotzdem esse ich Pizza.' Understanding this distinction prevents common word order errors.

items.question: Er lernt Deutsch, _____ er in Deutschland arbeiten kann.
items.translation: (He learns German so that he can work in Germany)
items.explanation: 'Damit' (so that/in order that) expresses purpose and is a subordinating conjunction — verb goes to the end. 'Er lernt Deutsch, damit er in Deutschland arbeiten KANN.' When the subject is the same in both clauses, you can use 'um...zu + infinitive' instead: 'Er lernt Deutsch, um in Deutschland zu arbeiten.' This 'um...zu' construction is often preferred for same-subject purpose clauses because it's shorter and more elegant. But when subjects differ, only 'damit' works. Both structures express purpose but with different grammatical requirements.

items.question: _____ ich nach Hause komme, esse ich zu Abend.
items.translation: (When I come home, I eat dinner)
items.explanation: German has three words where English uses 'when': 'wenn,' 'wann,' and 'als.' 'Wenn' means 'when/whenever' for present, future, or repeated past events. 'Wann' is for questions: 'Wann kommst du?' (When are you coming?). 'Als' is for single past events: 'Als ich ein Kind war' (When I was a child). Here, coming home is a repeated/habitual action, so 'wenn' is correct. After 'wenn' (subordinating conjunction), the verb goes to the end: 'wenn ich nach Hause KOMME.' Then the main clause starts with the verb (verb-second rule after a subordinate clause).

items.question: Ich bleibe zu Hause, _____ es regnet.
items.explanation: 'Wenn' serves double duty in German — it means both 'when' (temporal, for present/future/repeated) and 'if' (conditional). Context determines the meaning. 'Wenn es regnet, bleibe ich zu Hause' can mean 'When it rains' or 'If it rains.' For clarity, Germans sometimes add 'falls' (in case) for conditional meaning: 'Falls es regnet...' The verb goes to the end after 'wenn': 'wenn es REGNET.' Note the inverted word order in the main clause that follows: 'bleibe ich' — verb first, then subject. This inversion is mandatory when a sentence begins with a subordinate clause.

items.question: Ich glaube, _____ er recht hat.
items.explanation: 'Dass' (that) introduces noun clauses and is one of the most frequently used subordinating conjunctions in German. Don't confuse it with 'das' (one 's') — the article/pronoun. 'Dass' (two s's) is always a conjunction: 'Ich glaube, dass er recht HAT.' The verb 'hat' goes to the end. In spoken German, 'dass' is sometimes dropped (like English): 'Ich glaube, er hat recht' — but this changes the word order back to normal! When you include 'dass,' verb-final is mandatory. When you omit it, you get two independent-looking clauses with normal word order.

items.question: _____ du fertig bist, können wir gehen.
items.translation: (As soon as you are ready, we can go)
items.explanation: 'Sobald' (as soon as) is a temporal subordinating conjunction. Like all subordinating conjunctions, it sends the verb to the end of its clause: 'sobald du fertig BIST.' Other temporal conjunctions include: 'bevor' (before), 'nachdem' (after), 'während' (while), 'bis' (until), 'seitdem' (since). Each has its own tense requirements — 'nachdem' typically requires a tense shift (past perfect + past: 'Nachdem er gegessen hatte, ging er'). Temporal conjunctions give German its precise time-relationship expressing ability, more structured than English equivalents.

items.question: Er arbeitet weiter, _____ er krank ist.
items.translation: (He continues working although he is sick)
items.explanation: 'Obwohl' (although) expresses that something happens despite a contradicting circumstance. The verb goes to the end: 'obwohl er krank IST.' Synonyms include 'obgleich' and 'obschon' (more literary). A common mistake is confusing 'obwohl' (subordinating conjunction, verb-final) with 'trotzdem' (adverb, verb-second): CORRECT: 'Obwohl er krank ist, arbeitet er.' ALSO CORRECT: 'Er ist krank. Trotzdem arbeitet er.' WRONG: 'Trotzdem er krank ist...' — 'trotzdem' cannot introduce a subordinate clause (though colloquially some speakers do this).

items.question: Ich rufe dich an, _____ ich angekommen bin.
items.translation: (I'll call you after I have arrived)
items.explanation: 'Nachdem' (after) is a temporal subordinating conjunction with a special tense requirement. It typically demands a tense shift: the 'nachdem' clause uses a tense ONE STEP further in the past than the main clause. Here: 'Nachdem ich angekommen BIN (present perfect)' → 'rufe ich dich an (present).' In past contexts: 'Nachdem er gegessen HATTE (past perfect)' → 'ging er spazieren (simple past).' This tense-shifting rule makes 'nachdem' trickier than English 'after,' which doesn't require tense changes. Compare with 'bevor' (before), which uses the SAME tense in both clauses.

items.question: Ich weiß, dass er Deutsch _____.
items.explanation: In German subordinate clauses (Nebensätze), the conjugated verb moves to the FINAL position. 'Ich weiß, dass er Deutsch spricht' — the verb 'spricht' sits at the very end. In a main clause, it would be: 'Er spricht Deutsch' (verb in position 2). This verb-final rule is the hallmark of all German subordinate clauses and applies after every subordinating conjunction (dass, weil, wenn, ob, obwohl, etc.). English never moves its verbs this way, making this one of the most challenging adjustments for English speakers learning German.

items.question: Er fragt, ob sie morgen _____.
items.explanation: Indirect questions in German use 'ob' (whether) for yes/no questions and question words (wer, was, wo, etc.) for information questions. Both create subordinate clauses with verb-final order. Direct: 'Kommt sie morgen?' → Indirect: 'Er fragt, ob sie morgen KOMMT.' The verb moves from first position (question format) to the end. Notice that the subordinate clause loses its question mark and question word order. The conjugation 'kommt' matches 'sie' (she) — third person singular. This transformation from direct to indirect questions is essential for polite, formal German communication.

items.question: Wenn es regnet, _____ ich zu Hause.
items.translation: (When/If it rains, I stay at home)
items.explanation: When a subordinate clause comes FIRST in a sentence, the main clause begins with the verb — this creates a verb-verb pattern at the comma. 'Wenn es regnet, BLEIBE ich...' — the subordinate clause occupies position 1, so the main verb takes position 2 immediately after the comma. Think of the entire subordinate clause as one big 'first element.' This produces German's characteristic 'verb, verb' pattern at the junction: '...regnet, bleibe...' This rule is absolute. If you started with the main clause, normal order applies: 'Ich bleibe zu Hause, wenn es regnet.'

items.question: Sie sagt, dass sie das Buch gelesen _____.
items.translation: (She says that she has read the book)
items.explanation: In subordinate clauses with compound tenses (like Perfekt), the auxiliary verb (haben/sein) goes to the very end — AFTER the past participle. Main clause: 'Sie HAT das Buch gelesen' (auxiliary in position 2). Subordinate: 'dass sie das Buch gelesen HAT' (auxiliary at the end). The past participle 'gelesen' comes second-to-last, and the conjugated auxiliary anchors the clause at the end. This is the opposite of English word order and catches many learners off guard. The rule: in subordinate clauses, the conjugated verb (whatever it is) ALWAYS goes last.

items.question: Ich verstehe nicht, warum er nicht kommen _____.
items.translation: (I don't understand why he cannot come)
items.explanation: With modal verbs in subordinate clauses, the modal (conjugated) verb goes to the end, AFTER the infinitive. Main clause: 'Er KANN nicht kommen' (modal in position 2). Subordinate: 'warum er nicht kommen KANN' (modal at the end). The infinitive 'kommen' precedes the modal 'kann.' This reversal of modal + infinitive order is a key feature of German subordinate clauses. When question words (warum, wo, wie, wann, etc.) introduce indirect questions, they function exactly like subordinating conjunctions — verb goes to the end.

items.question: Das ist der Mann, _____ ich gestern gesehen habe.
items.translation: (That is the man whom I saw yesterday)
items.explanation: Relative clauses are subordinate clauses introduced by relative pronouns. The relative pronoun 'den' (whom, masculine accusative) refers back to 'der Mann.' In the relative clause, 'den' is the direct object of 'gesehen habe' (saw), requiring accusative case. The verb 'habe' goes to the end (subordinate clause rule). German relative pronouns must match their antecedent in gender and number, but take their case from their function in the relative clause. 'Der Mann' is masculine — and since he's being seen (direct object), the pronoun takes accusative: 'den.'

items.question: Bevor ich zur Arbeit gehe, _____ ich.
items.translation: (Before I go to work, I eat breakfast)
items.explanation: 'Bevor' (before) is a temporal subordinating conjunction. The subordinate clause 'bevor ich zur Arbeit gehe' has verb-final order ('gehe' at the end). Since this clause comes first, the main clause starts with the verb: 'frühstücke ich.' This verb-first pattern in the main clause after an initial subordinate clause is non-negotiable in German. The entire subordinate clause counts as position 1, forcing the verb into position 2. 'Frühstücken' (to have breakfast) conjugates to 'frühstücke' for 'ich' — dropping the -n and adding -e, the standard first-person singular ending.

items.question: Während er _____, höre ich Musik.
items.translation: (While he works, I listen to music)
items.explanation: 'Während' (while) introduces a temporal subordinate clause expressing simultaneous actions. The verb 'arbeitet' goes to the end of the 'während' clause. Since the subordinate clause comes first, the main clause inverts: 'höre ich' (verb before subject). 'Während' can also be a preposition meaning 'during': 'während der Arbeit' (during work) — followed by genitive case. As a conjunction, it introduces a full clause with subject and verb. This dual function (conjunction and preposition) is common in German. Context and structure tell you which role it's playing.

items.question: Seitdem er hier _____, spricht er besser Deutsch.
items.translation: (Since he has been living here, he speaks better German)
items.explanation: 'Seitdem' (since, in a temporal sense) marks the starting point of an ongoing situation. The verb 'wohnt' goes to the end of the subordinate clause. A key difference from English: German uses present tense for ongoing situations that started in the past. English says 'since he HAS BEEN living' (present perfect continuous), but German says 'seitdem er hier WOHNT' (present tense). This is because German views the action as currently happening, not as a duration from past to present. This tense difference between German and English with 'seit/seitdem' trips up many learners.

items.question: Ich lerne Deutsch, damit ich in Berlin arbeiten _____.
items.translation: (I learn German so that I can work in Berlin)
items.explanation: 'Damit' (so that) expresses purpose and creates a subordinate clause with verb-final order. The modal verb 'kann' goes to the end, after the infinitive 'arbeiten.' When subject differs between clauses, 'damit' is required: 'Ich lerne, damit ICH arbeiten kann' vs. 'Er erklärt, damit WIR verstehen.' When the subject is the same, the shorter 'um...zu' construction is preferred: 'Ich lerne Deutsch, um in Berlin zu arbeiten' — no subject needed, infinitive with 'zu.' This choice between 'damit' and 'um...zu' is a style point German teachers emphasize.

items.question: Obwohl es kalt _____, gehen wir spazieren.
items.translation: (Although it is cold, we go for a walk)
items.explanation: 'Obwohl' (although) introduces a concessive subordinate clause. The verb 'ist' goes to the end: 'obwohl es kalt IST.' Since the subordinate clause comes first, the main clause inverts: 'gehen wir.' Concessive clauses express something unexpected — it's cold, but we walk anyway. The subject 'es' (it) takes 'ist' (is) — third person singular of 'sein.' In German weather and temperature expressions, 'es' is used like English 'it': 'Es ist kalt/warm/heiß' (It is cold/warm/hot). This impersonal 'es' is essential for weather, time, and general state expressions.

items.question: Er fragt, wo die Bibliothek _____.
items.explanation: Indirect questions using question words (wo, wer, was, wie, wann, warum) follow subordinate clause word order — verb at the end. Direct question: 'Wo IST die Bibliothek?' (verb in position 2). Indirect: 'wo die Bibliothek IST' (verb at the end). The question word acts as the subordinating element. This is one of German's most elegant patterns: any question word can transform a direct question into an embedded clause by simply moving the verb to the end. The politeness formula uses this: 'Können Sie mir sagen, wo die Bibliothek ist?' — much softer than a direct question.

items.question: Ich bin froh, dass du mich _____ hast.
items.translation: (I am glad that you called me)
items.explanation: In subordinate clauses with separable verbs in the Perfekt, the past participle stays together (prefix reattaches) and the auxiliary goes to the end. Main clause: 'Du HAST mich angerufen' (auxiliary in position 2, participle at end). Subordinate: 'dass du mich angerufen HAST' (participle second-to-last, auxiliary last). Separable verbs like 'anrufen' form their past participle by inserting 'ge-' between prefix and stem: an-ge-rufen. In subordinate clauses, the formerly separated prefix stays attached to the verb — no splitting occurs. The auxiliary 'hast' (have, for du) closes the clause.

items.question: Bis er nach Hause _____, warte ich hier.
items.translation: (Until he comes home, I'll wait here)
items.explanation: 'Bis' (until) is a temporal subordinating conjunction. The verb 'kommt' goes to the end of the 'bis' clause. Like 'wenn,' German uses present tense after 'bis' for future events, while English might use present or future: 'Until he comes.' The main clause inverts because the subordinate clause comes first: 'warte ich.' 'Bis' can also function as a preposition: 'bis morgen' (until tomorrow), 'bis nächste Woche' (until next week). As a preposition, it often pairs with 'zu': 'bis zum Bahnhof' (up to/until the train station). Context determines its grammatical role.

items.question: Falls es morgen _____, nehme ich einen Regenschirm mit.
items.translation: (In case it rains tomorrow, I'll take an umbrella)
items.explanation: 'Falls' (in case/if) is more specific than 'wenn' for conditional meaning. While 'wenn' can mean both 'when' and 'if,' 'falls' exclusively means 'if/in case,' removing ambiguity. The verb 'regnet' goes to the end of the subordinate clause. German uses present tense for future conditional events (like English): 'falls es regnet' not 'falls es regnen wird.' The main clause shows typical inversion after a leading subordinate clause: 'nehme ich.' Using 'falls' instead of 'wenn' signals that you're talking about a possibility, not a certainty or temporal event — a subtle but appreciated precision in German communication.

items.question: Der Mann, _____ dort steht, ist mein Lehrer.
items.translation: (The man who stands there is my teacher)
