items.explanation: German relative pronouns must match their antecedent in gender and number, but take their case from their role in the relative clause. 'Der Mann' is masculine singular. In the relative clause, the pronoun is the SUBJECT (who stands there) — so it takes nominative case: 'der.' The relative pronoun forms mirror the definite articles with some exceptions in genitive and dative plural. Nominative relative pronouns: der (masc.), die (fem.), das (neut.), die (plural). The verb goes to the end of the relative clause: 'der dort STEHT.' English simply uses 'who' regardless of case — German requires case precision.

items.question: Die Frau, _____ ich geholfen habe, ist meine Nachbarin.
items.translation: (The woman whom I helped is my neighbor)
items.explanation: This is tricky! 'Die Frau' is feminine, and 'helfen' (to help) takes the DATIVE case in German — not accusative like English 'to help someone.' So the relative pronoun needs feminine dative: 'der.' Yes, feminine dative looks the same as masculine nominative! Dative relative pronouns: dem (masc.), der (fem.), dem (neut.), denen (plural). The verb 'helfen' is one of many German verbs that govern dative: helfen, danken, gratulieren, folgen, gehören. This dative requirement doesn't exist in English, making it a classic pitfall. Always check: does the verb require accusative or dative?

items.question: Das Buch, _____ auf dem Tisch liegt, gehört mir.
items.translation: (The book that lies on the table belongs to me)
items.explanation: 'Das Buch' is neuter singular. The relative pronoun is the subject of the relative clause (the book LIES) — so it takes nominative case: 'das' (neuter nominative). German relative clauses are always set off by commas — unlike English, where 'the book that lies on the table' has no commas. This is a fixed punctuation rule in German: EVERY relative clause gets commas. The verb 'liegt' goes to the end. Note: 'liegen' (to lie/be located) is different from 'legen' (to lay/put) — 'liegen' describes a state, 'legen' describes an action. Both are important positional verbs in German.

items.question: Die Kinder, _____ im Garten spielen, sind laut.
items.translation: (The children who play in the garden are loud)
items.explanation: 'Die Kinder' is plural. Plural relative pronouns in nominative and accusative are 'die' — same form for both cases. Here, the children are the SUBJECT of the relative clause (they play), so nominative plural 'die' is correct. The verb 'spielen' goes to the end of the relative clause. German relative clauses always follow their antecedent immediately — you can't separate them like sometimes in English. The main verb 'sind' comes after the entire relative clause closes. This can create long distances between subject and main verb, which is characteristic of German sentence structure.

items.question: Der Film, _____ wir gestern gesehen haben, war toll.
items.translation: (The film that we saw yesterday was great)
items.explanation: 'Der Film' is masculine singular. In the relative clause, the film is the OBJECT being seen (wir haben den Film gesehen) — so accusative case is needed: 'den' (masculine accusative). The auxiliary 'haben' goes to the end of the relative clause (Perfekt in subordinate clause). Accusative relative pronouns: den (masc.), die (fem.), das (neut.), die (plural). Notice that only masculine singular changes in accusative (der → den) — all others stay the same as nominative. This parallel to definite articles makes the system learnable: if you know article declension, you know most relative pronouns.

items.question: Die Stadt, in _____ ich wohne, ist sehr schön.
items.translation: (The city in which I live is very beautiful)
items.explanation: When a relative pronoun follows a preposition, the preposition determines the case. 'In' with location (wo?) takes dative: 'in der Stadt' → 'in der.' 'Die Stadt' is feminine, and feminine dative is 'der.' The preposition + relative pronoun combination stays together at the beginning of the relative clause. Unlike English, where you can say 'the city I live in' (preposition stranded at the end), German MUST front the preposition: 'in der ich wohne' — never 'der ich in wohne.' This is a strict rule: prepositions in relative clauses always precede their pronoun in German.

items.question: Der Lehrer, _____ Klasse ich besuche, ist nett.
items.translation: (The teacher whose class I attend is nice)
items.explanation: 'Dessen' is the masculine/neuter genitive relative pronoun meaning 'whose.' 'Der Lehrer' is masculine, so 'dessen' (masculine genitive) is used. Genitive relative pronouns: dessen (masc./neut.), deren (fem./plural). Key rule: the noun after 'dessen/deren' has NO article: 'dessen Klasse' (NOT 'dessen die Klasse'). The possessive meaning replaces the article. 'Dessen/deren' is actually easier than English 'whose' in one way — it never changes based on the following noun's gender. But it requires remembering genitive forms, which many German speakers themselves sometimes avoid in casual speech.

items.question: Die Frau, _____ Kinder hier spielen, ist meine Kollegin.
items.translation: (The woman whose children play here is my colleague)
items.explanation: 'Die Frau' is feminine, so the genitive relative pronoun is 'deren' (feminine genitive = whose). 'Deren Kinder' means 'whose children.' Like 'dessen,' 'deren' replaces the article before the following noun — never write 'deren die Kinder.' The verb 'spielen' goes to the end of the relative clause. 'Deren' is also used for all plural antecedents regardless of gender: 'Die Männer, deren Frauen...' (The men whose wives...). This genitive relative pronoun system is more regular than the English 'whose' and once learned, consistently applies across all contexts.

items.question: Alles, _____ er sagt, ist wahr.
items.explanation: After indefinite pronouns like 'alles' (everything), 'nichts' (nothing), 'etwas' (something), 'vieles' (much), the relative pronoun is 'was' — not 'das.' 'Alles, was er sagt' (everything that he says). This also applies after superlatives used as nouns: 'Das Beste, was ich kenne' (The best thing I know) and after entire clauses: 'Er hat bestanden, was mich freut' (He passed, which makes me happy). This 'was' as relative pronoun is unique to German — English uses 'that' or 'which' in all these cases without distinction. It's a small but important rule for sounding correct.

items.question: Der Hund, _____ ich jeden Tag füttere, ist sehr groß.
items.translation: (The dog that I feed every day is very big)
items.explanation: 'Der Hund' is masculine singular. In the relative clause, the dog is being fed — it's the direct object of 'füttern' (to feed), which takes accusative: 'den' (masculine accusative). The verb 'füttere' goes to the end of the relative clause. Notice the pattern for determining case: identify the verb in the relative clause, determine what role the relative pronoun plays (subject = nominative, direct object = accusative, indirect object = dative, possession = genitive), then select the correct form. This systematic approach to case selection works for every German relative clause.

items.question: Die Studenten, _____ er das Buch geliehen hat, sind dankbar.
items.translation: (The students to whom he lent the book are grateful)
items.explanation: Plural dative relative pronoun is 'denen.' 'Leihen' (to lend) is a ditransitive verb taking dative (person) + accusative (thing): 'Er hat DEN STUDENTEN (dative) das Buch (accusative) geliehen.' In the relative clause, the students are the indirect object (dative), so 'denen' is required. Note: 'denen' (dative plural relative pronoun) is different from 'den' (accusative masculine singular). The extra '-en' signals dative plural. The auxiliary 'hat' goes to the end of the relative clause. Dative plural 'denen' only appears as a relative pronoun — the regular dative plural article is 'den.'

items.question: Das Haus, _____ wir wohnen, ist alt.
items.translation: (The house in which we live is old)
items.explanation: 'Das Haus' is neuter. 'Wohnen in' (to live in) with location takes dative: 'in dem Haus.' The neuter dative relative pronoun is 'dem.' The preposition 'in' combines with the relative pronoun at the start of the clause: 'in dem wir wohnen.' In formal written German, 'in dem' can be replaced by 'worin' (where-in): 'Das Haus, worin wir wohnen.' These 'wo(r)+preposition' forms (worin, worauf, worüber) are elegant alternatives for relative clauses with prepositions, especially when the antecedent is a thing rather than a person.

items.question: Das ist etwas, _____ ich nicht verstehe.
items.translation: (That is something that I don't understand)
items.explanation: After 'etwas' (something), the relative pronoun must be 'was,' not 'das.' This rule applies to all indefinite neuter pronouns: etwas, nichts, alles, vieles, weniges, manches, einiges. It also applies after neuter superlatives: 'das Schönste, was ich je gesehen habe' (the most beautiful thing I've ever seen). The logic: these words refer to undefined, non-specific things, and 'was' (what) matches this indefiniteness better than 'das' (that/which). The verb 'verstehe' goes to the end. In accusative, 'was' stays 'was' — it doesn't decline like other relative pronouns.

items.question: Der Arzt, zu _____ ich gehe, ist sehr erfahren.
items.translation: (The doctor to whom I go is very experienced)
items.explanation: 'Zu' always takes dative case. 'Der Arzt' is masculine, so masculine dative is 'dem.' The preposition 'zu' + relative pronoun stays together at the beginning: 'zu dem ich gehe.' In everyday German, 'zu dem' can be shortened to 'wozu' only when referring to things, not people. For people, you must keep 'zu dem/der/denen.' The verb 'gehe' goes to the end of the relative clause. This preposition + relative pronoun construction is very common in German and requires knowing both which case the preposition governs AND the gender of the antecedent.

items.question: Er hat die Prüfung bestanden, _____ uns alle freut.
items.translation: (He passed the exam, which makes us all happy)
items.explanation: When a relative clause refers to an entire preceding clause (not a specific noun), German uses 'was': 'Er hat die Prüfung bestanden, was uns alle freut.' The 'was' refers to the whole fact of passing the exam. English uses 'which' in this context. If the relative pronoun referred to a specific noun like 'Prüfung' (feminine), you'd use 'die.' But here, it refers to the entire situation — a sentential relative clause. This distinction (referring to a noun vs. referring to a whole clause) determines whether you use standard relative pronouns (der/die/das) or 'was.'

items.question: Das Haus _____ gebaut.
items.explanation: German forms the present passive (Vorgangspassiv) with 'werden' + past participle. 'Das Haus wird gebaut' = The house is being built. The auxiliary 'werden' conjugates for person and number while the past participle stays at the end. This 'process passive' (Vorgangspassiv) describes an action being performed. German actually has TWO types of passive: Vorgangspassiv (werden + participle) for processes/actions, and Zustandspassiv (sein + participle) for resulting states: 'Das Haus ist gebaut' = The house is built (completed state). English doesn't distinguish these two passive types formally.

items.question: Das Buch _____ von dem Schüler gelesen.
items.translation: (The book is being read by the student)
items.explanation: In German passive, the agent (who performs the action) is introduced by 'von' + dative: 'von dem Schüler' (by the student). Compare with English 'by the student.' The passive structure is: Subject + werden (conjugated) + ... + past participle. 'Von' always takes dative: 'von dem/einem Schüler.' There's also 'durch' (through/by means of), used for impersonal agents or instruments: 'Das Fenster wurde durch den Sturm zerstört' (The window was destroyed by the storm). 'Von' = personal agent, 'durch' = means/instrument. This distinction helps express passive nuances precisely.

items.question: Die Tür _____ geschlossen.
items.explanation: Past tense passive (Präteritum Passiv) uses 'wurde' (past of werden) + past participle. 'Die Tür wurde geschlossen' = The door was closed (someone closed it — action). This is different from 'Die Tür ist geschlossen' (sein-passive) which means the door IS closed (current state, result). The werden-passive emphasizes the process/action: someone performed the closing. The sein-passive emphasizes the resulting state: the door is now in a closed state. English 'The door was closed' is ambiguous — it could mean either. German eliminates this ambiguity through its two-passive system.

items.question: Der Brief ist schon _____ worden.
items.explanation: Perfekt passive combines: sein + past participle + 'worden' (NOT 'geworden'!). 'Der Brief ist geschickt worden' = The letter has been sent. Note: in passive Perfekt, 'werden' loses its 'ge-' prefix and becomes 'worden' (not 'geworden'). 'Geworden' is used only when 'werden' means 'to become': 'Er ist Arzt geworden' (He has become a doctor). This distinction is crucial: 'worden' = passive auxiliary, 'geworden' = 'become.' The past participle of the main verb ('geschickt') comes before 'worden.' This three-part construction (sein + participle + worden) looks complex but follows logical rules.

items.question: Hier _____ nicht geraucht.
items.translation: (Smoking is not allowed here / One doesn't smoke here)
items.explanation: German has an 'impersonal passive' — passive without a subject, using 'es' or nothing. 'Hier wird nicht geraucht' (No smoking here — literally: here is not smoked). This construction doesn't exist in English! It's used for general rules, prohibitions, and descriptions of activities: 'Es wird getanzt' (There's dancing / People are dancing), 'Hier wird gearbeitet' (Work is being done here). The impersonal passive focuses entirely on the ACTION, with no agent or patient specified. 'Es' can appear as a placeholder subject in position 1: 'Es wird hier nicht geraucht' — but disappears when another element takes that position.

items.question: Das Auto muss _____ werden.
items.explanation: Passive with modal verbs follows: modal + past participle + 'werden' (infinitive). 'Das Auto muss repariert werden' = The car must be repaired. The modal verb 'muss' is conjugated, while 'werden' stays as an infinitive at the end. This creates a two-verb ending: '...repariert werden.' Other examples: 'kann gemacht werden' (can be done), 'soll gebaut werden' (should be built), 'darf nicht geöffnet werden' (must not be opened). In subordinate clauses, the modal goes to the very end: 'weil das Auto repariert werden MUSS.' The modal anchors the clause finale.

items.question: Die Rechnung _____ gestern bezahlt.
items.explanation: 'Wurde' (simple past of werden) + past participle forms the Präteritum passive. 'Die Rechnung wurde bezahlt' = The bill was paid. The Präteritum passive is preferred in written German and formal contexts, while spoken German might use Perfekt passive: 'Die Rechnung ist bezahlt worden.' Both mean the same thing but differ in register. The past participle 'bezahlt' comes from 'bezahlen' (to pay) — a regular verb with inseparable prefix 'be-.' Inseparable prefix verbs don't add 'ge-' in the past participle: bezahlen → bezahlt (NOT gebezahlt). This is the same in active and passive voice.

items.question: Die Fenster sind _____.
items.explanation: This is the Zustandspassiv (state passive): 'sein' + past participle. 'Die Fenster sind geöffnet' = The windows are open (they're in an opened state). Compare with Vorgangspassiv: 'Die Fenster werden geöffnet' = The windows are being opened (the action of opening). The Zustandspassiv describes a completed result, while the Vorgangspassiv describes an ongoing process. This distinction is one of German's precise grammatical tools. Many adjective-like past participles function this way: 'geschlossen' (closed), 'geöffnet' (opened), 'repariert' (repaired). The state passive often overlaps with predicate adjective usage.

items.question: Dem Kind _____ geholfen.
items.explanation: Verbs that take dative objects form passive differently. 'Helfen' takes dative: 'Man hilft dem Kind' → Passive: 'Dem Kind wird geholfen.' The dative object stays in dative — it does NOT become a nominative subject! There's no grammatical subject, making this an impersonal passive. 'Dem Kind' remains dative. If you put it first, there's no 'es': 'Dem Kind wird geholfen.' If something else starts: 'Es wird dem Kind geholfen.' Other dative verbs that form passive this way: danken, gratulieren, folgen, antworten. English converts all objects to subjects in passive — German only promotes accusative objects.

items.question: Das Problem kann leicht _____ werden.
items.explanation: Passive with modal verbs: modal (conjugated) + past participle + werden (infinitive). 'Kann gelöst werden' = can be solved. The adverb 'leicht' (easily) sits between the modal and the rest. Position of adverbs in passive: they go between the modal/conjugated verb and the participle group. 'Lösen' (to solve) → past participle 'gelöst.' German passives with modals are extremely common in professional and academic writing: 'muss beachtet werden' (must be noted), 'sollte vermieden werden' (should be avoided), 'kann erreicht werden' (can be achieved). Master these to read and write formal German.

items.question: Die E-Mail ist noch nicht _____ worden.
items.translation: (The email has not been sent yet)
items.explanation: Perfekt passive with negation: 'ist noch nicht geschickt worden.' The negation 'nicht' goes before the main past participle. 'Noch nicht' (not yet) frames the temporal aspect. The three-part passive Perfekt construction: sein (conjugated) + past participle + worden. Remember: 'worden' (passive) NOT 'geworden' (become). 'Geschickt' is the past participle of 'schicken' (to send) — a regular weak verb (schicken → geschickt). In passive Perfekt, 'sein' is always the auxiliary, never 'haben.' This contrasts with active Perfekt where 'schicken' uses 'haben': 'Ich habe geschickt' vs. passive 'Es ist geschickt worden.'

items.question: Das Konzert wurde von vielen Menschen _____.
items.translation: (The concert was attended by many people)
items.explanation: 'Wurde besucht' forms the Präteritum passive. 'Von vielen Menschen' identifies the agent (by many people). 'Besuchen' (to visit/attend) has the inseparable prefix 'be-,' so NO 'ge-' in the past participle: besuchen → besucht. Inseparable prefixes (be-, emp-, ent-, er-, ge-, miss-, ver-, zer-) are never stressed and never get 'ge-' added. This contrasts with separable prefixes (an-, auf-, aus-, etc.) which DO get 'ge-' inserted: anrufen → angerufen. When building passive sentences, correctly forming the past participle is essential — inseparable prefix verbs are a common source of errors.

items.question: In Deutschland _____ viel Bier getrunken.
items.translation: (A lot of beer is drunk in Germany)
items.explanation: This is another impersonal passive, commonly used for general statements about habits or customs. 'In Deutschland wird viel Bier getrunken' — no specific agent, just a general observation. Note that 'Bier' stays in accusative (direct object) — it doesn't become the subject. Technically, this sentence has no grammatical subject (or 'es' is implied). When an adverbial phrase ('In Deutschland') opens the sentence, 'es' disappears. Compare: 'Es wird in Deutschland viel Bier getrunken' — 'es' as placeholder in position 1. These impersonal passives are very useful for describing cultural observations and general truths.

items.question: Die Hausaufgaben müssen bis morgen _____ werden.
items.translation: (The homework must be done by tomorrow)
items.explanation: Passive with 'müssen': obligation combined with passive construction. 'Müssen gemacht werden' = must be done. 'Bis morgen' (by tomorrow) sets the deadline. 'Machen' → 'gemacht' (regular past participle). In subordinate clauses, this becomes even more complex: 'weil die Hausaufgaben bis morgen gemacht werden MÜSSEN' — the modal goes to the very end. This stacking of verb forms at clause endings is quintessential German: 'gemacht werden müssen' piles three verbal elements together. While it looks daunting, each piece has a clear function: participle (what), werden (passive), müssen (obligation).

items.question: Der Patient wurde erfolgreich _____.
items.explanation: Präteritum passive: 'wurde operiert' (was operated on). 'Operieren' is a verb ending in '-ieren' — these NEVER add 'ge-' in the past participle: operieren → operiert, studieren → studiert, telefonieren → telefoniert, funktionieren → funktioniert. This is a helpful rule: any verb ending in '-ieren' (mostly from French/Latin) skips 'ge-' entirely. The adverb 'erfolgreich' (successfully) goes between the auxiliary and participle. German medical and scientific passive is extremely common: 'wurde diagnostiziert' (was diagnosed), 'wurde behandelt' (was treated), 'wurde untersucht' (was examined). Professional German relies heavily on passive constructions.

items.question: _____ bitte die Tür!
items.explanation: The informal singular imperative (du-form) is formed by taking the verb stem and dropping the '-st' ending: 'du machst' → 'Mach!' No pronoun is used. For most verbs, it's simply the stem: machen → mach, kommen → komm, gehen → geh. The older form with '-e' (Mache!) is sometimes seen but considered old-fashioned in spoken German. Adding 'bitte' (please) softens the command. Unlike English, German has three imperative forms for different levels of formality: du (informal singular), ihr (informal plural), and Sie (formal). Each uses a different verb form.

items.question: _____ Sie bitte hier!
items.explanation: The formal imperative (Sie-form) uses the infinitive + 'Sie': 'Warten Sie!' The pronoun 'Sie' is ALWAYS included in formal imperatives — unlike du/ihr forms where pronouns are dropped. The word order mimics a question (verb + Sie), but end punctuation distinguishes: 'Warten Sie?' (question) vs. 'Warten Sie!' (command). The formal imperative is identical to the infinitive form, making it the easiest to form. Use it with strangers, in business, and with anyone you'd address as 'Sie.' In shops, offices, and professional settings, the Sie-imperative is the default.

items.question: _____ eure Bücher!
items.explanation: The informal plural imperative (ihr-form) uses the ihr-conjugation without the pronoun: 'ihr öffnet' → 'Öffnet!' This form is used when addressing a group you're familiar with — classmates, family members, friends. It's identical to the ihr-present tense form, just without 'ihr.' In a classroom, a teacher might use Sie-form with adults ('Öffnen Sie Ihre Bücher!') or ihr-form with children ('Öffnet eure Bücher!'). 'Eure' (your, informal plural possessive) matches the ihr-level of address. Choosing the right imperative level is a social decision about formality and relationship.

items.question: _____ schneller!
items.explanation: Verbs with 'a→äu' stem change in du-form (laufen: du läufst) do NOT keep the umlaut in the imperative! 'Lauf!' — not 'Läuf!' This is a key rule: a→ä and a→äu stem changes DISAPPEAR in the imperative. So: 'du fährst' → 'Fahr!' (not Fähr!), 'du schläfst' → 'Schlaf!' (not Schläf!), 'du läufst' → 'Lauf!' (not Läuf!). However, e→i and e→ie stem changes ARE kept: 'du gibst' → 'Gib!', 'du liest' → 'Lies!' This asymmetry (umlaut changes dropped, vowel-shift changes kept) is one of German's imperative quirks that learners must memorize.

items.question: _____ mir bitte das Buch!
items.translation: (Give me the book please! — informal singular)
items.explanation: Verbs with e→i stem change KEEP the change in the du-imperative. 'Geben' has the stem change e→i in 'du gibst,' and the imperative preserves it: 'Gib!' (not 'Geb!'). Similarly: sprechen → Sprich!, nehmen → Nimm!, lesen → Lies!, sehen → Sieh!, essen → Iss!, helfen → Hilf! These e→i forms never add the optional '-e': it's always 'Gib!' never 'Gibe!' This contrasts with regular verbs where '-e' is optional (Mach/Mache). The combination of stem-change retention + no optional '-e' makes strong verb imperatives sound distinctly punchy and commanding.

items.question: _____ nicht so laut!
items.explanation: 'Sein' (to be) has irregular imperatives: du → 'Sei!', ihr → 'Seid!', Sie → 'Seien Sie!' These must be memorized as they follow no regular pattern. 'Sei nicht so laut!' (Don't be so loud!) — 'sei' is the most common imperative form you'll encounter. Other examples: 'Sei vorsichtig!' (Be careful!), 'Seid ruhig!' (Be quiet! — plural), 'Seien Sie geduldig!' (Be patient! — formal). The formal form 'Seien' is the ONLY imperative that doesn't match the infinitive (infinitive: sein, formal imperative: seien). This triple irregularity makes 'sein' imperatives uniquely challenging.

items.question: _____ deine Hausaufgaben!
items.explanation: Standard du-imperative: take the stem of 'machen' → 'mach.' The possessive 'deine' (your, informal) matches the du-level address. Imperatives with possessives must use matching formality: 'Mach deine Hausaufgaben!' (du + deine), 'Macht eure Hausaufgaben!' (ihr + eure), 'Machen Sie Ihre Hausaufgaben!' (Sie + Ihre). Mixing levels is a grammar error and social faux pas: 'Machen Sie deine...' mixes formal verb with informal possessive. This consistency requirement extends throughout German — once you choose du/ihr/Sie, ALL pronouns, possessives, and verb forms must match that register.

items.question: _____ Sie sich bitte!
items.explanation: Formal imperative of reflexive verbs: infinitive + Sie + reflexive pronoun. 'Setzen Sie sich!' (Sit down!). The reflexive pronoun 'sich' must be included. For du-form: 'Setz dich!' — reflexive pronoun changes to match the subject. For ihr-form: 'Setzt euch!' 'Sich setzen' (to sit down) vs. 'sitzen' (to be sitting) — German distinguishes the action of sitting down from the state of being seated. Other reflexive imperatives: 'Beeilen Sie sich!' (Hurry up!), 'Melden Sie sich!' (Report/Register!). The reflexive pronoun always follows the verb and pronoun in formal imperatives.

items.question: _____ bitte das Fenster auf!
items.explanation: Separable verb imperatives: the prefix separates and goes to the end. 'Aufmachen' (to open) → 'Mach... auf!' The prefix 'auf' moves to the end of the clause. This is the same separation rule as in main clauses: 'Ich mache das Fenster auf' → imperative: 'Mach das Fenster auf!' Compare with inseparable verbs which stay together: 'Verstehen' → 'Versteh mich!' (no separation). In everyday German, separable verb imperatives are extremely common: 'Ruf mich an!' (Call me!), 'Steh auf!' (Get up!), 'Hör auf!' (Stop!), 'Pass auf!' (Watch out!). The prefix always anchors at the end.

items.question: _____ das nicht!
items.explanation: 'Vergessen' has the e→i stem change: 'du vergisst' → imperative 'Vergiss!' (not 'Vergess!'). Negative imperatives simply add 'nicht': 'Vergiss das nicht!' (Don't forget that!). German doesn't use a 'do' auxiliary for negative imperatives like English ('Don't forget'). Instead, just add 'nicht': 'Komm nicht!' (Don't come!), 'Mach das nicht!' (Don't do that!), 'Geh nicht weg!' (Don't go away!). The position of 'nicht' follows standard negation rules — it goes before what's being negated or at the end. This directness makes German negative imperatives simpler than English structurally.

items.question: _____ mal her!
items.explanation: The particle 'mal' softens an imperative, making it less demanding — similar to 'just' or 'quickly' in English commands. 'Komm mal her!' = 'Just come here!' vs. 'Komm her!' = 'Come here!' (more direct). 'Her' indicates direction toward the speaker. German uses many particles to soften imperatives: 'mal' (just/real quick), 'doch' (go ahead), 'ruhig' (feel free): 'Setz dich doch!' (Go ahead, sit down!), 'Nimm ruhig noch eins!' (Feel free to take another one!). These modal particles are essential for natural, polite German and distinguish advanced speakers from beginners.

items.question: _____ uns bitte an, wenn du ankommst!
items.translation: (Please call us when you arrive! — informal singular)
items.explanation: 'Anrufen' (to call/phone) is a separable verb: 'Ruf uns an!' — prefix 'an' goes to the end. The du-imperative of 'rufen' is 'Ruf' (stem, no umlaut change needed). When the imperative sentence contains a subordinate clause, the prefix still goes to the end of the imperative clause, before the comma: 'Ruf uns an, wenn du ankommst!' Note the subordinate clause 'wenn du ankommst' has its own verb-final order ('ankommst' at end). This double-layer structure (imperative main clause + subordinate clause) is very common in real German communication: commands with conditions.

items.question: _____ doch nicht so viel!
items.explanation: Verbs whose stems end in '-t,' '-d,' '-n,' or '-m' (preceded by a different consonant) KEEP the '-e' in the du-imperative: 'arbeiten' → 'Arbeite!' (not 'Arbeit!'). Other examples: 'warten' → 'Warte!', 'öffnen' → 'Öffne!', 'atmen' → 'Atme!' This is for pronunciation ease — 'Arbeit!' would be hard to distinguish from the noun 'Arbeit' (work). The particle 'doch' here adds a reproachful or urging tone: 'Arbeite doch nicht so viel!' implies 'Come on, don't work so much!' Modal particles like 'doch' add emotional nuance that's crucial for natural German.

items.question: _____ Sie bitte Platz!
items.explanation: Formal imperative of 'nehmen': 'Nehmen Sie!' Important: the formal imperative uses the INFINITIVE form, NOT the du-stem change. So it's 'Nehmen Sie' (not 'Nimm Sie' or 'Nehm Sie'). The du-form would be 'Nimm!' (with e→i change), but the Sie-form ignores stem changes entirely. 'Platz nehmen' is a common expression meaning 'to take a seat.' The formal imperative is the politest command form in German, used in business, service, and with anyone you don't know well. 'Bitte' makes it even more courteous. This phrase is standard in restaurants, offices, and waiting rooms.

items.question: _____ das Licht aus, wenn du gehst!
items.translation: (Turn off the light when you leave! — informal singular)
items.explanation: 'Ausmachen' (to turn off) is a separable verb. Du-imperative: 'Mach das Licht aus!' — the prefix 'aus' separates and goes to the end of the main clause. The subordinate clause 'wenn du gehst' follows after the comma. This sentence combines several German grammar concepts: separable verb imperative, object placement between stem and prefix, and a conditional subordinate clause with verb-final order. In daily German life, you'll hear countless separable verb imperatives: 'Mach das Licht an!' (Turn on), 'Mach die Tür zu!' (Close), 'Räum dein Zimmer auf!' (Clean up your room!). Separability is core to German verb mechanics.

items.question: Mein Bruder ist _____ als ich.
items.explanation: German comparatives add '-er' to the adjective stem, just like English. But many common one-syllable adjectives also get an umlaut: alt → älter, jung → jünger, groß → größer. This umlaut shift is one of the trickiest parts of German comparatives — there's no shortcut, you simply need to memorize which adjectives change. The comparison word is always 'als' (than), never 'wie' — 'wie' is only used for equality (so ... wie). So: 'älter als ich' = older than me.

items.question: Dieses Buch ist _____ als das andere.
items.translation: (This book is more interesting than the other one)
items.explanation: Unlike English, which uses 'more' for longer adjectives (more interesting), German ALWAYS adds '-er' regardless of word length: interessant → interessanter, kompliziert → komplizierter. There is no 'mehr interessant' construction in German — this is a common mistake English speakers make. Every single German adjective forms its comparative with '-er', making the rule beautifully simple once you know it. The only complication is the occasional umlaut on short adjectives.

items.question: Sie läuft _____ als er.
items.explanation: When a comparative adjective is used as an adverb (modifying a verb rather than a noun), it stays uninflected — just the base form + '-er'. 'Schneller' here modifies 'läuft' (runs), so no additional ending is needed. Compare: 'ein schnelleres Auto' (a faster car) where 'schneller' gets the adjective ending '-es' because it's before a neuter noun. This distinction between predicative/adverbial (no ending) and attributive (with ending) use is crucial in German.

items.question: Berlin ist _____ als München.
items.explanation: 'Groß' (big) is one of the key adjectives that takes an umlaut in the comparative: groß → größer. Notice the 'ö' replaces the 'o'. The core umlaut-changing adjectives you must know: alt→älter, jung→jünger, groß→größer, kurz→kürzer, lang→länger, stark→stärker, schwach→schwächer, kalt→kälter, warm→wärmer. Learning these ~15 adjectives will cover the vast majority of comparative situations. The pattern: a/o/u in the stem often becomes ä/ö/ü.

items.question: Das Wetter ist heute _____ als gestern.
items.translation: (The weather is better today than yesterday)
items.explanation: Just like English 'good → better' (not 'gooder'), German has irregular comparatives: gut → besser (good → better), viel → mehr (much → more), gern → lieber (gladly → more gladly/preferably). These three irregular forms are among the most frequently used words in German and must simply be memorized. 'Besser' is used constantly in everyday speech: 'Das ist besser' (that's better), 'Es geht mir besser' (I feel better). No umlaut rule applies here — it's a completely different stem.

items.question: Ich trinke _____ Tee als Kaffee.
items.translation: (I prefer drinking tea to coffee / I drink tea more gladly than coffee)
items.explanation: 'Gern' (gladly/with pleasure) has the irregular comparative 'lieber' (rather/preferably). This is how German expresses preference — there's no direct equivalent of 'I prefer': instead you say 'Ich trinke lieber Tee' (I drink tea more gladly). This construction is extremely common: 'Ich esse lieber Pizza' (I prefer pizza), 'Ich gehe lieber ins Kino' (I'd rather go to the cinema). The superlative is 'am liebsten' (most gladly/favorite). This gern→lieber→am liebsten trio is essential everyday German.

items.question: Ein _____ Haus wäre schön.
items.explanation: When a comparative adjective comes BEFORE a noun (attributive position), it must take the normal adjective ending on top of '-er'. Here: groß → größer (comparative) + -es (neuter nominative with 'ein'). This stacking is what makes German comparatives challenging: you need the comparative '-er' AND the declension ending. 'Ein größeres Haus' — the '-es' comes from ein-word declension (neuter nominative). Compare: 'ein größerer Garten' (masculine), 'eine größere Wohnung' (feminine).

items.question: Je mehr ich lerne, _____ besser verstehe ich.
items.translation: (The more I learn, the better I understand)
items.explanation: The German correlative comparative construction 'je ... desto/umso' (the ... the ...) is a key structure. 'Je mehr ich lerne, desto besser verstehe ich' = The more I learn, the better I understand. You can use either 'desto' or 'umso' — both are correct and interchangeable. Important word order: after 'je', the verb goes to the end (subordinate clause); after 'desto', the verb comes immediately (inverted main clause). This elegant construction is used constantly in both spoken and written German.

items.question: Das ist _____ so teuer wie das andere.
items.translation: (That is just as expensive as the other one)
items.explanation: For equality comparisons (as ... as), German uses 'so ... wie' or 'genauso ... wie'. 'Genau so teuer wie' = exactly as expensive as. This is fundamentally different from comparative 'als' — a common error is mixing them up. Remember: equality = 'so/genauso ... wie'; superiority = comparative + 'als'. You can also add 'fast' (almost): 'fast so teuer wie' = almost as expensive as. Or 'nicht so teuer wie' = not as expensive as. The adjective stays in its base form, no '-er' added.

items.question: Sie ist viel _____ als ihre Schwester.
items.translation: (She is much taller than her sister)
items.explanation: To intensify comparatives, German uses 'viel' (much), 'weit' (far), 'noch' (even/still), or 'etwas' (somewhat): 'viel größer' = much bigger, 'noch besser' = even better, 'etwas kleiner' = somewhat smaller. Never use 'sehr' (very) with comparatives — 'sehr' only works with base adjectives. This mirrors English: 'much better' (✓) vs 'very better' (✗). These intensifiers are essential for natural-sounding German: 'Das ist weit besser' sounds much more natural than just 'Das ist besser'.

items.question: Er wird immer _____.
items.explanation: 'Immer + comparative' is the German way to express 'more and more' or '-er and -er': 'immer klüger' = smarter and smarter, 'immer besser' = better and better, 'immer schneller' = faster and faster. Unlike English which repeats the comparative ('bigger and bigger'), German uses the elegant 'immer' construction. This is very common in everyday speech: 'Es wird immer kälter' (It's getting colder and colder), 'Die Preise werden immer höher' (Prices are getting higher and higher).

items.question: Meine Wohnung ist _____ als deine.
items.explanation: 'Klein' (small) forms a regular comparative: kleiner (smaller). No umlaut change here, even though it has an 'ei' diphthong — only a/o/u can become umlauts (ä/ö/ü). When the comparative is used predicatively (after 'ist'), no adjective ending is added: 'ist kleiner' not 'ist kleinerer'. This predicative use without endings is the simplest comparative construction. Compare with attributive: 'eine kleinere Wohnung' (a smaller apartment), where the ending '-e' is required.

items.question: Das Essen hier schmeckt _____ als dort.
items.translation: (The food here tastes better than there)
items.explanation: Reinforcing the irregular comparative: gut → besser. When 'besser' modifies a verb (here: 'schmeckt besser' = tastes better), it functions as an adverb and takes no ending. The three essential irregulars: gut→besser→am besten (good/better/best), viel→mehr→am meisten (much/more/most), gern→lieber→am liebsten (gladly/preferably/most gladly). All other German adjectives follow the regular '-er' pattern, possibly with umlaut. These three exceptions appear in virtually every conversation.

items.question: Heute ist es _____ als gestern.
items.explanation: 'Warm' takes an umlaut in the comparative: wärmer. This follows the pattern where monosyllabic adjectives with a/o/u often gain an umlaut: warm→wärmer, kalt→kälter, hart→härter, nah→näher, scharf→schärfer. Not ALL monosyllabic adjectives umlaut though — 'bunt' stays 'bunter', 'schlank' stays 'schlanker'. Unfortunately, there's no perfect rule for which ones change — frequency and practice are the best teachers. Weather descriptions use comparatives constantly in German.

items.question: Dieses Problem ist _____ als das letzte.
items.translation: (This problem is more difficult than the last one)
items.explanation: Even multi-syllable adjectives like 'schwierig' (difficult) simply add '-er': schwieriger. This is the beauty of German comparatives — no 'mehr schwierig' construction exists (unlike English 'more difficult'). The rule is absolute: EVERY adjective gets '-er'. This makes German comparatives arguably simpler than English ones, where you must decide between '-er' and 'more'. The only complications are umlauts on short adjectives and the three irregular forms (besser, mehr, lieber). Master those, and comparatives become straightforward.

items.question: Das ist das _____ Gebäude der Stadt.
items.translation: (That is the tallest building in the city)
items.explanation: German superlatives are formed with '-(e)st' + adjective ending. 'Hoch' (tall/high) has an irregular superlative stem: höchst- (the 'c' drops and umlaut is added). Before a neuter noun with 'das', the ending is '-e' (definite article declension): 'das höchste Gebäude'. The superlative in German ALWAYS needs either 'am ___sten' (predicative) or 'der/die/das ___ste' (attributive with article). You can never use a bare superlative like English 'tallest' — it must have context.

items.question: Sie ist _____ in der Klasse.
items.translation: (She is the smartest in the class)
items.explanation: When the superlative is used predicatively (after 'ist/sind'), German uses the 'am ___sten' construction: 'am klügsten' = the smartest. This 'am + superlative + -en' pattern is unique to German — it literally means 'at the smartest'. The umlaut on 'klug → klügst-' follows the same pattern as comparatives. You could also say 'Sie ist die Klügste' (she is the smartest one), using the superlative as a noun. Both forms are common, but 'am + sten' is the default predicative form.

items.question: Das war der _____ Tag meines Lebens.
items.translation: (That was the best day of my life)
items.explanation: Irregular superlative: gut → besser → best- (good → better → best). Before a masculine noun with 'der', add '-e': 'der beste Tag'. The three key irregular superlatives: gut→am besten/der beste, viel→am meisten/der meiste, gern→am liebsten. These mirror English irregularities (good/better/best). Note that 'der beste Tag meines Lebens' uses the genitive 'meines Lebens' — a common and elegant German construction meaning 'of my life'. This phrase is extremely common in German everyday speech.

items.question: Welches ist das _____ Land Europas?
items.translation: (Which is the smallest country in Europe?)
items.explanation: With 'das' (definite article, neuter), the superlative ending is '-e': 'das kleinste Land'. Klein doesn't take an umlaut (it has 'ei', not a/o/u). For attributive superlatives (before nouns), you always use the definite article pattern: der/die/das + superlative + adjective ending. The endings follow standard definite article adjective declension: -e for nominative singular (der/die/das), -en for most other cases. 'Europas' is the genitive of 'Europa' — German adds '-s' for proper nouns in genitive.

items.question: Das Essen schmeckt hier _____.
items.explanation: When the superlative modifies a verb (here: 'schmeckt am besten' = tastes best), you must use the 'am ___sten' form. This is the adverbial superlative. 'Am besten' literally means 'at the best' — this construction with 'am' is always used when no noun follows. Compare: 'Das beste Essen' (attributive, with noun) vs 'Das Essen schmeckt am besten' (adverbial, modifying verb). This 'am' construction has no equivalent in English and is one of the distinctive features of German grammar.

items.question: Er fährt _____ von allen.
items.explanation: Adverbial superlative with 'am': 'am schnellsten' = the fastest (when modifying a verb). Note the '-sten' ending, not '-esten' — adjectives ending in a consonant cluster usually just add '-sten': schnell→am schnellsten. However, adjectives ending in -d, -t, -s, -ß, -z, -sch need '-esten' for pronunciation: kalt→am kältesten, süß→am süßesten. 'Von allen' (of all) is the standard way to express 'of all' in superlative comparisons, equivalent to English 'of all/among all'.

items.question: Das ist die _____ Frage.
items.explanation: Even long adjectives form superlatives with '-st-' + ending: wichtig → wichtigst- + -e (feminine nominative with 'die'). There is no 'am meisten wichtig' or 'mehr wichtig' in German — unlike English which uses 'most important'. German is consistent: EVERY adjective uses the '-st' suffix. This makes the rule simpler than English: just add '-st-' and the appropriate adjective ending. The adjective ending '-e' here comes from definite article feminine nominative: die wichtigste Frage.

items.question: Der _____ Fluss Deutschlands ist der Rhein.
items.translation: (The longest river in Germany is the Rhine)
items.explanation: 'Lang' (long) takes an umlaut in the superlative: längst-. Combined with the masculine nominative definite article ending: 'der längste Fluss'. The umlaut pattern in superlatives matches the comparative: if the comparative has an umlaut (länger), the superlative will too (längst-). 'Deutschlands' is genitive of 'Deutschland' — German proper nouns form genitive by adding '-s' (no apostrophe needed, unlike English possessive). This is a common pattern for geographic facts: 'der höchste Berg der Alpen'.

items.question: Ich lese _____ Krimis.
items.translation: (I like reading crime novels the most / Crime novels are my favorite to read)
items.explanation: Irregular superlative: gern → lieber → am liebsten (gladly → preferably → most gladly/favorite). 'Am liebsten' expresses your top preference: 'Ich lese am liebsten Krimis' = I most enjoy reading crime novels. This is how Germans express favorites. There's no 'am gernsten' — it's fully irregular. The gern→lieber→am liebsten trio is one of the three essential irregular comparison sets (along with gut→besser→am besten and viel→mehr→am meisten). Use it daily!

items.question: Das war die _____ Reise, die ich je gemacht habe.
items.translation: (That was the most beautiful trip I have ever taken)
items.explanation: 'Schön' (beautiful) → superlative 'schönst-' + '-e' (feminine nominative with 'die'): 'die schönste Reise'. Note that 'schön' doesn't change its umlaut — it already has 'ö' in the base form. The relative clause 'die ich je gemacht habe' (that I have ever made) uses 'je' (ever) — a key word in superlative contexts. German superlative + 'je' constructions are very common: 'das beste Buch, das ich je gelesen habe' (the best book I've ever read).

items.question: Wo ist der _____ Supermarkt?
items.explanation: 'Nah' (near/close) has a highly irregular superlative: nah → näher → nächst- (near → nearer → nearest). The '-chst' pattern is unique to this adjective. 'Der nächste Supermarkt' with masculine nominative '-e' ending. This word is essential for daily life: 'die nächste Haltestelle' (the next/nearest stop), 'nächstes Mal' (next time), 'nächste Woche' (next week). 'Nächst-' serves double duty meaning both 'nearest' and 'next' — context tells you which meaning applies.

items.question: Das _____ Kind bekommt den Preis.
items.explanation: 'Jung' (young) follows the umlaut pattern: jung → jünger → jüngst-. With 'das' (neuter nominative), the ending is '-e': 'das jüngste Kind'. This is the same '-e' ending as masculine and feminine nominative with definite articles — all three genders share '-e' in nominative singular after der/die/das. This simplification makes definite article superlatives easier: just remember '-e' for nominative, '-en' for almost everything else (accusative masculine, dative, genitive).

items.question: Er hat die _____ Arbeit geleistet.
items.explanation: Irregular superlative: viel → mehr → meist- (much → more → most). 'Die meiste Arbeit' = the most work. Note: 'Arbeit' is feminine, so 'die meiste' with '-e' ending. 'Meist-' is also used as an adverb: 'meistens' (mostly/usually). This irregular trio (viel/mehr/meist-) parallels English much/more/most. Never say 'mehrste' or 'vielste' — these don't exist. 'Die meisten Leute' (most people) and 'am meisten' (the most) are among the most frequently used expressions in German.

items.question: Das ist der _____ Film, den ich kenne.
items.translation: (That is the oldest film I know)
items.explanation: 'Alt' → älter → ältest-: umlaut throughout the comparison chain. 'Der älteste Film' uses masculine nominative '-e'. The insertion of '-e-' before '-st' is required when the stem ends in -t (alt-est-), -d, -s, -ß, -z, or -sch — this makes pronunciation possible. Without it, 'ältst-' would be nearly impossible to say. Other examples: kalt→kältest-, wild→wildest-, süß→süßest-. This phonological rule applies to both 'am ___esten' and attributive '___este' forms.

items.question: Von allen Jahreszeiten mag ich den Frühling _____.
items.translation: (Of all seasons, I like spring the most)
items.explanation: Adverbial superlative of 'viel': 'am meisten' (the most). Used when modifying a verb: 'mag... am meisten' = like the most. 'Von allen Jahreszeiten' (of all seasons) sets the comparison scope. This construction — 'von allen + noun, verb + am ___sten' — is the standard pattern for expressing superlative comparisons in German. Remember: predicative/adverbial = 'am ___sten', attributive = 'der/die/das ___ste + noun'. Getting this distinction right is essential for natural-sounding German.

items.question: Er spricht _____ Deutsch.
items.explanation: One of the beautiful simplicities of German: adjectives used as adverbs take NO ending at all. In English, you often add '-ly' (good → well, quick → quickly). In German, you simply use the base adjective: 'gut' serves as both 'good' (adjective) and 'well' (adverb). 'Er spricht gut' = He speaks well. No declension, no modification — just the plain form. This applies universally: schnell (fast/quickly), langsam (slow/slowly), schön (beautiful/beautifully). One less thing to worry about!

items.question: Ich gehe _____ ins Kino.
items.explanation: 'Oft' (often) is a frequency adverb — one of the most common adverbs in German. German frequency adverbs from highest to lowest: immer (always), meistens (mostly), oft/häufig (often), manchmal (sometimes), selten (rarely), nie/niemals (never). These typically go right after the conjugated verb in a main clause: 'Ich gehe oft ins Kino'. In subordinate clauses, the adverb stays near the verb cluster at the end. 'Öfter' exists but means 'more often' (comparative).

items.question: Sie kommt _____ zu spät.
items.explanation: 'Nie' (never) is a negation adverb — different from 'nicht' (not) and 'kein' (no/none). 'Nie' negates frequency: 'Sie kommt nie zu spät' = She never comes late. 'Nicht' negates actions/qualities: 'Sie kommt nicht' = She doesn't come. 'Kein' negates nouns: 'Sie hat kein Geld' = She has no money. These three negation tools each have distinct roles — mixing them up is a common learner error. 'Nie' can also be strengthened: 'niemals' (never ever), 'noch nie' (never yet).

items.question: Wir fahren _____ nach Hause.
items.explanation: 'Jetzt' (now) is a temporal adverb and one of the most frequently used words in German. It's invariable — never changes form. Key temporal adverbs: jetzt/nun (now), heute (today), morgen (tomorrow), gestern (yesterday), bald (soon), sofort (immediately), gerade (just now/right now), schon (already), noch (still/yet). These words are the backbone of time expression in German. Unlike adjectives, true adverbs in German are always uninflected — they remain in their dictionary form regardless of context.

items.question: Das Auto fährt sehr _____.
items.explanation: 'Sehr' (very) is an intensifying adverb that modifies another adjective or adverb. 'Sehr schnell' = very fast. Other intensifiers: ziemlich (quite), besonders (especially), extrem (extremely), unglaublich (incredibly), wahnsinnig (insanely — colloquial), total (totally — colloquial). 'Schnell' here is an adverb modifying the verb 'fährt', so it stays uninflected. If it were before a noun ('ein schnelles Auto'), it would need an adjective ending. Position matters in German: after verbs = adverb (no ending), before nouns = adjective (with ending).

items.question: Er hat _____ gut gespielt.
items.explanation: 'Wirklich' (really/truly) is a modal adverb expressing degree of truth. As an adverb, it takes no ending. German modal adverbs add nuance to statements: vielleicht (perhaps), wahrscheinlich (probably), sicher/bestimmt (certainly), tatsächlich (actually/indeed), eigentlich (actually/basically), leider (unfortunately), hoffentlich (hopefully), glücklicherweise (fortunately). These go after the conjugated verb in main clauses. They're essential for expressing opinions and degrees of certainty in German.

items.question: _____ regnet es in Hamburg.
items.explanation: 'Häufig' (frequently/often) is synonymous with 'oft' but slightly more formal. When an adverb begins a sentence, the subject and verb swap (inversion): 'Häufig regnet es' instead of 'Es regnet häufig'. This V2 (verb-second) rule is fundamental to German: the conjugated verb MUST be the second element. If anything other than the subject starts the sentence, the subject moves after the verb. This applies to all adverbs, time expressions, and objects placed at the beginning for emphasis.

items.question: Sie singt _____.
items.explanation: 'Wunderschön' (wonderfully beautiful) is a compound adjective/adverb formed with 'wunder-' (wonder) + 'schön' (beautiful). German loves creating compound adverbs this way: steinreich (stone-rich = filthy rich), blitzschnell (lightning-fast), todmüde (dead tired), eiskalt (ice cold), brandneu (brand new). These compounds are used as adverbs without any ending. They're very common in spoken German and add vivid expressiveness. Like all adverbs modifying verbs, 'wunderschön' stays uninflected here.

items.question: Ich bin _____ fertig.
items.explanation: 'Fast' in German means 'almost/nearly' — a classic false friend for English speakers! It has nothing to do with speed. 'Fast fertig' = almost finished. Other German words that are false friends: 'also' means 'so/therefore' (not 'also'), 'bekommen' means 'to get/receive' (not 'to become'), 'Gift' means 'poison' (not 'gift'). For 'fast' meaning quick in German, use 'schnell'. 'Bald' means 'soon', 'gleich' means 'right away/in a moment' — similar but distinct temporal adverbs.

items.question: Er hat das _____ gemacht.
items.explanation: The suffix '-lich' is one of the most productive adverb/adjective suffixes in German (similar to English '-ly'): absichtlich (intentionally), natürlich (naturally), wahrscheinlich (probably), glücklich (happily), freundlich (friendly/in a friendly way), ehrlich (honestly). Many '-lich' words serve as both adjective and adverb with no form change: 'eine freundliche Person' (adjective with ending) vs 'sie lächelt freundlich' (adverb, no ending). This dual function is typical of German.

items.question: Wir haben uns _____ getroffen.
items.explanation: 'Gestern' (yesterday) is a temporal adverb that never changes form. Key temporal adverbs used with past: gestern (yesterday), vorgestern (day before yesterday), letzte Woche (last week), letzten Monat (last month), letztes Jahr (last year), vor kurzem (recently), neulich (the other day). Notice how 'gestern' appears after the conjugated verb 'haben' and before the past participle 'getroffen' — this is standard word order in perfect tense sentences with adverbs.

items.question: Das Konzert war _____ toll.
items.explanation: 'Echt' as an adverb means 'really/truly/genuinely' in colloquial German. It's extremely common in spoken language: 'echt toll' = really great, 'echt schade' = really a pity, 'echt blöd' = really stupid. Other colloquial intensifiers: 'mega' (mega), 'voll' (totally), 'total' (totally), 'krass' (intense/crazy), 'hammer' (awesome). These informal adverbs are constant in younger German speakers' vocabularies. In formal writing, prefer 'wirklich', 'tatsächlich', or 'äußerst' instead.

items.question: Er spricht _____ drei Sprachen.
items.explanation: 'Schon' (already) is one of German's most versatile modal particles. As an adverb: 'Er spricht schon drei Sprachen' = He already speaks three languages. But 'schon' also works as a particle expressing reassurance: 'Das wird schon klappen' (It'll work out, don't worry). Its counterpart 'noch' means 'still/yet': 'Er spricht noch kein Deutsch' (He doesn't speak German yet). The schon/noch pair is essential: 'schon' = something has already happened; 'noch' = something continues or hasn't happened yet.

items.question: Arbeite bitte _____!
items.explanation: Adverbs can be compared just like adjectives: leise → leiser → am leisesten (quietly → more quietly → most quietly). 'Leiser' here is the comparative adverb modifying the verb 'arbeite'. Since adverbs take no endings anyway, the comparative adverb looks identical to the comparative adjective in predicative position: both are just 'leiser'. This is a request (imperative + 'bitte'): the speaker asks someone to work more quietly. German comparative adverbs are straightforward — just add '-er' with possible umlaut.

items.question: _____ gehe ich lieber spazieren.
items.translation: (Instead of that, I'd rather go for a walk)
items.explanation: 'Stattdessen' (instead of that) is a pronominal adverb — a category almost unique to German. These combine a preposition with 'da(r)-' or 'hier-': da+mit = damit (with that), da+für = dafür (for that), da+von = davon (of that), da+rüber = darüber (about that). Stattdessen = statt + dessen (instead + of that). These replace 'preposition + pronoun' constructions: 'damit' instead of 'mit dem/mit das'. Pronominal adverbs are essential in German and have no direct English equivalent. They're always uninflected.

items.question: Ich verstehe das _____.
items.explanation: 'Nicht' is the main negation word in German, used to negate verbs, adjectives, adverbs, and prepositional phrases. It's placed at the end of the sentence when negating the entire statement, or directly before the element being specifically negated. 'Ich verstehe das nicht' places 'nicht' at the end because the whole action is negated. Unlike English, German doesn't use auxiliary 'do' for negation — no 'I do not understand'. You simply add 'nicht' to the existing sentence. This makes German negation structurally simpler than English.

items.question: Ich habe _____ Auto.
items.explanation: 'Kein' negates nouns that would otherwise have an indefinite article (ein) or no article. 'Ein Auto' → 'kein Auto'. Think of it as 'ein' + negation = 'kein'. This is the critical rule: use 'nicht' for verbs and specific things, 'kein' for indefinite nouns. 'Kein' declines like 'ein': kein/keine/kein (nom.), keinen/keine/kein (acc.), keinem/keiner/keinem (dat.), keines/keiner/keines (gen.). English uses 'no' or 'not a/not any', but German has this elegant single word 'kein' that covers both.

items.question: Sie trinkt _____ Kaffee.
items.explanation: 'Kaffee' is masculine and is the direct object (accusative case), so 'kein' becomes 'keinen' (masculine accusative). The full kein declension in accusative: keinen (masc.), keine (fem.), kein (neuter), keine (plural). This is identical to the 'ein' declension but with 'k-' prefix. Remember: 'keinen' = masculine accusative only. A common mistake is using 'kein' everywhere — you must decline it like any ein-word. 'Sie trinkt keinen Kaffee' could also translate as 'She drinks no coffee' — same meaning, different English phrasing.

items.question: Das ist _____ mein Buch.
items.explanation: Use 'nicht' (not 'kein') when negating a noun with a possessive adjective (mein, dein, sein, etc.) or definite article (der, die, das). 'Nicht mein Buch' = not my book. The rule: 'kein' replaces 'ein' or zero article; 'nicht' is used with everything else (definite articles, possessives, proper nouns). So: 'nicht der Mann', 'nicht mein Freund', 'nicht Maria'. This distinction between nicht and kein is one of the first hurdles for German learners, but the rule is actually quite logical once understood.

items.question: Er geht _____ gern ins Theater.
items.translation: (He doesn't like going to the theater)
items.explanation: When negating an adverb, 'nicht' goes directly before it: 'nicht gern' = not gladly/doesn't like to. 'Nicht' placement is one of German's trickiest aspects. General rules: 'nicht' goes before what it negates specifically — before adjectives ('nicht schön'), adverbs ('nicht gern'), prepositional phrases ('nicht im Haus'). When negating the whole sentence, it goes as far to the end as possible, but before: past participles, infinitives, separable prefixes, and predicate adjectives/nouns. This ordering takes practice to master.

items.question: Ich habe _____ Geschwister.
items.explanation: 'Geschwister' (siblings) is always plural, so 'kein' takes the plural form 'keine'. In the plural, 'kein-' always becomes 'keine' in nominative and accusative (just like 'meine', 'deine', 'seine'). Important: German uses 'Geschwister' only in plural — there's no singular form meaning 'sibling'. You'd say 'ein Bruder' or 'eine Schwester' for a single sibling. 'Keine Geschwister haben' is a very common personal information phrase. The kein-plural pattern is consistent: keine (nom./acc.), keinen (dat.), keiner (gen.).

items.question: Er ist _____ gekommen.
items.explanation: In perfect tense sentences, 'nicht' typically goes before the past participle: 'ist nicht gekommen'. This is part of the general rule that 'nicht' goes before sentence-final verb elements (past participles, infinitives, separable prefixes). Structure: Subject + auxiliary + nicht + past participle. 'Er ist nicht gekommen' vs 'Er kommt nicht' — in both cases, 'nicht' positions itself before the verbal element at the end. With modal verbs: 'Er kann nicht kommen' (nicht before infinitive). This consistent end-positioning makes the pattern predictable.

items.question: _____, ich bin nicht müde.
items.explanation: 'Nein' is the simple standalone 'no' — used to answer questions or contradict statements. It's always separate from the sentence: 'Nein, ich bin nicht müde.' Note that 'nein' and 'nicht' can coexist: 'nein' answers the question, 'nicht' negates the adjective 'müde'. Compare: 'Ja' (yes), 'Nein' (no), 'Doch' (yes, contradicting a negative — unique to German!). If someone says 'Du bist nicht müde' and you want to disagree, you'd say 'Doch!' (Yes, I am!), not 'Ja'. This 'doch' concept doesn't exist in English.

items.question: Er hat _____ Ahnung.
items.explanation: 'Ahnung' is feminine, so 'kein' → 'keine' (feminine accusative, same as nominative). 'Keine Ahnung' is an extremely common German expression meaning 'no idea' — used in casual speech constantly: 'Keine Ahnung!' (No idea! / I have no clue!). Other common 'kein-' expressions: 'kein Problem' (no problem), 'keine Sorge' (no worry/don't worry), 'kein Wunder' (no wonder), 'auf keinen Fall' (in no case/absolutely not). These fixed expressions are essential for natural conversational German.

items.question: Das Essen ist _____ schlecht.
items.explanation: When negating a predicate adjective (adjective after sein/werden), use 'nicht' directly before it: 'nicht schlecht'. 'Nicht schlecht' is actually a common understatement meaning 'not bad = pretty good' — a classic German understatement. Placement rule: 'nicht' before predicate adjectives and predicate nouns ('Er ist nicht Lehrer' = He is not a teacher, but note: 'Er ist kein Lehrer' is more natural with indefinite). Using 'nicht' before adjectives is one of the clearest placement rules and a good starting point for mastering negation word order.

items.question: Es gibt hier _____ Restaurant.
items.explanation: 'Restaurant' is neuter, and 'es gibt' takes accusative — neuter accusative of 'kein' is 'kein' (same as nominative). 'Es gibt' (there is/there are) is one of German's most useful expressions, and it always takes the accusative case: 'Es gibt einen Park' (masc.acc.), 'Es gibt eine Schule' (fem.acc.), 'Es gibt ein Problem' (neut.acc.). With negation: 'Es gibt keinen Park', 'Es gibt keine Schule', 'Es gibt kein Restaurant'. The neuter accusative is the easiest — it looks identical to nominative.

items.question: Wir haben _____ mehr Zeit.
items.explanation: 'Zeit' (time) is feminine, requiring 'keine' (feminine accusative). 'Keine ... mehr' is an important pattern meaning 'no more/not any more': 'keine Zeit mehr', 'kein Geld mehr' (no more money), 'keine Fragen mehr' (no more questions). The 'mehr' (more) goes after the noun. Similarly: 'nicht mehr' = 'not anymore': 'Ich arbeite nicht mehr' (I don't work anymore). These 'kein/nicht ... mehr' constructions are extremely common in everyday German for expressing exhaustion of resources or ending of actions.

items.question: _____ der Kinder war krank.
items.explanation: 'Keines' is the pronoun form of 'kein' (neuter nominative) standing alone without a noun: 'Keines der Kinder' = none of the children. When 'kein' functions as a pronoun (no noun follows), it takes strong endings: keiner (masc.), keine (fem.), keines (neut.), keine (pl.). Compare: 'kein Kind' (adjective, before noun) vs 'keines' (pronoun, standing alone). 'Keiner der Schüler' (none of the students, masc.), 'Keine war da' (none was there, fem.). The genitive 'der Kinder' expresses 'of the children'.

items.question: Ich bin _____ sicher, ob das stimmt.
items.translation: (I am not sure whether that is correct)
items.explanation: 'Nicht' before the adjective 'sicher' negates the state of being sure. The subordinate clause with 'ob' (whether/if) is introduced by a comma and has the verb at the end: 'ob das stimmt' (whether that is correct). 'Ob' introduces indirect yes/no questions — whenever you'd use 'if/whether' in English for reported questions: 'Ich weiß nicht, ob er kommt' (I don't know if he's coming). This 'nicht sicher, ob...' construction is very natural in German for expressing uncertainty.

items.question: Hier darf man _____ rauchen.
items.translation: (One must not smoke here / Smoking is not allowed here)
items.explanation: 'Nicht' before the infinitive 'rauchen' at the end of a sentence with a modal verb. The word order is: modal verb (darf) in position 2, 'nicht' before the infinitive (rauchen) at the end. 'Man darf nicht' = one must not / it's not allowed. 'Man' is the impersonal pronoun (like English 'one' or French 'on') — very common in German for general rules and instructions. With modal verbs, 'nicht' always goes before the infinitive: 'kann nicht kommen', 'muss nicht arbeiten', 'soll nicht sprechen'. This is one of the most reliable placement rules.

items.question: _____ wohnst du?
items.explanation: 'Wo' (where) asks about location — a W-Frage (W-question). German question words (W-Wörter) all begin with 'w': wo (where), was (what), wer (who), wie (how), warum (why), wann (when), welch- (which), wohin (where to), woher (where from). In W-questions, the question word comes first, followed by the conjugated verb, then the subject: 'Wo wohnst du?' Note: German doesn't use an auxiliary 'do' — the verb simply moves to second position. This makes German questions structurally simpler than English ones.

items.question: _____ du Deutsch?
items.explanation: Yes/no questions (Ja/Nein-Fragen) in German are formed by placing the conjugated verb FIRST: 'Sprichst du Deutsch?' No question word, no auxiliary 'do' — just verb-first. This is beautifully simple compared to English: 'Do you speak German?' requires the auxiliary 'do'. German just inverts subject and verb: Statement: 'Du sprichst Deutsch' → Question: 'Sprichst du Deutsch?' This inversion rule applies to all yes/no questions. Note 'sprichst' has the stem change e→i for 'du' (sprechen is a strong verb).

items.question: _____ kostet das?
items.explanation: Both 'Was kostet das?' and 'Wie viel kostet das?' are correct, but 'Was kostet das?' is the more common colloquial form in everyday German. 'Was' literally means 'what' but is idiomatically used for 'how much' with prices. 'Wie viel' (how much) is more precise and slightly more formal. For quantities: 'Wie viel Zucker?' (How much sugar? — uncountable), 'Wie viele Äpfel?' (How many apples? — countable, note the '-e' ending). The was/wie viel distinction is a common question learners ask — both are perfectly acceptable for prices.

items.question: _____ alt bist du?
items.explanation: 'Wie' (how) is used in many fixed question patterns: 'Wie alt?' (how old?), 'Wie groß?' (how tall?), 'Wie weit?' (how far?), 'Wie lange?' (how long?), 'Wie oft?' (how often?), 'Wie spät?' (how late? = what time?). 'Wie' + adjective is the German way to ask about degree or measurement. Interestingly, German asks 'Wie alt bist du?' (How old are you?) while expressing age differently than English: 'Ich bin 25 Jahre alt' (I am 25 years old — using 'sein' not 'have'). French and Spanish use 'have' for age, but German uses 'be' like English.

items.question: _____ hast du das gemacht?
items.explanation: 'Warum' (why) asks for reasons. German has three words for 'why': warum, wieso, weshalb — all interchangeable in most contexts, though 'warum' is most common. The answer typically uses 'weil' (because): 'Warum? — Weil ich müde war.' Important: 'weil' sends the verb to the end of its clause ('weil ich müde war', not 'weil ich war müde'). In spoken German, some speakers break this rule, but in writing and formal speech, verb-final after 'weil' is required. 'Darum/deshalb/deswegen' all mean 'that's why/therefore'.

items.question: _____ Buch liest du?
items.explanation: 'Welch-' (which) is a question determiner that declines for gender, case, and number — just like 'dies-' (this). 'Buch' is neuter, and it's the accusative object of 'liest', so: welches (neuter accusative). Declension: welcher (masc.nom.), welche (fem.nom.), welches (neut.nom.), welche (pl.nom.). Accusative: welchen (masc.), welche (fem.), welches (neut.), welche (pl.). 'Welch-' follows the strong adjective ending pattern (like der/die/das endings). This is essential for shopping, choosing, and specifying: 'Welchen Kaffee möchten Sie?'

items.question: _____ gehört dieses Handy?
items.translation: (Whose phone is this? / Who does this phone belong to?)
items.explanation: 'Wem' is the dative form of 'wer' (who): wer (nominative), wen (accusative), wem (dative), wessen (genitive). 'Gehören' (to belong to) takes the dative case, so you need 'wem': 'Wem gehört das?' (Who does that belong to?). The four forms of 'wer' mirror English who/whom but are actually used more consistently in German. 'Wen' = whom (accusative): 'Wen siehst du?' (Whom do you see?). 'Wessen' = whose: 'Wessen Buch ist das?' (Whose book is that?). Knowing these four forms is crucial.

items.question: _____ fährst du in den Urlaub?
items.explanation: 'Wann' (when) is the question word — not to be confused with 'wenn' (when/if in statements) or 'als' (when, for single past events). This trio is one of German's most confusing areas: 'Wann' = question word only ('Wann kommst du?'), 'wenn' = repeated/future events and conditionals ('Wenn ich Zeit habe, komme ich' = When/If I have time, I'll come), 'als' = single completed past events ('Als ich klein war' = When I was small). Getting wann/wenn/als right is a major milestone in German learning.

items.question: _____ fährt der nächste Zug?
items.translation: (Where does the next train go to?)
items.explanation: German distinguishes between 'wo' (where — static location), 'wohin' (where to — direction/destination), and 'woher' (where from — origin). 'Wohin fährt der Zug?' asks about destination. English uses just 'where' for all three, but German requires this distinction — it's connected to the two-way prepositions and dative/accusative choice. 'Wo bist du?' (Where are you? — dative), 'Wohin gehst du?' (Where are you going? — accusative), 'Woher kommst du?' (Where do you come from?). This three-way distinction is fundamental.

items.question: Du kommst aus Berlin, _____?
items.explanation: German tag questions are simpler than English ones. Instead of complex constructions like 'don't you?' or 'isn't it?', German typically adds: 'oder?' (or?), 'nicht wahr?' (not true?), 'nicht?' (not?), 'gell?' (right? — Southern German), 'ne?' (right? — Northern German/colloquial). These are invariable — no need to match the subject or tense like English tag questions. 'Oder?' is the most common and versatile, usable in any context. Just add it to any statement to turn it into a confirmation question.

items.question: _____ kann ich Ihnen helfen?
items.explanation: 'Wie kann ich Ihnen helfen?' is the standard polite service phrase (How can I help you?). Note 'Ihnen' (formal dative 'you') — 'helfen' takes the dative case, not accusative. This is one of German's key dative verbs. 'Ihnen' (capital I) = formal you (dative), 'ihnen' (lowercase) = them (dative). The formal 'Sie/Ihnen' is always capitalized in writing. This question structure — W-word + modal verb + dative pronoun + infinitive — is a template for many polite inquiries: 'Was darf ich Ihnen bringen?' (What may I bring you?).

items.question: Weißt du, _____ er kommt?
items.translation: (Do you know when he is coming?)
items.explanation: Indirect W-questions use the same question word but change to subordinate clause word order (verb at end): 'Weißt du, wann er kommt?' (Do you know when he comes?). Direct: 'Wann kommt er?' (V2 word order) → Indirect: '...wann er kommt' (verb-final). This transformation applies to all W-words: 'Wo wohnt sie?' → 'Weißt du, wo sie wohnt?', 'Was macht er?' → 'Ich weiß nicht, was er macht.' The comma before the indirect question clause is required in German. Notice: 'wann' (not 'wenn') because it's still a question, just an indirect one.

items.question: Kannst du mir sagen, _____ der Bahnhof ist?
items.translation: (Can you tell me where the train station is?)
items.explanation: Another indirect question: 'wo der Bahnhof ist' — verb 'ist' goes to the end. 'Kannst du mir sagen, wo...?' is the polite way to ask for directions — much softer than 'Wo ist der Bahnhof?' This indirectness is culturally important in German-speaking countries. Similar polite question frames: 'Könnten Sie mir sagen, ...?' (Could you tell me...? — most polite), 'Wissen Sie, ...?' (Do you know...?), 'Darf ich fragen, ...?' (May I ask...?). Using indirect questions shows good manners and advanced German ability.

items.question: _____ von euch hat das gesagt?
items.explanation: 'Wer von euch' (who of you / which one of you) uses 'wer' as a pronoun. When asking about a person's identity from a group, 'wer' is used, not 'welcher'. 'Welcher' would be used if a noun follows: 'Welcher Schüler hat das gesagt?' (Which student said that?). Without a noun, 'wer' is preferred: 'Wer hat angerufen?' (Who called?). 'Von euch' (of you, informal plural) uses the dative after 'von'. Formal: 'Wer von Ihnen?' (Who among you?). This distinction between 'wer' (pronoun) and 'welch-' (determiner before noun) is important.

items.question: _____ denkst du darüber?
items.explanation: 'Was denkst du darüber?' (What do you think about it?) uses the pronominal adverb 'darüber' (about it) — 'da+r+über'. You cannot say 'über was' in standard German for 'about what'; instead use 'worüber': 'Worüber denkst du nach?' (What are you thinking about?). Pronominal adverbs with 'wo(r)-' are the question forms: wo+mit → womit (with what), wo+für → wofür (for what), wo+von → wovon (about/from what), wo+ran → woran (on/at what). These replace 'preposition + was' and are essential for fluent German.

items.question: Das ist _____ Buch.
items.explanation: Possessive pronouns in German must agree with the gender, case, and number of the noun they modify — not the gender of the owner. 'Buch' is neuter (das Buch) and here it's in the nominative case (subject). For neuter nominative nouns, 'mein' takes no ending — it stays as 'mein'. This is identical to the indefinite article pattern: 'ein Buch' → 'mein Buch'. English simply uses 'my' regardless of the noun, but German requires you to think about what you're possessing, not who possesses it.

items.question: Ist das _____ Tasche?
items.explanation: 'Tasche' is feminine (die Tasche) and in the nominative case here (subject of 'ist'). For feminine nominative, the possessive pronoun takes the -e ending: 'deine'. The pattern follows the indefinite article: 'eine Tasche' → 'deine Tasche'. All possessive pronouns (mein, dein, sein, ihr, unser, euer, ihr, Ihr) follow the same ending pattern based on gender and case. Think of possessive pronouns as 'ein-words' — they take the same endings as 'ein/eine/einen/einem/einer/eines'.

items.question: Er liebt _____ Hund.
items.explanation: 'Hund' is masculine (der Hund) and is the direct object of 'liebt' (loves), so it's in the accusative case. For masculine accusative, possessive pronouns take the -en ending: 'seinen'. This is a crucial distinction — masculine is the ONLY gender where the article changes in accusative (der → den, ein → einen, sein → seinen). Feminine, neuter, and plural stay the same in accusative as in nominative. This masculine accusative -en ending is one of the most frequently tested German grammar points.

items.question: Wir besuchen _____ Großmutter.
items.explanation: 'Großmutter' is feminine (die Großmutter) and is the direct object (accusative). For feminine accusative, the ending is -e: 'unsere'. Note that feminine nouns keep the same article form in both nominative and accusative — 'unsere Großmutter' works as both subject and object. 'Unser' (our) is slightly irregular: with endings, it can optionally drop the -e- from the stem: 'unsre' instead of 'unsere' (both correct in spoken German). In formal writing, always use the full form 'unsere'.

items.question: Sie gibt _____ Kind einen Apfel.
items.explanation: 'Kind' is neuter (das Kind) and is the indirect object (to whom she gives), so it takes the dative case. For neuter dative, possessive pronouns take -em: 'ihrem'. Note the ambiguity: 'ihr' can mean 'her' (from sie = she) or 'their' (from sie = they) — context resolves this. In this sentence, 'Sie gibt' (she gives) makes it clear 'ihrem' means 'her'. The dative endings -em (m/n), -er (f), -en (pl) are the same for all ein-words and must be memorized as a core pattern.

items.question: Das Haus _____ Eltern ist groß.
items.explanation: 'Eltern' is plural, and it follows the genitive case here (possession — the house OF my parents). For genitive plural, possessive pronouns take -er: 'meiner'. German genitive replaces English 's or 'of' constructions. Notice the word order: 'Das Haus meiner Eltern' literally translates as 'the house of-my parents'. In spoken German, many people replace genitive with dative + von: 'Das Haus von meinen Eltern', but in writing and formal speech, the genitive form is preferred and considered more educated.

items.question: Ich habe _____ Schlüssel verloren.
items.explanation: 'Schlüssel' is masculine (der Schlüssel) and is the direct object of 'verloren' (lost), making it accusative. Masculine accusative requires -en: 'meinen'. This sentence uses the Perfekt tense (habe... verloren), and the possessive pronoun is attached to the object, not affected by the tense. A common error is using 'mein' (nominative) when 'meinen' (accusative) is needed. Remember: if a masculine noun is receiving the action, the possessive always ends in -en.

items.question: _____ Bruder wohnt in Berlin.
items.explanation: 'Bruder' is masculine (der Bruder) and is the subject (nominative case). For masculine nominative, 'ihr' (her) takes no ending: 'Ihr Bruder'. Note: 'Ihr' with a capital I can mean the formal 'your' (Sie → Ihr). In this context, the lowercase 'ihr' means 'her', but at the start of a sentence both are capitalized, creating ambiguity. Only context tells you whether 'Ihr Bruder' means 'her brother' or 'your brother' (formal). This is a common source of confusion even for advanced learners.

items.question: Wir helfen _____ Freunden.
items.explanation: 'Freunden' is plural dative (the verb 'helfen' requires dative case). For plural dative, possessive pronouns take -en: 'unseren'. Notice that 'Freunden' itself also adds -n in the dative plural — this is a German rule: almost all plural nouns add -n in the dative (unless they already end in -n or -s). The verb 'helfen' is one of many German verbs that always take dative instead of accusative: helfen, danken, folgen, gehören, gefallen, glauben, gratulieren. This dative requirement overrides the normal direct object pattern.

items.question: Er fährt mit _____ Auto zur Arbeit.
