The subjunctive here expresses a wish that God receive thanks. Similar fossilized K1 expressions: 'Es lebe die Freiheit!' (Long live freedom!), 'Ruhe in Frieden' (Rest in peace — though this uses imperative in German), 'Es sei denn, ...' (unless/except if...), 'Dem sei, wie es wolle' (Be that as it may). 'Sei es ... oder ...' (be it ... or ...) is used for alternatives: 'Sei es heute oder morgen' (Whether today or tomorrow).

items.question: Wenn es regnet, _____ wir zu Hause.
items.translation: (If it rains, we stay at home)
items.explanation: Real conditionals (Realis) use indicative in both clauses: 'Wenn es regnet, bleiben wir zu Hause.' This describes a realistic, possible situation — it might actually rain. German real conditionals mirror English Type 1: 'If it rains, we stay home.' Note the word order: 'wenn' sends the verb to the END of the wenn-clause ('wenn es regnet'), and the main clause starts with the verb because the wenn-clause occupies first position: 'bleiben wir.' This verb-first-in-main-clause pattern after a subordinate clause is essential German word order.

items.question: Wenn ich viel Geld _____, würde ich ein Haus kaufen.
items.translation: (If I had a lot of money, I would buy a house)
items.explanation: Unreal conditionals (Irrealis Gegenwart) use Konjunktiv II: 'Wenn ich viel Geld hätte, würde ich ein Haus kaufen.' This expresses a situation contrary to reality — I DON'T have much money. The wenn-clause uses 'hätte' (K2 of haben), the main clause uses 'würde + infinitive.' Key distinction from English: German clearly marks unreality through Konjunktiv II, while English relies on context ('If I had' could be past or unreal). German is unambiguous: indicative = real possibility, Konjunktiv II = unreal/hypothetical.

items.question: _____ ich mehr gelernt, hätte ich die Prüfung bestanden.
items.translation: (Had I studied more, I would have passed the exam)
items.explanation: Unreal past conditional (Irrealis Vergangenheit): 'Hätte ich mehr gelernt, hätte ich die Prüfung bestanden.' Both clauses use Konjunktiv II Vergangenheit: 'hätte + past participle.' No 'wenn' here — instead, the verb starts the clause (inversion replaces 'wenn'). With 'wenn': 'Wenn ich mehr gelernt hätte, hätte ich die Prüfung bestanden.' This expresses regret about an unchangeable past: I DIDN'T study enough, so I DIDN'T pass. German past unreal conditionals always use 'hätte/wäre + Partizip II.'

items.question: Wenn du früher _____ wärst, hättest du den Zug noch erreicht.
items.translation: (If you had left earlier, you would have caught the train)
items.explanation: 'Wärst ... gegangen' uses 'wäre + past participle' because 'gehen' (to go) takes 'sein' in the perfect tense, not 'haben.' Past unreal: 'Wenn du früher gegangen wärst' — the verb 'wärst' goes to the end of the wenn-clause, after the participle. Verbs of movement (gehen, fahren, laufen, fliegen) and state change (werden, sterben, einschlafen) use 'sein' in both indicative Perfekt AND Konjunktiv II past. The main clause: 'hättest du ... erreicht' — 'erreichen' uses 'haben,' so it gets 'hätte.'

items.question: _____ es morgen schön, gehen wir wandern.
items.translation: (If the weather is nice tomorrow, we'll go hiking)
items.explanation: Real conditional without 'wenn' — the verb comes first: 'Ist es morgen schön, gehen wir wandern.' This is equivalent to 'Wenn es morgen schön ist...' but feels slightly more casual and spontaneous. Dropping 'wenn' and using verb-first is common in spoken German: 'Hast du Hunger, bestellen wir Pizza' (If you're hungry, we'll order pizza). This only works with real conditionals (indicative). With unreal conditionals, verb-first also works but uses Konjunktiv II: 'Hätte ich Zeit, käme ich mit.' Both patterns — with and without 'wenn' — are equally correct.

items.question: Wenn er _____, wird er anrufen.
items.explanation: German real conditionals use PRESENT tense in the wenn-clause, even when referring to the future. 'Wenn er kommt' (present) = 'If he comes' (future implication). The main clause can use present ('ruft er an') or future ('wird er anrufen'). Unlike English where future conditionals use 'will,' German wenn-clauses NEVER use 'werden': NOT 'Wenn er kommen wird, ...' — this is a common English-speaker mistake. The wenn-clause stays in present tense; the future meaning is understood from context. Only the main clause may optionally use 'werden.'

items.question: Wenn ich _____, hätte ich dir geholfen.
items.translation: (If I had known, I would have helped you)
items.explanation: 'Wenn ich gewusst hätte' — Konjunktiv II Vergangenheit with 'haben.' In the wenn-clause, the word order places 'hätte' at the very end (verb-final in subordinate clauses): 'wenn ich es gewusst hätte.' The main clause: 'hätte ich dir geholfen' — verb-first because the wenn-clause occupies position zero. This sentence expresses regret: 'I didn't know, so I didn't help — but I would have.' Past unreal conditionals are the primary way Germans express regret, missed opportunities, and 'if only' scenarios.

items.question: Wenn es nicht so teuer _____, würde ich es kaufen.
items.translation: (If it weren't so expensive, I would buy it)
items.explanation: Present unreal conditional with 'sein': 'Wenn es nicht so teuer wäre, würde ich es kaufen.' The item IS expensive (reality), but you're imagining a world where it isn't (hypothetical). Note 'nicht so teuer' — 'so' intensifies: 'so teuer' = 'that expensive / so expensive.' Konjunktiv II of 'sein' always uses 'wäre' directly, never 'würde sein' (which is technically possible but sounds awkward). Similarly, common verbs like 'haben,' 'wissen,' 'gehen,' 'kommen' prefer their direct K2 forms: hätte, wüsste, ginge, käme.

items.question: _____ du mir helfen, wenn ich dich darum bitte?
items.translation: (Will you help me if I ask you?)
items.explanation: Real conditional — indicative in both clauses: 'Wirst du mir helfen, wenn ich dich darum bitte?' This is a genuine question about a real future possibility. The main clause comes FIRST here, with 'wenn' clause at the end — conditional clauses can go either way in German. 'Darum bitten' = 'to ask for it' — 'darum' refers back to the help. If this were unreal: 'Würdest du mir helfen, wenn ich dich darum bitten würde?' — K2 in both clauses changes the tone to 'hypothetically, would you...'

items.question: Wenn ich du _____, würde ich das anders machen.
items.translation: (If I were you, I would do it differently)
items.explanation: 'Wenn ich du wäre' is the standard German way to say 'If I were you.' Unlike English which always uses 'were' for all persons in the subjunctive, German properly conjugates: 'wenn ich ... wäre' (first person). The main clause: 'würde ich das anders machen' — 'anders' (differently) is key vocabulary. This construction is THE go-to advice formula in German. Variations: 'Wenn ich an deiner Stelle wäre' (if I were in your place), 'Wäre ich du' (were I you — more dramatic, without 'wenn'). Both convey diplomatic advice through the hypothetical mood.

items.question: Wenn er pünktlich _____, brauchen wir nicht zu warten.
items.translation: (If he is on time, we don't need to wait)
items.explanation: Real conditional: 'Wenn er pünktlich ist' — indicative because being on time is a real possibility. 'Pünktlich' (punctual/on time) is a key German cultural concept — punctuality is taken very seriously. 'Brauchen + zu + infinitive' means 'to need to' — it functions like a modal verb in negative sentences: 'brauchen nicht zu warten' = 'don't need to wait.' Note: 'brauchen' with 'zu' is technically correct, though in colloquial German, 'zu' is often dropped: 'brauchen nicht warten.' Prescriptive grammarians insist on 'zu,' but spoken German evolves.

items.question: Hätte ich das _____, wäre ich nicht gekommen.
items.translation: (Had I known that, I wouldn't have come)
items.explanation: Past unreal without 'wenn': 'Hätte ich das gewusst, wäre ich nicht gekommen.' Both clauses use K2 Vergangenheit. The if-clause: 'hätte + gewusst' (wissen uses haben). The main clause: 'wäre + gekommen' (kommen uses sein). Without 'wenn,' the verb leads: 'Hätte ich...' This is more dramatic and common in emphatic speech. The double K2 past structure always expresses what WOULD have happened differently in an alternate past. This is high-level grammar that marks proficient German.

items.question: Wenn du _____, kannst du bleiben.
items.explanation: Simple real conditional: 'Wenn du willst, kannst du bleiben.' Both verbs in indicative — this is a genuine offer. 'Wollen' (to want): du willst. In the main clause: 'kannst du bleiben' — verb-first after the wenn-clause. This is Level 1 conditional: straightforward, no special mood required. Compare the three levels: (1) Real: 'Wenn du willst, kannst du bleiben.' (2) Present unreal: 'Wenn du wolltest, könntest du bleiben.' (3) Past unreal: 'Wenn du gewollt hättest, hättest du bleiben können.' Each level adds complexity and changes meaning.

items.question: Selbst wenn er mich _____, würde ich nicht gehen.
items.translation: (Even if he invited me, I wouldn't go)
items.explanation: 'Selbst wenn' / 'Auch wenn' (even if) introduces a concessive conditional — even in that hypothetical scenario, the result wouldn't change. 'Selbst wenn er mich einladen würde' — K2 with 'würde + infinitive' because 'einlüde' (direct K2 of 'einladen') would sound extremely archaic. In the wenn-clause, 'würde' goes to the end: 'wenn er mich einladen würde.' 'Selbst wenn' adds emphasis that the condition wouldn't change the outcome. Similar concessive conditionals: 'Auch wenn es regnen sollte...' (Even if it should rain...).

items.question: Falls du Hilfe _____, ruf mich an.
items.translation: (In case you need help, call me)
items.explanation: 'Falls' (in case/if) is an alternative to 'wenn' for real conditionals, with a nuance of 'just in case.' 'Falls du Hilfe brauchst, ruf mich an' — indicative because it's a real possibility. 'Falls' vs. 'wenn': 'falls' implies less certainty — it's more like 'should it happen that...' rather than 'when this happens.' 'Falls' is always conditional; 'wenn' can also mean 'when/whenever.' Other conditional conjunctions: 'sofern' (provided that), 'vorausgesetzt, dass' (provided that), 'es sei denn' (unless). Each adds a different shade of conditionality.

items.question: Er sagte, er _____ müde.
items.explanation: German reported speech (indirekte Rede) uses Konjunktiv I: 'Er sagte, er sei müde.' While English shifts tenses ('is' → 'was'), German uses a completely different mood — Konjunktiv I. The form 'sei' comes from 'sein' in K1. This signals to the listener: 'I'm reporting what someone else said — I'm not confirming it's true.' Konjunktiv I is the hallmark of formal reported speech in German, used extensively in journalism, academic writing, and official communication. In casual speech, Germans often use indicative instead.

items.question: Sie sagte, sie _____ keine Zeit.
items.explanation: Konjunktiv I of 'haben' for 'sie' (she) is 'habe': 'Sie sagte, sie habe keine Zeit.' The K1 forms are built from the infinitive stem: hab-e, hab-est, hab-e, hab-en, hab-et, hab-en. Notice that for 'sie' (she), K1 'habe' differs from indicative 'hat' — this is the ideal case where K1 is visually distinct. When K1 would be identical to indicative (e.g., 'sie haben' = both indicative and K1), German switches to Konjunktiv II ('hätten') to maintain the reported speech signal.

items.question: Der Lehrer meinte, die Schüler _____ fleißig.
items.translation: (The teacher said the students were diligent)
items.explanation: For plural subjects in reported speech, use K1 plural: 'seien' (from sein). 'Der Lehrer meinte, die Schüler seien fleißig.' The K1 plural of 'sein' is 'seien' — distinct from indicative 'sind.' This is important: when K1 is distinguishable from indicative, always use K1. The reporting verb 'meinen' (to say/think) introduces indirect speech just like 'sagen.' Other common reporting verbs: behaupten (claim), berichten (report), erklären (explain), betonen (emphasize). Each introduces a K1 clause.

items.question: Er berichtete, er _____ gestern angekommen.
items.translation: (He reported that he had arrived yesterday)
items.explanation: Past events in reported speech use K1 + past participle: 'Er sei gestern angekommen.' German reported speech doesn't shift tenses like English does. Instead, it uses K1 of the auxiliary (sein/haben) + past participle for past events. So 'Ich bin angekommen' (I arrived) becomes 'Er sei angekommen' in reported speech. This is more elegant than English, which must navigate complex tense-shifting rules. The auxiliary choice (sein vs. haben) follows the same rules as Perfekt: movement/change-of-state verbs take 'sein.'

items.question: Sie erzählte, sie _____ nach Berlin fahren.
items.translation: (She told us she would drive to Berlin)
items.explanation: Future in reported speech uses K1 of 'werden': 'werde' + infinitive. 'Sie erzählte, sie werde nach Berlin fahren.' This reports a future intention. K1 of werden: ich werde, du werdest, er/sie werde, wir werden, ihr werdet, sie werden. For 'sie' (she), K1 'werde' differs from indicative 'wird' — clear signal of reported speech. Don't confuse 'werde' (K1, reported speech) with 'würde' (K2, hypothetical). 'Sie werde fahren' = she reportedly will drive. 'Sie würde fahren' = she would drive (if...).

items.question: Die Zeitung schrieb, der Präsident _____ eine Rede gehalten.
items.translation: (The newspaper wrote that the president had given a speech)
items.explanation: Journalistic reported speech consistently uses K1: 'Der Präsident habe eine Rede gehalten.' German newspapers and news broadcasts use K1 extensively — it's the standard for reporting others' statements without taking personal responsibility for their truth. This 'distancing' function is crucial: 'Der Präsident hat eine Rede gehalten' (indicative) = the journalist confirms this happened. 'Der Präsident habe eine Rede gehalten' (K1) = the journalist reports this claim. This distinction doesn't exist in English journalism.

items.question: Er fragte, ob ich morgen _____ könne.
items.translation: (He asked whether I could come tomorrow)
items.explanation: Reported questions use 'ob' (whether) + K1: 'Er fragte, ob ich morgen kommen könne.' The modal 'können' becomes 'könne' in K1, and the main verb stays as infinitive at the end. Yes/no questions in reported speech always use 'ob': 'Kannst du kommen?' → 'Er fragte, ob ich kommen könne.' W-questions keep their question word: 'Wo wohnst du?' → 'Er fragte, wo ich wohne.' Note: the verb goes to the end in both cases because 'ob' and question words function as subordinating elements.

items.question: Die Freunde sagten, sie _____ gern helfen.
items.translation: (The friends said they would gladly help)
items.explanation: When K1 is identical to indicative, switch to K2: 'sie würden' instead of 'sie werden.' For 'sie' (they), K1 of werden = 'werden' — same as indicative! This creates ambiguity: 'Sie sagten, sie werden helfen' — is this reported speech or direct statement? Solution: use K2 'würden' to clearly mark reported speech. This K1→K2 substitution rule is fundamental: always check if K1 is distinguishable from indicative. If not, use K2. This mainly affects 'wir' and 'sie/Sie' forms where K1 often matches indicative.

items.question: Man berichtet, das Wetter _____ sich ändern.
items.translation: (It is reported that the weather will change)
items.explanation: Impersonal reported speech with 'man berichtet' (it is reported): 'Das Wetter werde sich ändern.' This construction is very common in German news and formal communication. 'Man' (one/people) as subject creates distance — nobody specific is making the claim. The K1 'werde' signals reported information. Compare: 'Das Wetter wird sich ändern' (direct claim) vs. 'Das Wetter werde sich ändern' (reported claim). German achieves through grammar what English needs extra words for ('reportedly,' 'allegedly,' 'it is said that').

items.question: Er sagte, er _____ das nicht verstehen.
items.explanation: K1 of 'können' for er/sie/es is 'könne': 'Er sagte, er könne das nicht verstehen.' Modal verbs in K1: können→könne, müssen→müsse, dürfen→dürfe, sollen→solle, wollen→wolle, mögen→möge. These forms sound archaic to many German speakers, which is why colloquial German often skips K1 entirely and uses indicative: 'Er sagte, er kann das nicht verstehen.' But formal writing, journalism, and academic German require K1. Learning K1 modal forms marks you as a sophisticated German speaker.

items.question: Sie behauptete, sie _____ alles allein gemacht.
items.translation: (She claimed she had done everything alone)
items.explanation: 'Behaupten' (to claim) introduces reported speech with a hint of skepticism: 'Sie behauptete, sie habe alles allein gemacht.' The choice of reporting verb in German carries nuance: 'sagen' is neutral, 'behaupten' implies possible doubt, 'betonen' (emphasize) suggests the speaker stressed this point, 'zugeben' (admit) implies reluctance. K1 'habe' + past participle 'gemacht' reports a past action. The reporting verb's meaning combines with K1's distancing effect to create layers of meaning absent in English reported speech.

items.question: Der Arzt erklärte, der Patient _____ sich erholen.
items.translation: (The doctor explained that the patient would recover)
items.explanation: 'Erklären' (to explain) + K1 for professional/medical contexts: 'Der Patient werde sich erholen.' Medical, legal, and scientific German extensively uses K1 reported speech. When a doctor says 'Der Patient wird sich erholen' (indicative), it's a direct statement of belief. When reported as 'Der Patient werde sich erholen' (K1), it attributes the prognosis to the doctor without the reporter guaranteeing it. This precision is valued in professional German communication and is tested in advanced language certifications (C1/C2).

items.question: Er wollte wissen, wann der Bus _____.
items.translation: (He wanted to know when the bus would arrive)
items.explanation: W-question reported speech: 'Er wollte wissen, wann der Bus komme.' The question word 'wann' replaces 'ob' for specific-information questions. 'Wann kommt der Bus?' → 'Er wollte wissen, wann der Bus komme.' The verb moves to the end (subordinate clause word order). Other W-words work identically: 'wo' (where), 'warum' (why), 'wie' (how), 'wer' (who). Notice: 'wollte wissen' (wanted to know) is a common alternative to 'fragte' (asked) for reporting questions, especially for information-seeking rather than yes/no questions.

items.question: Laut dem Bericht _____ die Firma 100 neue Stellen schaffen.
items.translation: (According to the report, the company would create 100 new jobs)
items.explanation: 'Laut' (according to) + dative introduces reported information without K1 subordination: 'Laut dem Bericht werde die Firma 100 neue Stellen schaffen.' Here K1 appears in a main clause — no 'dass' or 'ob' needed. This is characteristic of journalistic German: the source attribution comes first ('laut dem Bericht'), then K1 signals reported speech. Other source phrases: 'nach Angaben' (according to information), 'wie [Quelle] mitteilte' (as [source] announced). This structure is everywhere in German news writing.

items.question: Sie teilte mit, dass sie nächste Woche nicht _____ werde.
items.translation: (She informed them that she would not come next week)
items.explanation: With 'dass' (that), the conjugated verb goes to the very end: 'dass sie nächste Woche nicht kommen werde.' Here 'werde' (K1 of werden) sits at the clause end, and the infinitive 'kommen' precedes it. Compare without 'dass': 'Sie werde nächste Woche nicht kommen' — here 'werde' is in second position. Both are correct, but 'dass' clauses are more common in everyday German. 'Mitteilen' (to inform/announce) is a formal reporting verb used in official communication, business, and government contexts.

items.question: Es ist wichtig, pünktlich _____.
items.explanation: German infinitive clauses use 'zu' + infinitive at the end: 'Es ist wichtig, pünktlich zu sein.' Unlike English 'to be punctual,' German places 'zu sein' at the very end of the clause. The comma before the infinitive clause is mandatory in German when the clause has additional words beyond 'zu + infinitive.' These clauses replace full subordinate clauses when the subject is implied: 'Es ist wichtig, dass man pünktlich ist' = 'Es ist wichtig, pünktlich zu sein.' The infinitive clause is more elegant and concise.

items.question: Ich habe vergessen, das Buch _____.
items.explanation: With separable verbs, 'zu' is inserted between prefix and verb: 'mit-zu-bringen' → 'mitzubringen.' This is a key German rule: separable prefixes 'open up' to accommodate 'zu.' Examples: aufzustehen (to get up), einzukaufen (to shop), anzurufen (to call). The entire combination is written as ONE word. 'Vergessen' (to forget) naturally takes infinitive clauses: 'Ich habe vergessen, X zu tun.' Other verbs requiring infinitive clauses: versuchen (to try), beginnen (to begin), hoffen (to hope), planen (to plan).

items.question: Er hat mir versprochen, morgen pünktlich _____.
items.translation: (He promised me to be on time tomorrow)
items.explanation: 'Versprechen' (to promise) triggers an infinitive clause: 'Er hat mir versprochen, morgen pünktlich zu kommen.' The infinitive clause works here because the subject of both clauses is the same person — HE promised and HE will come. This is a critical rule: infinitive clauses can only be used when the implied subject matches the main clause subject. If different subjects are involved, you must use a full 'dass' clause: 'Er hat mir versprochen, dass er morgen pünktlich kommt.' Infinitive clauses are always more elegant when grammatically possible.

items.question: Wir haben vor, im Sommer nach Italien _____.
items.translation: (We plan to travel to Italy in summer)
items.explanation: 'Vorhaben' (to plan/intend) splits as 'haben vor' and takes an infinitive clause: 'Wir haben vor, im Sommer nach Italien zu reisen.' The expression 'vorhaben' is itself separable! In the main clause it splits: 'Wir haben [etwas] vor.' The infinitive clause then fills in what is planned. Note the word order within the infinitive clause: time ('im Sommer'), then place ('nach Italien'), then 'zu reisen' at the end. German infinitive clauses follow standard German word order — time before place before manner — with zu+infinitive always last.

items.question: Statt zu Hause zu bleiben, bin ich _____.
items.translation: (Instead of staying at home, I went out)
items.explanation: 'Statt...zu' (instead of) creates a prepositional infinitive clause: 'Statt zu Hause zu bleiben...' Three key prepositions combine with 'zu + infinitive': 'statt...zu' (instead of), 'ohne...zu' (without), 'um...zu' (in order to). These are mandatory comma-separated constructions. The main clause uses Perfekt: 'bin ich ausgegangen.' Note: 'statt' can also be 'anstatt' — both are correct. Unlike English 'instead of staying' (gerund), German always uses 'statt...zu + infinitive.' This pattern has no gerund alternative in German.

items.question: Er ging ins Kino, ohne mich _____.
items.translation: (He went to the cinema without asking me)
items.explanation: 'Ohne...zu' (without doing) is the second key prepositional infinitive: 'Er ging ins Kino, ohne mich zu fragen.' English uses a gerund ('without asking'), but German requires 'ohne...zu + infinitive.' The accusative object 'mich' comes before 'zu fragen.' This construction implies the subjects are identical: HE went and HE didn't ask. If different subjects: 'Er ging ins Kino, ohne dass ich es wusste' (without me knowing — uses 'ohne dass' + full clause). 'Ohne...zu' expresses something that was expected but didn't happen — a missing accompaniment.

items.question: Ich bin hier, um Deutsch _____.
items.translation: (I am here in order to learn German)
items.explanation: 'Um...zu' (in order to) expresses purpose — the most common prepositional infinitive: 'Ich bin hier, um Deutsch zu lernen.' This is one of the first advanced structures German learners should master. While English can say either 'to learn' or 'in order to learn,' German MUST use 'um...zu' for purpose — you cannot say '*Ich bin hier, Deutsch zu lernen.' The 'um' is not optional. Direct object 'Deutsch' comes before 'zu lernen.' This structure answers the question 'Wozu?' (What for?) or 'Warum?' (Why?).

items.question: Sie versucht, das Problem _____.
items.translation: (She is trying to solve the problem)
items.explanation: 'Versuchen' (to try) takes an infinitive clause: 'Sie versucht, das Problem zu lösen.' 'Versuchen' is one of many verbs that naturally connect to infinitive clauses. The direct object 'das Problem' sits inside the infinitive clause before 'zu lösen.' Important: the comma before the infinitive clause is required when the clause contains more than just 'zu + infinitive.' Writing 'Sie versucht zu schlafen' (no comma needed — only zu+infinitive) vs. 'Sie versucht, das Problem zu lösen' (comma required — additional elements). This comma rule is strictly enforced in written German.

items.question: Ich habe keine Lust, heute _____.
items.explanation: 'Lust haben' (to feel like) + infinitive clause: 'Ich habe keine Lust, heute zu arbeiten.' Many German noun+verb combinations take infinitive clauses: 'Lust haben' (feel like), 'Angst haben' (be afraid), 'Zeit haben' (have time), 'die Möglichkeit haben' (have the opportunity). The negation 'keine' modifies 'Lust' in the main clause. The adverb 'heute' sits in the infinitive clause before 'zu arbeiten.' These expressions are extremely common in everyday German and provide natural-sounding alternatives to more formal constructions.

items.question: Es fällt mir schwer, früh _____.
items.translation: (It is difficult for me to get up early)
items.explanation: 'Es fällt mir schwer' (it's difficult for me) + infinitive clause. Separable verb 'aufstehen' becomes 'aufzustehen' — 'zu' inserts between 'auf' and 'stehen,' written as one word. 'Mir' is dative (to me). The impersonal 'es' as subject is typical German: 'Es fällt mir schwer/leicht, X zu tun.' Similarly: 'Es macht mir Spaß' (it's fun for me), 'Es gelingt mir' (I succeed in). These structures are fundamentally different from English, which uses personal subjects: 'I find it difficult.' German's impersonal constructions are more common and natural.

items.question: Er bat mich, ihm dabei _____.
items.translation: (He asked me to help him with it)
items.explanation: 'Bitten' (to ask/request) + accusative person + infinitive clause: 'Er bat mich, ihm dabei zu helfen.' This is important: 'bitten' takes accusative for the person asked ('mich') and introduces an infinitive clause for the request. Don't confuse with 'fragen' (to ask a question) which doesn't take infinitive clauses. The dative 'ihm' and 'dabei' belong inside the infinitive clause. 'Bitten' is irregular: bat (past), gebeten (participle). This verb bridges formal requests: 'Ich bitte Sie, Platz zu nehmen' (I ask you to take a seat — very polite).

items.question: Ich empfehle dir, mehr Wasser _____.
items.explanation: 'Empfehlen' (to recommend) + dative person + infinitive clause: 'Ich empfehle dir, mehr Wasser zu trinken.' Note: the person being advised takes DATIVE ('dir'), not accusative. German recommendation verbs use dative: 'Ich rate dir' (I advise you), 'Ich empfehle Ihnen' (I recommend to you, formal). The infinitive clause contains the recommended action. Interesting: while English says 'I recommend you drink,' this ambiguity about whether 'you' is subject or object doesn't exist in German — the dative 'dir' is clearly the indirect object.

items.question: Sie scheint, die Antwort _____.
items.explanation: 'Scheinen' (to seem) + infinitive clause: 'Sie scheint, die Antwort zu wissen.' This construction mirrors English 'she seems to know.' 'Scheinen' + zu + infinitive is one of the few German structures that closely parallels English. Other verbs in this pattern: 'pflegen zu' (to be accustomed to), 'drohen zu' (to threaten to — for situations: 'Es droht zu regnen' = it threatens to rain), 'brauchen zu' (to need to — with negation: 'Du brauchst nicht zu kommen' = you don't need to come). These are called 'semi-modal' constructions.

items.question: Ich freue mich darauf, dich _____.
items.explanation: 'Sich freuen auf' + 'darauf' + infinitive clause: 'Ich freue mich darauf, dich zu sehen.' The 'da(r)+preposition' construction ('darauf') is a bridge between the main clause and the infinitive clause. It anticipates the infinitive clause: 'worauf freue ich mich? → darauf, dich zu sehen.' Other examples: 'Ich denke daran, zu kündigen' (I'm thinking of quitting), 'Er gewöhnt sich daran, früh aufzustehen' (He's getting used to getting up early). The 'da(r)+prep' is essential when verbs with fixed prepositions connect to infinitive clauses.

items.question: Es ist schwer, diese Regel _____.
items.translation: (It is hard to understand this rule)
items.explanation: 'Es ist + adjective' + infinitive clause: 'Es ist schwer, diese Regel zu verstehen.' Impersonal adjective constructions with infinitive clauses are extremely productive in German: 'Es ist leicht/schwierig/wichtig/möglich/unmöglich/nötig/schön, X zu tun.' Note that 'verstehen' is inseparable (ver- prefix) — 'zu' simply goes before it: 'zu verstehen.' Inseparable prefixes (be-, emp-, ent-, er-, ge-, miss-, ver-, zer-) never separate, so 'zu' precedes the whole verb. This contrasts with separable verbs where 'zu' inserts in the middle.

items.question: Das _____ Kind schläft friedlich.
items.explanation: Present participle as adjective: 'schlafend' (sleeping) + adjective ending. 'Das schlafende Kind' — the present participle 'schlafend' functions as an attributive adjective and MUST take adjective endings. Here: neuter nominative with definite article → '-e' ending = 'schlafende.' German present participles are formed by adding '-d' to the infinitive: schlafen→schlafend, lesen→lesend, spielen→spielend. When used before nouns, they follow ALL normal adjective declension rules. This is fundamentally different from English, where 'the sleeping child' needs no ending changes.

items.question: Der _____ Brief liegt auf dem Tisch.
items.translation: (The written letter is on the table)
items.explanation: Past participle as adjective: 'geschrieben' (written) + adjective ending. 'Der geschriebene Brief' — masculine nominative with definite article → '-e' ending = 'geschriebene.' Past participles used as adjectives typically have PASSIVE meaning: 'der geschriebene Brief' = the letter THAT WAS written. Compare with present participle which has ACTIVE meaning: 'der schreibende Mann' = the man WHO IS writing. This active/passive distinction between Partizip I (present) and Partizip II (past) as adjectives is essential for reading German texts, especially formal and academic writing.

items.question: Die in Berlin _____ Frau spricht drei Sprachen.
items.translation: (The woman living in Berlin speaks three languages)
items.explanation: Extended participle construction: 'Die in Berlin lebende Frau.' This is a hallmark of formal German — a participial phrase EMBEDDED between article and noun. English would say 'The woman living in Berlin' (after the noun) or use a relative clause. German places it BEFORE the noun: 'Die [in Berlin lebende] Frau.' This structure can become very long in academic German: 'Die seit drei Jahren in Berlin lebende und an der Universität studierende Frau.' Such constructions condense information that would require relative clauses in English. They're common in newspapers, laws, and scientific texts.

items.question: Das von meinem Vater _____ Auto ist rot.
items.translation: (The car repaired by my father is red)
items.explanation: Extended past participle: 'Das von meinem Vater reparierte Auto.' Past participle 'repariert' + adjective ending '-e' (neuter nominative, definite article). The passive meaning is clear: the car WAS REPAIRED by my father. 'Von meinem Vater' (by my father) indicates the agent — just like in a passive sentence. This construction replaces: 'Das Auto, das von meinem Vater repariert wurde.' The participial version is more concise. Notice: the entire phrase 'von meinem Vater reparierte' sits between 'Das' and 'Auto' — this frame structure is key to German noun phrases.

items.question: Die gut _____ Nachricht verbreitete sich schnell.
items.explanation: Past participle with adverb modifier: 'Die gut versteckte Nachricht.' The adverb 'gut' (well) modifies 'versteckt' (hidden), creating a richer participle construction. Feminine nominative with definite article → '-e' ending = 'versteckte.' Adverbs commonly modify participles in these constructions: 'der schnell fahrende Zug' (the fast-driving train), 'die sorgfältig geschriebene Arbeit' (the carefully written paper). These multi-word attributive constructions before the noun are characteristic of German and can trip up English speakers who expect modifiers after the noun.

items.question: _____ vor Freude, umarmte sie ihre Mutter.
items.translation: (Crying with joy, she hugged her mother)
items.explanation: Free-standing present participle clause: 'Weinend vor Freude, umarmte sie ihre Mutter.' Here 'weinend' starts the sentence as an adverbial participle — describing HOW or WHILE she hugged her mother. No adjective endings because it's not modifying a noun directly — it functions as an adverb. This mirrors English: 'Crying with joy, she hugged her mother.' Free participle clauses are less common in everyday German than in English, but appear in literary and journalistic writing. They add stylistic elegance and are always set off by a comma.

items.question: Er las das Buch, auf dem Sofa _____.
items.translation: (He read the book, sitting on the sofa)
items.explanation: Post-positioned participle clause: 'auf dem Sofa sitzend' (sitting on the sofa). This adverbial participial clause comes AFTER the main clause, separated by a comma. It describes the manner/circumstances of the main action. The present participle 'sitzend' has no endings because it functions adverbially. German uses these less frequently than English, often preferring a subordinate clause: 'Er las das Buch, während er auf dem Sofa saß.' But the participial version is more elegant in written German. Note: 'auf dem Sofa' uses dative because 'sitzen' indicates location (no movement).

items.question: Die vom Sturm _____ Bäume blockierten die Straße.
items.translation: (The trees knocked down by the storm blocked the road)
items.explanation: Extended past participle with separable prefix: 'umgestürzt' → 'umgestürzten' (plural nominative, definite article → '-en'). 'Die vom Sturm umgestürzten Bäume.' Separable verb 'umstürzen' (to knock over/topple) forms past participle 'umgestürzt.' As attributive adjective, it takes normal declension: die + plural nominative → '-en.' The agent 'vom Sturm' (by the storm) sits inside the extended construction. This is a complex but very natural German sentence — newspapers use these daily. English must restructure: 'The trees knocked down by the storm' or 'The storm-felled trees.'

items.question: Die _____ Sonne wärmte die Erde.
items.explanation: Present participle of separable verb as adjective: 'aufgehen' → 'aufgehend' → 'aufgehende' (feminine nominative, definite article → '-e'). 'Die aufgehende Sonne' — the sun THAT IS rising (active, ongoing). Compare: 'die aufgegangene Sonne' = the sun that HAS risen (completed action). This distinction between Partizip I (present, ongoing) and Partizip II (past, completed) carries real meaning. Scientific and nature writing loves these: 'das fließende Wasser' (flowing water), 'der steigende Meeresspiegel' (rising sea level), 'die wachsende Bevölkerung' (growing population).

items.question: Ich fand den _____ Hund im Park.
items.translation: (I found the lost dog in the park)
items.explanation: Past participle as adjective: 'verloren' (lost) → 'verlorenen' (masculine accusative, definite article → '-en'). 'Den verlorenen Hund' — the dog THAT WAS lost. Accusative case because 'finden' (to find) takes a direct object. Past participles of inseparable verbs keep their 'ge-' prefix when used as adjectives: 'verloren→verlorenen,' 'vergessen→vergessenen,' 'zerbrochen→zerbrochenen.' Each still follows standard adjective declension. Memorizing a few key participle adjectives helps reading comprehension enormously: verloren (lost), vergessen (forgotten), verboten (forbidden), geschlossen (closed).

items.question: Der lange nicht _____ Film wurde endlich gezeigt.
items.translation: (The film not seen for a long time was finally shown)
items.explanation: Negated extended past participle: 'Der lange nicht gesehene Film.' The adverb 'lange' (for a long time) and negation 'nicht' modify 'gesehen' (seen), all sitting between 'Der' and 'Film.' This construction is dense but precise: 'the film that has not been seen for a long time.' German can pack this much information into a single noun phrase without a relative clause. Past participle 'gesehen' (masculine nominative, definite article) → 'gesehene.' The main clause uses passive: 'wurde gezeigt' (was shown). Such layered constructions are typical of written German at C1+ level.

items.question: Die im Garten _____ Blumen duften wunderbar.
items.translation: (The flowers blooming in the garden smell wonderful)
items.explanation: Extended present participle, plural: 'Die im Garten blühenden Blumen.' Present participle 'blühend' (blooming) + plural nominative ending '-en' = 'blühenden.' The prepositional phrase 'im Garten' (in the garden) is embedded in the participial construction. 'Blumen' is plural, and with definite article 'Die,' all attributive adjectives/participles take '-en.' This verb 'duften' (to smell/be fragrant) is intransitive and rather poetic. Extended participial constructions with present participles always indicate something CURRENTLY happening — the flowers ARE blooming right now.

items.question: _____ angekommen, packte er sofort seinen Koffer aus.
items.translation: (Having arrived, he immediately unpacked his suitcase)
items.explanation: 'Kaum angekommen' (hardly/barely arrived) is an elliptical participial construction — extremely common in German. It omits the auxiliary and subject: full form would be 'Kaum war er angekommen, packte er...' This compressed form is natural in both spoken and written German. Similar patterns: 'Einmal angefangen, konnte er nicht aufhören' (Once started, he couldn't stop), 'Dort angekommen, rief sie mich an' (Having arrived there, she called me). 'Kaum' (hardly/scarcely) adds immediacy — he barely arrived and was already unpacking. These constructions add pace and dynamism to narrative German.

items.question: Das _____ Essen schmeckte ausgezeichnet.
items.explanation: Compound participle adjective: 'frisch gekocht' (freshly cooked) → 'frisch gekochte' (neuter nominative, definite article → '-e'). The adverb 'frisch' modifies the past participle 'gekocht,' and together they function as an attributive adjective before the noun. Note: two words, not hyphenated (unlike English 'freshly-cooked'). German handles these naturally: 'das kalt gestellte Bier' (the cold-stored beer), 'die neu renovierte Wohnung' (the newly renovated apartment). Only the LAST word (the participle) takes the adjective ending — 'frisch' stays unchanged. This is consistent with German compound adjective rules.

items.question: Ein _____ Geräusch weckte mich auf.
items.explanation: Present participle with indefinite article: 'beunruhigend' (disturbing) → 'beunruhigendes' (neuter nominative, indefinite article → '-es'). 'Ein beunruhigendes Geräusch.' The present participle conveys active, ongoing quality: the noise IS disturbing (right now, to the listener). With indefinite article 'ein,' neuter nominative takes '-es' — strong declension applies. Compare: 'das beunruhigende Geräusch' (with definite article, weak '-e'). Many present participles have become permanent adjectives in German: 'anstrengend' (exhausting), 'aufregend' (exciting), 'überraschend' (surprising), 'enttäuschend' (disappointing).

items.question: _____ Tisch ist groß.
items.explanation: 'Der Tisch' — masculine. Most nouns ending in '-isch' are masculine. German has three genders (der/die/das) that MUST be memorized with each noun — there's no reliable way to guess from meaning alone. However, certain endings predict gender: '-er' (der Computer), '-ling' (der Frühling), '-ismus' (der Tourismus) tend to be masculine. Unlike Romance languages where gender often follows vowel patterns, German gender can seem arbitrary: 'der Löffel' (spoon) is masculine, 'die Gabel' (fork) feminine, 'das Messer' (knife) neuter. Always learn the article WITH the noun!

items.question: _____ Lampe ist neu.
items.explanation: 'Die Lampe' — feminine. Nouns ending in '-e' are very often feminine (about 90%): die Lampe, die Blume (flower), die Straße (street), die Schule (school). This is one of the most reliable gender rules in German. Exceptions exist but are few: der Junge (boy), der Name (name), das Ende (end). The '-e' ending rule is your best friend when guessing gender. Other feminine endings: '-ung' (die Zeitung), '-heit' (die Freiheit), '-keit' (die Möglichkeit), '-schaft' (die Freundschaft), '-tion' (die Nation). Feminine gender patterns are actually the most predictable in German.

items.question: _____ Kind spielt im Garten.
items.explanation: 'Das Kind' — neuter. German neuter nouns include most diminutives and many basic nouns. Diminutives with '-chen' and '-lein' are ALWAYS neuter: das Mädchen (girl — neuter despite referring to a female!), das Brötchen (bread roll), das Büchlein (little book). This is a fundamental principle: grammatical gender overrides natural gender. Other neuter patterns: '-ment' (das Instrument), '-um' (das Museum), '-tum' (das Eigentum), most words with 'Ge-' prefix (das Gebäude, das Gefühl). Neuter rules are fewer but highly reliable when they apply.

items.question: _____ Mädchen ist klug.
items.explanation: 'Das Mädchen' — neuter, despite meaning 'girl.' This is the most famous German gender surprise: 'Mädchen' is neuter because it's a diminutive (from 'die Magd' + -chen). ALL diminutives in '-chen' and '-lein' are neuter, regardless of the person or thing they describe. 'Das Bübchen' (little boy — neuter), 'das Fräulein' (young woman — neuter). Even pronouns traditionally follow grammatical gender: 'Das Mädchen hat seinen Hund verloren' (neuter 'seinen'). Modern German increasingly uses 'ihr' (feminine) for 'Mädchen' in informal speech, but grammar exams expect neuter agreement.

items.question: _____ Freiheit ist wichtig.
items.explanation: 'Die Freiheit' — feminine. ALL nouns ending in '-heit' are feminine, without exception. This is one of German's absolute gender rules. Similarly absolute: '-keit' (die Möglichkeit — possibility), '-ung' (die Bildung — education), '-schaft' (die Gesellschaft — society), '-tion' (die Information), '-tät' (die Universität). These suffixes create abstract nouns and are always feminine. Memorizing these suffix rules dramatically reduces the gender-guessing burden. With '-heit,' '-keit,' '-ung,' '-schaft,' '-tion,' and '-tät,' you can correctly assign gender to hundreds of nouns automatically.

items.question: _____ Auto fährt schnell.
items.explanation: 'Das Auto' — neuter. Many loanwords from other languages are neuter in German: das Auto, das Hotel, das Restaurant, das Telefon, das Internet. This tendency for foreign words to receive neuter gender has a linguistic basis — neuter serves as the 'default' gender for words that don't fit established patterns. However, this isn't absolute: der Computer (masculine), die E-Mail (feminine). For newer technology terms, gender is often debated: 'der/das Blog,' 'der/das Emoji.' Regional variation exists too: 'das Cola' (Austria) vs. 'die Cola' (Germany). Context and region matter!

items.question: _____ Zeitung liegt auf dem Tisch.
items.explanation: 'Die Zeitung' — feminine. The suffix '-ung' guarantees feminine gender — no exceptions in standard German. 'Die Zeitung' (newspaper), 'die Wohnung' (apartment), 'die Übung' (exercise), 'die Erfahrung' (experience), 'die Bedeutung' (meaning). This suffix is extremely productive — German creates new '-ung' nouns regularly from verbs: 'erfinden' (to invent) → 'die Erfindung' (invention), 'entwickeln' (to develop) → 'die Entwicklung' (development). Learning the verb→'-ung' noun pattern gives you both vocabulary expansion and guaranteed correct gender.

items.question: _____ Universität ist groß.
items.explanation: 'Die Universität' — feminine. The Latin-origin suffix '-tät' (equivalent to English '-ty') is always feminine: die Qualität (quality), die Realität (reality), die Aktivität (activity), die Identität (identity), die Elektrizität (electricity). Similarly, '-tion' words are always feminine: die Situation, die Information, die Kommunikation, die Tradition. German absorbed thousands of Latin/French words with these suffixes, and they ALL became feminine. This consistency is a gift for learners — these international words are easy to recognize AND their gender is guaranteed.

items.question: _____ Frühling kommt bald.
items.explanation: 'Der Frühling' — masculine. Nouns ending in '-ling' are masculine: der Frühling (spring), der Schmetterling (butterfly), der Lehrling (apprentice), der Flüchtling (refugee). Also, ALL seasons are masculine: der Frühling, der Sommer, der Herbst, der Winter. ALL months are masculine: der Januar, der Februar, etc. ALL days of the week are masculine: der Montag, der Dienstag, etc. Time-related nouns being masculine is a helpful pattern, though 'die Woche' (week) and 'die Stunde' (hour) are feminine exceptions. Calendar and weather vocabulary tends strongly masculine.

items.question: _____ Gebäude ist alt.
items.explanation: 'Das Gebäude' — neuter. The collective prefix 'Ge-' typically creates neuter nouns: das Gebäude (building), das Gebirge (mountain range), das Gemüse (vegetables), das Getränk (drink), das Gefühl (feeling), das Gespräch (conversation). 'Ge-' prefix nouns often describe collections, results, or abstract concepts derived from verbs: 'bauen' (build) → 'das Gebäude,' 'fühlen' (feel) → 'das Gefühl,' 'sprechen' (speak) → 'das Gespräch.' This prefix pattern is very reliable and covers many common German nouns. Exceptions are rare: der Gesang (singing), der Gedanke (thought).

items.question: _____ Freundschaft ist wichtig.
items.explanation: 'Die Freundschaft' — feminine. The suffix '-schaft' always produces feminine nouns: die Freundschaft (friendship), die Gesellschaft (society), die Wirtschaft (economy), die Wissenschaft (science), die Landschaft (landscape), die Mannschaft (team). '-Schaft' often transforms adjectives or nouns into abstract concepts: 'Freund' (friend) → 'Freundschaft' (friendship), 'bereit' (ready) → 'Bereitschaft' (readiness). This parallels English '-ship' (friendship) but German is more consistent — while English has both '-ship' and '-ness,' German '-schaft' is always feminine.

items.question: _____ Computer funktioniert nicht.
items.explanation: 'Der Computer' — masculine. English loanwords ending in '-er' (agent nouns) tend to be masculine in German: der Computer, der Drucker (printer), der Browser, der Server, der Manager, der Trainer. This follows an existing German pattern where '-er' agent nouns (person/thing that does something) are masculine: der Lehrer (teacher), der Fahrer (driver), der Arbeiter (worker). The gender assignment isn't random — German integrates borrowed words into existing morphological patterns. Technology vocabulary from English usually maintains this '-er = masculine' assignment.

items.question: _____ Museum ist interessant.
items.explanation: 'Das Museum' — neuter. Words ending in '-um' (from Latin) are neuter: das Museum, das Studium (studies), das Zentrum (center), das Datum (date), das Aquarium, das Stadium (stadium). These Latin neuter nouns kept their neuter gender when entering German. Their plurals often return to the Latin pattern: Museum→Museen, Studium→Studien, Zentrum→Zentren, Datum→Daten. This '-um' = neuter rule is 100% reliable for Latin-origin words. It's one of the easiest gender rules because these words are often internationally recognizable.

items.question: _____ Möglichkeit besteht.
items.explanation: 'Die Möglichkeit' — feminine. The suffix '-keit' (and its variant '-igkeit') always produces feminine nouns: die Möglichkeit (possibility), die Schwierigkeit (difficulty), die Geschwindigkeit (speed), die Fähigkeit (ability), die Abhängigkeit (dependency). '-Keit' typically attaches to adjectives ending in '-ig,' '-lich,' or '-bar': möglich→Möglichkeit, schwierig→Schwierigkeit, fähig→Fähigkeit. Together with '-heit,' these suffixes transform adjectives into abstract nouns. Knowing that '-heit' and '-keit' are ALWAYS feminine covers an enormous number of German abstract nouns.

items.question: _____ Hund bellt laut.
items.explanation: 'Der Hund' — masculine. Animal nouns in German have assigned grammatical genders that must be memorized: der Hund (dog), die Katze (cat), das Pferd (horse), der Vogel (bird), die Maus (mouse), der Fisch (fish). The gender doesn't follow animal sex — 'der Hund' is grammatically masculine regardless of whether the dog is male or female. For specific sex, German adds words: 'die Hündin' (female dog), 'der Rüde' (male dog). The '-in' suffix to feminize nouns is productive: der Hund→die Hündin, der Löwe→die Löwin, der Lehrer→die Lehrerin. Base animal nouns simply must be learned with their articles.

items.question: Das Schlaf + Zimmer = Das _____.
items.explanation: German famously creates compound nouns by joining words directly: 'Schlaf' (sleep) + 'Zimmer' (room) = 'Schlafzimmer' (bedroom). The LAST word determines the gender and plural: 'das Zimmer' → 'das Schlafzimmer.' This is a core principle: in German compounds, the last element is the 'head' — it carries gender, plural form, and the basic category. Everything before it modifies or specifies. No spaces, no hyphens (in most cases). English uses separate words ('bed room') or hyphens, but German writes them as one word. There is theoretically no limit to compound length in German!

items.question: Die Hand + der Schuh = Der _____.
items.explanation: 'Hand' + 'Schuh' (shoe) = 'Handschuh' (glove — literally 'hand-shoe'!). Gender follows the last element: 'der Schuh' → 'der Handschuh.' German compound nouns often reveal cultural thinking: a glove is a 'shoe for your hand,' a turtle is a 'shield-toad' (Schildkröte), a lighter is a 'fire-thing' (Feuerzeug). The first element typically stays in its base form (Hand, not Hände). These literal compound meanings can be charming or hilarious to English speakers, but they follow strict logic — one word specifies, the other categorizes.

items.question: Die Geburt + der Tag = Der _____.
items.explanation: 'Geburt' + 's' + 'Tag' = 'Geburtstag' (birthday). The '-s-' between elements is a 'Fugenlaut' (linking sound) — a connecting letter that makes compounds flow better phonetically. Common Fugenlaute: '-s-' (Geburtstag, Arbeitszimmer), '-n-' (Straßenbahn), '-er-' (Kindergarten), '-e-' (Hundehütte). There are NO simple rules for when to use which linking element — it's essentially convention. 'Geburtstag' needs '-s-' while 'Haustür' (front door) doesn't. This is one of the trickiest aspects of German compounds for learners, even advanced ones. Native speakers learn these by exposure.

items.question: Das Wasser + die Flasche = Die _____.
items.explanation: 'Wasser' + 'Flasche' (bottle) = 'Wasserflasche.' Gender from last element: 'die Flasche' → 'die Wasserflasche.' No linking element needed here — 'Wasser' ends in '-er,' which provides enough phonetic cushion before 'Flasche.' Notice the meaning logic: a Wasserflasche is a bottle FOR water. The first element can indicate purpose (Wasserflasche — for water), material (Holztisch — wooden table), location (Stadtpark — city park), or time (Morgensonne — morning sun). Understanding these semantic relationships helps you decode unfamiliar compounds.

items.question: Der Kühl + der Schrank = Der _____.
items.explanation: 'Kühl' (cool) + 'Schrank' (cabinet/closet) = 'Kühlschrank' (refrigerator — literally 'cool cabinet'). Here the first element is an adjective stem, not a noun. German compounds can combine: noun+noun (Haustür), adjective+noun (Kühlschrank), verb+noun (Schlafzimmer), and more. 'Der Schrank' → 'der Kühlschrank.' Other adjective+noun compounds: 'Hochhaus' (high-rise), 'Rotwein' (red wine), 'Schnellzug' (express train). The adjective always takes its base/stem form without endings — 'kühl,' not 'kühler' or 'kühles.' This is because it's part of a word, not a separate modifier.

items.question: Die Straße + die Bahn = Die _____.
items.explanation: 'Straße' + 'n' + 'Bahn' = 'Straßenbahn' (tram). The linking '-n-' is common after feminine nouns ending in '-e': Straße→Straßen-, Sonne→Sonnen- (Sonnenblume = sunflower), Blume→Blumen- (Blumenladen = flower shop). This '-n-' linking element mirrors the plural form of these nouns: 'die Straßen' (streets). But it's NOT about plurality — 'Straßenbahn' isn't 'streets-train,' it's 'street-train.' The linking sound evolved from historic genitive/plural forms but no longer carries that meaning. It's purely phonetic glue in modern German.

items.question: Das Kranken + das Haus = Das _____.
items.explanation: 'Kranken' (sick people) + 'Haus' (house) = 'Krankenhaus' (hospital — 'house of the sick'). Gender: 'das Haus' → 'das Krankenhaus.' The first element 'Kranken' is a nominalized adjective in its plural/weak form. German can use adjective-turned-nouns in compounds: 'der Kranke' (sick person) → 'Kranken-' in compounds. This construction reveals the original meaning beautifully: a hospital is literally a house for sick people. Similarly: 'Altenheim' (old people's home), from 'der Alte' (old person). These compound nouns preserve semantic transparency that English borrowings from Latin/Greek often hide.

items.question: Die Kinder + der Garten = Der _____.
items.explanation: 'Kinder' + 'Garten' (garden) = 'Kindergarten' — a word so good that English borrowed it! The linking element '-er-' appears because 'Kind' uses its plural form 'Kinder' as the first element. Gender: 'der Garten' → 'der Kindergarten.' Plural: 'die Kindergärten' (the umlaut on 'ä' applies to the last element, because that's the head). German gave English several compound nouns: 'Kindergarten,' 'Wanderlust,' 'Zeitgeist,' 'Angst,' 'Doppelgänger,' 'Rucksack.' Each reveals German's compound-building genius — packing rich concepts into single, self-explanatory words.

items.question: Die Arbeit + das Zimmer = Das _____.
items.explanation: 'Arbeit' + 's' + 'Zimmer' = 'Arbeitszimmer' (study/workroom). The Fugen-s after 'Arbeit' is typical for nouns ending in '-heit,' '-keit,' '-ung,' '-tion,' '-tät,' '-schaft,' '-ling,' and '-ment': Freiheitskampf (freedom fight), Geschwindigkeitsbegrenzung (speed limit), Zeitungsartikel (newspaper article). Notice: 'Zimmerarbeit' would mean 'room work' — the ORDER of elements in German compounds is crucial and reverses the meaning entirely. The modifier always comes FIRST. This is the opposite of Romance languages where modifiers typically follow.

items.question: Der Rat + das Haus = Das _____.
items.explanation: 'Rat' (council) + 'Haus' (house) = 'Rathaus' (town hall/city hall). No linking element needed — the combination flows naturally. Note: 'Hausrat' (household goods) exists too and means something completely different — proving that word ORDER determines meaning in German compounds. 'Rathaus' = house OF council (where the council meets). 'Hausrat' = goods OF a house (household items). This reversibility creates minimal pairs that German speakers navigate effortlessly. For learners, context always clarifies, but understanding the modifier-head structure prevents confusion.

items.question: Die Sonne + die Blume = Die _____.
items.explanation: 'Sonne' + 'n' + 'Blume' = 'Sonnenblume' (sunflower). The '-n-' link after 'Sonne' follows the standard pattern for feminine nouns ending in '-e.' Gender: 'die Blume' → 'die Sonnenblume.' German nature vocabulary is rich with descriptive compounds: 'Schneeglöckchen' (snowdrop — literally 'snow bell'), 'Vergissmeinnicht' (forget-me-not — literally 'forget me not'), 'Löwenzahn' (dandelion — literally 'lion's tooth'). These compounds are self-explanatory once you know the component words, making German vocabulary highly decodable compared to English's Latin/Greek borrowings.

items.question: Die Fahrt + der Karte = Die _____.
items.explanation: 'Fahr' (drive/travel) + 'Karte' (card/ticket) = 'Fahrkarte' (travel ticket). Here the verb stem 'fahr-' (from 'fahren') combines with a noun. The '-t' of 'Fahrt' (trip) drops because it's the verb stem being used, not the noun. Gender: 'die Karte' → 'die Fahrkarte.' Verb+noun compounds are very productive: 'Schreibtisch' (writing desk — schreib+Tisch), 'Wohnzimmer' (living room — wohn+Zimmer), 'Spielplatz' (playground — spiel+Platz). The verb always appears in its stem form (without endings), creating action-defined nouns.

items.question: Der Schutz + die Brille = Die _____.
items.explanation: 'Schutz' (protection) + 'Brille' (glasses) = 'Schutzbrille' (protective glasses/goggles). No linking element — 'Schutz' ends in a consonant cluster that flows directly into 'Brille.' Gender: 'die Brille' → 'die Schutzbrille.' The first element 'Schutz' indicates PURPOSE: glasses FOR protection. German regularly builds purpose compounds: 'Regenschirm' (rain umbrella), 'Sonnencreme' (sun cream), 'Sportschuhe' (sport shoes). Note: 'Brillenschutz' would mean 'protection FOR glasses' — again, order matters! A Schutzbrille protects you; Brillenschutz protects your glasses.

items.question: Das Geschwindigkeits + die Begrenzung = Die _____.
items.explanation: 'Geschwindigkeit' + 's' + 'Begrenzung' = 'Geschwindigkeitsbegrenzung' (speed limit) — 30 letters! This exemplifies German's ability to create infinitely long compounds. The Fugen-s after '-keit' is mandatory. Gender: 'die Begrenzung' → 'die Geschwindigkeitsbegrenzung.' Famous long compounds: 'Donaudampfschifffahrtsgesellschaft' (Danube steamship company — 39 letters). While these extreme examples are fun, everyday German uses 2-3 element compounds. The genius is that these words are self-decoding: speed+limit, no dictionary needed if you know the parts.

items.question: Der Haupt + der Bahnhof = Der _____.
items.explanation: 'Haupt' (main/chief) + 'Bahnhof' (train station) = 'Hauptbahnhof' (main train station). But 'Bahnhof' is itself a compound: 'Bahn' (track/railway) + 'Hof' (yard/courtyard) = 'track-yard.' So 'Hauptbahnhof' is a triple compound, abbreviated 'Hbf' in German. Gender: 'der Hof' → 'der Bahnhof' → 'der Hauptbahnhof.' Each city's central station is 'der Hauptbahnhof': München Hauptbahnhof, Berlin Hauptbahnhof. These recursive compounds — compounds of compounds — showcase German's systematic word-building power. Every German city sign at the station reads 'Hbf!'

items.question: Ich sehe den _____ im Park.
items.translation: (I see the boy in the park)
items.explanation: Welcome to Germany's trickiest noun class: weak nouns (schwache Nomen or n-Deklination). These masculine nouns add -n or -en in EVERY case except nominative singular. 'Der Junge' (the boy) becomes 'den Jungen' in accusative, 'dem Jungen' in dative, 'des Jungen' in genitive. Most weak nouns refer to living beings — people, animals, nationalities. The key signal is: if a masculine noun ends in -e and refers to a person/animal, it's almost certainly weak. This is one of German's most common error traps for learners because English never changes noun forms based on case.

items.question: Er gibt dem _____ das Buch.
items.explanation: 'Der Student' is a classic weak noun — it ends in -ent, which is one of the foreign-origin suffixes that always trigger n-Deklination. Other such suffixes include -ist (Journalist), -ant (Elefant), -at (Soldat), -oge (Biologe), -graf (Fotograf). These words came into German from Latin, Greek, or French, and they ALL follow the weak declension pattern. In dative: dem Studenten. The rule is consistent: if the masculine noun has a foreign-origin suffix like -ent, -ist, -ant, add -en in all cases except nominative singular.

items.question: Das ist das Auto des _____.
items.explanation: 'Der Kunde' (the customer) follows weak declension — genitive is 'des Kunden,' NOT 'des Kundes.' This is the crucial difference from strong nouns where genitive adds -s or -es (des Mannes). Weak nouns NEVER take -s in genitive. Instead, they add -n throughout: den Kunden (acc.), dem Kunden (dat.), des Kunden (gen.). The weak declension system exists because these nouns historically belonged to a different noun class in Old German. Remembering this pattern prevents one of the most common mistakes German learners make.

items.question: Wir helfen dem _____ bei der Arbeit.
items.translation: (We help the colleague with the work)
items.explanation: 'Der Kollege' (the colleague) is weak — it ends in -e and refers to a person. In dative after 'helfen' (which always takes dative), it becomes 'dem Kollegen.' A helpful mnemonic: almost every masculine noun ending in -e that denotes a living being takes n-Deklination. Think: der Junge, der Kunde, der Kollege, der Löwe (lion), der Affe (monkey), der Neffe (nephew). The pattern is remarkably consistent. Only a handful of exceptions exist, like 'der Käse' (cheese), which isn't alive and follows normal declension.

items.question: Der Journalist schreibt einen Artikel über den _____.
items.translation: (The journalist writes an article about the president)
items.explanation: 'Der Präsident' follows weak declension because of its Latin-origin suffix -ent. In accusative (after 'über' + acc. for direction/topic), it becomes 'den Präsidenten.' German absorbed many Latin and Greek nouns, and they consistently take n-Deklination. The pattern extends to titles and professions: der Assistent → den Assistenten, der Produzent → den Produzenten, der Patient → den Patienten. If you hear a masculine noun that sounds Latin or Greek and ends in a stressed suffix, it's almost certainly weak.

items.question: Sie spricht mit dem _____ über seine Reise.
items.translation: (She speaks with the Frenchman about his trip)
items.explanation: Nationality nouns ending in -e are weak: der Franzose → dem Franzosen. This applies to many nationalities: der Chinese → den Chinesen, der Grieche → den Griechen, der Russe → den Russen, der Türke → den Türken, der Pole → den Polen, der Däne → den Dänen. NOT all nationality nouns are weak — 'der Amerikaner' follows normal declension because it doesn't end in -e. The -e ending on nationality nouns is your reliable signal that n-Deklination applies. This is consistent across all four cases.

items.question: Der Löwe jagt den _____.
items.explanation: Animal nouns ending in -e are also weak: der Hase (hare) → den Hasen. Other weak animal nouns include: der Löwe (lion) → den Löwen, der Affe (monkey) → den Affen, der Rabe (raven) → den Raben, der Bulle (bull) → den Bullen. The pattern holds across the animal kingdom — if a masculine animal noun ends in -e, apply n-Deklination. Notice both subject and object in this sentence are weak nouns: 'der Löwe' stays nominative (subject), while 'den Hasen' takes accusative (direct object).

items.question: Das Herz des _____ schlägt schnell.
items.translation: (The heart of the athlete beats fast)
items.explanation: 'Der Athlet' becomes 'des Athleten' in genitive — NOT 'des Athletes.' Words ending in -et from Greek/Latin follow n-Deklination: der Athlet, der Poet, der Asket, der Prophet. This is part of the larger pattern where foreign-origin masculine nouns take weak declension. Remember: weak nouns NEVER add -s in genitive. If you catch yourself writing 'des Athletes,' that's the signal to switch to weak declension. The genitive -s ending is reserved for strong masculine and neuter nouns only.

items.question: Ich schreibe dem _____ einen Brief.
items.explanation: 'Der Nachbar' is a special weak noun — it doesn't end in -e, yet it still takes n-Deklination: den Nachbarn, dem Nachbarn, des Nachbarn. A small group of weak nouns lack the typical -e ending: der Nachbar, der Bauer (farmer), der Herr (gentleman/Mr.). 'Der Herr' is unique — it adds only -n in singular (den/dem/des Herrn) but -en in plural (die Herren). These exceptions must simply be memorized. Fortunately, there are only about a dozen such nouns, and they are among the most frequently used.

items.question: Sie kennt den _____ aus der Universität.
items.translation: (She knows the biologist from the university)
items.explanation: Nouns ending in -oge always take n-Deklination: der Biologe → den Biologen. Other -oge nouns: der Psychologe, der Soziologe, der Theologe, der Pädagoge (educator). These come from Greek '-logos' (study/science) and consistently follow weak declension. The same applies to -graf nouns: der Fotograf → den Fotografen, der Geograf → den Geografen. Learning these suffix patterns (-e, -ent, -ist, -ant, -at, -oge, -graf) gives you a reliable system for identifying weak nouns without memorizing each individual word.

items.question: Der Arzt untersucht den _____.
items.explanation: 'Der Patient' → 'den Patienten' in accusative. Medical and professional terms from Latin are reliably weak: der Patient, der Assistent, der Absolvent (graduate), der Dozent (lecturer), der Referent (speaker). These all end in -ent or -ent-like suffixes. In medical contexts, you'll encounter many weak nouns: der Patient, der Chirurg (surgeon — irregular weak noun), der Biologe, der Therapeut. Knowing that professional titles of Latin/Greek origin are weak helps you navigate formal and academic German with confidence.

items.question: Das Bild zeigt einen _____ mit seiner Frau.
items.translation: (The picture shows a prince with his wife)
items.explanation: 'Der Prinz' is a weak noun ending in -z, which seems unusual but follows the pattern of foreign-origin words (from French 'prince'). Accusative: einen Prinzen. Some weak nouns end in consonants but still have foreign origins: der Prinz → den Prinzen, der Mensch (human) → den Menschen, der Bär (bear) → den Bären. 'Der Mensch' is especially important — it's one of the most common German words and ALWAYS takes weak declension. Saying 'des Mensches' instead of 'des Menschen' is a telltale learner mistake.

items.question: Wir haben mit dem _____ gesprochen.
items.explanation: 'Der Experte' ends in -e and refers to a person → weak declension: dem Experten. Notice that this noun could also be 'der Expert' in some registers, but the -e form is standard. Many German nouns of profession have adopted the -e ending specifically to signal weak declension: der Experte, der Kollege, der Kunde, der Zeuge (witness), der Erbe (heir). The -e ending serves as a grammatical marker in German — it tells you how the noun behaves in cases, unlike English where profession nouns are invariable.

items.question: Er ist der Sohn des berühmten _____.
items.translation: (He is the son of the famous architect)
items.explanation: 'Der Architekt' uses n-Deklination due to its Greek-origin suffix -ekt (related to Greek 'architekton'). Genitive: des Architekten. This word belongs to the pattern where stressed final syllables of foreign origin trigger weak declension. Similarly: der Monarch → des Monarchen, der Patriarch → des Patriarchen, der Automat → des Automaten. Note that 'Automat' (vending machine) is not a person, showing that n-Deklination sometimes extends to non-living things when the word has a recognizable foreign suffix.

items.question: Die Kinder spielen mit dem _____.
items.explanation: 'Der Bär' (bear) is a weak noun — one of the few monosyllabic ones. Dative: dem Bären. Other short weak nouns: der Mensch → den Menschen, der Held (hero) → den Helden, der Fürst (prince/ruler) → den Fürsten, der Graf (count) → den Grafen. These must be memorized individually since they lack the usual surface signals (-e ending or foreign suffix). A practical tip: when you learn a new masculine noun, always check the dictionary notation. Dictionaries mark weak nouns with '(n)' or '(-en)' after the entry, showing the genitive form.

items.question: Er _____ gestern viel Wasser.
items.translation: (He drank a lot of water yesterday)
items.explanation: Strong verbs change their stem vowel in past tense — this is called 'Ablaut.' 'Trinken' (to drink) changes i→a in simple past: ich/er trank. English does this too (drink→drank), but German has FAR more strong verbs. The vowel change pattern for 'trinken' is i→a→u: trinken (present) → trank (simple past) → getrunken (past participle). These three forms are called the 'Stammformen' (principal parts) and must be memorized. Unlike weak verbs which add -te (kaufte, spielte), strong verbs NEVER use -te in past tense.

items.question: Sie _____ letzte Woche nach Berlin.
items.explanation: 'Fahren' (to drive/go) follows the strong pattern a→u→a: fahren → fuhr → gefahren. In simple past, the vowel changes from 'a' to 'u'. Notice that ich/er/sie forms have NO ending: ich fuhr, er fuhr, sie fuhr. Second person adds -st: du fuhrst. Plural adds -en: wir fuhren. This is different from weak verbs which always have an ending (ich kaufTE, er kaufTE). The zero ending in 1st/3rd person singular simple past is a hallmark of German strong verbs and helps you recognize them instantly.

items.question: Wir _____ den ganzen Film.
items.explanation: 'Sehen' (to see) changes e→a→e: sehen → sah → gesehen. For 'wir' (we), the simple past adds -en: wir sahen. This e→a→e pattern is shared by several common verbs: lesen → las → gelesen, geben → gab → gegeben, essen → aß → gegessen. Interestingly, the present participle keeps 'e' while the simple past shifts to 'a', then the past participle returns to 'e'. German groups strong verbs into 'Ablautreihen' (ablaut series) — learning which series a verb belongs to makes memorizing the forms much more systematic.

items.question: Das Kind _____ die ganze Nacht.
items.explanation: 'Schlafen' (to sleep) follows a→ie→a: schlafen → schlief → geschlafen. The 'ie' in the past tense is pronounced as a long 'ee' sound. Other verbs in this series: fallen → fiel, lassen → ließ, raten → riet, halten → hielt, laufen → lief. Notice that some verbs in this group also change consonants (lassen → ließ adds ß). The a→ie shift is one of the more dramatic vowel changes and can cause confusion because 'ie' looks like it belongs to a different verb entirely. Context and conjugation patterns help resolve this.

items.question: Er _____ mir gestern eine E-Mail.
items.explanation: 'Schreiben' (to write) changes ei→ie→ie: schreiben → schrieb → geschrieben. This is the 'ei→ie' ablaut series. Fellow verbs: bleiben → blieb, steigen → stieg, treiben → trieb, scheinen → schien, leihen → lieh. The 'ei' diphthong consistently shifts to long 'ie' in past forms. In English, 'write→wrote→written' shows a similar pattern of vowel change. German's system is more regular — once you identify the ablaut series, you can predict the past forms. The ei→ie series is one of the easiest to remember because the change is very audible.

items.question: Die Sonne _____ den ganzen Tag.
items.explanation: 'Scheinen' (to shine/seem) follows the same ei→ie series: scheinen → schien → geschienen. This verb has two meanings — 'to shine' (die Sonne scheint) and 'to seem/appear' (es scheint so). Both meanings use identical conjugation. In simple past: ich/er schien (no ending for 1st/3rd person singular, as always with strong verbs). Many strong verbs are among the most commonly used German words — their frequency is precisely WHY they remained irregular. Frequently used words resist regularization across all languages because speakers never 'forget' the irregular forms.

items.question: Sie _____ das Buch auf den Tisch.
items.translation: (She took the book onto the table)
items.explanation: 'Nehmen' (to take) undergoes dramatic changes: nehmen → nahm → genommen (e→a→o, plus consonant changes). This verb is highly irregular even among strong verbs: the 'h' disappears in past tense and the 'mm' doubles in the past participle. Present tense also shows stem change in 2nd/3rd person: du nimmst, er nimmt (e→i). This triple irregularity — stem vowel change in present, different vowel in past, yet another vowel plus consonant change in participle — makes 'nehmen' one of the most challenging German verbs. Practice all three forms as a unit.

items.question: Wir _____ gestern im Restaurant.
items.explanation: 'Essen' (to eat) follows the e→a→e pattern: essen → aß → gegessen. The 'ß' (Eszett) appears because after a long vowel or diphthong, German uses ß instead of ss. For 'wir,' add -en: wir aßen. Like 'nehmen,' 'essen' also shows vowel change in present tense: du isst, er isst (e→i). This present-tense stem change happens only in 2nd and 3rd person singular — a feature called 'Stammvokalwechsel.' Verbs with e→i or e→ie change in present AND have ablaut in past, making them doubly irregular but crucial to master.

items.question: Der Hund _____ aus dem Garten.
items.translation: (The dog ran out of the garden)
items.explanation: 'Laufen' (to run) follows the au→ie→au pattern: laufen → lief → gelaufen. The diphthong 'au' shifts to long 'ie' in simple past, then returns to 'au' in the past participle. Present tense also shows a change: du läufst, er läuft (au→äu in 2nd/3rd singular). 'Laufen' uses 'sein' as its auxiliary because it involves movement from A to B: er ist gelaufen. This is important — movement verbs (gehen, laufen, fahren, fliegen, kommen) always use 'sein' in the perfect tenses, not 'haben.' We'll cover this in detail in haben_vs_sein.

items.question: Er _____ die Tür hinter sich zu.
items.translation: (He pulled the door shut behind him)
items.explanation: 'Ziehen' (to pull/move) changes ie→o→o: ziehen → zog → gezogen. This verb is versatile — it means 'to pull' (er zog die Tür zu), 'to move/relocate' (sie zog nach München), and appears in compounds like 'einziehen' (to move in), 'ausziehen' (to move out/undress), 'anziehen' (to attract/dress). The ie→o vowel shift is shared by: fliegen → flog, biegen → bog, verlieren → verlor, frieren → fror. All these verbs share the pattern of a long 'ie' becoming short 'o.' This consistency within ablaut series is what makes German strong verbs learnable.

items.question: Die Kinder _____ im Fluss.
items.explanation: 'Schwimmen' (to swim) follows i→a→o: schwimmen → schwamm → geschwommen. For 'die Kinder' (plural), add -en: schwammen. This three-vowel pattern (each form has a different vowel) is shared by: beginnen → begann → begonnen, gewinnen → gewann → gewonnen, singen → sang → gesungen, finden → fand → gefunden. These are among the oldest Germanic strong verb patterns, preserved for over a thousand years. English retains some: swim→swam→swum, sing→sang→sung, begin→began→begun.

items.question: Sie _____ den ganzen Abend lang.
items.explanation: 'Singen' (to sing) follows i→a→u: singen → sang → gesungen. This pattern is almost identical to English: sing → sang → sung. The similarity is no coincidence — both languages inherited these patterns from Proto-Germanic, their common ancestor. Other i→a→u verbs: trinken → trank → getrunken, klingen → klang → geklungen, zwingen → zwang → gezwungen, springen → sprang → gesprungen. When you encounter a strong verb with 'i' in the infinitive, check if it follows this familiar pattern — your English intuition often leads you to the correct German form.

items.question: Er _____ plötzlich stehen.
items.explanation: 'Bleiben' (to stay/remain) follows ei→ie→ie: bleiben → blieb → geblieben. It uses 'sein' as auxiliary: er ist geblieben. The verb 'bleiben' is crucial in German — it's used for physical staying ('Ich bleibe zu Hause') and figurative permanence ('Das bleibt ein Problem'). Other ei→ie verbs: schreiben → schrieb, steigen → stieg, treiben → trieb, verzeihen → verzieh. Worth noting: 'bleiben stehen' means 'to stop' (literally 'to remain standing'). These verb+infinitive combinations add nuance that English often expresses with entirely different words.

items.question: Wir _____ gestern viel Neues.
items.translation: (We found out a lot of new things yesterday)
items.explanation: 'Erfahren' (to learn/experience) is a prefix verb based on 'fahren' and follows the same pattern a→u→a: erfahren → erfuhr → erfahren. For 'wir': erfuhren. Prefix verbs inherit their base verb's conjugation pattern — this is a golden rule. If you know that 'fahren → fuhr,' you automatically know: erfahren → erfuhr, abfahren → fuhr ab (separable!), mitfahren → fuhr mit. This principle works across ALL strong verbs: geben → gab means vergeben → vergab, aufgeben → gab auf. Master the base verbs and you unlock dozens of compounds.

items.question: Das Eis _____ in der Sonne.
items.explanation: 'Schmelzen' (to melt) follows e→o→o: schmelzen → schmolz → geschmolzen. This pattern also applies to: helfen → half → geholfen, werfen → warf → geworfen, sterben → starb → gestorben (though these show e→a→o). 'Schmelzen' can be both transitive (Die Sonne schmilzt das Eis — the sun melts the ice) and intransitive (Das Eis schmilzt — the ice melts). The intransitive form uses 'sein': Das Eis ist geschmolzen. Present tense shows e→i: du schmilzt, er schmilzt. Strong verbs with 'e' in the stem almost always show this present-tense shift.

items.question: Ich _____ nicht, dass er kommt.
items.translation: (I didn't know that he was coming)
items.explanation: Mixed verbs combine traits of BOTH strong and weak verbs. 'Wissen' (to know) changes its stem vowel like a strong verb (i→u) BUT adds the -te ending of a weak verb: wusste. Compare: strong 'trinken → trank' (vowel change, no -te) vs. weak 'kaufen → kaufte' (-te, no vowel change) vs. mixed 'wissen → wusste' (BOTH vowel change AND -te). There are only about 9 mixed verbs in German — they must be memorized individually. 'Wissen' is unique: its present tense conjugates like a modal verb: ich weiß, du weißt, er weiß.

items.question: Er _____ das Buch auf den Tisch.
items.translation: (He brought the book to the table)
items.explanation: 'Bringen' (to bring) is a classic mixed verb: bringen → brachte → gebracht. It changes the stem vowel (i→a) AND adds -te, plus the consonant changes from ng to ch. Compare with English: bring → brought — English does the same thing! Both languages inherited this mixed pattern from Proto-Germanic. The past participle 'gebracht' also shows the mix: it has the ge- prefix and -t ending of weak verbs, but with a changed stem. 'Bringen' appears in many compounds: mitbringen (to bring along), verbringen (to spend time), anbringen (to attach).

items.question: Sie _____ an ihre Kindheit.
items.explanation: 'Denken' (to think) is mixed: denken → dachte → gedacht. The vowel changes (e→a) and the consonant shifts (nk→ch), plus the weak -te ending. English 'think → thought' shows the same pattern — both languages preserve this ancient irregularity. 'Denken an' (to think of) uses accusative: Sie dachte an ihre Kindheit. Key compounds: nachdenken über (to reflect on), ausdenken (to think up), bedenken (to consider), umdenken (to rethink). The preposition choice matters: 'denken an' (think of/remember) vs. 'nachdenken über' (ponder/reflect upon).

items.question: Wir _____ gestern unsere Freunde.
