It conveys inevitability without frustration, more philosophical than emotional. Other uses of 'nun': as a conversation opener 'Nun, ...' (Well,...), or temporal 'nun' (now). As a particle, it exclusively signals resigned acceptance of unchangeable circumstances.

items.question: Kannst du _____ leiser sein?
items.translation: (Could you be a bit quieter, please?)
items.explanation: 'Mal' in polite requests softens what could otherwise be a sharp demand. 'Kannst du mal leiser sein?' = 'Could you be a bit quieter?' — much friendlier than 'Sei leiser!' With 'können + mal,' you create natural, everyday requests: 'Kannst du mal aufmachen?' (Can you open up?), 'Könnten Sie mir mal helfen?' (Could you help me for a moment?). 'Mal' combined with 'könnten' (could) creates the politest request form in spoken German, softer than any textbook formulation. It implies the action is small and brief, reducing the perceived burden on the listener. Essential for sounding polite without being stiff.

items.question: Du hast _____ recht gehabt.
items.translation: (You were right after all / You were right, as it turns out.)
items.explanation: 'Doch' as a particle in statements expresses that the speaker now realizes something was true despite earlier doubt. 'Du hast doch recht gehabt' = 'You were right after all (I see that now).' This acknowledging 'doch' admits a previous wrong assumption: 'Es war doch nicht so schlimm' (It wasn't so bad after all), 'Er ist doch gekommen!' (He came after all!). It creates a contrast between expectation and reality. This is yet another face of 'doch' — making it arguably the most multifunctional word in German: contradiction, expectation, confirmation, and now retrospective acknowledgment. Each use feels subtly different to native ears.

items.question: Wo ist er _____ geblieben?
items.explanation: 'Nur' in questions (especially with 'wo/was/wie') expresses wondering or mild frustration — 'where on earth...?' 'Wo ist er nur geblieben?' = 'Where has he gotten to? / Where on earth is he?' This questioning 'nur' signals the speaker is puzzled or impatient: 'Was hat er nur gemeint?' (What could he have meant?), 'Wie konnte das nur passieren?' (How could that possibly happen?). It's different from 'nur' meaning 'only' and from 'nur' in imperatives (reassuring/warning). German modal particles often have three or four distinct functions depending on sentence type — mastering them is the final frontier of German fluency.

items.question: _____, ich wollte fragen, ob du morgen Zeit hast.
items.translation: (By the way / Say, I wanted to ask if you have time tomorrow.)
items.explanation: 'Übrigens' (by the way) is one of the most common discourse markers for introducing a new topic or side thought into conversation. It signals: 'This isn't directly related to what we were discussing, but...' Unlike English 'by the way' which can sound casual, 'übrigens' works in both formal and informal German. Placement is flexible: 'Übrigens, ich...' (sentence-initial) or 'Ich wollte übrigens fragen...' (mid-sentence). Discourse markers like these don't change meaning but organize conversation flow — they're the traffic signals of spoken German, guiding listeners through topic changes.

items.question: _____, das Wetter ist heute wirklich schön.
items.translation: (Actually / Anyway, the weather is really nice today.)
items.explanation: 'Jedenfalls' (anyway, in any case, at any rate) redirects conversation back to a main point or steers away from a tangent. 'Jedenfalls, das Wetter ist schön' = 'Anyway, the weather is nice.' It can also mean 'at least': 'Ich war jedenfalls pünktlich' (At least I was on time). This marker is essential for natural conversation management — it says 'let's get back on track' without being rude. Similar markers: 'auf jeden Fall' (definitely/in any case), 'wie auch immer' (however that may be/anyway). These organizational words make the difference between choppy and fluid German conversation.

items.question: Er kommt nicht. Er ist _____ krank.
items.translation: (He's not coming. He's sick, you see.)
items.explanation: 'Nämlich' is a unique German discourse marker meaning 'you see / namely / the thing is' — it provides the reason or explanation for what was just said. 'Er ist nämlich krank' = 'He's sick, you see.' IMPORTANT: 'nämlich' NEVER starts a sentence — it always comes after the verb in position 3 or later. This is a common mistake for English speakers who want to start with 'Namely,...' Wrong: 'Nämlich ist er krank.' Correct: 'Er ist nämlich krank.' It's an adverb that explains the previous statement: 'Ich kann nicht kommen. Ich habe nämlich einen Termin' (I can't come. I have an appointment, you see).

items.question: _____ habe ich eine andere Meinung.
items.explanation: 'Allerdings' serves as a discourse marker meaning 'however/though/admittedly' — it introduces a qualification or contrast to what was previously said. 'Allerdings habe ich eine andere Meinung' = 'However, I have a different opinion.' It's softer than 'jedoch' (however) and more conversational. 'Allerdings' can also express emphatic agreement: 'War das schwer?' — 'Allerdings!' (Was that hard? — It sure was!). This dual function makes it versatile: contrastive ('however') or confirmative ('indeed'). In first position, it triggers inversion (verb second): 'Allerdings habe ich...' This is standard V2 word order for German main clauses.

items.question: Ich mag Kaffee. _____ trinke ich auch gern Tee.
items.translation: (I like coffee. Besides, I also like drinking tea.)
items.explanation: 'Außerdem' (besides/moreover/in addition) adds information to what was already said — it's additive, building on the previous point. 'Außerdem trinke ich auch Tee' = 'Besides, I also drink tea.' As an adverb in position 1, it triggers verb-second order. 'Außerdem' is more common in spoken German than 'darüber hinaus' (furthermore, which is more formal/written). Other additive markers: 'zudem' (in addition to that), 'dazu' (on top of that), 'obendrein' (on top of everything). These connectors build arguments and lists naturally: 'Es ist billig, außerdem ist die Qualität gut' (It's cheap, plus the quality is good).

items.question: _____, was ich sagen wollte: Wir treffen uns um 8.
items.translation: (Anyway / So, what I wanted to say: We're meeting at 8.)
items.explanation: 'Also' is perhaps the most ubiquitous German discourse marker — but be careful: it does NOT mean 'also' in English! It means 'so/well/right then.' 'Also, was ich sagen wollte...' = 'So, what I wanted to say...' Germans use 'also' to: start explanations ('Also, es ist so:' = 'So, here's the thing:'), summarize ('Also, wir machen das so' = 'Right, we'll do it this way'), fill pauses ('Also...ähm...' = 'Well...um...'). It's a conversation organizer, not an addition marker. The English equivalent of 'also' (= too/as well) is 'auch' in German. This false friend trips up many English speakers.

items.question: Es war schwierig. _____ haben wir es geschafft.
items.translation: (It was difficult. Nevertheless, we managed it.)
items.explanation: 'Trotzdem' (nevertheless/despite that/still) is a concessive connector — it acknowledges the previous point but presents a contrasting outcome. 'Es war schwierig. Trotzdem haben wir es geschafft.' = 'It was difficult. Nevertheless, we managed.' As a connector adverb in position 1, it triggers inversion. 'Trotzdem' is the spoken equivalent of the more formal 'dennoch' or 'nichtsdestotrotz' (nevertheless). Similar markers: 'immerhin' (at least/after all), 'gleichwohl' (nonetheless — very formal). These concessive markers are essential for balanced argumentation: acknowledging challenges while presenting positive outcomes. It's how mature, nuanced German sounds.

items.question: _____ gesagt bin ich nicht sicher.
items.explanation: 'Ehrlich gesagt' (honestly speaking) is a common discourse marker that prefaces an honest or potentially uncomfortable opinion. It signals sincerity and transparency: 'Ehrlich gesagt bin ich nicht sicher' = 'To be honest, I'm not sure.' Similar framing markers: 'offen gesagt' (frankly speaking), 'genau genommen' (strictly speaking), 'im Grunde' (basically/fundamentally), 'unter uns gesagt' (between us). These markers manage the social dynamics of conversation — they prepare the listener for what's coming and frame how it should be received. They're the verbal equivalent of raising your hand before speaking.

items.question: _____ muss man auch die andere Seite sehen.
items.translation: (On the other hand, one must also see the other side.)
items.explanation: 'Andererseits' (on the other hand) presents the opposing viewpoint in a balanced argument. It typically pairs with 'einerseits' (on the one hand): 'Einerseits ist es teuer, andererseits ist die Qualität gut' (On one hand it's expensive, on the other the quality is good). Used alone, 'andererseits' signals a shift to a contrasting perspective. This pair (einerseits...andererseits) is fundamental to German academic writing and structured argumentation. Both trigger verb-second order in position 1. Less formal alternatives: 'auf der anderen Seite' (on the other side). These balanced-argument markers show sophisticated thinking in German.

items.question: Wir müssen sparen. _____ sollten wir weniger ausgeben.
items.translation: (We need to save. Therefore, we should spend less.)
items.explanation: 'Deshalb' (therefore/that's why) introduces a consequence or conclusion from what was just said — it's causal-conclusive. 'Wir müssen sparen. Deshalb sollten wir weniger ausgeben.' Similar markers in this group: 'deswegen' (because of that — slightly more colloquial), 'daher' (therefore — slightly more formal), 'darum' (that's why), 'folglich' (consequently — formal/written). All take position 1 with verb inversion. The choice between them is mostly register: 'deshalb/deswegen' for everyday speech, 'daher/folglich' for formal writing. These causal connectors are the backbone of German argumentation and essential for clear, logical German expression.

items.question: Das ist, _____, eine schwierige Situation.
items.translation: (That is, so to speak, a difficult situation.)
items.explanation: 'Sozusagen' (so to speak) hedges or qualifies a statement, indicating the speaker is using an expression figuratively or approximately. 'Das ist sozusagen unmöglich' = 'That's impossible, so to speak.' It sits naturally in mid-sentence position, often set off by commas. Similar hedging markers: 'gewissermaßen' (in a way/as it were), 'quasi' (quasi/sort of — very colloquial), 'praktisch' (practically/virtually). These markers soften absolute statements and show linguistic sophistication — the speaker acknowledges that language is approximate. They're especially common in academic and intellectual German discourse.

items.question: _____, ich muss jetzt los.
items.translation: (Alright then / Well, I have to go now.)
items.explanation: 'Gut' as a discourse marker signals a conversation transition or conclusion — it's not evaluating anything as 'good' but rather saying 'alright/okay/right then.' 'Gut, ich muss jetzt los' = 'Right, I need to head off now.' Similar closing/transitioning markers: 'na gut' (alright then), 'so' (right/well), 'na' (well — very colloquial), 'tja' (well... — with resignation). These markers manage conversation endings gracefully: 'So, das war's' (Right, that was it), 'Na gut, bis morgen!' (Alright then, see you tomorrow!). They prevent conversations from ending abruptly, which matters in German social culture.

items.question: Das Projekt war erfolgreich. _____ war es nicht einfach.
items.translation: (The project was successful. Admittedly, it wasn't easy.)
items.explanation: 'Zugegeben' (admittedly/granted) is a concessive discourse marker that acknowledges a point before presenting a contrasting view. 'Zugegeben, es war nicht einfach' = 'Admittedly, it wasn't easy (but we did it).' It's a past participle used as a discourse marker — literally 'given/admitted.' This transparent, honest acknowledging makes your argument stronger, not weaker. Similar markers: 'zugegebenermaßen' (admittedly — more formal), 'eingestandenermaßen' (confessedly). These concessive openers build trust with the listener by showing you've considered counterarguments. Essential for persuasive speaking and writing in German.

items.question: _____ ist das Problem nicht so groß.
items.translation: (Actually / When you look closely, the problem isn't so big.)
items.explanation: 'Eigentlich' (actually/really) is a versatile discourse marker that contrasts appearance with reality or expectation with result. 'Eigentlich ist das Problem nicht so groß' = 'Actually, the problem isn't so big.' It can express: unexpected truth ('Eigentlich mag ich das' = Actually, I like it), polite disagreement ('Eigentlich stimmt das nicht' = Actually, that's not right), or reluctance ('Eigentlich wollte ich nicht...' = Actually, I didn't want to...). 'Eigentlich' is distinctly different from 'wirklich' (really/truly): 'eigentlich' suggests contrast with expectation, while 'wirklich' simply emphasizes truth.

items.question: _____ ist es besser, früh aufzustehen.
items.translation: (In general / Generally, it's better to get up early.)
items.explanation: 'Generell' (generally/in general) frames a statement as a broad principle rather than a specific claim. 'Generell ist es besser, früh aufzustehen' = 'Generally, it's better to get up early.' This generalizing marker hedges against exceptions — the speaker acknowledges that this doesn't apply to every case. Similar markers: 'im Allgemeinen' (in general), 'grundsätzlich' (fundamentally — but can imply 'in principle'), 'normalerweise' (normally), 'in der Regel' (as a rule). These markers are essential for academic writing and balanced arguments in German, preventing overgeneralization while still making clear points.

items.question: Ich gehe spazieren, _____ es regnet.
items.translation: (I'm going for a walk although it's raining.)
items.explanation: 'Obwohl' (although/even though) is a concessive subordinating conjunction — it introduces a subordinate clause where the verb goes to the end. 'Ich gehe spazieren, obwohl es regnet' (I'm going walking although it rains). The verb 'regnet' moves to final position after 'obwohl.' This is the key grammatical difference between 'obwohl' (subordinating — verb last) and 'trotzdem' (adverbial — verb second): 'Obwohl es regnet, gehe ich spazieren' vs. 'Es regnet. Trotzdem gehe ich spazieren.' Both express concession, but the clause structure differs fundamentally. Learners must master this verb-position distinction.

items.question: Er lernt Deutsch, _____ er in Deutschland arbeiten will.
items.translation: (He's learning German because he wants to work in Germany.)
items.explanation: 'Weil' (because) is the most common causal conjunction in German. As a subordinating conjunction, it sends the verb to the end: 'weil er arbeiten will' (because he wants to work). In formal/written German, 'weil' always takes verb-final order. However, in modern spoken German, you'll increasingly hear 'weil' with verb-second order: 'weil er will arbeiten' — this is technically non-standard but extremely common in speech. The formal alternative is 'da' (since/as), which sounds more literary: 'Da er in Deutschland arbeiten will, lernt er Deutsch.' Both 'weil' and 'da' are causal, but 'da' often introduces known reasons.

items.question: Ich lerne fleißig, _____ ich die Prüfung bestehe.
items.translation: (I'm studying hard so that I pass the exam.)
items.explanation: 'Damit' (so that/in order to) expresses purpose — why someone does something. 'Ich lerne, damit ich bestehe' = 'I study so that I pass.' As a subordinating conjunction, verb goes to end. IMPORTANT distinction: 'damit' vs. 'um...zu': When both clauses have the SAME subject, use 'um...zu + infinitive': 'Ich lerne, um die Prüfung zu bestehen' (same person studying and passing). When subjects DIFFER, you MUST use 'damit': 'Ich erkläre es, damit du es verstehst' (I explain so that YOU understand). This same-subject/different-subject rule is a classic exam question in German grammar courses.

items.question: _____ du möchtest, können wir ins Kino gehen.
items.translation: (If you'd like, we can go to the cinema.)
items.explanation: 'Falls' (if/in case) introduces a conditional clause with an element of uncertainty — something that might or might not happen. 'Falls du möchtest, können wir gehen' = 'If you'd like, we can go.' 'Falls' is more specific than 'wenn' (if/when): 'falls' implies the condition is genuinely uncertain, while 'wenn' can also mean 'when/whenever.' Compare: 'Wenn es regnet' (When/If it rains — could be either) vs. 'Falls es regnet' (In case it rains — leaning toward unlikely). For truly hypothetical conditions, 'falls' is clearer than 'wenn.' Both are subordinating conjunctions with verb-final order in their clause.

items.question: Er spielte Gitarre, _____ sie sang.
items.explanation: 'Während' (while/whereas) has two functions: temporal (simultaneous actions) and adversative (contrast). Here it's temporal: 'while she sang.' As a subordinating conjunction, verb goes to end: 'während sie sang.' Temporal: 'Während ich kochte, las er Zeitung' (While I cooked, he read the paper) — simultaneous events. Adversative: 'Während er fleißig ist, ist sie faul' (Whereas he is diligent, she is lazy) — contrast. Context usually makes the meaning clear. 'Während' as a conjunction is different from 'während' as a preposition (+ genitive): 'während des Sommers' (during the summer). Same word, different grammar.

items.question: _____ er krank war, ging er zur Arbeit.
items.translation: (Although he was sick, he went to work.)
items.explanation: 'Obwohl' at the start of a sentence creates a concessive subordinate clause followed by the main clause with inverted word order. 'Obwohl er krank war, ging er zur Arbeit.' Structure breakdown: subordinate clause (verb last: 'war') + comma + main clause (verb first after comma: 'ging'). This pattern — subordinate clause first, then main clause with inversion — is fundamental to complex German sentence construction. It works the same with all subordinating conjunctions: 'Weil es regnete, blieben wir zu Hause.' The verb in the main clause must immediately follow the comma when the subordinate clause comes first.

items.question: Ich warte, _____ du fertig bist.
items.explanation: 'Bis' (until) defines a temporal endpoint — the action continues up to a certain point. 'Ich warte, bis du fertig bist' = 'I'll wait until you're done.' As a subordinating conjunction, verb goes to end. 'Bis' can also be a preposition: 'bis morgen' (until tomorrow), 'bis 8 Uhr' (until 8 o'clock). The conjunction/preposition distinction matters: 'bis' + clause (subordinating conjunction, verb last) vs. 'bis' + noun/time (preposition, no verb change). Related temporal conjunctions: 'seit/seitdem' (since — ongoing from past), 'bevor' (before), 'nachdem' (after). These temporal connectors are the scaffolding of narrative German.

items.question: _____ ich nach Hause kam, war es schon dunkel.
items.translation: (When I came home, it was already dark.)
items.explanation: 'Als' vs. 'wenn' is one of the trickiest distinctions in German. 'Als' = 'when' for SINGLE events in the PAST. 'Als ich nach Hause kam' = 'When I came home (that one time).' Use 'wenn' for: repeated past events ('Wenn ich ankam, freute er sich immer' = Whenever I arrived, he was always happy), present/future events ('Wenn ich ankomme, rufe ich an' = When I arrive, I'll call). Rule of thumb: single past event → als; everything else → wenn. Both are subordinating conjunctions (verb last). This als/wenn distinction doesn't exist in English, making it one of the most common errors for English-speaking German learners.

items.question: Ich dusche mich, _____ ich frühstücke.
items.explanation: 'Bevor' (before) establishes temporal sequence — what happens first. 'Ich dusche mich, bevor ich frühstücke' = 'I shower before I have breakfast.' Both clauses can use the same tense in German (unlike 'nachdem,' which requires tense shifting). 'Bevor' = subordinating conjunction (verb last in its clause). Synonym: 'ehe' (before — slightly more literary/formal). Common usage: 'Bevor du gehst, vergiss nicht...' (Before you go, don't forget...). 'Bevor' contrasts with 'nachdem' (after): 'Nachdem ich geduscht hatte, frühstückte ich' (After I had showered, I had breakfast). Note: 'nachdem' requires past perfect in its clause.

items.question: _____ ich gegessen hatte, ging ich spazieren.
items.translation: (After I had eaten, I went for a walk.)
items.explanation: 'Nachdem' (after) requires a specific tense pattern: the 'nachdem' clause must use a tense ONE STEP FURTHER in the past than the main clause. Past perfect + simple past: 'Nachdem ich gegessen hatte (Plusquamperfekt), ging ich spazieren (Präteritum).' For present contexts: 'Nachdem ich gegessen habe (Perfekt), gehe ich spazieren (Präsens).' This tense-shifting rule is unique to 'nachdem' — it logically marks that one event was completed before the other began. This is a frequent exam topic and a hallmark of grammatically precise German. Other temporal conjunctions (bevor, während) don't require this tense shift.

items.question: Ich gehe nicht raus, _____ es aufhört zu regnen.
items.translation: (I'm not going outside unless it stops raining.)
items.explanation: 'Es sei denn' (unless) is an important German exception clause — it introduces the one condition under which the previous statement wouldn't apply. 'Ich gehe nicht raus, es sei denn, es hört auf zu regnen' = 'I'm not going out unless it stops raining.' Grammatically, 'es sei denn' uses Konjunktiv I ('sei') in a fixed expression. Despite the subjunctive form, it's used in factual contexts. Alternative: 'außer wenn' (except if) or 'sofern nicht' (provided that not). 'Es sei denn' is common in both spoken and written German and sounds neither too formal nor too casual — it's right in the register sweet spot.

items.question: Je mehr ich lerne, _____ besser verstehe ich.
items.translation: (The more I learn, the better I understand.)
items.explanation: 'Je...desto/umso' (the more...the more) is a correlative construction expressing proportional relationship. 'Je mehr ich lerne, desto besser verstehe ich' = 'The more I study, the better I understand.' Structure: 'je + comparative + verb-last' (subordinate), then 'desto/umso + comparative + verb-second' (main). 'Desto' and 'umso' are interchangeable: 'Je älter, desto/umso weiser.' This construction is powerful for expressing cause-and-effect relationships: 'Je früher du kommst, desto besser' (The earlier you come, the better). Note the verb positions: last in the 'je' clause, second in the 'desto' clause.

items.question: Er ging zur Arbeit, _____ er Fieber hatte.
items.translation: (He went to work even though he had a fever.)
items.explanation: 'Obgleich' (although/even though) is a more formal synonym of 'obwohl' — both are concessive subordinating conjunctions with verb-last order. 'Obgleich er Fieber hatte, ging er zur Arbeit.' The family of concessive conjunctions from casual to formal: 'obwohl' (standard/most common) > 'obgleich' (formal) > 'obschon' (literary/archaic) > 'wenngleich' (very formal/literary). All follow the same grammar: subordinating, verb at end. In everyday speech, stick with 'obwohl.' In academic writing, 'obgleich' or 'wenngleich' show sophistication. Knowing these registers is key to adapting your German to different contexts.

items.question: _____ du Hilfe brauchst, ruf mich an.
items.translation: (In case / If you need help, call me.)
items.explanation: 'Solltest du...' (Should you...) is an elegant conditional construction using inverted Konjunktiv II of 'sollen' — it replaces 'wenn/falls' for a more formal, polished tone. 'Solltest du Hilfe brauchen, ruf mich an' = 'Should you need help, call me.' This construction mirrors English 'Should you need' and is considered elegant in both languages. The structure is: 'Sollte + subject + infinitive' (no 'wenn' needed). Other examples: 'Sollte es regnen, bleiben wir drinnen' (Should it rain, we'll stay inside). This is more formal than 'Wenn es regnet' and shows grammatical sophistication. Common in business correspondence and formal communication.

items.question: Er spricht Deutsch, _____ er Englisch spricht.
items.translation: (He speaks German as well as he speaks English.)
items.explanation: 'So...wie' (as...as) creates equal comparisons — fundamental to German comparison structures. 'Er spricht Deutsch so gut, wie er Englisch spricht' = 'He speaks German as well as he speaks English.' Key distinction: 'so...wie' for EQUAL comparison (as...as) vs. 'comparative + als' for UNEQUAL comparison (more...than). 'So groß wie' (as tall as) vs. 'größer als' (taller than). Common error: using 'als' for equal comparisons ('so groß als' — WRONG). This wie/als distinction is non-negotiable in correct German: 'wie' for equality, 'als' for inequality. No exceptions. It's one of the most tested grammar points in German language certification exams.

items.question: _____ bin ich, der das gesagt hat.
items.translation: (I am the one who said that.)
items.explanation: German uses fronting (Topikalisierung) to emphasize elements by moving them to position 1. 'ICH bin es, der das gesagt hat' = 'I am the one who said that.' In German, almost any sentence element can be moved to position 1 for emphasis — the verb always stays in position 2 (V2 rule). This flexibility doesn't exist in English. Compare neutral: 'Ich habe das gestern gesagt' vs. emphasized: 'GESTERN habe ich das gesagt' (YESTERDAY I said that). Whatever sits in position 1 receives special emphasis or serves as the topic. This V2 with flexible position 1 is one of German's most powerful expressive tools.

items.question: _____ dieses Buch habe ich gelesen, nicht jenes.
items.translation: (THIS book I have read, not that one.)
items.explanation: Object fronting for emphasis: moving the accusative object to position 1. 'Dieses Buch habe ich gelesen' places 'dieses Buch' in focus — contrasting it with 'jenes' (that one). Normal order would be: 'Ich habe dieses Buch gelesen.' By fronting the object, the speaker emphasizes WHAT was read, not WHO read it. This contrastive fronting is very common: 'DEN Film mag ich' (THAT film I like), 'DIESEN Fehler mache ich nie wieder' (THIS mistake I'll never make again). The case marking (accusative 'dieses/den') ensures there's no ambiguity about subject vs. object, even with unusual word order.

items.question: Was mich ärgert, _____ seine Arroganz.
items.explanation: 'Was...ist' creates a pseudo-cleft sentence (Sperrsatz) — a topic-comment structure that highlights information. 'Was mich ärgert, ist seine Arroganz' = 'What annoys me is his arrogance.' This puts 'seine Arroganz' into sharp focus as new, important information. The 'was' clause creates suspense, making the listener wait for the key point. Similar patterns: 'Was ich brauche, ist Ruhe' (What I need is quiet), 'Was zählt, ist die Qualität' (What counts is quality). This construction works like English 'What...is...' and is equally powerful in both languages for rhetorical emphasis, especially in speeches, presentations, and persuasive writing.

items.question: Ich habe das _____ getan, nicht er!
items.explanation: 'Selbst' (or colloquial 'selber') adds emphatic reflexive meaning: 'I MYSELF did that.' 'Ich selbst habe das getan' emphasizes personal involvement. Placement matters: 'Ich habe das selbst gemacht' (I did it myself — emphasis on doing it personally) vs. 'Selbst ich habe das verstanden' (Even I understood that — 'selbst' meaning 'even'). Two distinct meanings: after noun/pronoun = 'myself/yourself' (reflexive emphasis); before noun/pronoun = 'even' (inclusive emphasis). 'Selber' is synonymous with reflexive 'selbst' but considered more colloquial: 'Mach's doch selber!' (Do it yourself then!). Both forms are essential for emphatic German.

items.question: Es ist der Lehrer, _____ uns das erklärt hat.
items.translation: (It is the teacher who explained that to us.)
items.explanation: 'Es ist...der/die/das...' creates a German cleft sentence (Spaltsatz), directly equivalent to English 'It is...who/that...' 'Es ist der Lehrer, der uns das erklärt hat' = 'It is the teacher who explained it to us.' This construction isolates one element for maximum emphasis. The relative pronoun (der/die/das) must agree in gender and number with the focused noun. Examples: 'Es war Maria, die angerufen hat' (It was Maria who called), 'Es sind die Kosten, die mich stören' (It's the costs that bother me). While less common in German than in English, Spaltsätze are powerful rhetorical devices for formal contexts and arguments.

items.question: Nie _____ ich das vergessen!
items.explanation: Negative adverbs in position 1 create dramatic emphasis through inversion. 'Nie werde ich das vergessen!' (Never will I forget that!) sounds more emphatic than 'Ich werde das nie vergessen.' This works with various negative/restrictive adverbs: 'Niemals hätte ich das gedacht' (Never would I have thought that), 'Selten habe ich so gelacht' (Rarely have I laughed so much), 'Kaum war er weg, klingelte das Telefon' (Scarcely was he gone when the phone rang). This construction adds dramatic flair and is common in literature, speeches, and emotional statements. The inversion is natural in German — it follows regular V2 rules.

items.question: Schön _____ es hier!
items.translation: (It IS beautiful here! — How beautiful it is!)
items.explanation: Adjective fronting creates exclamatory emphasis. 'Schön ist es hier!' moves the predicate adjective to position 1, making it an exclamation rather than a neutral statement (neutral: 'Es ist schön hier'). This pattern works with many adjectives: 'Toll war das!' (That was great!), 'Interessant ist das!' (That's interesting!), 'Wichtig ist dabei Folgendes...' (What's important here is the following...). In spoken German, intonation reinforces the emphasis. In written German, the unusual position alone creates emphasis. This is a quintessentially German way to express strong feelings — it leverages the language's flexible word order.

items.question: Gerade _____ habe ich an dich gedacht!
items.translation: (Just now / At this very moment I was thinking of you!)
items.explanation: 'Gerade eben' (just now/at this very moment) is an intensive temporal expression that creates emphasis on immediacy. 'Gerade eben habe ich an dich gedacht!' = 'I was JUST NOW thinking of you!' 'Gerade' alone means 'just/right now,' and 'eben' intensifies it to 'at this very moment.' Other emphatic temporal phrases: 'ausgerechnet heute' (of all days, today), 'genau in diesem Moment' (at exactly this moment), 'erst gestern' (only/just yesterday). These time-emphasis constructions add drama and coincidence to storytelling: 'Ausgerechnet dann rief er an!' (Of all times, THAT's when he called!). They make narratives vivid and engaging.

items.question: Nicht ich, _____ er hat den Fehler gemacht.
items.translation: (Not I, but HE made the mistake.)
items.explanation: 'Nicht...sondern' (not...but rather) is the German contrastive construction for correcting a wrong assumption. 'Nicht ich, sondern er' = 'Not me, but rather HIM.' This is fundamentally different from 'aber' (but): 'sondern' corrects and replaces, while 'aber' simply contrasts. Rule: use 'sondern' only after negation (nicht/kein) when the second part REPLACES the first: 'Er ist nicht dumm, sondern faul' (He's not stupid, but rather lazy). Use 'aber' for additive contrast: 'Er ist klug, aber faul' (He's smart, but lazy). This nicht/sondern pair is one of the most effective emphasis tools in German argumentation.

items.question: Erst wenn du es _____, wirst du es verstehen.
items.translation: (Only when you experience it will you understand it.)
items.explanation: 'Erst wenn...' (only when/not until) creates emphasis on a prerequisite condition. 'Erst wenn du es erlebst, wirst du es verstehen' = 'Only when you experience it will you understand.' 'Erst' adds exclusivity to 'wenn': without 'erst,' it's a simple condition; with 'erst,' it means this is the ONLY way. Similar restrictive emphatic phrases: 'nur wenn' (only if), 'erst dann' (only then), 'erst nachdem' (only after). These structures are powerful in persuasive contexts: 'Erst wenn wir zusammenarbeiten, können wir gewinnen' (Only by working together can we win). The 'erst' elevates a condition from possible to essential.

items.question: Was ich wirklich _____, ist deine Ehrlichkeit.
items.translation: (What I really appreciate is your honesty.)
items.explanation: The 'Was ich...ist...' pseudo-cleft sentence puts maximum focus on the final element. 'Was ich wirklich schätze, ist deine Ehrlichkeit' builds suspense through the relative clause before revealing the key information: 'your honesty.' Adding 'wirklich' (really) intensifies the emphasis further. This pattern is extremely versatile: 'Was mir gefällt, ist die Atmosphäre' (What I like is the atmosphere), 'Was fehlt, ist Geld' (What's missing is money). For plural subjects: 'Was mich stören, sind die Preise' (What bothers me are the prices — note 'sind' agrees with 'Preise'). This construction is equally powerful in conversation and formal writing.

items.question: _____ er das gesagt hat, glaube ich einfach nicht.
items.translation: (THAT he said that, I simply don't believe.)
items.explanation: Fronting a 'dass' clause creates emphasis on the content of disbelief. 'Dass er das gesagt hat, glaube ich nicht' moves the subordinate clause to position 1, making it the topic and focus: 'The fact THAT he said it — THAT I don't believe.' Normal order would be: 'Ich glaube nicht, dass er das gesagt hat.' The fronted version is more dramatic and colloquial, and common in emotional speech: 'Dass du das vergessen hast!' (I can't believe you forgot that!), 'Dass so etwas passieren konnte!' (That something like this could happen!). Standalone 'dass' exclamations express disbelief or frustration without needing a main clause.

items.question: Auf _____ Fall werde ich das tun!
items.translation: (Under no circumstances will I do that!)
items.explanation: 'Auf keinen Fall' (under no circumstances) is an emphatic negative phrase — much stronger than simple 'nicht.' 'Auf keinen Fall werde ich das tun!' = 'There's absolutely no way I'll do that!' Compare emphasis levels: 'Ich tue das nicht' (I won't do it — neutral) vs. 'Auf keinen Fall tue ich das!' (Absolutely not!). Other emphatic negatives: 'auf gar keinen Fall' (absolutely under no circumstances — intensified with 'gar'), 'niemals' (never), 'unter keinen Umständen' (under no circumstances — formal), 'um nichts in der Welt' (not for anything in the world). These phrases add emotional intensity to refusals and denials.

items.question: Gerade DU solltest das _____ wissen.
items.translation: (YOU of all people should know that better.)
items.explanation: 'Gerade du' (you of all people / especially you) creates personal emphasis — singling out one person as particularly expected to know or act. 'Gerade DU solltest das besser wissen!' = 'YOU of all people should know better!' 'Gerade' before a pronoun means 'especially/particularly' — it implies the person has special reason or obligation: 'Gerade du weißt, wie schwer das ist' (YOU especially know how hard that is). Similar constructions: 'ausgerechnet du' (of all people, you — with more surprise/irony), 'gerade jetzt' (right now of all times). This is powerfully direct and often used in arguments or moral appeals.

items.question: Erst recht _____ ich das jetzt tun!
items.translation: (Now I'm going to do it all the more! / Now more than ever!)
items.explanation: 'Erst recht' (all the more/now more than ever) is an emphatic expression meaning that opposition or obstacles INCREASE determination rather than decrease it. 'Erst recht werde ich das tun!' = 'Now I'm going to do it even more!' (defiant). It's the opposite of backing down: 'Du willst mich aufhalten? Erst recht mache ich weiter!' (You want to stop me? Now I'll keep going even more!). This expression captures German directness and determination. Related emphatic phrases: 'um so mehr' (all the more), 'jetzt erst recht' (now more than ever — slightly more colloquial). It's a favorite in motivational contexts and arguments.

items.question: Heute _____ es den ganzen Tag.
items.explanation: German weather expressions use the impersonal pronoun 'es' (it) as subject — just like English 'it rains.' The verb 'regnen' (to rain) conjugates as 'es regnet' in the present. This impersonal construction is standard for all weather verbs: es schneit (it snows), es hagelt (it hails), es donnert (it thunders). The subject 'es' is grammatically required but has no real meaning — it's a placeholder subject. Unlike English where you might say 'raining!' casually, German always keeps 'es' in formal speech.

items.question: Im Winter _____ es oft sehr kalt.
items.translation: (In winter it is often very cold)
items.explanation: To describe temperature, German uses 'es ist kalt/warm/heiß' — literally 'it is cold/warm/hot.' This parallels English directly. However, German also uses 'es wird kalt' (it's getting cold) for changing conditions — a key distinction. The preposition 'im' is a contraction of 'in dem' (dative) used with months and seasons: im Januar, im Sommer, im Herbst. 'Oft' (often) is a frequency adverb that typically follows the verb in German main clauses.

items.question: Morgen _____ die Sonne scheinen.
items.explanation: Future tense for weather forecasts uses 'werden' + infinitive. 'Die Sonne wird scheinen' = 'The sun will shine.' Be careful: German 'will' does NOT mean English 'will' — German 'will' means 'wants to.' This is a classic false friend! Future weather predictions always use 'werden': Es wird regnen (it will rain), es wird schneien (it will snow). Note the word order: 'morgen' at position 1 pushes the subject after the verb (V2 rule).

items.question: Es ist _____ bewölkt mit einzelnen Schauern.
items.translation: (It is partly cloudy with isolated showers)
items.explanation: 'Teilweise' (partly/partially) is an adverb — it does NOT decline! Adverbs in German never change form regardless of the noun they modify. This differs from adjectives which must agree in gender, number, and case. 'Bewölkt' (cloudy) comes from 'Wolke' (cloud) — notice how German creates adjectives from nouns with the prefix 'be-' and past participle ending '-t'. 'Schauer' (showers) is masculine: der Schauer. Weather vocabulary is essential for daily conversation in German-speaking countries.

items.question: Bei diesem Gewitter solltest du lieber zu Hause _____.
items.translation: (In this thunderstorm you should better stay at home)
items.explanation: After modal verbs like 'solltest' (should), the main verb goes to the end in infinitive form: 'bleiben' (to stay). The construction 'solltest du lieber' means 'you'd better' — very common in spoken German advice. 'Bei' + dative describes conditions: 'bei Regen' (in rain), 'bei Schnee' (in snow), 'bei Gewitter' (during a thunderstorm). 'Zu Hause' means 'at home' (location), while 'nach Hause' means 'to home' (direction) — a crucial German distinction.

items.question: Die Temperatur _____ gestern auf minus zehn Grad.
items.translation: (The temperature dropped to minus ten degrees yesterday)
items.explanation: 'Fallen' (to fall) is a strong verb with the stem change a→ie in Präteritum: fiel, fielst, fiel. For temperature changes, Germans use 'fallen' (to fall/drop) and 'steigen' (to rise). 'Auf minus zehn Grad' uses 'auf' + accusative to indicate the target temperature. In weather reports, Präteritum is preferred over Perfekt — this is formal/written style. 'Grad' doesn't add an -e in plural when preceded by a number: zehn Grad, not 'zehn Grade.'

items.question: Im Frühling _____ die Blumen zu blühen.
items.translation: (In spring the flowers begin to bloom)
items.explanation: 'Die Blumen' (the flowers) is plural, so the verb needs plural conjugation: 'beginnen' (they begin). The infinitive clause 'zu blühen' (to bloom) follows 'beginnen' — German uses 'zu' before the infinitive in these constructions, similar to English 'to.' 'Im Frühling' uses the contraction 'im' = 'in dem.' The four seasons in German: der Frühling (spring), der Sommer (summer), der Herbst (autumn), der Winter (winter) — all masculine!

items.question: Der Wind _____ heute besonders stark.
items.translation: (The wind is blowing especially strong today)
items.explanation: 'Wehen' (to blow — of wind) conjugates regularly: weht for er/sie/es and for 'der Wind.' While 'blasen' also means 'to blow,' it's used for deliberate blowing (blowing out candles), not weather. This semantic distinction matters! 'Besonders stark' means 'especially strong' — 'besonders' is an intensifying adverb. German weather reports frequently use 'der Wind weht' as a fixed collocation. Note: 'der Wind' is masculine in German.

items.question: Nach dem Regen _____ ein wunderschöner Regenbogen am Himmel.
items.translation: (After the rain a beautiful rainbow appeared in the sky)
items.explanation: 'Erscheinen' (to appear) is a strong verb: erschien in Präteritum. The past tense is used here for narrative effect — telling what happened. 'Nach dem Regen' uses 'nach' + dative (dem). 'Wunderschön' is a compound adjective: 'wunder' (wonder) + 'schön' (beautiful) = wonderfully beautiful. German loves compound words! 'Der Regenbogen' (rainbow) is itself a compound: 'Regen' (rain) + 'Bogen' (arc/bow). 'Am Himmel' = 'an dem Himmel' (in the sky), dative with 'an.'

items.question: Es _____ draußen so neblig, dass man kaum etwas sehen kann.
items.translation: (It is so foggy outside that you can barely see anything)
items.explanation: 'Es ist neblig' follows the standard weather pattern: es ist + adjective. 'Neblig' (foggy) derives from 'der Nebel' (fog) + '-ig' suffix — a productive pattern: sonnig (sunny), windig (windy), wolkig (cloudy). The 'so...dass' construction (so...that) creates a result clause. 'Kaum' (barely/hardly) is an important adverb for nuance. 'Man' is the impersonal pronoun meaning 'one/you/people' — very common in German where English would use passive voice.

items.question: Wegen des schlechten _____ wurde das Spiel abgesagt.
items.translation: (Because of the bad weather, the game was cancelled)
items.explanation: 'Wegen' (because of) takes the genitive case! 'Das Wetter' (neuter) becomes 'des Wetters' in genitive — neuter/masculine nouns add '-s' or '-es' in genitive. The adjective 'schlecht' gets the ending '-en' after the definite article in genitive: 'des schlechten Wetters.' This is strong declension. 'Wurde abgesagt' is passive voice (Vorgangspassiv): werden + past participle. Genitive with 'wegen' is formal; colloquially, some Germans use dative: 'wegen dem schlechten Wetter.'

items.question: Im Sommer kann es in Deutschland bis zu 35 Grad _____ werden.
items.translation: (In summer it can get up to 35 degrees hot in Germany)
items.explanation: After 'werden' (to become), predicative adjectives remain undeclined — they take NO ending. 'Es kann heiß werden' = 'it can get hot.' This is different from attributive adjectives before nouns which must decline. 'Bis zu' (up to) indicates a maximum. The modal 'kann' sends the infinitive 'werden' to the end. German uses Celsius, so 35°C is very hot (95°F)! Weather small talk ('Smalltalk über das Wetter') is just as popular in Germany as in English-speaking countries.

items.question: Gestern hat es den ganzen Tag _____.
items.explanation: 'Hat geschneit' is the Perfekt (present perfect) of 'schneien' (to snow). Weather verbs use 'haben' as auxiliary in Perfekt — even though they describe events: es hat geregnet, es hat geschneit, es hat gedonnert. 'Den ganzen Tag' (all day) uses accusative for duration — 'den' is accusative masculine (der Tag). This time expression pattern is very productive: die ganze Nacht (all night), das ganze Jahr (all year), die ganze Woche (all week).

items.question: Der Wetterbericht sagt, dass es morgen _____ soll.
items.translation: (The weather report says that it should clear up tomorrow)
items.explanation: 'Aufklaren' (to clear up — weather) is a separable verb. In the 'dass' subordinate clause, the conjugated verb 'soll' goes to the END, and the infinitive 'aufklaren' precedes it. Separable prefixes stay attached in infinitive form. 'Der Wetterbericht' (weather report) is a compound noun. 'Soll' (should/is supposed to) indicates reported information — what the forecast predicts. This use of 'sollen' for hearsay/reports is distinctly German and has no direct English equivalent.

items.question: Bei _____ Wetter gehe ich gern spazieren.
items.translation: (In nice weather I like to go for a walk)
items.explanation: 'Bei' takes the dative case. 'Das Wetter' is neuter, so in dative without an article, the adjective takes the strong ending '-em': 'bei schönem Wetter.' Without an article, adjectives must carry the full case signal themselves — this is strong (unpreceded) declension. Compare: 'bei dem schönen Wetter' (with article, weak ending '-en'). 'Spazieren gehen' (to go for a walk) is a fixed verb combination where 'spazieren' is an infinitive used adverbially. This is a beloved German pastime!

items.question: Ich _____ gern einen Kaffee bestellen.
items.translation: (I would like to order a coffee)
items.explanation: 'Möchte' (would like) is the polite way to order in German — far more appropriate than 'will' (want), which sounds demanding. 'Ich möchte' is technically Konjunktiv II of 'mögen' but functions as its own modal verb for polite requests. 'Einen Kaffee' is accusative masculine (der Kaffee → einen Kaffee). As a modal verb, 'möchte' sends the infinitive 'bestellen' to the sentence end. This phrase is your survival toolkit in any German restaurant or café!

items.question: Könnten Sie mir bitte die _____ bringen?
items.translation: (Could you please bring me the menu?)
items.explanation: 'Die Speisekarte' (menu, literally 'food card') is the standard German word for a restaurant menu. It's a compound: 'Speise' (dish/food) + 'Karte' (card/map). The polite 'Könnten Sie' (Could you — formal) uses Konjunktiv II of 'können.' 'Mir' is dative (indirect object — to me), 'die Speisekarte' is accusative (direct object). The word order follows: Could you [to-me] please [the menu] bring? Infinitive 'bringen' goes to the end after the modal.

items.question: Als Vorspeise _____ ich den Salat.
items.translation: (As a starter, I'll have the salad)
items.explanation: 'Nehmen' (to take) is used for ordering food: 'Ich nehme...' = 'I'll have...' It's a strong verb with stem change e→i in du/er: du nimmst, er nimmt, but ich nehme (no change for ich!). 'Als Vorspeise' (as a starter) uses 'als' without an article — a common pattern with role/function descriptions. German meal courses: die Vorspeise (starter), die Hauptspeise/das Hauptgericht (main course), die Nachspeise/der Nachtisch (dessert). 'Den Salat' is accusative masculine.

items.question: Die Rechnung, _____!
items.explanation: 'Die Rechnung, bitte!' is the standard way to ask for the bill in a German restaurant. 'Bitte' (please) is the magic word in German politeness — it works for requests, acknowledgments, and even as 'you're welcome' ('Bitte schön!'). 'Die Rechnung' (the bill/check) comes from 'rechnen' (to calculate). In Germany, you typically ask your server directly rather than waiting — they won't bring the bill automatically. Tipping ('Trinkgeld') in Germany is usually 5-10%, not 15-20% as in the US.

items.question: Ist dieser Tisch noch _____?
items.explanation: 'Frei' (free/available) is a predicative adjective here — it comes after the verb 'ist' and therefore takes NO ending. This is different from attributive use: 'ein freier Tisch' (a free table) where the adjective declines. 'Noch' (still) implies: is it still unoccupied, or has someone taken it? This question is essential when entering a restaurant in Germany — you often seat yourself rather than waiting to be seated. 'Dieser' (this) is a demonstrative pronoun in nominative masculine.

items.question: Ich bin allergisch _____ Nüsse.
items.explanation: 'Allergisch gegen' (allergic to) is a fixed adjective-preposition combination — you must memorize that it takes 'gegen' + accusative, not 'zu' or 'auf.' German preposition usage often differs from English. Other food-related fixed phrases: 'empfindlich gegen' (sensitive to), 'unverträglich für' (intolerable for). 'Die Nüsse' is plural of 'die Nuss.' In Germany, restaurants are legally required to label allergens — look for 'Allergene' on menus. This phrase could literally save your life!

items.question: Was _____ Sie uns als Hauptgericht empfehlen?
items.translation: (What would you recommend as a main course?)
items.explanation: 'Würden Sie empfehlen' (would you recommend) uses Konjunktiv II of 'werden' for polite questions — the most courteous way to ask for recommendations. 'Uns' (us, dative) is the indirect object. 'Als Hauptgericht' (as a main course) uses 'als' for role descriptions. 'Empfehlen' (to recommend) is a strong verb: empfiehlt, empfahl, empfohlen. The infinitive goes to the end. This polite construction is used constantly in German service situations.

items.question: Das Schnitzel schmeckt _____!
items.explanation: 'Schmecken' (to taste) takes an adverb or predicative adjective — no ending needed! 'Ausgezeichnet' (excellent) functions as an adverb modifying how something tastes. Other useful restaurant compliments: 'Das schmeckt hervorragend!' (That tastes outstanding!), 'Sehr lecker!' (Very delicious!). 'Das Schnitzel' is neuter — like most German diminutives and words ending in '-el.' The original Wiener Schnitzel is made from veal (Kalbfleisch), paniert und gebraten (breaded and fried).

items.question: Wir möchten getrennt _____.
items.explanation: 'Getrennt zahlen' (to pay separately) is the standard phrase when splitting the bill. After 'möchten' (would like), the infinitive 'zahlen' goes to the end. 'Zahlen' and 'bezahlen' both mean 'to pay' — 'zahlen' is used without a direct object or with amounts ('Ich zahle 20 Euro'), while 'bezahlen' takes a direct object ('Ich bezahle die Rechnung'). In Germany, splitting the bill ('getrennt zahlen') is completely normal and not considered rude — just tell the waiter!

items.question: Haben Sie _____ vegetarische Gerichte?
items.explanation: 'Auch' (also/too) is the right choice here — asking if the restaurant also has vegetarian options. 'Noch' would mean 'still' (do you still have...), 'schon' means 'already,' and 'erst' means 'only/not until.' 'Vegetarische Gerichte' uses adjective + plural noun — 'vegetarisch' gets the ending '-e' in nominative/accusative plural. 'Das Gericht' (dish, pl. Gerichte) is neuter. German restaurants increasingly offer vegetarian and vegan options — look for 'V' or plant symbols on menus.

items.question: Zum _____ hätte ich gern ein Stück Apfelstrudel.
items.translation: (For dessert, I would like a piece of apple strudel)
items.explanation: 'Zum' = 'zu dem' (dative contraction). 'Der Nachtisch' (dessert, literally 'after-table') stays in base form after 'zum' because it's a masculine noun in dative without additional ending. 'Hätte gern' (would like — literally 'would have gladly') is an alternative to 'möchte' for polite requests. 'Ein Stück' (a piece of) — note: no preposition needed in German! Compare: 'ein Glas Wein' (a glass of wine), 'eine Tasse Kaffee' (a cup of coffee). 'Apfelstrudel' is a famous Austrian/German pastry.

items.question: Das Essen war so gut, dass ich es jedem _____ würde.
items.translation: (The food was so good that I would recommend it to everyone)
items.explanation: In a 'dass' subordinate clause, the conjugated verb goes to the END. With Konjunktiv II 'würde' + infinitive, the infinitive comes first, then 'würde' at the very end: '...dass ich es jedem empfehlen würde.' This is the standard word order for modal constructions in subordinate clauses. 'Jedem' is dative of 'jeder' (everyone) — dative because 'empfehlen' takes a dative object (recommend TO someone). 'So gut, dass...' creates a result clause.

items.question: Darf ich Sie _____ ein Glas Wein einladen?
items.translation: (May I invite you to a glass of wine?)
items.explanation: 'Jemanden auf etwas einladen' (to treat/invite someone to something) is a fixed expression using 'auf' + accusative. 'Darf ich' (may I) is the polite way to ask permission. 'Einladen' is a separable verb — but as an infinitive after 'darf,' it stays together. 'Sie' (you, formal — capitalized) shows respect. 'Ein Glas Wein' — no preposition 'of' needed between container and contents in German. This phrase is useful for social dining situations and shows generosity.

items.question: Die Bedienung in diesem Restaurant ist immer sehr _____.
items.translation: (The service in this restaurant is always very friendly)
items.explanation: 'Freundlich' (friendly) is a predicative adjective after 'ist' — no ending! Compare: 'eine freundliche Bedienung' (attributive, with ending). 'Die Bedienung' means both 'the service' and 'the server/waiter.' 'In diesem Restaurant' uses dative (in + location = dative). 'Immer' (always) and 'sehr' (very) are adverbs that never decline. Complimenting service in German: 'ausgezeichnete Bedienung' (excellent service), 'sehr aufmerksam' (very attentive).

items.question: Entschuldigung, ich habe eine Reservierung _____ den Namen Müller.
items.translation: (Excuse me, I have a reservation under the name Müller)
items.explanation: 'Eine Reservierung auf den Namen...' (a reservation under the name...) is a fixed phrase using 'auf' + accusative. German uses 'auf' here where English uses 'under' — a notable difference! 'Entschuldigung' (excuse me) is the standard polite opener. 'Die Reservierung' (reservation) — in spoken German, you might also hear 'Ich habe reserviert' (I have reserved) or 'Ich habe einen Tisch reserviert' (I reserved a table). Always arrive on time — punctuality is highly valued in German culture!

items.question: Was _____ das?
items.explanation: 'Was kostet das?' is the most common way to ask 'how much?' in German shops. 'Das' (that) is the subject, so the verb conjugates in third person singular: 'kostet.' For plural items: 'Was kosten die?' (How much do those cost?). Alternative: 'Wie viel kostet das?' or 'Wie teuer ist das?' (How expensive is that?). 'Kosten' is a regular verb that rarely uses Perfekt in everyday speech — you just ask in present tense. This is your most essential shopping phrase!

items.question: Kann ich das _____?
items.explanation: 'Anprobieren' specifically means to try on clothing — it's a separable verb (an|probieren). 'Probieren' alone means to taste/sample food. 'Ausprobieren' means to test/try out a device or method. 'Versuchen' means to attempt/try in general. These distinctions matter! After the modal 'kann,' the infinitive stays together: 'anprobieren.' Separable verbs only split in main clauses without modals: 'Ich probiere das an.' In shops, you'll also hear: 'Wo ist die Umkleidekabine?' (Where is the fitting room?).

items.question: Haben Sie das in einer _____ Größe?
items.translation: (Do you have that in a smaller size?)
items.explanation: 'In einer kleineren Größe' — 'einer' signals dative feminine (after 'in' for manner), and the comparative adjective 'kleineren' needs the weak ending '-en' after the indefinite article in dative. Comparative formation: klein → kleiner + dative ending -en = kleineren. Sizes in German: XS (extra klein), S (klein), M (mittel), L (groß), XL (extra groß). You can also ask: 'Haben Sie das eine Nummer größer/kleiner?' (Do you have that one size bigger/smaller?).

items.question: Das ist mir leider zu _____.
items.translation: (That is unfortunately too expensive for me)
items.explanation: 'Zu teuer' (too expensive) — predicative adjective after 'ist' = no ending. 'Mir' (dative, to/for me) expresses personal opinion: 'Das ist mir zu teuer' = 'That's too expensive for me.' This dative of personal judgment is very German! The 'mir' is optional but adds personal nuance. 'Leider' (unfortunately) softens the refusal — very important for German politeness! Other useful price adjectives: 'günstig' (affordable/reasonable), 'preiswert' (good value), 'billig' (cheap — can sound negative).

items.question: Ich _____ nur, danke.
items.explanation: 'Sich umschauen' (to look around/browse) is a separable reflexive verb. In a main clause: 'Ich schaue mich um' — the reflexive pronoun 'mich' stays near the verb, and the prefix 'um' goes to the end. Word order: subject + verb + reflexive + ... + separable prefix. This phrase is essential when shop assistants approach you with 'Kann ich Ihnen helfen?' (Can I help you?). 'Nur' (just/only) signals you don't need assistance. Adding 'danke' makes it polite.

items.question: Nehmen Sie _____ Kreditkarte?
items.explanation: 'Nehmen Sie auch Kreditkarte?' asks whether credit cards are ALSO accepted (in addition to cash). Germany is still more cash-oriented than many other countries — 'Bargeld' (cash) is king in many smaller shops! 'Auch' (also) is the right word because you're asking if this is an additional payment option. 'EC-Karte' (debit card) is more widely accepted than Kreditkarte in Germany. Useful phrase: 'Kann ich mit Karte zahlen?' (Can I pay by card?).

items.question: Das Kleid _____ Ihnen ausgezeichnet!
items.explanation: 'Stehen' + dative means 'to suit/look good on someone' — 'Das steht Ihnen gut!' is a common compliment in German shops. Note the distinction: 'stehen' = suits (looks good), 'passen' = fits (right size), 'sitzen' = sits well (fit/cut). All three take dative! 'Ihnen' is formal dative 'you.' Shop assistants use this to encourage purchases. 'Ausgezeichnet' (excellent) is an adverb here — no ending. 'Das Kleid' (dress) is neuter in German.

items.question: Gibt es _____ einen Rabatt?
items.explanation: 'Vielleicht' (perhaps/maybe) — watch the spelling! It's a common error to misspell this word. The 'ei' comes first, then 'eicht.' 'Gibt es' (is there / are there) is the impersonal construction with 'es gibt' + accusative: 'einen Rabatt' (masculine accusative). Bargaining is not common in German shops, but asking politely about discounts is acceptable. Look for: 'Angebot' (offer/deal), 'Sonderangebot' (special offer), 'Ausverkauf' (clearance sale), 'Schlussverkauf' (end-of-season sale).

items.question: Ich hätte gern _____ Kilo Äpfel.
items.translation: (I would like two kilograms of apples)
items.explanation: 'Zwei' (two) is the cardinal number — it doesn't decline before nouns in most cases! Only in genitive ('zweier Freunde') or dative without article ('mit zweien') does it change. 'Kilo' stays unchanged in plural — no '-s' needed: ein Kilo, zwei Kilo. 'Die Äpfel' is plural of 'der Apfel' — note the umlaut ä! At German markets ('auf dem Markt'), you might also hear: 'Ein Pfund Tomaten, bitte' (a pound of tomatoes). 'Hätte gern' is a polite alternative to 'möchte.'

items.question: Der Rock passt wie _____.
items.translation: (The skirt fits like a glove — perfectly)
items.explanation: 'Passen wie angegossen' (to fit like a glove — literally 'like cast on') is a fixed German idiom. 'Angegossen' is the past participle of 'angießen' (to cast on/pour on), referring to metal casting that produces a perfect mold. This is a wonderful example of how German idioms differ from English ones — same meaning, completely different image! Other fit-related expressions: 'wie maßgeschneidert' (like tailor-made), 'das sitzt perfekt' (that fits perfectly). 'Der Rock' means skirt (not rock!).

items.question: Kann ich das _____, wenn es nicht passt?
items.translation: (Can I return it if it doesn't fit?)
items.explanation: 'Umtauschen' (to exchange/return) is the specific verb for returning purchases. 'Tauschen' = to swap, 'austauschen' = to exchange (ideas/information), 'eintauschen' = to trade in. German consumer law gives you 14 days for online purchases ('Widerrufsrecht'), but in-store returns depend on store policy. After 'kann,' the infinitive stays together. 'Wenn' introduces a conditional clause: 'wenn es nicht passt' (if it doesn't fit) — verb goes to the end of the 'wenn' clause.

items.question: Diese Schuhe sind im _____ um 30 Prozent reduziert.
items.translation: (These shoes are reduced by 30 percent in the sale)
items.explanation: 'Im Angebot' (on sale/on offer) is a fixed prepositional phrase — 'im' = 'in dem' (dative). 'Das Angebot' (neuter) stays in base form in dative singular after the article 'dem.' 'Um 30 Prozent reduziert' means 'reduced by 30 percent' — 'um' indicates the amount of change. 'Reduziert' is the past participle used as adjective. 'Diese Schuhe' (these shoes — plural) uses the demonstrative 'diese.' German shoe sizes differ from US/UK: US 9 is approximately EU 42.

items.question: Wo finde ich die _____?
items.translation: (Where can I find the fitting rooms?)
items.explanation: 'Die Umkleidekabinen' (fitting rooms, plural) — the plural is most natural since stores have multiple booths. This is a triple compound: 'um' (change) + 'Kleide' (dress/clothe) + 'Kabinen' (cabins/booths). German compound nouns take the gender of the LAST component: die Kabine → die Umkleidekabine. 'Wo finde ich...' (Where do I find...) is the standard way to ask for locations in stores. 'Finden' is a strong verb: fand, gefunden.

items.question: Ich suche ein Geschenk _____ meine Mutter.
items.translation: (I'm looking for a gift for my mother)
items.explanation: 'Für' (for) + accusative is the correct preposition for recipients of gifts. 'Meine Mutter' is accusative feminine (die Mutter → meine Mutter). 'Suchen' (to search/look for) takes a direct accusative object: 'ein Geschenk' (neuter accusative). Useful shopping phrases: 'Können Sie mir etwas empfehlen?' (Can you recommend something?), 'Es ist als Geschenk gedacht' (It's meant as a gift), 'Können Sie es einpacken?' (Can you wrap it?).

items.question: Die Kasse ist dort _____.
items.explanation: 'Drüben' (over there) is a locative adverb indicating a position across from the speaker — it implies some distance. 'Dort drüben' (right over there) combines two location words for emphasis and precision. 'Die Kasse' (checkout/register) is feminine. 'Rüber' means 'over' but implies movement (herüber/hinüber shortened), while 'drüben' is static location. At German supermarkets you bag your own groceries, and you need to be fast — there is no bagging area like in some countries!

items.question: Guten Tag, hier _____ Frau Schmidt.
items.translation: (Good day, this is Mrs. Schmidt speaking)
items.explanation: 'Hier spricht [Name]' (This is [Name] speaking) is the standard way Germans answer the phone professionally. 'Spricht' is third person singular because the speaker refers to themselves in third person — a formal telephone convention. In casual contexts, Germans simply say their last name: 'Schmidt!' or 'Hier Schmidt.' Never answer with 'Hallo' in a business call — always identify yourself! This phone etiquette is distinctly German and surprises many English speakers.

items.question: Könnte ich bitte Herrn Meyer _____?
items.translation: (Could I please speak to Mr. Meyer?)
items.explanation: 'Könnte ich sprechen' — after the modal 'könnte' (could), the infinitive 'sprechen' goes to the sentence end. 'Herrn' is the accusative of 'Herr' — masculine weak nouns add '-n' in all cases except nominative! So: Herr Meyer (nom.) → Herrn Meyer (acc./dat.). This is a formal, polite phone request. The word order: modal verb in position 2, everything else in the middle, infinitive at the end. 'Bitte' (please) adds politeness.

items.question: Es tut mir leid, er ist gerade nicht _____.
items.translation: (I'm sorry, he is currently not available)
items.explanation: 'Erreichbar' (reachable/available) is a predicative adjective after 'ist' — no ending needed! The word is built from 'erreichen' (to reach) + '-bar' (suffix meaning '-able'): erreich-bar = reachable. This productive suffix works like English '-able': machbar (doable), lesbar (readable), essbar (edible). 'Gerade' means 'right now/currently' — very common in phone conversations. 'Es tut mir leid' (I'm sorry — literally 'it does me sorrow') is the standard German apology.

items.question: Kann ich eine Nachricht _____?
items.explanation: 'Hinterlassen' (to leave behind — a message, a trace) is an inseparable verb with the prefix 'hinter-.' Unlike separable verbs, inseparable prefixes (be-, er-, ver-, zer-, ent-, emp-, miss-, hinter-) NEVER separate! So: 'Ich hinterlasse eine Nachricht' (not 'Ich lasse eine Nachricht hinter'). 'Verlassen' means to leave (a place), 'lassen' alone means to let/allow. After 'kann,' the infinitive stays whole. 'Die Nachricht' (message) is feminine.

items.question: Könnten Sie ihn bitten, mich _____?
items.translation: (Could you ask him to call me back?)
items.explanation: 'Zurückzurufen' — this is the infinitive with 'zu' of the separable verb 'zurückrufen' (to call back). With separable verbs, 'zu' is inserted BETWEEN the prefix and the verb: zurück-zu-rufen. This happens after 'bitten' (to ask/request), which requires 'zu' + infinitive. Compare: 'Er ruft zurück' (He calls back — separated in main clause) vs. 'ihn bitten, zurückzurufen' (to ask him to call back — 'zu' inserted). This zu-insertion rule applies to all separable verbs!

items.question: Ich _____ Sie leider nicht verstehen. Die Verbindung ist schlecht.
items.translation: (I unfortunately cannot understand you. The connection is bad)
items.explanation: 'Ich kann Sie nicht verstehen' — present tense 'kann' (can) because the poor connection is happening RIGHT NOW. 'Sie' (formal you, capitalized) is the accusative object. 'Leider' (unfortunately) softens the complaint. 'Die Verbindung' (connection) is feminine. Phone-related vocabulary: 'der Empfang' (reception), 'das Signal' (signal), 'auflegen' (to hang up), 'dranbleiben' (to hold the line). In German phone calls, identifying connection problems directly is considered practical, not rude.

items.question: Ich _____ Sie gern mit der Abteilung verbinden.
items.translation: (I would be happy to connect you with the department)
items.explanation: 'Würde... verbinden' (would connect) uses Konjunktiv II for polite offers — essential in professional phone calls. 'Würde' is more polite than 'werde' (will) or 'will' (want). 'Verbinden mit' + dative (to connect with) — 'der Abteilung' is dative feminine. 'Gern' (gladly/happily) adds willingness. Professional phone language in German relies heavily on Konjunktiv II forms: würde, könnte, hätte, wäre. These signal courtesy and professionalism.

items.question: Unter welcher Nummer sind Sie _____?
items.translation: (At what number can you be reached?)
items.explanation: 'Unter welcher Nummer' (at which number) — 'unter' + dative describes the means of contact. 'Welcher' is dative feminine interrogative (die Nummer). 'Erreichbar' (reachable) is again predicative — no ending after 'sind.' German phone numbers are typically given in pairs: 030 / 12 34 56 is read as 'null-drei-null, eins-zwei, drei-vier, fünf-sechs.' Country code for Germany: +49, Austria: +43, Switzerland: +41. 'Können Sie mir Ihre Nummer geben?' is an alternative way to ask.

items.question: Worum _____ es sich?
items.explanation: 'Worum handelt es sich?' (What is it about/regarding?) is a formal phone phrase used by receptionists and assistants. 'Sich handeln um' (to be about/concern) is a reflexive verb. 'Worum' = 'um was' (about what) — the 'wo-' question word replaces the preposition + question pronoun. Alternatives: 'Worum geht es?' (more casual), 'In welcher Angelegenheit rufen Sie an?' (very formal — In what matter are you calling?). This gatekeeping phrase is very common in German offices.

items.question: Bitte bleiben Sie am _____, ich verbinde Sie.
items.translation: (Please stay on the line, I'll connect you)
items.explanation: 'Am Apparat bleiben' (to stay on the line — literally 'at the apparatus') is the traditional German phone phrase, still widely used in formal contexts. 'Der Apparat' (apparatus) is an old-fashioned word for telephone. Alternatives: 'Bleiben Sie bitte in der Leitung' (stay on the line), 'Einen Moment bitte' (one moment please). 'Verbinden' (to connect) — 'Ich verbinde Sie' means 'I'll put you through.' Note: no modal verb needed — present tense expresses immediate future action.

items.question: Ich rufe _____ des Inserats in der Zeitung an.
items.translation: (I'm calling regarding the advertisement in the newspaper)
items.explanation: 'Wegen' (because of/regarding) + genitive is the standard preposition for stating the reason for a call. 'Des Inserats' — 'das Inserat' (advertisement) in genitive neuter: des Inserats. 'In der Zeitung' — 'der' is dative feminine (die Zeitung → in der Zeitung). 'Anrufen' (to call) is separable: 'Ich rufe an.' German formal letters and calls often begin by stating the purpose immediately: 'Ich rufe an wegen...' or 'Ich schreibe Ihnen bezüglich...' (I'm writing regarding...).

items.question: Können Sie bitte etwas lauter _____?
items.translation: (Can you please speak a little louder?)
items.explanation: 'Lauter sprechen' (to speak louder) — 'lauter' is the comparative of 'laut' (loud). After 'können,' the infinitive goes to the end. 'Etwas' here means 'somewhat/a bit' — not 'something'! This dual meaning of 'etwas' is important: 'etwas lauter' (a bit louder), 'etwas langsamer' (a bit slower). Other useful phone requests: 'Können Sie das bitte wiederholen?' (Can you repeat that?), 'Können Sie bitte buchstabieren?' (Can you spell that please?).

items.question: Dann _____ ich mich morgen noch einmal.
items.explanation: 'Sich melden' (to get in touch/call again) is a reflexive verb: 'Ich melde mich' = 'I'll be in touch.' It's softer than 'Ich rufe an' and implies a general intention to reconnect. 'Noch einmal' (once more/again) — 'noch' + 'einmal.' 'Dann' (then) at sentence start triggers V2 word order: 'Dann melde ich mich' — verb in second position, subject after verb. 'Sich melden' is arguably the most versatile German phone and communication verb.

items.question: Ich bin leider in einer _____ und kann gerade nicht sprechen.
items.translation: (I'm unfortunately in a meeting and can't speak right now)
items.explanation: 'In einer Besprechung' (in a meeting) — 'einer' is dative feminine indefinite article (die Besprechung). 'Besprechung' stays in base form in dative singular after the article. The word comes from 'besprechen' (to discuss): be-sprech-ung. German nominalization with '-ung' is extremely productive: Meinung (opinion), Wohnung (apartment), Erfahrung (experience) — all feminine! 'Gerade' (right now) combined with 'nicht' creates 'can't right now.' Two clauses are joined by 'und.'

items.question: Vielen Dank _____ Ihren Anruf!
items.translation: (Thank you very much for your call!)
items.explanation: 'Vielen Dank für' (many thanks for) + accusative is the standard formal thank-you phrase. 'Ihren Anruf' — 'Ihr' (your, formal) in accusative masculine: Ihren. 'Der Anruf' (the call) comes from 'anrufen.' Professional phone sign-offs: 'Auf Wiederhören!' (Goodbye — on the phone, literally 'until hearing again'), NOT 'Auf Wiedersehen' (that's for in-person). 'Einen schönen Tag noch!' (Have a nice day!). Ending calls politely is just as important as starting them well.

items.question: Sehr _____ Damen und Herren,
items.explanation: 'Sehr geehrte Damen und Herren' is the standard formal German letter/email salutation when you don't know the recipient's name. 'Geehrte' takes the ending '-e' because it modifies the plural 'Damen und Herren' — plural nominative adjective after 'sehr' (treated as unpreceded). When you know the name: 'Sehr geehrter Herr Müller' (masculine -er), 'Sehr geehrte Frau Schmidt' (feminine -e). Note the comma after the greeting — German letters use a comma, NOT a colon like English!

items.question: Ich beziehe mich _____ Ihr Schreiben vom 15. März.
items.translation: (I am referring to your letter of March 15th)
items.explanation: 'Sich beziehen auf' + accusative (to refer to) is a fixed reflexive verb-preposition combination essential in formal correspondence. 'Ihr Schreiben' (your letter/writing — formal) is neuter accusative. 'Vom' = 'von dem' (dative, from the). Dates in German: '15. März' — ordinal number with period, read as 'fünfzehnter März.' This opening line establishes context in formal letters. Alternative: 'Bezugnehmend auf Ihr Schreiben...' (Referring to your letter...) — very formal participial construction.

items.question: Hiermit _____ ich mich für die Stelle als Projektleiter.
items.translation: (I hereby apply for the position of project manager)
items.explanation: 'Sich bewerben um/für' (to apply for) is a strong reflexive verb. 'Ich bewerbe mich' — the 'ich' form keeps the 'e': bewerbe (not 'bewirbe'!). The stem change e→i only happens in du/er forms: du bewirbst, er bewirbt. 'Hiermit' (hereby) is classic formal German — it signals the letter's purpose immediately. 'Die Stelle' (position/job) is feminine. 'Als' + noun without article describes the role. German job applications ('Bewerbungen') typically include a cover letter ('Anschreiben'), CV ('Lebenslauf'), and certificates ('Zeugnisse').

items.question: Für Rückfragen _____ ich Ihnen jederzeit zur Verfügung.
items.translation: (For any questions, I am available to you at any time)
items.explanation: 'Jemandem zur Verfügung stehen' (to be available to someone) is a fixed formal expression — literally 'to stand at someone's disposal.' 'Ihnen' is dative formal 'you.' 'Jederzeit' (at any time) is an adverb formed from 'jeder' + 'Zeit.' 'Rückfragen' (follow-up questions) is a compound: 'rück' (back) + 'Fragen.' This sentence is a standard closing formula in German business letters and applications. It signals willingness and professionalism.

items.question: Mit freundlichen _____ verbleibe ich
items.explanation: 'Mit freundlichen Grüßen' (with kind regards) is THE standard formal closing in German letters and emails — abbreviated 'MfG.' 'Grüßen' is dative plural of 'der Gruß' (greeting): mit + dative = freundlichen Grüßen. The adjective 'freundlichen' takes the '-en' ending: dative plural after 'mit.' Less formal alternatives: 'Beste Grüße' (best regards), 'Viele Grüße' (many greetings), 'Liebe Grüße' (warm regards — for people you know). Never use 'Liebe Grüße' in a formal business letter!

items.question: Ich _____ Sie höflichst, den Termin zu bestätigen.
items.translation: (I most politely request you to confirm the appointment)
items.explanation: 'Ich bitte Sie' (I ask you) — present tense, first person. 'Bitten' is a strong verb: bat (past), gebeten (participle). 'Höflichst' (most politely) uses the superlative form of 'höflich' + adverbial '-st' — extremely formal! 'Zu bestätigen' is an infinitive clause with 'zu' — required after 'bitten.' The comma before 'den Termin zu bestätigen' is mandatory in German (infinitive clauses with 'zu' get commas when they have objects). 'Der Termin' (appointment) is masculine.

items.question: _____ meines Urlaubs bin ich vom 1. bis 15. August nicht erreichbar.
items.translation: (Due to my vacation, I am not reachable from August 1st to 15th)
items.explanation: 'Aufgrund' (due to/on account of) + genitive is the most formal alternative to 'wegen.' 'Meines Urlaubs' is genitive masculine (der Urlaub → meines Urlaubs). 'Aufgrund' is preferred in formal writing over 'wegen' which is acceptable but less elevated. 'Vom...bis' (from...to) for date ranges. 'Nicht erreichbar' — the negation 'nicht' comes before the predicative adjective. Out-of-office messages (Abwesenheitsnotiz) in German use this formal structure. 'Weil' and 'Denn' are wrong because they require full clauses, not noun phrases.

items.question: Beiliegend _____ Sie die angeforderten Unterlagen.
items.translation: (Enclosed you will find the requested documents)
items.explanation: 'Beiliegend finden Sie...' (Enclosed you will find...) is a standard formal letter phrase. 'Sie finden' — formal 'you' with matching verb form. 'Beiliegend' (enclosed) is a present participle used as adverb: bei-lieg-end (lying with). 'Die angeforderten Unterlagen' — 'angefordert' (requested) is a past participle used as adjective with '-en' ending (accusative plural after 'die'). 'Die Unterlagen' (documents, always plural) is essential business vocabulary. Alternative: 'Anbei finden Sie...' (Attached you'll find...).

items.question: Ich würde es begrüßen, wenn wir _____ zeitnah treffen könnten.
items.translation: (I would welcome it if we could meet soon)
items.explanation: 'Sich treffen' (to meet) is reflexive — 'wir' needs the reflexive pronoun 'uns.' In the 'wenn' subordinate clause, 'könnten' (could — Konjunktiv II) goes to the very end. 'Zeitnah' (soon/promptly — literally 'time-near') is formal German for 'bald' (soon). 'Begrüßen' (to welcome) is used formally to express positive reception of an idea. This entire sentence demonstrates triple politeness: 'würde' (would) + 'wenn...könnten' (if we could) + 'begrüßen' (welcome) — peak German formality!

items.question: Unter _____ der oben genannten Bedingungen stimmen wir dem Vertrag zu.
items.translation: (Subject to the above-mentioned conditions, we agree to the contract)
items.explanation: 'Unter Vorbehalt' (subject to/with reservation) is a fixed formal phrase. 'Der Vorbehalt' (reservation/caveat) stays in base form in this prepositional phrase — dative masculine without article change. 'Der oben genannten Bedingungen' — genitive plural: 'oben genannt' (above-mentioned) is a participial adjective typical of legal/business German. 'Zustimmen' + dative (to agree to): 'dem Vertrag' (dative masculine). This is legal/contractual language — very formal and precise.

items.question: Bezüglich Ihrer Anfrage _____ ich Ihnen Folgendes mitteilen.
items.translation: (Regarding your inquiry, I would like to inform you of the following)
items.explanation: 'Bezüglich' (regarding) + genitive is the formal equivalent of 'wegen.' 'Ihrer Anfrage' is genitive feminine (die Anfrage → Ihrer Anfrage). 'Möchte... mitteilen' — 'mitteilen' (to inform/communicate) is separable, but after 'möchte' the infinitive stays together at the end. 'Folgendes' (the following) is a nominalized adjective — neuter because it refers to abstract content. 'Ihnen' is dative (to inform YOU). This opening is standard in formal business responses to inquiries.

items.question: Wir _____ uns, von Ihnen zu hören.
items.translation: (We look forward to hearing from you)
items.explanation: 'Sich freuen' (to be pleased/look forward to) is reflexive — 'wir freuen uns.' This closing formula expresses anticipation of a response. 'Von Ihnen zu hören' is an infinitive clause: 'zu hören' (to hear) + 'von Ihnen' (from you). The comma before 'von Ihnen' is required (infinitive clause with 'zu' that has dependent elements). Alternatives: 'Wir freuen uns auf Ihre Antwort' (We look forward to your response) — 'auf' + accusative for future-directed anticipation.

items.question: Ich _____ Ihnen für Ihre schnelle Antwort.
items.translation: (I thank you for your quick response)
items.explanation: 'Danken' + dative (to thank someone): 'Ich danke Ihnen.' The person thanked is in DATIVE — this differs from English where 'thank' takes a direct object. 'Für' + accusative gives the reason. 'Schnelle' gets the weak '-e' ending after possessive 'Ihre' in accusative feminine.
