items.explanation: 'Der Bruder' (brother) stays in nominative base form — it's the predicate noun after 'ist.' 'Meiner Mutter' is genitive feminine: 'meine' becomes 'meiner' in genitive. German family relationships: 'der Onkel' (uncle), 'die Tante' (aunt), 'der Cousin' (male cousin, pronounced French-style), 'die Cousine/Kusine' (female cousin), 'der Neffe' (nephew), 'die Nichte' (niece). 'Onkel' and 'Tante' are used with first names by children: 'Onkel Hans,' 'Tante Maria.'

items.question: Die _____ zwischen Eltern und Kindern ist nicht immer einfach.
items.translation: (The relationship between parents and children is not always easy)
items.explanation: 'Die Beziehung' (relationship) is singular — 'die' article confirms nominative singular. 'Zwischen' (between) + dative: 'Eltern und Kindern' (parents and children — both dative). 'Kindern' gets the dative plural '-n' ending! Almost all German nouns add '-n' in dative plural if they don't already end in '-n.' 'Nicht immer einfach' (not always easy) — 'einfach' is predicative, no ending. Relationship types: 'die Partnerschaft' (partnership), 'die Freundschaft' (friendship), 'die Verwandtschaft' (kinship/relatives).

items.question: Ich bin nach meinem Vater _____.
items.explanation: 'Benannt nach' + dative (named after) — 'nach meinem Vater' is dative masculine. 'Benannt' is the past participle of 'benennen' (to name — inseparable: be-). Don't confuse with 'genannt' from 'nennen' (to call/name directly). 'Benennen nach' = named in honor of someone, while 'nennen' = to call someone a name. 'Mein Vater' → 'meinem Vater' (dative after 'nach'). Naming traditions: firstborn sons are sometimes named after fathers or grandfathers. 'Der Vorname' (first name), 'der Nachname/Familienname' (surname).

items.question: Meine Schwägerin ist die Frau meines _____.
items.explanation: 'Meines Bruders' — genitive masculine! Masculine/neuter nouns add '-s' or '-es' in genitive: der Bruder → des Bruders, meines Bruders. 'Die Schwägerin' (sister-in-law) is from 'der Schwager' (brother-in-law) + '-in' feminine suffix. In-law vocabulary: 'die Schwiegermutter' (mother-in-law), 'der Schwiegervater' (father-in-law), 'die Schwiegereltern' (in-laws). 'Schwager/Schwägerin' also covers spouse's siblings. The genitive construction 'die Frau meines Bruders' shows possession — German uses genitive where English uses 's or of.

items.question: Wir haben uns auf einer Party _____.
items.explanation: 'Haben kennengelernt' — Perfekt of 'kennenlernen' (to meet/get to know). This compound verb forms one participle: kennen-ge-lernt (ge- inserted in the middle). 'Uns' is reciprocal: 'we got to know each other.' 'Auf einer Party' — 'auf' + dative for events: einer Party (feminine dative). Meeting contexts: 'sich vorstellen' (to introduce oneself), 'Freut mich, Sie kennenzulernen' (Pleased to meet you). Note: 'treffen' means to meet someone you already know; 'kennenlernen' means to meet for the first time!

items.question: Mein Vater hat mir immer gute _____ gegeben.
items.translation: (My father has always given me good advice)
items.explanation: 'Gute Ratschläge' (good pieces of advice) — plural is most natural with 'immer' (always, implying many times). 'Der Ratschlag' (piece of advice), plural: 'Ratschläge' (umlaut!). 'Geben' + dative (mir) + accusative (Ratschläge): double object. Interesting: 'der Rat' (advice — uncountable, like English!) vs. 'der Ratschlag' (a specific piece of advice — countable). 'Gegeben' — Perfekt of strong verb 'geben': gab, gegeben. Family wisdom: 'ein guter Rat ist teuer' (good advice is hard to find — proverb).

items.question: Die Kinder spielen im Garten, _____ die Eltern kochen.
items.translation: (The children play in the garden while the parents cook)
items.explanation: 'Während' (while) introduces a temporal subordinate clause with verb at the end: 'während die Eltern kochen.' It describes simultaneous actions. 'Weil' (because) would give a reason, 'damit' (so that) a purpose, 'obwohl' (although) a concession — all wrong here. 'Im Garten' (in the garden) — dative location. 'Kochen' (to cook) at clause end follows subordinate clause word order. Family life vocabulary: 'das Abendessen' (dinner), 'die Familienzeit' (family time), 'zusammen' (together), 'gemeinsam' (jointly).

items.question: Meine Tochter _____ ihrem Großvater sehr ähnlich.
items.translation: (My daughter looks very much like her grandfather)
items.explanation: 'Ähnlich sehen' + dative (to look like/resemble someone): 'sieht ihrem Großvater ähnlich.' 'Ihrem Großvater' is dative masculine after 'ähnlich.' 'Sehen' is a strong verb: sah, gesehen. The stem change e→ie in du/er: du siehst, er/sie sieht. 'Meine Tochter' (my daughter) — 'die Tochter' is feminine. Family resemblance: 'Sie kommt nach ihrer Mutter' (She takes after her mother), 'ganz der Vater!' (just like the father!), 'das Ebenbild' (spitting image).

items.question: Er hat sich von seiner Frau _____ lassen.
items.explanation: 'Sich scheiden lassen' (to get divorced) — 'lassen' + infinitive construction. 'Hat...scheiden lassen' — when 'lassen' is used with another infinitive in Perfekt, it creates a double infinitive (Ersatzinfinitiv): 'hat scheiden lassen' instead of 'hat scheiden gelassen.' This applies to modals too: 'hat kommen können.' 'Von seiner Frau' — 'von' + dative for separation. Divorce vocabulary: 'die Scheidung' (divorce), 'das Sorgerecht' (custody), 'der Unterhalt' (alimony/support), 'getrennt leben' (to live separately).

items.question: Wir feiern nächste Woche den Geburtstag _____ Großvaters.
items.translation: (We are celebrating our grandfather's birthday next week)
items.explanation: 'Den Geburtstag unseres Großvaters' — 'unseres Großvaters' is genitive masculine! The possessive 'unser' adds '-es' for genitive masculine/neuter, and the masculine noun adds '-s': Großvater → Großvaters. Genitive chain: birthday OF our grandfather. 'Nächste Woche' (next week) — accusative time expression, no preposition! 'Feiern' (to celebrate) is regular. Birthday traditions: 'Alles Gute zum Geburtstag!' (Happy Birthday!), 'die Geburtstagstorte' (birthday cake). In Germany, the birthday person traditionally brings cake to work/school!

items.question: Wir _____ in einer kleinen Wohnung im Stadtzentrum.
items.translation: (We live in a small apartment in the city center)
items.explanation: 'Wohnen' (to live/reside) specifically refers to where you DWELL. 'Leben' (to live) is broader and refers to existence or lifestyle. 'Wir wohnen' — present tense. 'In einer kleinen Wohnung' — 'in' + dative (location): 'einer' is dative feminine indefinite, 'kleinen' has weak ending '-en.' 'Im Stadtzentrum' = 'in dem Stadtzentrum' (neuter dative). Housing: 'die Wohnung' (apartment), 'das Haus' (house), 'die WG/Wohngemeinschaft' (shared apartment — extremely common in Germany, especially for students).

items.question: Die Miete für unsere Wohnung _____ leider gestiegen.
items.translation: (The rent for our apartment has unfortunately gone up)
items.explanation: 'Ist gestiegen' — 'steigen' (to rise) uses 'sein' in Perfekt because it's an intransitive verb of change! Strong verb: stieg, gestiegen. 'Die Miete' (the rent) is feminine. 'Für unsere Wohnung' — 'für' + accusative. 'Leider' (unfortunately) expresses regret. German housing: 'Kaltmiete' (cold rent — without utilities), 'Warmmiete' (warm rent — including heating/water), 'die Nebenkosten' (additional costs — utilities, garbage, etc.). Rising rents ('Mietpreise') are a major issue in German cities, especially Berlin and Munich.

items.question: Ich suche eine Wohnung mit einem großen _____.
items.translation: (I am looking for an apartment with a large balcony)
items.explanation: 'Mit einem großen Balkon' — 'mit' + dative: 'einem' is dative masculine indefinite, 'großen' has weak ending '-en.' 'Der Balkon' stays in base form in dative masculine singular. 'Suchen' (to search for) takes accusative: 'eine Wohnung.' Apartment features: 'der Balkon' (balcony), 'die Terrasse' (terrace), 'der Keller' (basement), 'der Dachboden' (attic), 'die Einbauküche' (fitted kitchen), 'der Aufzug/Fahrstuhl' (elevator). German apartment listings specify 'Zimmer': '3-Zimmer-Wohnung' means 3 rooms (excluding kitchen/bathroom).

items.question: Können Sie mir helfen, die Möbel _____?
items.translation: (Can you help me move the furniture?)
items.explanation: 'Umzustellen' — infinitive with 'zu' of separable verb 'umstellen' (to rearrange) : um-zu-stellen. After 'helfen,' the infinitive clause requires 'zu.' 'Die Möbel' (furniture — typically plural in German, singular 'das Möbelstück'). 'Mir' is dative (help ME). Compare: 'umstellen' (to rearrange — separable, accent on 'um') vs. 'umstellen' (to surround — inseparable, accent on 'stellen'). Moving vocabulary: 'umziehen' (to move house), 'der Umzug' (the move), 'einziehen' (to move in), 'ausziehen' (to move out).

items.question: Das Schlafzimmer liegt _____ dem Badezimmer.
items.translation: (The bedroom is next to the bathroom)
items.explanation: 'Neben' + dative (next to — location): 'dem Badezimmer' (neuter dative). 'Liegen' (to lie/be located) uses 'sein' in Perfekt and describes static position. Room vocabulary using compounds: 'das Schlafzimmer' (bedroom — Schlaf + Zimmer), 'das Badezimmer' (bathroom), 'das Wohnzimmer' (living room), 'das Esszimmer' (dining room), 'das Arbeitszimmer' (study/office), 'die Küche' (kitchen), 'der Flur' (hallway). All room names are neuter (das Zimmer)!

items.question: Der Vermieter hat den Mietvertrag _____.
items.explanation: 'Hat gekündigt' — Perfekt of regular verb 'kündigen' (to terminate/give notice). 'Den Mietvertrag' (the lease/rental agreement) — masculine accusative. Compound: 'Miet-' (rental) + 'Vertrag' (contract). 'Der Vermieter' (landlord — literally 'the renter-outer') from 'vermieten' (to rent out). The opposite: 'der Mieter' (tenant). German rental law strongly protects tenants! 'Die Kündigungsfrist' (notice period) is typically 3 months. 'Die Kaution' (security deposit) is usually 2-3 months' cold rent.

items.question: Wir haben das Haus letztes Jahr renovieren _____.
items.translation: (We had the house renovated last year)
items.explanation: 'Haben renovieren lassen' — double infinitive (Ersatzinfinitiv)! When 'lassen' combines with another infinitive in Perfekt, it uses its infinitive form instead of past participle: 'haben lassen' (not 'haben gelassen'). This also applies to modals: 'habe kommen können.' 'Renovieren' is an '-ieren' verb. 'Letztes Jahr' (last year) — accusative time expression, neuter. Home improvement: 'renovieren' (to renovate), 'sanieren' (to refurbish), 'streichen' (to paint walls), 'tapezieren' (to wallpaper).

items.question: Die Nachbarn _____ sind sehr freundlich.
items.translation: (The neighbors above us are very friendly)
items.explanation: 'Die Nachbarn über uns' (the neighbors above us) — 'über' + accusative/dative describes vertical position. In apartment buildings, you reference neighbors by position: 'über uns' (above), 'unter uns' (below), 'neben uns' (next to). 'Freundlich' is predicative after 'sind' — no ending. German apartment living etiquette: 'die Hausordnung' (house rules), 'die Ruhezeiten' (quiet hours — typically 10 PM to 6 AM and Sundays!), 'die Nachtruhe' (nighttime quiet). Being considerate of neighbors is taken very seriously!

items.question: Ich muss noch die Wäsche _____ und das Bad putzen.
items.translation: (I still need to do the laundry and clean the bathroom)
items.explanation: 'Waschen' (to wash) goes to the end as infinitive after 'muss.' Strong verb with stem change a→ä: du wäschst, er wäscht. 'Die Wäsche waschen' (to do the laundry) and 'das Bad putzen' (to clean the bathroom) are coordinated with 'und.' 'Noch' (still/yet) implies the chores haven't been done. 'Putzen' (to clean) is for surfaces/rooms. Household chores: 'staubsaugen' (to vacuum), 'bügeln' (to iron), 'aufräumen' (to tidy up), 'abwaschen/spülen' (to do dishes), 'den Müll rausbringen' (to take out the trash).

items.question: In der Küche steht ein neuer _____.
items.translation: (There is a new refrigerator in the kitchen)
items.explanation: 'Ein neuer Kühlschrank' — masculine nominative with mixed declension: 'neuer' after 'ein' (no ending on ein for masculine = adjective carries the strong ending -er). 'Stehen' (to stand) describes the position of furniture — German uses position verbs precisely: 'stehen' (standing upright), 'liegen' (lying flat), 'hängen' (hanging). 'Der Kühlschrank' (refrigerator — literally 'cool cupboard') is a compound. Kitchen appliances: 'der Herd' (stove), 'der Backofen' (oven), 'die Spülmaschine' (dishwasher), 'die Mikrowelle' (microwave).

items.question: Wir ziehen nächsten Monat in eine _____ Wohnung um.
items.translation: (We are moving to a bigger apartment next month)
items.explanation: 'Eine größere Wohnung' — comparative adjective 'größer-' with mixed declension after 'eine' (accusative feminine): '-e' ending. Comparative: groß → größer (with umlaut!). 'Umziehen' is separable: 'ziehen um' in main clause. 'Nächsten Monat' — accusative time expression. 'In eine Wohnung' — accusative after 'in' (direction — moving INTO). Moving is common in Germany — average Germans move about 4 times in their life. 'Der Umzug' (the move), 'der Umzugswagen' (moving van), 'Kisten packen' (to pack boxes).

items.question: Die Heizung funktioniert nicht. Könnten Sie einen Handwerker _____?
items.translation: (The heating isn't working. Could you call a repairman?)
items.explanation: 'Rufen' (to call) goes to the end after 'könnten.' 'Die Heizung' (the heating) is feminine. 'Funktionieren' (to function/work) — '-ieren' verb. 'Der Handwerker' (repairman/craftsman) is masculine. In German rental law, the landlord is responsible for major repairs! Tenants report issues to the 'Hausverwaltung' (property management). Home maintenance: 'der Installateur/Klempner' (plumber), 'der Elektriker' (electrician), 'der Schlosser' (locksmith). 'Könnten Sie' is the polite Konjunktiv II request form.

items.question: Ich habe die Wände im Wohnzimmer neu _____.
items.translation: (I have freshly painted the walls in the living room)
items.explanation: 'Habe gestrichen' — Perfekt of strong verb 'streichen' (to paint walls/surfaces): strich, gestrichen. Don't confuse with 'malen' (to paint art/pictures)! 'Streichen' is specifically for surfaces: walls, fences, doors. 'Die Wände' (walls, plural of 'die Wand'). 'Neu' (freshly/newly) functions as adverb. 'Im Wohnzimmer' — dative location. In Germany, when moving out ('Auszug'), tenants are often expected to repaint walls white — this obligation ('Schönheitsreparaturen') is a common source of landlord-tenant disputes!

items.question: Das Haus hat einen schönen Garten mit alten _____.
items.translation: (The house has a beautiful garden with old trees)
items.explanation: 'Mit alten Bäumen' — 'mit' + dative! And dative PLURAL nouns add '-n' if they don't already end in '-n': Bäume → Bäumen. This is the dative plural '-n' rule — one of German's most consistent rules! 'Alten' has weak ending '-en' after no article in dative plural (strong declension would also give '-en' here). 'Der Baum' (tree), plural 'Bäume' (umlaut!). 'Einen schönen Garten' — masculine accusative. German gardens: 'der Schrebergarten/Kleingarten' (allotment garden) is uniquely German — small rented garden plots in cities!

items.question: Vergiss nicht, die Tür _____, wenn du gehst.
items.translation: (Don't forget to lock the door when you leave)
items.explanation: 'Abzuschließen' — infinitive with 'zu' of separable verb 'abschließen' (to lock): ab-zu-schließen. After 'vergiss nicht' (don't forget), the infinitive requires 'zu' — inserted between prefix and stem for separable verbs. 'Die Tür' (the door) is feminine. 'Wenn du gehst' — temporal clause: 'when you leave.' Home security: 'der Schlüssel' (key), 'das Schloss' (lock), 'die Klingel' (doorbell), 'die Gegensprechanlage' (intercom). German apartment buildings typically have a main entrance door ('Haustür') that is always locked.

items.question: Wie _____ ich zum Bahnhof?
items.translation: (How do I get to the train station?)
items.explanation: 'Wie komme ich zum...?' is the standard German phrase for asking directions. While 'gehen' means to walk and 'fahren' means to drive, 'kommen' is used generically for 'getting to' a destination. The preposition 'zu' contracts with the masculine article 'dem' to form 'zum' (zu + dem). This is one of the most essential travel phrases in German — memorize it as a fixed expression.

items.question: Der Zug _____ um 14:30 Uhr ab.
items.explanation: 'Abfahren' is the separable verb for 'to depart' (for vehicles). The prefix 'ab-' separates and goes to the end: 'Der Zug fährt ab.' German uses the 24-hour clock in schedules — 14:30 means 2:30 PM. For trains and buses, always use 'abfahren' (depart) and 'ankommen' (arrive). Notice 'fahren' has a stem-vowel change: a→ä for du/er/sie.

items.question: Biegen Sie an der nächsten Kreuzung _____ ab.
items.explanation: 'Rechts abbiegen' means 'to turn right.' 'Rechts' is the directional adverb (right), while 'recht' means 'right/correct' as an adjective. 'Abbiegen' is the separable verb for turning. Common direction words: rechts (right), links (left), geradeaus (straight ahead), zurück (back). 'Die Kreuzung' means intersection. In formal directions, use 'Sie' form: 'Biegen Sie... ab.'

items.question: Ich muss am nächsten Halt _____.
items.translation: (I need to get off at the next stop.)
items.explanation: 'Aussteigen' means 'to get off/out' of a vehicle. German has precise verbs for public transport: 'einsteigen' (get on/in), 'aussteigen' (get off/out), 'umsteigen' (transfer/change). These separable verbs split in main clauses but stay together after modals like 'muss': 'Ich muss aussteigen.' 'Der Halt' or 'die Haltestelle' means the stop. Master these three verbs for smooth navigation of German public transport.

items.question: Die Straßenbahn _____ alle zehn Minuten.
items.explanation: 'Kommen' is used for scheduled arrivals — 'Die Straßenbahn kommt alle zehn Minuten' (the tram comes every ten minutes). 'Alle zehn Minuten' is the German way to say 'every ten minutes.' German cities have excellent public transport: Straßenbahn (tram), U-Bahn (subway), S-Bahn (commuter rail), Bus. Each has its own schedule and 'Fahrplan' (timetable).

items.question: Gehen Sie _____ bis zur Ampel.
items.translation: (Go straight ahead to the traffic light.)
items.explanation: 'Geradeaus' means 'straight ahead' and is one word in German. Don't confuse it with 'gerade' (just now/straight as adjective). 'Die Ampel' is the traffic light. 'Bis zu' means 'up to/until' and contracts with articles: 'bis zur' (feminine). Direction phrases often start with imperatives: 'Gehen Sie...' (Go...), 'Biegen Sie ab...' (Turn...), 'Nehmen Sie...' (Take...). These are essential for navigating German cities.

items.question: Ich hätte gern eine _____ nach München.
items.translation: (I would like a ticket to Munich.)
items.explanation: 'Die Fahrkarte' is the standard German word for a travel ticket. While 'Ticket' is increasingly used colloquially, 'Fahrkarte' remains the proper term at counters and machines. 'Ich hätte gern...' (I would like...) is the polite way to request something — it uses Konjunktiv II of 'haben.' Related terms: Einzelfahrkarte (single ticket), Tageskarte (day pass), Monatskarte (monthly pass), Hin- und Rückfahrkarte (round trip ticket).

items.question: Das Flugzeug _____ um 18 Uhr.
items.explanation: 'Landen' means 'to land' and is conjugated regularly: ich lande, du landest, er/sie/es landet. 'Das Flugzeug' (the airplane) is a compound noun: Flug (flight) + Zeug (thing/craft). Related aviation vocabulary: starten/abfliegen (take off), der Flughafen (airport), das Gate (gate), die Landebahn (runway). German uses 24-hour time in schedules, so 18 Uhr = 6 PM.

items.question: Die U-Bahn-Station ist _____ der Ecke.
items.translation: (The subway station is around the corner.)
items.explanation: 'Um die Ecke' (around the corner) is a fixed expression using the preposition 'um' with accusative. 'Die Ecke' (corner) is feminine. Prepositions of location are crucial for directions: um die Ecke (around the corner), an der Kreuzung (at the intersection), gegenüber dem Park (across from the park), neben der Kirche (next to the church). Each preposition demands its specific case.

items.question: Fährst du mit dem Bus oder mit der _____?
items.translation: (Are you taking the bus or the tram?)
items.explanation: 'Die Straßenbahn' (tram/streetcar) is a compound noun: Straße (street) + n (linking letter) + Bahn (rail). The 'ß' (Eszett) appears after long vowels. 'Mit dem/der' uses dative case after the preposition 'mit.' Transportation modes with 'mit': mit dem Bus, mit der U-Bahn, mit dem Zug, mit dem Auto, mit dem Fahrrad. The article changes based on the noun's gender.

items.question: Der Stau auf der _____ ist drei Kilometer lang.
items.translation: (The traffic jam on the highway is three kilometers long.)
items.explanation: 'Die Autobahn' is Germany's famous highway system, known for sections without speed limits. It's a compound: Auto (car) + Bahn (track/road). 'Der Stau' (traffic jam) is a common word in traffic reports. Related terms: die Ausfahrt (exit), die Auffahrt (on-ramp), die Raststätte (rest stop), die Baustelle (construction site). Germany's Autobahn network is one of the densest highway systems in Europe.

items.question: Entschuldigung, wo ist die nächste _____?
items.translation: (Excuse me, where is the nearest bus stop?)
items.explanation: 'Die Haltestelle' (stop/station for buses and trams) is a compound: halten (to stop) + Stelle (place). This is different from 'der Bahnhof' (train station). 'Entschuldigung' (excuse me) is the polite way to approach strangers for directions. For subway stops, you can also say 'U-Bahn-Station.' Always use 'nächste' (nearest) with the correct adjective ending for the gender.

items.question: Wir müssen hier _____ und die Linie 5 nehmen.
items.translation: (We must transfer here and take line 5.)
items.explanation: 'Umsteigen' means 'to transfer/change' between transport lines. The prefix 'um-' indicates a change or switch. After modal verbs like 'müssen,' the infinitive stays together: 'Wir müssen umsteigen.' 'Die Linie' (line) refers to a specific route number. German public transport networks are well-connected, and transfers are usually seamless with coordinated schedules (Anschluss).

items.question: Das Taxi _____ vor dem Hotel.
items.translation: (The taxi is waiting in front of the hotel.)
items.explanation: 'Warten' means 'to wait' and is the natural choice when a taxi is waiting for someone. 'Vor dem Hotel' uses dative after 'vor' because it indicates location (no movement). Compare: 'stehen' (to stand — emphasizes position), 'halten' (to stop briefly), 'parken' (to park — longer duration). German distinguishes these precisely: a taxi 'wartet' (waits actively), a car 'parkt' (is parked).

items.question: Können Sie mich am Hauptbahnhof _____?
items.translation: (Can you drop me off at the main train station?)
items.explanation: 'Absetzen' means 'to drop off' (a person from a vehicle). It's a separable verb: 'Setzen Sie mich bitte ab.' The 'Hauptbahnhof' (main train station, abbreviated Hbf) is the central station in every German city. Other transport verbs with 'ab-': abfahren (depart), abholen (pick up), abbiegen (turn). 'Können Sie...' is the polite request form using the modal verb 'können.'

items.question: Die Nachrichten _____ jeden Abend um 20 Uhr gesendet.
items.translation: (The news is broadcast every evening at 8 PM.)
items.explanation: 'Werden + Partizip II' forms the passive voice: 'werden gesendet' (are broadcast). 'Die Nachrichten' (the news) is always plural in German, unlike English. 'Senden' (to broadcast/send) has the past participle 'gesendet.' The passive is common in media language for objective reporting. 'Jeden Abend' uses accusative for time expressions. German TV news (Tagesschau, heute) traditionally air at 20:00.

items.question: Der Journalist hat einen Artikel über die Wahl _____.
items.translation: (The journalist wrote an article about the election.)
items.explanation: 'Schreiben' (to write) is a strong verb with the past participle 'geschrieben' (ge- + schrieb + -en). In perfect tense with 'hat': 'hat geschrieben.' Don't confuse the past participle with the simple past 'schrieb.' 'Über die Wahl' (about the election) uses accusative after 'über.' German journalism vocabulary: der Bericht (report), die Schlagzeile (headline), die Pressemitteilung (press release).

items.question: Hast du die _____ schon gelesen?
items.explanation: 'Die Zeitung' is a daily newspaper, while 'die Zeitschrift' is a magazine/periodical and 'das Magazin' is a glossy magazine. German distinguishes these clearly. Famous German newspapers: die Süddeutsche Zeitung, die Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung (FAZ), die Bild. 'Schon' (already) typically sits between auxiliary and participle: 'schon gelesen.' Reading German news is excellent language practice.

items.question: Die Sendung wird _____ ausgestrahlt.
items.explanation: German uses the English loanword 'live' (pronounced 'laif') for live broadcasts. While 'direkt' can also mean 'live' in broadcast context, 'live' is the standard media term. 'Die Sendung' (program/broadcast) and 'ausstrahlen' (to broadcast/air) are core media vocabulary. German absorbs many English media terms: der Podcast, streamen, der Livestream, die Show. This reflects English influence on German media culture.

items.question: Er hat die Nachricht per E-Mail _____.
items.explanation: 'Verschicken' (to send/dispatch) uses the inseparable prefix 'ver-,' so the past participle has no 'ge-': 'verschickt.' Compare: 'schicken' → 'geschickt' (sent), 'senden' → 'gesendet' (sent/broadcast). 'Per E-Mail' uses 'per' (by/via) — in German, 'E-Mail' can be feminine (die) or neuter (das), though 'die E-Mail' is more common. The prefix 'ver-' often adds a sense of completion or dispatching.

items.question: Die Werbung _____ überall zu sehen.
items.explanation: 'Ist... zu sehen' (is to be seen/visible) is a passive alternative construction: 'sein + zu + Infinitiv.' It expresses possibility or necessity passively. 'Die Werbung' means advertising/advertisement. 'Überall' (everywhere) is a common adverb. Other 'sein + zu' examples: 'ist zu hören' (can be heard), 'ist zu erwarten' (is to be expected). This construction is more elegant than a standard passive in formal writing.

items.question: Ich _____ mir jeden Abend die Nachrichten an.
items.explanation: 'Sich etwas anschauen' or 'sich etwas ansehen' (to watch/look at something) is a reflexive separable verb. 'Ich schaue mir... an' — note the dative reflexive 'mir' and the separated prefix 'an.' Regional variations exist: 'anschauen' (southern Germany/Austria), 'ansehen' (standard), 'angucken' (northern Germany, colloquial). The dative reflexive indicates watching for oneself.

items.question: Der Podcast hat über eine Million _____.
items.translation: (The podcast has over a million listeners.)
items.explanation: 'Der Hörer' (listener) comes from 'hören' (to hear/listen). In media context, 'Hörer' refers to radio/podcast listeners, while 'Zuhörer' implies active, attentive listening (like at a lecture). 'Hörende' is a gender-neutral participle form gaining popularity. Don't confuse with 'Hörige' (serf/devotee). German compounds: Hörbuch (audiobook), Kopfhörer (headphones), Hörspiel (radio play).

items.question: Die sozialen _____ beeinflussen die öffentliche Meinung.
items.explanation: 'Soziale Medien' is the German equivalent of 'social media.' 'Die Medien' (media) is always plural — the singular 'das Medium' is rarely used in this context. 'Beeinflussen' (to influence) is inseparable (be- prefix). 'Die öffentliche Meinung' (public opinion) is a key phrase in political discourse. German forms the adjective 'öffentlich' from 'die Öffentlichkeit' (the public). Media literacy (Medienkompetenz) is taught in German schools.

items.question: Der Bericht wurde von mehreren Zeitungen _____.
items.translation: (The report was published by several newspapers.)
items.explanation: 'Veröffentlichen' (to publish) has the inseparable prefix 'ver-,' so no 'ge-' in the past participle: 'veröffentlicht.' It derives from 'öffentlich' (public) — making something public. 'Von mehreren Zeitungen' uses dative after passive 'von' (by). The passive 'wurde... veröffentlicht' (was published) is extremely common in academic and media German. Related: die Veröffentlichung (publication), der Verlag (publishing house).

items.question: Sie hat ein Video auf YouTube _____.
items.explanation: 'Hochladen' (to upload) is a separable verb: hoch (up) + laden (to load). Past participle: 'hochgeladen' — the 'ge-' goes between prefix and stem. Compare: 'herunterladen' (to download) → 'heruntergeladen.' Don't confuse with 'aufladen' (to charge a battery). Digital German vocabulary uses many such compounds: abspeichern (save), weiterleiten (forward), löschen (delete). These separable verbs follow standard rules.

items.question: Die Schlagzeile hat viel _____ erregt.
items.translation: (The headline attracted a lot of attention.)
items.explanation: 'Aufmerksamkeit erregen' (to attract attention) is a fixed collocation. 'Die Aufmerksamkeit' literally means 'up-noticing-ness.' Compare: 'Achtung' (caution/attention as warning), 'Beachtung' (notice/consideration), 'Beobachtung' (observation). 'Die Schlagzeile' (headline) literally means 'striking line.' German uses many such precise distinctions between related concepts that English covers with one word.

items.question: Der Film _____ nächste Woche im Kino.
items.translation: (The movie starts next week in the cinema.)
items.explanation: 'Laufen' (to run) is used idiomatically for movies showing in cinemas: 'Der Film läuft im Kino.' This is a fixed expression — you wouldn't say 'spielt' or 'zeigt' for a movie screening. 'Nächste Woche' (next week) uses accusative for time. German cinema vocabulary: die Vorstellung (showing), die Leinwand (screen), der Vorspann (opening credits), der Abspann (end credits), die Vorpremiere (preview).

items.question: Kannst du die Lautstärke bitte _____?
items.translation: (Can you please turn down the volume?)
items.explanation: 'Leiser machen' (to make quieter) or 'leiser stellen' (to turn down) is how Germans say 'turn down the volume.' 'Die Lautstärke' (volume) is a compound: laut (loud) + Stärke (strength). The comparative 'leiser' (quieter) comes from 'leise' (quiet). Opposite: 'lauter machen/stellen' (turn up). German often uses 'adjective + machen' where English uses specific verbs: größer machen (enlarge), kürzer machen (shorten).

items.question: Der Moderator hat die Gäste zum Thema _____.
items.translation: (The host interviewed the guests on the topic.)
items.explanation: 'Befragen' (to interview/question formally) is inseparable (be- prefix) → past participle 'befragt' without 'ge-.' Compare: 'fragen' → 'gefragt' (asked casually), 'ausfragen' → 'ausgefragt' (interrogated), 'erfragen' → 'erfragt' (inquired). 'Der Moderator' (host/presenter) is the standard term for TV/radio presenters. 'Zum Thema' (on the topic) uses dative contraction. Each prefix adds a specific nuance to the base verb.

items.question: Der Verdächtige wurde von der Polizei _____.
items.translation: (The suspect was arrested by the police.)
items.explanation: 'Verhaften' (to arrest) is inseparable (ver- prefix), so past participle is 'verhaftet' without 'ge-.' This is the legal/police term for arrest. 'Der Verdächtige' (the suspect) is an adjectival noun that declines like an adjective. 'Von der Polizei' uses dative in passive constructions. Related legal terms: die Festnahme (arrest as noun), in Untersuchungshaft nehmen (to remand in custody), der Haftbefehl (arrest warrant).

items.question: Das Gericht hat den Angeklagten für _____ befunden.
items.explanation: 'Schuldig' (guilty) is the adjective form. 'Für schuldig befinden' (to find guilty) is a legal fixed expression. Don't confuse with 'schuld sein' (to be at fault — predicative only). 'Das Gericht' (the court), 'der Angeklagte' (the defendant/accused) — another adjectival noun. German legal system vocabulary: der Richter (judge), der Staatsanwalt (prosecutor), der Verteidiger (defense attorney), das Urteil (verdict/sentence).

items.question: Der Zeuge hat vor Gericht _____.
items.explanation: 'Aussagen' (to testify/make a statement) is a separable verb — 'ausgesagt' in past participle. This is the legal term for giving testimony. 'Vor Gericht' (in court/before the court) uses dative after 'vor.' 'Der Zeuge' (witness, masc.) / 'die Zeugin' (witness, fem.) — note the weak masculine noun ending. Related: die Aussage (testimony/statement), der Eid (oath), unter Eid (under oath).

items.question: Der Dieb wurde auf frischer _____ ertappt.
items.explanation: 'Auf frischer Tat ertappt' (caught in the act/red-handed) is a fixed legal expression. 'Die Tat' (the deed/act) is used in criminal contexts. 'Der Dieb' (thief) has an irregular plural: 'die Diebe.' 'Ertappen' means 'to catch/discover someone doing something wrong.' Related crime vocabulary: der Einbruch (burglary), der Raub (robbery), der Betrug (fraud), die Straftat (criminal offense). German distinguishes Diebstahl (theft) from Raub (robbery with force).

items.question: Die Strafe für dieses Vergehen ist eine _____ von 500 Euro.
items.translation: (The penalty for this offense is a fine of 500 euros.)
items.explanation: 'Die Geldstrafe' (fine as criminal penalty) is a compound: Geld (money) + Strafe (punishment). 'Die Geldbuße' is used for administrative fines (Ordnungswidrigkeit). 'Das Vergehen' is a minor criminal offense (misdemeanor), vs. 'das Verbrechen' (felony). German criminal law distinguishes: Ordnungswidrigkeit (infraction), Vergehen (misdemeanor), Verbrechen (felony). Each carries different penalty ranges.

items.question: Der Anwalt hat _____ gegen das Urteil eingelegt.
items.translation: (The lawyer filed an appeal against the verdict.)
items.explanation: 'Berufung einlegen' (to file an appeal) is a fixed legal expression. Don't confuse 'die Berufung' (appeal in law) with 'der Beruf' (profession). 'Das Urteil' (verdict/judgment) is a core legal term. Deutsche Gerichtsbarkeit (German judiciary) has multiple levels: Amtsgericht (local court), Landgericht (regional court), Oberlandesgericht (Higher Regional Court), Bundesgerichtshof (Federal Court of Justice).

items.question: Die Ermittlungen in dem Fall dauern noch _____.
items.translation: (The investigation in the case is still ongoing.)
items.explanation: 'Andauern' (to continue/last) is a separable verb — 'dauern noch an' (are still ongoing). 'Die Ermittlungen' (investigations) is always plural in this legal context. 'Der Fall' (the case) functions like English 'case.' 'Noch' (still) emphasizes continuation. Related: die Ermittlungsbehörde (investigating authority), der Ermittler (investigator), das Ermittlungsverfahren (investigation proceedings). German crime novels (Krimis) are hugely popular.

items.question: Der Angeklagte hat die Tat _____.
items.explanation: 'Gestehen' (to confess) has the irregular past participle 'gestanden.' 'Die Tat gestehen' means to confess to the crime. Don't confuse with 'stehen' (to stand) which also has 'gestanden' as past participle — context makes it clear. 'Das Geständnis' (confession) is the noun form. Legal principle: 'Im Zweifel für den Angeklagten' (in dubio pro reo / innocent until proven guilty) is fundamental to German law.

items.question: Der Häftling wurde nach fünf Jahren aus dem Gefängnis _____.
items.translation: (The prisoner was released from prison after five years.)
items.explanation: 'Entlassen' (to release/discharge) is inseparable (ent- prefix), past participle: 'entlassen' (no ge-!). 'Der Häftling' (prisoner/inmate) comes from 'die Haft' (custody/imprisonment). 'Das Gefängnis' (prison) is neuter. 'Freilassen' (to set free) is also correct but more general. 'Aus dem Gefängnis' uses dative after 'aus.' German prison vocabulary: die Bewährung (probation), vorzeitige Entlassung (early release), lebenslänglich (life sentence).

items.question: Die Polizei sucht nach dem _____ des Überfalls.
items.translation: (The police are searching for the perpetrator of the assault.)
items.explanation: 'Der Täter' (perpetrator/offender) derives from 'die Tat' (deed/act). The umlaut ä is essential — 'Tater' doesn't exist. 'Suchen nach' (to search for) requires dative: 'nach dem Täter.' 'Der Überfall' (assault/robbery/raid) uses the prefix 'über-.' German criminal vocabulary: Tatort (crime scene), Tatwaffe (murder weapon), Tatmotiv (motive), Tathergang (sequence of events). The TV show 'Tatort' is Germany's longest-running crime series.

items.question: Das Opfer hat eine Anzeige bei der Polizei _____.
items.translation: (The victim filed a report with the police.)
items.explanation: 'Eine Anzeige erstatten' (to file a police report) is a fixed legal expression. 'Erstatten' is inseparable (er- prefix), past participle: 'erstattet.' 'Das Opfer' (victim) is neuter regardless of the person's gender. 'Bei der Polizei' uses dative after 'bei.' This phrase is essential if you ever need to report a crime in Germany. Related: die Strafanzeige (criminal complaint), Anzeige gegen Unbekannt (report against unknown persons).

items.question: Der Richter hat eine _____ von zwei Jahren verhängt.
items.translation: (The judge imposed a sentence of two years.)
items.explanation: 'Die Freiheitsstrafe' (custodial sentence, literally: freedom-punishment) is the official legal term for a prison sentence. While 'Gefängnisstrafe' and 'Haftstrafe' are used colloquially, 'Freiheitsstrafe' is the correct legal terminology. 'Verhängen' (to impose a sentence) is inseparable → 'verhängt.' 'Der Richter' (judge) passes sentence after the trial (die Verhandlung). German sentencing also includes 'Bewährungsstrafe' (suspended sentence).

items.question: Die Beweise _____ eindeutig auf den Verdächtigen hin.
items.translation: (The evidence clearly points to the suspect.)
items.explanation: 'Hindeuten auf' (to point to/indicate) is a separable verb with the prefix 'hin-.' 'Die Beweise' (evidence, plural) is always plural in legal German. 'Eindeutig' (clearly/unambiguously) is a compound adjective: ein (one) + deutig (meaning). Compare: 'hinweisen auf' (to point out, more direct), 'sprechen für' (to speak for). German legal discourse values precision: Indiz (circumstantial evidence), Beweis (proof/evidence), Beweismittel (means of evidence).

items.question: Der Verbrecher ist aus dem Gefängnis _____.
items.explanation: 'Ausbrechen' (to break out/escape) is the standard term for prison escapes: 'aus dem Gefängnis ausbrechen.' Past participle: 'ausgebrochen' (strong verb). Compare: 'flüchten' → 'geflüchtet' (to flee — more general), 'entkommen' → 'entkommen' (to escape — inseparable, no ge-). 'Der Verbrecher' (criminal) comes from 'das Verbrechen' (crime/felony). Related: der Ausbruchsversuch (escape attempt), die Flucht (flight/escape).

items.question: Das Gesetz _____ die Rechte der Bürger.
items.translation: (The law protects the rights of citizens.)
items.explanation: 'Schützen' (to protect) is the standard legal term for law protecting rights. Compare: 'beschützen' (to protect a person — more personal/physical), 'verteidigen' (to defend — active), 'bewahren' (to preserve). 'Das Gesetz' (the law) — plural: 'die Gesetze.' 'Die Rechte' (rights) from 'das Recht' (right/law). 'Der Bürger' (citizen) forms the genitive plural without change. The German Grundgesetz (Basic Law/constitution) is the supreme legal document.

items.question: Die Bundeskanzlerin hat eine wichtige Rede _____.
items.explanation: 'Eine Rede halten' (to give/deliver a speech) is a fixed collocation. 'Halten' (to hold) has many idiomatic uses in German beyond its literal meaning. 'Die Bundeskanzlerin' is the feminine form of 'der Bundeskanzler' (Federal Chancellor) — the head of government in Germany. 'Wichtig' (important) is an essential adjective. German political titles: Bundespräsident (Federal President), Bundesminister (Federal Minister).

items.question: Bei der Wahl haben 70 Prozent der Bürger ihre Stimme _____.
items.translation: (In the election, 70 percent of citizens cast their vote.)
items.explanation: 'Seine Stimme abgeben' (to cast one's vote) is the standard election expression. 'Abgeben' is separable: past participle 'abgegeben.' 'Die Wahl' (election/choice) can mean both. 'Die Stimme' means both 'voice' and 'vote.' 'Bei der Wahl' uses dative. German elections: Bundestagswahl (federal election), Landtagswahl (state election), Kommunalwahl (local election). Voter turnout (Wahlbeteiligung) is typically high in Germany.

items.question: Die Regierung hat ein neues Gesetz _____.
items.explanation: 'Ein Gesetz verabschieden' (to pass/enact a law) is the correct political term. 'Verabschieden' is inseparable (ver-), past participle: 'verabschiedet' (no ge-). Don't confuse with 'sich verabschieden' (to say goodbye). 'Die Regierung' (government) is the executive branch. 'Beschließen' (to decide/resolve) is also used but more general. The legislative process: Gesetzentwurf (bill) → Lesung (reading) → Verabschiedung (passing) → Verkündung (promulgation).

items.question: Die Demonstration _____ friedlich.
items.explanation: 'Verlaufen' (to proceed/unfold) is the standard verb for describing how events transpired. Past tense: 'verlief' (inseparable, strong verb). 'Friedlich' (peacefully) comes from 'der Frieden' (peace). 'Die Demonstration' or colloquially 'die Demo' — the right to demonstrate (Demonstrationsrecht) is guaranteed by the German Grundgesetz (Article 8). Related: der Protest, die Kundgebung (rally), der Marsch (march).

items.question: Die Partei hat ihren _____ für die nächste Wahl vorgestellt.
items.translation: (The party presented its candidate for the next election.)
items.explanation: 'Der Kandidat' is a weak (n-declension) noun — in accusative, it becomes 'den Kandidaten.' Weak nouns add '-n' or '-en' in all cases except nominative singular. Other weak nouns in politics: der Präsident → den Präsidenten, der Demokrat → den Demokraten. 'Vorstellen' (to present/introduce) is separable. German political parties: CDU, SPD, Grüne, FDP, Linke, AfD. The multi-party system often requires coalition building.

items.question: Die Bürger _____ gegen die neuen Steuererhöhungen.
items.translation: (The citizens are protesting against the new tax increases.)
items.explanation: 'Protestieren gegen' (to protest against) takes accusative. In present tense, third person plural: 'die Bürger protestieren.' 'Die Steuererhöhung' (tax increase) is a triple compound: Steuer (tax) + Erhöhung (increase). German loves such compounds: Steuererklärung (tax return), Steuersenkung (tax cut), Steuerpflicht (tax obligation). Compound nouns take the gender of the last component.

items.question: Im Bundestag wird über den Haushalt _____.
items.translation: (The budget is being debated in the Bundestag.)
items.explanation: 'Debattieren' (to debate) is the formal parliamentary term. 'Wird debattiert' is present passive. 'Der Bundestag' is Germany's federal parliament (lower house). 'Der Haushalt' means both 'household' and 'budget' in political context. 'Im Bundestag' = 'in dem Bundestag.' The German parliament sits in the Reichstag building in Berlin. Related: der Bundesrat (upper house), die Sitzung (session), der Abgeordnete (member of parliament).

items.question: Die Meinungsfreiheit ist ein _____ Recht.
items.translation: (Freedom of expression is a fundamental right.)
items.explanation: 'Grundlegend' (fundamental/basic) comes from 'Grund' (foundation) + 'legend' (laying). With neuter 'Recht,' the strong adjective ending is '-es': 'ein grundlegendes Recht.' Don't confuse with 'gründlich' (thorough). 'Die Meinungsfreiheit' (freedom of expression/opinion) is protected by Article 5 of the Grundgesetz. German compound: Meinung (opinion) + Freiheit (freedom). These Grundrechte (fundamental rights) are outlined in Articles 1-19.

items.question: Die Koalition _____ aus drei Parteien.
items.explanation: 'Bestehen aus' (to consist of) requires dative: 'aus drei Parteien.' 'Bestehen' is inseparable (be- prefix). German coalition governments are common since no single party usually wins a majority. Famous coalitions: Große Koalition (grand coalition — CDU+SPD), Ampelkoalition (traffic light — SPD+Grüne+FDP), Jamaika-Koalition (CDU+Grüne+FDP, named after the flag colors). Koalitionsvertrag (coalition agreement) outlines shared policies.

items.question: Der Staat muss die Gleichberechtigung aller Bürger _____.
items.translation: (The state must ensure equal rights for all citizens.)
items.explanation: 'Gewährleisten' (to ensure/guarantee) is inseparable, past participle: 'gewährleistet.' It's more formal and legally binding than 'garantieren.' 'Die Gleichberechtigung' (equal rights) is a compound: gleich (equal) + Berechtigung (entitlement). 'Aller Bürger' uses genitive plural. Article 3 of the Grundgesetz establishes: 'Alle Menschen sind vor dem Gesetz gleich' (All people are equal before the law). German values precision in legal language.

items.question: Die Flüchtlinge haben in Deutschland _____ beantragt.
items.translation: (The refugees applied for asylum in Germany.)
items.explanation: 'Asyl beantragen' (to apply for asylum) is a fixed expression. 'Beantragen' (to apply for/request) is inseparable (be- prefix). 'Der Flüchtling' (refugee) adds '-e' for plural: 'die Flüchtlinge.' Article 16a of the Grundgesetz: 'Politisch Verfolgte genießen Asylrecht' (Politically persecuted persons have the right of asylum). Related: das Asylverfahren (asylum procedure), der Asylbewerber (asylum seeker), die Aufenthaltserlaubnis (residence permit).

items.question: Die Europäische Union _____ 27 Mitgliedstaaten.
items.explanation: 'Umfassen' (to comprise/encompass) is inseparable (um- prefix), fitting for describing the scope of an organization. 'Der Mitgliedstaat' (member state) is a compound: Mitglied (member) + Staat (state). Germany is a founding member of the EU. Related: der Binnenmarkt (internal market), die Europawahl (European election), der EU-Gipfel (EU summit). Germany's relationship with the EU is central to German political discourse.

items.question: Die Ministerin _____ sich für den Umweltschutz ein.
items.explanation: 'Sich einsetzen für' (to advocate for/commit to) is a reflexive separable verb. The prefix 'ein-' separates: 'setzt sich... ein.' 'Die Ministerin' is the feminine of 'der Minister.' 'Der Umweltschutz' (environmental protection): Umwelt (environment) + Schutz (protection). This verb is essential for political discourse — it conveys active commitment. 'Sich einsetzen' is stronger than 'befürworten' (to support/endorse).

items.question: Die Verhandlungen zwischen den Ländern waren _____.
items.translation: (The negotiations between the countries were successful.)
items.explanation: 'Erfolgreich' (successful) derives from 'der Erfolg' (success) + '-reich' (rich in). Don't confuse with 'erfolgt' (occurred — past participle of 'erfolgen'). 'Die Verhandlungen' (negotiations) is always plural. 'Zwischen den Ländern' uses dative after 'zwischen' (between — location). In political context, 'Land' means both 'country' and 'federal state.' Adjective endings: 'waren erfolgreich' — predicate adjectives don't decline.

items.question: Der Präsident hat die _____ des neuen Botschafters angenommen.
items.translation: (The president accepted the credentials of the new ambassador.)
items.explanation: 'Die Beglaubigung' (credentials/accreditation) is the diplomatic term for an ambassador's official papers. 'Beglaubigen' (to certify/accredit) is inseparable (be- prefix). 'Der Botschafter' (ambassador) works at 'die Botschaft' (embassy). 'Annehmen' (to accept) is separable: 'hat angenommen.' German diplomatic vocabulary: der Diplomat, das Konsulat (consulate), der Außenminister (foreign minister), die Außenpolitik (foreign policy).

items.question: Die Wissenschaftler haben eine neue Studie _____.
items.explanation: 'Veröffentlichen' (to publish) is inseparable (ver- prefix), past participle: 'veröffentlicht' (no ge-). 'Die Studie' (study) is feminine. 'Der Wissenschaftler' (scientist) derives from 'die Wissenschaft' (science/knowledge). German academic publishing: die Fachzeitschrift (specialist journal), der Aufsatz (paper/essay), die Abhandlung (treatise). Germany has a strong tradition of scientific research, with institutions like the Max-Planck-Gesellschaft.

items.question: Das Experiment hat interessante _____ geliefert.
items.explanation: 'Die Ergebnisse' (results) is the plural of 'das Ergebnis.' The neuter '-nis' ending forms plural with '-nisse' (→ Ergebnisse). 'Ergebnisse liefern' (to yield/deliver results) is a common academic collocation. 'Liefern' (to deliver) + past participle 'geliefert.' 'Das Experiment' uses the Latin-derived word directly. German academic vocabulary: die Hypothese (hypothesis), die Methode (method), die Analyse (analysis), die Schlussfolgerung (conclusion).

items.question: Die Forschung auf diesem _____ hat große Fortschritte gemacht.
items.translation: (Research in this field has made great progress.)
items.explanation: 'Das Gebiet' (field/area/domain) is the most common academic term for a research field. While 'Feld' and 'Bereich' can work, 'Gebiet' is preferred in scientific contexts. 'Fortschritte machen' (to make progress) is a fixed expression — 'der Fortschritt' (progress) is usually plural in this phrase. 'Die Forschung' (research) comes from 'forschen' (to research). German research institutions: Fraunhofer, Helmholtz, Leibniz-Gemeinschaft.

items.question: Die Hypothese konnte durch die Daten _____ werden.
items.translation: (The hypothesis could be confirmed by the data.)
items.explanation: 'Bestätigen' (to confirm/verify) is inseparable (be- prefix), past participle: 'bestätigt.' 'Konnte... werden' is the passive with modal verb in past tense. 'Die Hypothese' follows Greek origin. 'Die Daten' (data) is always plural in German (singular 'das Datum' means date). Scientific method terms: aufstellen (to formulate), überprüfen (to verify), widerlegen (to refute), belegen (to substantiate). Passive voice dominates German academic writing.

items.question: Der Professor _____ an der Universität Heidelberg.
items.explanation: 'Lehren' (to teach — at university level) vs. 'lernen' (to learn) — a critical distinction! 'Unterrichten' means to teach at school level. 'Studieren' means to study (at university). 'An der Universität' uses dative after 'an' (location). German academic titles matter: Professor/Professorin, Dozent/Dozentin (lecturer), Privatdozent (qualified lecturer). German universities (Heidelberg, founded 1386, is Germany's oldest) have a rich academic tradition.

items.question: Die Ergebnisse müssen noch _____ werden.
items.translation: (The results still need to be analyzed.)
items.explanation: 'Auswerten' (to analyze/evaluate data) is the scientific term for data analysis. Separable verb — past participle: 'ausgewertet.' Compare: 'bewerten' (to evaluate/grade — inseparable), 'verwerten' (to utilize/exploit), 'werten' (to value/judge). 'Müssen... werden' is passive with modal: 'must be analyzed.' 'Noch' (still/yet) indicates the analysis hasn't happened yet. Die Auswertung (analysis/evaluation) is the noun form.

items.question: Die Zelle _____ sich alle 24 Stunden.
items.explanation: 'Sich teilen' (to divide — reflexive) is the biological term for cell division. 'Die Zellteilung' (cell division) is a key biology concept. 'Alle 24 Stunden' (every 24 hours) uses 'alle + time period.' Compare: 'trennen' (to separate — objects), 'spalten' (to split — physical), 'brechen' (to break). German biology vocabulary: die Zelle (cell), der Zellkern (nucleus), die DNA/DNS (DNA), das Chromosom (chromosome), die Mitose (mitosis).

items.question: Der Klimawandel ist eine der größten _____ unserer Zeit.
items.translation: (Climate change is one of the greatest challenges of our time.)
items.explanation: 'Die Herausforderung' (challenge) comes from 'herausfordern' (to challenge). Don't confuse: 'Forderung' (demand), 'Anforderung' (requirement), 'Aufforderung' (request/prompt). 'Der Klimawandel' (climate change): Klima + Wandel (change). 'Eine der größten' (one of the greatest) uses genitive plural. Superlative 'größten' from 'groß' → 'größer' → 'am größten.' German environmental vocabulary: Treibhauseffekt (greenhouse effect), Erderwärmung (global warming).

items.question: Die künstliche _____ verändert viele Bereiche unseres Lebens.
items.translation: (Artificial intelligence is transforming many areas of our lives.)
items.explanation: 'Die künstliche Intelligenz' (artificial intelligence, KI) is the German equivalent of 'AI.' 'Künstlich' (artificial) from 'die Kunst' (art/skill). 'Verändern' (to change/transform) is inseparable (ver-). Compare: 'Klugheit' (cleverness), 'Weisheit' (wisdom), 'Vernunft' (reason) — each has a specific philosophical meaning in German. Technology terms often use English: Machine Learning, Big Data, but 'KI' is preferred over 'AI' in German.

items.question: Die Impfung hat sich als sehr _____ erwiesen.
items.translation: (The vaccination proved to be very effective.)
items.explanation: 'Wirksam' (effective/efficacious) from 'wirken' (to have an effect) + '-sam' (suffix indicating tendency). 'Sich erweisen als' (to prove to be) is a reflexive inseparable verb. 'Die Impfung' (vaccination) from 'impfen' (to vaccinate). Don't confuse: 'wirklich' (really/actual), 'wirkungsvoll' (effective — more emphasis on visible results). Medical German: die Nebenwirkung (side effect), die Wirksamkeit (efficacy), der Impfstoff (vaccine).

items.question: Die Forscher _____ an einem neuen Medikament.
items.translation: (The researchers are working on a new medication.)
items.explanation: 'Arbeiten an' (to work on) + dative is the standard phrase for ongoing work on a project. 'Die Forscher' (researchers) from 'forschen' (to research). 'Das Medikament' (medication) is neuter. While 'forschen' means research in general, 'arbeiten an' specifies working on a concrete project. German pharmaceutical terms: die Arznei (medicine), das Heilmittel (remedy), die klinische Studie (clinical trial), die Zulassung (approval).

items.question: Die Temperatur ist um fünf Grad _____.
items.explanation: 'Steigen' (to rise/climb) is a strong verb: steigen → stieg → gestiegen. It uses 'sein' as auxiliary: 'ist gestiegen.' 'Um fünf Grad' (by five degrees) — 'um' indicates the amount of change. 'Der Grad' (degree) stays singular after numbers: 'fünf Grad' (not 'Grade'). Compare: 'sinken' → 'gesunken' (to fall), 'zunehmen' → 'zugenommen' (to increase), 'abnehmen' → 'abgenommen' (to decrease). These verbs are essential for describing scientific measurements.

items.question: Die Entdeckung wurde _____ gemacht.
items.explanation: 'Zufällig' (by chance/accidentally) from 'der Zufall' (chance/coincidence). The umlaut and '-ig' ending are correct: zufällig. 'Die Entdeckung' (discovery) from 'entdecken' (to discover — inseparable). Many famous scientific discoveries were 'zufällig': Röntgenstrahlen (X-rays, discovered by Wilhelm Röntgen), Aspirin (developed by Bayer). German has given the world many scientific terms: Kindergarten, Angst, Zeitgeist, Wanderlust, and technical compounds.

items.question: Das Labor muss regelmäßig _____ werden.
items.explanation: 'Reinigen' (to clean/purify) past participle: 'gereinigt.' 'Muss... werden' is passive with modal verb. 'Das Labor' (laboratory) is shortened from 'das Laboratorium.' 'Regelmäßig' (regularly) from 'die Regel' (rule) + 'mäßig' (in accordance with). Compare: 'bereinigen' (to resolve/rectify — a situation), 'säubern' (to clean — more general), 'desinfizieren' (to disinfect). Lab vocabulary: das Reagenzglas (test tube), das Mikroskop, der Brenner (burner).

items.question: Die Theorie _____ bisher nicht widerlegt werden.
items.translation: (The theory has not been refuted so far.)
items.explanation: 'Konnte... widerlegt werden' (could not be refuted) combines past modal 'konnte' with passive infinitive 'widerlegt werden.' 'Widerlegen' (to refute/disprove) is inseparable (wider- prefix), past participle: 'widerlegt.' 'Bisher' (so far/until now) indicates the time frame. 'Die Theorie' (theory) follows Greek origin. Scientific discourse: bestätigen (confirm), widerlegen (refute), belegen (substantiate), hinterfragen (question/challenge). Passive with modals is typical of academic German.
